title: Lawrence County Coalition sends local students to DC for CADCA conference
byline: By Sara Morgan Black Hills Pioneer
description: LEAD — Students from Lead and Belle Fourche attended the Communities Anti-Drug Coalitions of America (CADCA) in Washington D.C. in February, where they gained valuable resources and trainings to empower educators and youths in their districts, spoke with legislators, and toured the capitol city.
_full_text:
LEAD — Students from Lead and Belle Fourche attended the Communities Anti-Drug Coalitions of America (CADCA) in Washington D.C. in February, where they gained valuable resources and trainings to empower educators and youths in their districts, spoke with legislators, and toured the capitol city.

Rhonda Britzman, Lead-Deadwood High School counselor, accompanied Starlett Canida, Shea Allen, and Isabell Wiegand to D.C. The trip is funded by the Lawrence County Coalition, who sponsors the trip for Spearfish School District and Lead-Deadwood on alternating years. Allen said the trip is part of her district’s continuing education for the Youth Empowerment Team. 

“CADCA is more like an advocacy group, so they do stuff more on a federal level,” said Allen. “They focus on treatment, resources for substance use, and mental health.”

Wiegand said those principals transfer neatly over to the Youth Empowerment Team, where they give quarterly presentations to the Lead-Deadwood middle and elementary schools about the dangers of substance abuse and the importance of mental health, including health fairs, teaching physical education classes, and being involved with Red Ribbon Week, the district’s campaign against drug abuse. 

“This last middle school health class was on Narcan — what it is, how to use it, and where it’s at,” said Wiegand. 

Narcan is an over-the-counter nasal spray that reverses the effects of an opioid overdose by rapidly bonding to the brain’s receptors, blocking opioids. 

Allen said the team focuses on promoting mental health resources as well. Recently, Allen said the team gave educators resources on the topic and made a presentation to the staff. 

Bringing education and resources home

Wiegand said CADCA focuses on prevention, believing that treatment costs can be lowered if children are educated young about the dangers of substances to their minds and bodies. 

In the prevention and education mindset, Allen said the group was allowed to choose from a list of workshops to choose which workshops would provide the most impact in Lead-Deadwood. 

“I liked the one where they were talking about how in schools, when people get caught with stuff instead of them getting in trouble right away, they could get treatment for it and get help instead of just getting in-school-suspension,” Canida said. “In-school-suspension doesn’t really help.”

“If they’re kicked out of the school, that’s not helping anybody,” said Allen. 

“If you think about it, you walk into the in-school-suspension room and you see the same faces every time,” said Wiegand. “It’s not like it’s changing anything.”

“And they’re getting in trouble for the same thing every time and they have no support at home,” said Allen. “So, if they have no support (in school), what’s different?”

The girls attended workshops on the dangers of product availability in gas stations, including a product dubbed “gas station heroin.” Products containing tianeptine are not approved by the Federal Drug Administration and are available to children without the requirement of an ID to purchase. 

“The presenter was talking about how a friend of hers had to go to rehab because she got addicted to things she bought legally at gas stations,” Wiegand said. “It looks like candy and it’s available right there. We learned how that affects kids growing up because their brains aren’t fully developed.”

Allen said her favorite workshop discussed how people’s childhood environments can put them into situations they wouldn’t have chosen for themselves.

“Sometimes people’s backgrounds, how they grow up and the type of situations that they’re put into, can affect what types of situations they’re put in as they become adults,” Allen said. “So, just recognizing that addiction isn’t necessarily anybody’s goal. A lot of the time you’re just playing into situations that you don’t necessarily want to be in and that’s just your life.”

Allen said she believes the team can use the resources to improve other student’s experiences in Lead-Deadwood. 

“We can take a lot of the skills that we learned from the workshops to cultivate a better school atmosphere or be a safe person that people can talk to about their mental health or if they are struggling,” said Allen. “We learned a lot about how to be that person at CADCA.”

Legislators, museums, and getting their steps in

While in D.C., students from Lead-Deadwood and Belle Fourche met with Sen. Mike Rounds, R-S.D. and gubernatorial candidate U.S. Rep. Dusty Johnson R-S.D., who gave the students House and Senate passes.

“We got to sit in the gallery and watch some votes, watched some speeches,” said Allen. 

Wiegand said watching the House and Senate was her favorite part of the trip. 

“I’ve watched it happen on the state level but seeing it on a bigger level was really cool,” said Wiegand. 

The students were given opportunities to speak with representatives, where they advocated for future funding of the Drug Free Communities Support Grant. 

“If we didn’t get that funding, this community specifically would suffer,” said Wiegand. “There are so many people that rely on that, and so many local programs that rely on it. If that went away it would be rough for this community. So, there was a big push for us to be there for us to be a voice for that, because that affects our community and that affects people in South Dakota.”

After a day at the Capitol, the students were able to visit the Holocaust Museum, 2 Smithsonian museums, the National Monument, and the Library of Congress. 

“We walked so many miles,” said Allen. “We walked for so long. (Britzman) didn’t want to get Ubers, so we walked everywhere.”

Students also attended a dance at their hotel, where students from across the globe attending the CADCA conference with a common goal. 

“We made a lot of friends while we were there,” said Allen. “Everyone there was really nice and inviting.”

“It felt like no one really felt left out and that was good,” said Canida. 

Bringing new materials into their senior years

Allen, Canida, and Wiegand said that they’re excited to bring new, updated, and relevant prevention materials back to Lead-Deadwood. 

“Our school has a lot of stuff that’s been used for 10 years,” said Wiegand. 

“The board and examples that we have in the middle school where there when I was in elementary school,” said Allen. “We have stuff about the dangers of cigarettes, when our focus now should be vaping.”

Wiegand said that the new materials will better prepare them to help prepare their students for an “ever changing battlefield.”

“That’s also why it’s important for (future students) to go to CADCA,” said Wiegand. “It’s going to be different and there’s going to be different ways of handling things, especially with mental health and prevention. It’s always evolving.”

title: Curtain call: Belle Fourche Area Community Theater gears up for series of one acts
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: Belle Fourche Area Community Theater is encouraging folks to secure their seats and watch “Curtain Call” at Community Hall this weekend.
_full_text:
Curtain Call 1.jpeg
Belle Fourche Area Community Theater is encouraging folks to secure their seats and watch “Curtain Call” at Community Hall this weekend.  

 Curtain Call 2.jpeg
Belle Fourche Area Community Theater prepares to perform a series of one act plays this weekend. Pioneer photo by Sierra Ferguson 

 Curtain Call 3.jpeg
Pioneer photo by Sierra Ferguson 

BELLE FOURCHE — This weekend, theater-goers in Belle Fourche will have the chance to sample a never-before-seen stage comedy, sink their teeth into a directorial debut, and savor a complex drama all in the course of a single evening.

Belle Fourche Area Community Theater (BFACT) is cooking up a veritable charcuterie board of one-act plays in “Curtain Call,” its first production of the year. Beginning Friday, performers will serve up a little something for every pop-culture palette — at least that’s the hope.

The menu of mini-shows is the result of a “director’s choice” approach to theater planning.

“It was a little bit of, ‘What do the directors want to do?’ Which was kind of fun, honestly,” said BFACT Executive Director Derek Olson. “We’ve definitely been encouraging our directors this year to pick shows that they are interested in. It is fun to be able to try something new. What do they want to have a go at?” 

Nurturing directors and trying new things seem to be points of focus for BFACT this year. The non-profit started 2026 hosting workshops that encouraged local thespians to acquire new skills after rounding out 2025 with a playwriting workshop.

“This year, we’ve got 3 directors with this show. 2 of the directors this was kind of their first time directing with us, 2 of them were actually involved with our playwriting festival last year. They did direct some of those short plays last year. They got a little bit of experience with that, but this is really their first time giving a go at a bigger, more official show. We are very excited about that,” Olson said.

Arianna Scherer is one of the directors who took part in last year’s workshop. This weekend, she is directing 2 of the 4 one-acts in “Curtain Call.” The shows are on opposite ends of the genre spectrum. She’ll tackle “Dissonance,” a drama that deals with grief, and “What’s Going On?” a 10-minute unconventional comedy.  

“The vibe in the room is very different when I am doing this comedy. I feel like, especially early in our rehearsals, I was like, ‘Energy! Energy! Energy!’ Trying to get them excited,” Scherer said. With ‘Dissonance,’ I kind of don’t want to talk (during rehearsal) because I don’t want to interfere with what they have going on. They get this really cool energy between them, and you want to stay out of it.”

Scherer wrote “What’s Going On?” specifically for “Curtain Call.”

“We wanted to add a bit more time to our production so that it feels worth the tickets that you are buying. Derek approached me about writing an original because I had taken part in the playwriting festival we did last year too. I have written and had something performed here already. He was like, ‘Something short. 10 minutes.’ Those were my parameters,” Scherer said. “I don’t usually write comedies, so I was pushing myself a little bit with it.”

Scherer played with concepts like breaking the fourth wall and is excited to see how audiences react to the brand-new show.

Crystal Woods, the director of “Will You Be My Nemesis?” a comedy that pokes fun at old-timey comic-book-based television programs, is also eager to find out how audiences receive her work.

“I just want to listen to the laughs. If they enjoy it as much as I do, that is going to be absolutely a pivotal moment for me,” Woods said.

She directed shows in high school and college, and has acted in BFACT productions, but this is her first time directing a community theater show in Belle Fourche.

“I have a really great cast. They are absolutely amazing,” Woods said. “This is a comedy show, but really at its base it’s about finding the person that you belong to.”

For Woods, the theme of belonging is much bigger than the one-act comedy she’s working on.

“BFACT gave me a place to belong,” Woods said. “I am a transgender individual, so it is the first time that I actually got to be myself on a stage and nobody felt weird or awkward about it. I was able to express myself. I really enjoyed it. They have just been very welcoming. I feel at home, and that it is a place where I can be creative. I love the stage.”

“Curtain Call” will be performed at Belle Fourche Community Hall on Friday and Saturday at 7 p.m. and Sunday at 3 p.m.

title: Powder House Pass looks to solve flooding issues
byline: By Sara Morgan Black Hills Pioneer
description: Stormwater drainage will be rerouted to the northwest in the fourth phase of the Powder House Pass housing development, ending flooding of some residents and the garage of the Lawrence County Search and Rescue building’s garage. Pioneer photo by Sara Morgan
_full_text:
Powder House Pass looks to solve flooding issues
Stormwater drainage will be rerouted to the northwest in the fourth phase of the Powder House Pass housing development, ending flooding of some residents and the garage of the Lawrence County Search and Rescue building’s garage. Pioneer photo by Sara Morgan 

DEADWOOD — Lawrence County Commissioners approved a plan March 10 from Advanced Engineering and Environmental Services (AE2S) of Spearfish, which reroutes the drainage in Powder House Pass’s fourth phase of development near Black Forest Road into a retaining pond, solving flooding and drainage issues. 

As development has continued in the housing development outside of Lead, existing drainage plans have caused flooding for some residents and the Lawrence County Emergency Management’s garage. 

In a technical memorandum, AE2S said that existing drainage patterns to the northeast have been restricted by stormwater, resulting in flooding in areas and an increased frequency and magnitude of flooding events. To address the concerns, a culvert is expected to be installed beneath Black Forest Road and a retention pond will be constructed. Existing stormwater pipes will be rerouted to the northwest.

Amber Vogt, director of planning and zoning, said these types of issues are “pretty normal” for large developments. 

“You end up having to move some stuff around and change some things,” Vogt said. “There’s kind of a natural drainage area around the search and rescue building and there’s all these structures out there now, so we had to readjust.”

Trent Walters, engineer for AE2S, said the retention pond will be built to provide temporary storage of stormwater, and will be designed to drain 1.9 cubic feet of water per second in the event of a 100-year event storm.  

Walters said that rerouting the stormwater drainage from the garage of the search and rescue building will require an eventual drainage easement from the county.

title: Governor’s approval opens  SD housing loan fund to  big-city airport projects
byline: By Seth Tupper Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: RAPID CITY — A state loan fund intended to incentivize housing construction will soon be available for airport improvement and maintenance projects in Sioux Falls and Rapid City.
_full_text:
SearchlightLogo

RAPID CITY — A state loan fund intended to incentivize housing construction will soon be available for airport improvement and maintenance projects in Sioux Falls and Rapid City.

The change is in legislation that lawmakers approved earlier this month and South Dakota Republican Gov. Larry Rhoden signed into law Tuesday while visiting the Rapid City Regional Airport.

“More gates, more flights and more destinations means more visitors, more business and more opportunity,” Rhoden said.

The money for the loans is held by the state’s Housing Infrastructure Financing Program. Legislators and then-Gov. Kristi Noem created the program three years ago. The money can be used for housing-related infrastructure such as streets, roads, sewer lines and water lines.

The fund has supported 85 projects across the state, helping create 7,421 single-family housing lots or units and 5,754 apartment or other multi-family units.

The program initially held a $200 million mix of state and federal funds. The state Housing Authority issued half of the money as grants. The other half, for loans, had about $65 million remaining as of late last year, which led the governor and lawmakers to propose tapping into the fund for airport projects.

The new law will take effect in July. It allows for loans from the fund to South Dakota airports in metropolitan statistical areas with populations of at least 125,000, which is a threshold that only the Sioux Falls and Rapid City areas currently meet. The loans will have 20-year terms at 2% annual interest.

Last year, lawmakers and Rhoden authorized a $15 million, zero-interest loan from the housing fund to help with construction of a new elementary school in the Douglas School District, based in Box Elder. The district, which borders Ellsworth Air Force Base, is experiencing rapid growth related to the impending addition of B-21 bomber planes.

This year’s bill is one of 210 that Rhoden has signed into law from the annual legislative session. Another bill Rhoden signed Tuesday will add “necessary site work” to the list of allowable uses for housing infrastructure money, and change the program’s maximum loans from one-third to one-half of total project costs.

Lawmakers concluded most of their work March 12 but will return Monday to the Capitol in Pierre to consider the governor’s vetoes. He has issued one veto so far against a ban on lab-grown meat, as part of his compromise support of a five-year moratorium that he approved.

title: Talks underway about state airplane purchase as new law requires consultation with expert panel
byline: By Joshua Haiar Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: Sen. Joy Hohn, R-Hartford, speaks in the South Dakota Senate at the Capitol in Pierre. Hohn was the sponsor of a new law that requires state departments to consult with the state Aeronautics Commission about aircraft purchases. Photo by Makenzie Huber, South Dakota Searchlight
_full_text:
Talks underway about state airplane purchase as new law requires consultation with expert panel
Sen. Joy Hohn, R-Hartford, speaks in the South Dakota Senate at the Capitol in Pierre. Hohn was the sponsor of a new law that requires state departments to consult with the state Aeronautics Commission about aircraft purchases. Photo by Makenzie Huber, South Dakota Searchlight 

PIERRE — When Republican state Sen. Joy Hohn, a pilot from Hartford, asked a state department head if he consulted with South Dakota’s Aeronautics Commission about an upcoming $5 million airplane purchase, the department head said he’d never heard of the commission.

That was despite the existence of a state law requiring the commission to advise state departments on aircraft purchases.

So, Hohn introduced a bill to strengthen the law. It passed both chambers of the Legislature, and the governor signed it earlier this month.

“It seemed obvious that the state should be using the commission of experts,” she said.

The governor-appointed, 7-member commission must have at least four members with aeronautics experience, and all of the current members are pilots. The commission is primarily tasked with helping to allocate funds for airport projects.

Existing state law says the commission “shall provide advice and expertise to state agencies regarding the purchase, transfer, and disposition of state-owned and operated aircraft, including those owned or operated by any state institution.”

Hohn and Rep. Tim Goodwin, R-Rapid City, the bill’s House sponsor, said they interpreted the law to mean agencies should already have been meeting with the commission about airplane purchases.

Gov. Larry Rhoden ’s spokesperson, Josie Harms, said in a statement that “the current law is permissive.”

“If a state agency wants advice or expertise, they can ask the Aeronautics Commission for it, and the Aeronautics Commission must give it,” Harms wrote. “The new law now requires the state agency to ask for that advice.”

Hohn’s Senate Bill 219, which takes effect in July, adds language clarifying that “a state agency shall consult the commission prior to purchasing, transferring, or disposing of state-owned and operated aircraft.” Hohn and Goodwin said the new language leaves no room for argument.

The new law also says the commission shall provide its advice and expertise in the form of a written report to the governor and the agency requesting the information. It does not require the governor or state agencies to follow the commission’s guidance.

Last week, Department of Public Safety officials discussed their proposed $5 million aircraft purchase with the commission, one day after the governor signed the bill.

A $5 million plan

The governor signed another bill Monday that would authorize up to $5 million in spending authority for the Highway Patrol to buy a plane.

Although the consultation law has yet to take effect, the Department of Public Safety has shared details about its chosen plane and fielded questions from Aeronautics Commission members. In a document shared with the commission March 11, the Highway Patrol explained its interest in a used, 35-foot-long, Kodiak 100 propeller plane to replace the 2004 Cessna it now uses to assist with high-speed pursuits and searches for missing people.

The patrol said the Kodiak would be an upgrade for law enforcement work because it can better handle camera equipment, carry heavier camera systems and use a mounting setup that would create less drag and fewer handling problems. The department would then sell the Cessna. A department spokesman did not provide an estimated value for the Cessna, but said the value will depend on whether it’s sold outright or traded.

Patrol officials spoke to lawmakers about the replacement on Jan. 20. Public Safety Secretary Bob Perry told the House Transportation Committee that the funding would allow the department to buy a more reliable aircraft less likely to break down.

“The aircraft we’re looking at are not luxury aircraft by any means,” Perry said.

Col. Casey Collins, the patrol’s commander, said the Cessna was down for about a quarter of last year for maintenance. A lot of that is tied to the airplane’s age, he said, and how long it takes to get replacement parts.

“We’ve had to reuse bolts for an exhaust, for example, where we shouldn’t probably be doing that,” Collins said. “The aircraft always gets an inspection, obviously, before we put it back up and we make sure it’s safe, but the aircraft is failing on a regular basis, way more than it should be.”

The last time the Highway Patrol purchased an aircraft was 2007, according to Department of Public Safety spokesman Brad Reiners.

During an Aeronautics Commission meeting last week, members suggested that the department consult additional experts on whether the Kodiak would be better than a Cessna, look into an aircraft called the Pilatus PC-12, and consult the commission throughout its deliberations.

According to Reiners, the department has not made a selection yet or engaged in the procurement process. He said in a written statement that “while we don’t have a deal pending, our goal is to find a suitable used plane and purchase updated camera equipment, for a total purchase not to exceed $5 million.”

South Dakota owns three planes, according to Department of Transportation spokesperson Julie Stevenson. The Department of Transportation has a King Air 90 and a King Air 350, and the Department of Public Safety has its Cessna 206.

The administration of former Gov. Kristi Noem acquired the King Air 350 for a net cost of $5 million after selling two older-model planes. It is used for state employee passenger travel. The King Air 90 is also used for passenger travel and providing aerial surveillance to assist with the fighting of wildfires.

title: 5 injured in  Black Hawk house fire
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: Firefighters work to extinguish a house fire in Black Hawk early Wednesday morning. Photo courtesy of the Black Hawk Volunteer Fire Department
_full_text:
5 injured in  Black Hawk house fire
Firefighters work to extinguish a house fire in Black Hawk early Wednesday morning. Photo courtesy of the Black Hawk Volunteer Fire Department 

BLACK HAWK — The Black Hawk Volunteer Fire Department, responded to a reported structure fire in the 6000 block of Polk St, incorporated Summerset, Meade County around 1:40 a.m. Wednesday.

Upon their arrival, firefighters found a free burning fire with a heavy smoke condition, issuing from the interior of a single story, residential structure with fire extending into the ceiling and roof assembly.

Additionally, fire extended to adjacent trees, vegetation, and residential structures, causing smoke, heat, fire damage and evacuations.

Rapid attack by firefighters contained the fire, stopping further threats to population and damage to infrastructure and other improvements, the incident did escalate to two alarms.

Of the 4 occupants of the structure, 3 received treatment on scene and were released, a fourth person was transported to Rapid City Monument Health by ambulance for further evaluation. A neighbor did receive treatment on scene for minor injuries and was released.

The American Red Cross Serving Central and Western South Dakota, family and neighbors are assisting the residents.

Unfortunately, 2 domestic animals perished as a result of the fire No injuries to firefighters, or livestock were reported.

Agencies assisting included: Pennington County 911, Meade County Dispatch, Meade County Sheriff’s Office, Summerset Police Department, Black Hawk Volunteer Fire Department, Black Hawk Fire Corp, Piedmont Fire and Ambulance Service, North Haines Volunteer Fire Department, Rapid City Fire Department and Ambulance Service, Whispering Pines Volunteer Fire Department, Ellsworth Air Force Base Fire Department, Box Elder Volunteer Fire Department, Rapid Valley Volunteer Fire Department, Doty Volunteer Fire Department, Johnson Siding Volunteer Fire Department, Black Hills Energy, Montana Dakota Utilities, American Red Cross Serving Central and Western South Dakota and the South Dakota Fire Marshalls Office.

title: Meade Co. Commissioners caution that special zoning option would not be quick
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: STURGIS — A special zoning district for the Interstate 90 corridor of Meade County would take more than a year to implement, and by then Simon Contractors plans to have a limestone mine fully operating, officials said Tuesday.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

STURGIS — A special zoning district for the Interstate 90 corridor of Meade County would take more than a year to implement, and by then Simon Contractors plans to have a limestone mine fully operating, officials said Tuesday.  

“If the intent on this special zoning district is to keep out one specific mine, I think you’ve missed the boat on that,” said Jennifer Sietsma, executive director of Black Hills Council of Local Governments. 

Amidst pushback from landowners about a proposed limestone mine that is planned near residential properties and along the Interstate 90 corridor, members of county staff and commissioners asked Sietsma to make a presentation about the special zoning district process during their regular meeting, Tuesday. Recently, landowners in that area have indicated to the county that they will initiate a petition, so voters can decide whether they want a special zoning district that could prevent mining near Piedmont. 

Meade County is one of many counties and municipalities that contract with Black Hills Council of Local Governments to help establish zoning, form comprehensive plans, craft disaster mitigation plans, and more. Sietsma said the process for the special zoning district is long and meticulous. It starts with a map of the zoning area, which cannot include any municipal areas. Then, she said there is a 20-day minimum for the county to display the map for affected residents and potential voters, something that needs to be done throughout the county, since the proposed special zoning district goes all the way up to Box Elder. 

Once the map has been displayed for the required time period, Sietsma said residents can get to work collecting signatures from one-third of the qualified voters who live within the map boundaries, but outside of any municipalities of the area. Sietsma said the number of signatures necessary wholly depends on how the boundaries of the map are drawn, and it could be between 2,700 to 4,300 signatures. Circulators could have 6 months to obtain the signatures. 

Once the signatures on the petitions are validated, a process which Sietsma said could take a few weeks due to the large number required, the county will be required to schedule a special election, for which only voters within the map boundary will be able to vote. 

If the voters approved the special zoning district, Sietsma said, nothing changes in the county right away. 

“You’re not going to be regulating anything until you’ve approved your rules for the zoning district,” Sietsma said. 

She added that when the county gets to the stage of making zoning rules, there will likely be an extended period of public comment. Since Meade County has never had a commercial district, setbacks, or any kind of zoning in the past, Sietsma acknowledged that the rules-making process could be lengthy. 

“We just did a zoning ordinance for a community, and it took 12 months,” she said. 

The only way the county could speed up the process, Sietsma said, is if the county staff started working on rules and regulations before the voters pass the special zoning district. But with limited staff, she said many government entities are not willing to expend the extra resources for an unknown election outcome. 

Commissioner Talbot Wieczorek said the process is necessarily drawn out, lengthy and complicated, because it involves governing how people can use their property. Additionally, as a county commissioner, he said his job is to make sure the special zoning district does not cause unintended consequences for the people. 

“One of the reasons this takes so long is you’re providing a map that is zoning everybody’s property,” Wieczorek said. “You get a lot of people who say ‘you’ve misjudged what my property is currently being used for.’ It’s a complicated process and it’s complicated so people have a voice. It’s appropriately complicated because you’re putting restrictions on people’s land.”  

If the petitioners move forward with a special zoning district, and that becomes the commission’s focus, Sietsma said she has concerns about the rest of the county. 

“You still leave a huge part of the county completely unprotected,” she said. “So if you have people in the county who want to preserve agriculture, they would be completely unprotected for the next big thing that wants to come in. I understand maybe they were the biggest opponent and the craziest discussion, but as people start finding that we have land and an abundance of it, I would guess that if you have no zoning in those areas that’s going to be one of the first places they look. I would worry about your ag folks not having an opportunity for a public hearing. If you go forward with just special zoning, you might feel like you left them out in the cold.” 

The process for a citizen-led petition for the special zoning district is slightly longer than if the county passed a resolution to designate zoning, Sietsma said. However, Commission Chairman Gary Deering said a key difference is that the county resolution can be referred to a vote, which would take the same amount of time or longer. In that scenario, Sietsma said, the county could pass a resolution by August or September, and citizens would have 20 days from the time it was published in the Black Hills Pioneer to submit a petition to refer the action to a vote. If there is a referral, the county would have to schedule an election within 60 days. If the election was favorable, it could be even longer before rules were put into place, due to required public comment periods. 

“It could take about the same amount of time, but the commission driving it would be at risk for referral,” Deering said. “The citizen driven one cannot be referred.” 

Sietsma said in her experience of helping governmental entities implement zoning, a realistic timeframe for getting it done is about 15 to 18 months, when her staff is actively working on it. However, she said the organization that includes four employees and serves all governments in western South Dakota, is actively working a backlog of contracts to provide various services. If Meade County wants to contract with Black Hills Council of Local Governments for help with zoning regulations, they will have to get in line, she said. 

Wieczorek said, no matter how the zoning is presented, residents need to realize that the process cannot be done quickly. 

“I don’t want your expectations to be that this can be done overnight,” he said. “I want to be clear on the process and the time.” 

The discussion came after Piedmont and Summerset residents approached the county with a proposed map for a special zoning district, and a plan to begin collecting signatures for a citizen-driven election. No official decisions have been made on the map for the proposed district.

title: Sturgis Alliance of Churches baked potato feed  raises more than $3,000 for community needs
byline: By Tim Potts Black Hills Pioneer
description: Bill Ateyo and Ginnie Jones serve up the toppings for the baked potato to Ross and Jan Lamphere during the Sturgis Alliances of Churches baked potato feed on Monday at the Sturgis Community Center.  Pioneer photo by Tim Potts
_full_text:
1. alliance.JPG
Bill Ateyo and Ginnie Jones serve up the toppings for the baked potato to Ross and Jan Lamphere during the Sturgis Alliances of Churches baked potato feed on Monday at the Sturgis Community Center.  Pioneer photo by Tim Potts 

STURGIS — A strong show of community support filled the Sturgis Community Center on Monday evening, as the Sturgis Alliance of Churches raised more than $3,000 during its annual baked potato feed to assist local individuals and families in need.

The event drew a steady crowd throughout the evening, with volunteers serving up baked potatoes and a variety of toppings, including chili and cheese.

“This is our annual baked potato feed that we do here at the Sturgis Community Center, and we get such incredible support,” said Grace Lutheran Church Pastor Kevin Jensen. “As you can see, the gymnasium is almost full, and we’ve had a steady stream of people come in. We prepared about 200 baked potatoes, and people are truly enjoying themselves. It’s a wonderful community event.”

Beyond the fellowship, the fundraiser plays a vital role in supporting those facing financial hardship in the Sturgis area.

“There are people who come to Sturgis all the time in need,” Jensen said. “They may need help with gas, food, or support for their families. There are also local residents whose utility bills have become overwhelming. We help where we can, and events like tonight really allow us to meet those needs.”

Individuals seeking assistance begin the process by filling out a request form through Deb Looby at the Sturgis high-rise. From there, requests are reviewed and directed toward available resources within the alliance.

The Sturgis Alliance of Churches includes about 20 congregations, with roughly a dozen actively participating on a regular basis. The organization also partners closely with Love INC, which, while not a church, plays a key role in coordinating efforts.  

“It’s truly a team effort,” Jensen said. “We can’t do it alone as individual churches, so working together is essential.”

Looby, treasurer of the alliance, said the demand for assistance remains high and continues to grow.  

“There’s a big need right now for rent and utilities, that’s our primary focus,” Looby said. “Groceries are becoming an even greater need, and gas assistance is starting to rise again as well.”

In 2025 alone, the alliance provided approximately $20,000 in assistance through around 400 vouchers, not including additional support for energy assistance programs.

Community members who would like to contribute can mail donations to: Sturgis Alliance of Churches, P.O. Box 713, Sturgis, SD 57785. Checks should be made payable to the Sturgis Alliance of Churches.

“We’re always grateful for the support,” Looby said. “It truly makes a difference in people’s lives.”

title: Monument Health Spearfish named a  top 100 rural hospital
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: Monument Health Spearfish ranks in the top 100 rural hospitals, according to Chartis Center for Rural Health. Image courtesy Monument Health
_full_text:
Monument Health Spearfish named a  top 100 rural hospital
Monument Health Spearfish ranks in the top 100 rural hospitals, according to Chartis Center for Rural Health. Image courtesy Monument Health 

SPEARFISH — Monument Health Spearfish has been named a 2026 Top 100 Rural Hospital by the Chartis Center for Rural Health. Chartis uses publicly available data to index the performance of rural hospitals across the country.

“It’s an honor and a privilege for our teams to serve the community,” said Thomas Worsley, Spearfish Monument Hospital president. “As we have worked diligently to provide great care, particularly in the midst of a transformational campus construction project, it’s a great honor to be recognized amongst these other fine hospitals. ”

Chartis has crafted its index for 16, tracking hospital performance in areas like quality of care, patient perspectives, and financial management.  

“The delivery of care within rural communities is more complex today than at any point in recent memory. This year’s Top 100 Critical Access Hospitals and Top 100 Rural & Community Hospitals have emerged as true leaders—committed to their mission through a powerful combination of resilience, dedication, and innovation,” said Michael Topchik, Executive Director of The Chartis Center for Rural Health. “We’re delighted to recognize this year’s winners and celebrate their Top 100 status.”

title: Data center restrictions signed into  SD law after push for incentives failed
byline: By Joshua Haiar Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: PIERRE — After lawmakers rejected proposals to incentivize the construction of large data centers in South Dakota, the governor signed a bill into law Tuesday that will place new limits on the industry.
_full_text:
SearchlightLogo

PIERRE — After lawmakers rejected proposals to incentivize the construction of large data centers in South Dakota, the governor signed a bill into law Tuesday that will place new limits on the industry.

The law applies to data centers with a peak electrical demand of 10 megawatts or greater. It will require data center companies to ensure their water use does not overburden local resources and to pay for the electrical infrastructure costs attributable to them. It also prohibits the state from overriding local ordinances limiting, prohibiting or otherwise regulating data centers.

Another bill Rhoden signed into law Tuesday allows the state Public Utilities Commission to assess data center companies the costs of regulatory reviews related to their projects.

The laws emerged from a broader fight about data centers during the recently concluded legislative session. 

Economic developers and some lawmakers unsuccessfully supported incentives such as a 50-year sales tax exemption on the equipment and software purchases for data centers, or the expansion of an existing sales tax rebate program to accommodate data centers.

Another bill that failed would have exempted large backup generators that are not connected to the grid — such as those used by large data centers — from regulatory and siting review by the state Public Utilities Commission.

Backers of those bills said data centers could bring more jobs to the state and more property tax revenue to local governments. Opponents raised concerns about the amount of water used by data centers for cooling, and the potential effect of data centers’ electrical demands on the rates of everyday electrical customers.

South Dakota’s biggest data center consumes a maximum of 30 megawatts, and the state has none of the vastly larger data centers that have proliferated elsewhere in response to the growth of artificial intelligence, cryptocurrency and other data-intensive industries.

title: ‘13 Reasons Why’ to remain on middle school library shelf after petition to remove it
byline: By Mark Watson Black Hills Pioneer
description: The Spearfish School Board voted to keep the book “13 Reasons Why” in the middle school library after a community member petitioned to have the book removed and placed in the high school library. Pioneer photo by Mark Watson
_full_text:
‘13 Reasons Why’ to remain on middle school library shelf after petition to remove it
The Spearfish School Board voted to keep the book “13 Reasons Why” in the middle school library after a community member petitioned to have the book removed and placed in the high school library. Pioneer photo by Mark Watson 

SPEARFISH — The Spearfish School Board declined to remove a book, challenged by a Spearfish pastor, which is in the middle school library.

Rev. Thomas Brown, the pastor at St. Paul Lutheran Church, petitioned the school board to have the book, “13 Reasons Why”, removed.

“This book, ‘13 Reasons Why’, details the 13 reasons why Hannah Baker chose to kill herself, to commit suicide,” Brown told school board members at their March meeting. “It contains two graphic depictions of sexual assault. One of them, she was a witness. One of them, she was a victim. In the case of her (assault), when she was the victim … she went into quite a bit of detail. It’s vivid. It’s graphic. So much so that at one point the narrator stops to ask if it’s too graphic to continue. I really wish I could share this with you tonight this section of the book, because I think it would make your decision easy for you. But I also know it would not be appropriate, in this context, to share those words. It is an open building, an open shelf and a building full of 12-year-olds over at the middle school library.”

He asked that the book be relocated to the high school library.

In material he presented to school board members, he said that he read the entire book Nov. 21-Dec. 1, and spoke with the middle school librarian and principal, per district policy, before he petitioned the school board to have the title removed from the middle school library. 

He added that the book also discusses ways that Baker might kill herself, and that the 13th reason why she chose to take her own life is when she went to discuss her problems, the school counselor was distracted and dismissive. 

“He didn’t have time for her, and so when the counselor failed her, Hannah goes home and considers how to kill herself,” Brown said. 

The book ends, he said, with Baker taking her own life. 

“There’s no intervention. There’s no rescue. No one comes to help her,” Brown said. “To any struggling teenager in this district who reads this book, the message is clear, the adults here will fail you too. So, I’m here to ask you tonight, as the school board, not to fail our kids. This isn’t a message that you want to send.”

Alesha Limbo, a school board member, opened the discussion.

Limbo has advocated for mental health awareness and suicide awareness. And she is in a unique position in her role as a school board member. She survived a suicide attempt more than 20 years ago and had a foster daughter who tool her own life. 

She said a committee of 7 formed to discuss Brown’s request. Members on the committee included herself, teachers, an administrator, as well as parents of students. 

“There was a group of us, and we all read the book, and had very powerful conversations on it,” Limbo said. “And there’s definitely some heaviness to the book. So, we did not feel that it would be appropriate for sixth or seventh graders, and we didn’t feel it would be appropriate for just any eighth graders as well.”

“We’re dealing with a lot of mental health issues, not just in school, but just in in society as a whole,” she later told the Pioneer. “We had a lot of powerful conversations, even in that meeting, of mental health awareness and suicide prevention and suicide health awareness as well, how can we help those around us?”

She added at the board meeting that student can’t simply take the book off the shelf and check it out. It is only available for checkout by eighth graders and then, only with expressed written parental permission. The book is located directly in front of the librarian’s desk.

She said the librarians would add a sticker on each copy referring people who may be struggling with suicidal ideation, to 988, the suicide and crisis hotline.

“We did feel that that was adequate for this book, so that’s why we came to that conclusion,” she said and noted the committee reached their decision unanimously.

Superintendent Kirk Easton joined the conversation addressing Brown’s. He discussed the Miller Test.

“The Miller Test that was established by the courts, and I’ll just read it really quick. It says, ‘the Miller Test, established in 1973 Supreme Court case, Miller versus California, consists of three prongs that must all be satisfied for the material to be deemed obscene,’” Easton said. “Prurient interest. The work must appeal to a prurient interest in sex, meaning it must be intended to incite lustful thoughts or desires.’ Second one. ‘Patently offensive work. The work must depict or describe sexual conduct in a patently offensive way, as defined by community standards.’ And number three. ‘Lack of serious value. The work, taken as a whole, must lack serious literary, artistic, political or scientific value. This prong is often interpreted through a national standard, rather than local community standards as established in Pope versus Illinois.’ So, this is what I asked the committee to consider, is utilizing the Miller Test. And Dr. Brown is correct that the board’s decision can be appealed to a circuit court, and they would apply the same test.”

In documents provided to the board, Brown agreed that the book passed the Miller Test.

Board Member Nathan Hoogshagen asked how many times the book had been checked out. 

Limbo didn’t have an exact number but said it had been checked out very seldomly.

The board voted to keep the book in the middle school library with Hoogshagen opposing the motion.

title: Lead moves forward with  GIS mapping system
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: LEAD — A new GIS mapping system will be a complete electronic record of every water line, building, and major infrastructure piece in Lead, City Administrator Robin Lucero told the Lead City Commission, Monday.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

LEAD — A new GIS mapping system will be a complete electronic record of every water line, building, and major infrastructure piece in Lead, City Administrator Robin Lucero told the Lead City Commission, Monday. 

The city commission unanimously approved spending $2,000 to finish a GIS mapping system that will include every building in Lead, as well as $160 per month subscription to the GIS website, for 5 months, to train staff in how to use the technology. 

Lucero told the commission the city has worked since the early 2000s to overlay all of Homestake’s paper maps of the water system, into an electronic GIS system. At that time, she said the city paid an engineering company $50,000 to create electronic water maps that shows every water tank, fire hydrant, and all water supply zone in the city. Now, Lucero said the city is working on maps of every building in town, to overlay on the water maps in the GIS system. 

“This will be the footprint of every building in the city of Lead, and that will be tied to the water meter and water usage,” Lucero said. “We can use this to find areas of town where there may be excessive water usage. If there is property that has a really high water bill, that doesn’t usually have a high water bill, we can identify them.” 

Lead Public Works Director Jason Bauernfeind said the system will save the city thousands of dollars. 

“With SCADA, GIS, our correlator, and a dedicated crew, we are consistently reducing water loss each year and saving the city a significant amount of money,” he said.  

In 2018, Lucero said the city lost $180,000 a year in water. But by 2024, that water loss was down to $68,000 a year, and in 2025 it was $78,000. 

“Our main focus right now is updating our water and sewer maps to make those Homestake maps more accurate,” Lucero said. “It’s going to help if they get a call to find a curb stop to turn the water off in the ice, and they’re digging to find it. Instead of having a 30-foot radius where the curb stop is, they’re going to know within 11 inches. So that will make everything more efficient.” 

Once the building and water maps are completed, Lucero said other city departments can input other GIS maps into the system. By doing this, she said citizens can go online to see interactive maps showing emergency snow routes, garbage routes, historic preservation locations, and more. 

“It’s a big time saver,” said Mayor Dustin Heupel. “It’s one of those things we need to update. Paper maps are going by the wayside and they are less accurate. This will be a huge benefit. The possibilities are really endless what we can do with it.”

title: Sturgis Golden K Kiwanis soup  supper raises funds for area youth
byline: By Tim Potts Black Hills Pioneer
description: The Sturgis Golden K Kiwanis served up soup during the annual spring soup supper fund raiser on Sunday at the Community Center. From left are:  Al Weidenbach, Barry Furze, Deb Loobey, and Bill Ateyo.  Pioneer photo by Tim Potts
_full_text:
Sturgis Golden K Kiwanis soup  supper raises funds for area youth
The Sturgis Golden K Kiwanis served up soup during the annual spring soup supper fund raiser on Sunday at the Community Center. From left are:  Al Weidenbach, Barry Furze, Deb Loobey, and Bill Ateyo.  Pioneer photo by Tim Potts 

STURGIS — The Sturgis Golden K Kiwanis Club brought the community together Sunday for its annual spring soup supper, raising nearly $2,000 to support youth programs throughout the Sturgis area.

A total of 157 community members attended the event, enjoying a variety of five homemade soups, along with salads and desserts. Additional donations helped push the fundraiser total to the $2,000 mark.

“This is our spring soup supper, and we host a similar event twice a year, once in the fall and once in the spring,” said club President Richelle Bruch. “It’s a great way to bring people together while supporting a cause that means a lot to us.”  

For $10, attendees were able to enjoy an all-you-can-eat meal, with all proceeds going directly back into the community. The Kiwanis Club focuses its efforts on supporting youth and families across Sturgis.

Bruch said the funds raised play a vital role in a wide range of local initiatives. Among the club’s ongoing efforts, Kiwanis members provide school lunches twice a year for students at Sturgis elementary, middle, and high schools. They also distribute dictionaries annually to third-grade students in the Meade School District.

Beyond those core programs, the organization supports numerous youth-focused opportunities and nonprofits.  “We also donate to local organizations that serve kids, whether that’s helping the public library with summer programs, supporting local 4-H groups, or assisting students with travel opportunities like educational trips to Washington, D.C.,” Bruch said. “If there’s a need that helps youth grow, whether it’s a basic necessity or helping fulfill a dream, we want to be part of that.”

Bruch, who has served as President since October 2025, said events like the soup supper highlight the generosity and spirit of the Sturgis community.

“It’s amazing to see people come out, enjoy a meal, and know they’re making a difference,” she said.

With continued support from the community, the Sturgis Golden K Kiwanis Club plans to carry on its mission of investing in the next generation, one event at a time.

title: Medal of Honor Day
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: SIOUX FALLS — Today, the nation will unite to observe Medal of Honor Day, a day dedicated to honoring the courage, heroism, and sacrifice of the men and women who have earned the United States highest military distinction, the Medal of Honor.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

SIOUX FALLS — Today, the nation will unite to observe Medal of Honor Day, a day dedicated to honoring the courage, heroism, and sacrifice of the men and women who have earned the United States highest military distinction, the Medal of Honor.

Established by President Abraham Lincoln in 1861, the Medal of Honor is awarded to members of the U.S. Armed Forces who distinguish themselves through acts of valor performed above and beyond the call of duty. Each recipient’s bravery embodies the very spirit of service and selflessness that defines America’s armed forces.

To date, more than 3,500 service members have received the Medal of Honor. Their acts of heroism span every major U.S. conflict, from the Civil War to the Global War on Terrorism. These individuals risked their lives to protect their fellow service members and civilians, demonstrating great personal sacrifice. 

A Medal of Honor tribute is displayed on the first floor of the west wing of the South Dakota State Capitol, honoring 10 veterans with ties to our state. Each name is accompanied by the community where their memorial can be found:

U.S. Army, Brigadier General Charles D. Roberts (Fort Meade)

U.S. Army, Captain Willibald C. Bianchi (Brookings)

U.S. Marine Corps, Captain Joe J. Foss (Sioux Falls)

U.S. Army, Captain Arlo L. Olson (Toronto)

U.S. Marine Corps, Private First-Class Herbert A. Littleton (Spearfish)

U.S. Army Master Sergeant Woodrow W. Keeble (Sisseton)

U.S. Air Force, Colonel Leo K. Thorsness (Brookings)

U.S. Air Force, Colonel George E. “Bud” Day (Vermillion)

U.S. Army, General Patrick H. Brady (Hot Springs)

U.S. Army, Specialist Four Michael J. Fitzmaurice (Hot Springs)

U.S. Navy, Captain Elmer Royce Williams

“Medal of Honor Day reminds us that freedom is not simply given, but safeguarded through extraordinary courage and unwavering sacrifice,” said Jeremiah Scheider, Secretary of the South Dakota Department of Veterans Affairs. “The heroes we honor today faced unimaginable challenges and chose valor over fear, showing us that true strength lies in selfless service. Their legacy calls on us to live with the same resolve, to support those who serve, and to ensure that their example continues to light the path for generations to come.”

title: S.D. Community Foundation earns national accreditation
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: PIERRE — The South Dakota Community Foundation (SDCF) was recently re-accredited through the Community Foundations National Standards program.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

PIERRE — The South Dakota Community Foundation (SDCF) was recently re-accredited through the Community Foundations National Standards program. 

SDCF President and Chief Executive Officer Stephanie Judson said this accreditation signifies that SDCF is committed to using best practices identified for community foundations nationwide, and to demonstrating accountability and excellence in its operations.

“Accreditation is a rigorous process that involves a third-party review,” Judson said. “SDCF takes price in the work that we do to build philanthropy across South Dakota, and it’s rewarding to  be recognized for meeting the national benchmarks for community foundations.” 

SDCF works with 90 communities across South Dakota through its Community Savings Accounts program and hundreds of nonprofit and charitable organizations. SDCF engages in the accreditation process every three years. The process looks at whether an organization is compliant with 26 national standards. The standards cover five areas: mission, structure and governance, resource development, stewardship and accountability, grant making, and donor relations. 

“Earning accreditation tells our donors, financial advisors, and nonprofit partners that we’re a credible organization with sound policies and practices — making us a trustworthy partner for meeting their need and goals,” said Dennis Daugaard, former governor of South Dakota and current chairman of the SDCF board of directors.

title: Prison rehabilitation, recidivism ‘quickly forgotten’  by lawmakers this session, legislator says
byline: By Makenzie Huber Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: SIOUX FALLS — Last year, lawmakers coalesced around the need to improve rehabilitation programming and reduce recidivism rates as part of a broader investment in South Dakota’s prison system. Those two issues were sticking points in an ultimately successful effort to approve construction of a men’s prison.
_full_text:
SearchlightLogo

SIOUX FALLS — Last year, lawmakers coalesced around the need to improve rehabilitation programming and reduce recidivism rates as part of a broader investment in South Dakota’s prison system. Those two issues were sticking points in an ultimately successful effort to approve construction of a men’s prison.

Yet during the 2026 legislative session that wrapped up earlier this month, few bills were brought forward to address inmate rehabilitation needs, lawmakers on a Downtown Sioux Falls Rotary Club panel said on Monday.

One bill passed that makes it easier for people released from prison to return to their home area, rather than remaining in the area the prison is located. It requires the Department of Corrections to transport released people to their home county, the county where they were sentenced, or to a location equivalent in distance to the closer of those two options. Gov. Larry Rhoden signed the bill into law earlier this month.

Another bill would have awarded $2.7 million to the state Department of Corrections to expand an existing rehabilitation program within the prison system, but it failed late in the session.

Rep. Brian Mulder, R-Sioux Falls, introduced the funding bill. He told attendees that the “prison population is overwhelming the state and how we operate.”

“Although that was the number one priority last year, it was quickly forgotten for this year,” Mulder said.

Half the people released from prison in South Dakota return within three years, according to the Department of Corrections’ 2025 annual report — the highest recidivism rate in at least the last 8 years. Lawmakers and prison reform advocates have pointed to a lack of rehabilitation programming as a cause, among other issues.

The drive to address rehabilitation needs this session was pushed aside by the desire to provide property tax relief across the state, Mulder told South Dakota Searchlight after the panel discussion. The result is “another year lost” in strengthening rehabilitation as a new Rapid City Women’s Prison nears completion and the state begins construction on a men’s prison in Sioux Falls that will replace the oldest parts of the penitentiary.

“When we’re not releasing individuals to the best of their potential from our prison, it just puts a strain on our community overall,” Mulder told Searchlight, adding, “it’s just spinning our wheels, costing us more and more dollars.”

He told members of the Rotary Club that lawmakers and state officials will “continue to have that conversation” through the state Correctional Rehabilitation Task Force. Gov. Larry Rhoden formed the group of lawmakers, government officials and nonprofit leaders last year while lawmakers considered approving the $650 million men’s prison.

The task force is focused on behavioral health, educational, faith-based and Native American-themed programs. Its next meeting will be in April, Lt. Gov. Tony Venhuizen confirmed on Monday.

Venhuizen told attendees he expects rehabilitation and recidivism legislation will be a “major topic” next winter as the task force provides recommendations. The inmate release bill from this session will put the responsibility on the task force to ensure post-release services are available across the state, he said, not just concentrated in Sioux Falls or Rapid City.

“So, if they go back to Mitchell or Chamberlain, or wherever they wind up, that they have the services there to be successful, too,” Venhuizen said, “because we don’t want repeat customers in this business.”

House Minority Leader Erin Healy, D-Sioux Falls, said she believes the task force and conversation around rehabilitation is a progressive move. Where South Dakota can improve, she said, is prevention and diversion.

That includes analyzing data and trauma-based care options to ensure South Dakotans “live a life where they thrive and don’t necessarily go down the wrong pathway.”

title: Sturgis mayor resigns amidst health issues
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: Kevin Forrester
_full_text:
Sturgis mayor resigns amidst health issues
Kevin Forrester 

STURGIS — Sturgis Mayor Kevin Forrester has resigned due to health reasons. 

Forrester officially notified the council about his resignation, Monday. On Tuesday, Sturgis City Council President Tony Dargatz and City Administrator Aaron Jordan said Dargatz will assume the role of mayor pro tem, until a new mayor is appointed or elected. The city council plans to hold a special meeting at 6:30 p.m. tonight, to discuss their next steps and the process they will follow for appointing a mayor until a special election can be held. Dargatz said the city could possibly hold a special election that coincides with the June 2 primary election. 

State law and Sturgis city ordinance states that if there is a vacancy from any cause in the office of the mayor, the vacancy may be filled by appointment by a majority vote of the council members, as soon as practicable after the vacancy occurs, to serve until the office is filled by election for the unexpired term at the next annual municipal election or by special election called for that purpose, as provided in SDCL 9-13-14 and city code.

Forrester’s mayoral term was not set to expire until 2028. 

Last fall, Forrester was diagnosed with pancreatic cancer. Since then, Jordan said Forrester has been busy working with city staff and the council, to ensure continuity of operations if he had to resign.

“Good leadership means planning ahead, not out of fear, but out of responsibility,” Forrester said in a prepared statement. “Those conversations were not difficult because our organization is strong. We have capable leadership, clear processes, and a team that understands how important stability is for our residents and for one another. The city will continue to function effectively, thoughtfully, and professionally.” 

Jordan and Dargatz said Tuesday that the resignation was not a surprise. 

Dargatz said he has been honored to serve on the council with Forrester, whom he said completely turned the city around during a turbulent time. 

“Kevin, he took the city when it was doom and gloom, and he has turned it into a well-oiled machine, in my eyes,” Dargatz said. “We have a great team and a great staff, and we’ll get through this. 

“One of the things when Kevin was appointed mayor, he hit the ground running,” Jordan said. “We were working through the appropriations, and so he basically took that by the bullhorn and kind of wrestled that through, working with the council and the staff. He had a vision of what he wanted, and from day one he was working towards it. It didn’t take a lot of time for him to determine that.”

Jordan said Forrester’s legacy as mayor has been advocating for transparency, fiscal responsibility, and building a strong and resilient team. 

“We’ve had a lot of turnover with the last 24 months, and one of his emphases to me is that he wanted to build some resiliency with the staff and the council,” Jordan said. “I think we are seeing that in spades here with this resignation. We still have our continuity of our operations and continuity of our government.” 

Forrester, who is a lifelong resident of Sturgis, was appointed in March 2021to the Sturgis City Council to fill the seat of Steve Keszler in Ward 4. He served in that capacity until he was appointed mayor in October 2024, after former mayor Angela Wilkerson resigned. He served the remainder of Wilkerson’s term, and was officially elected to the office in January 2025, when he ran unopposed in the regular election. 

Dargatz said that while he is prepared to serve as interim mayor temporarily, he would prefer to retain his Ward 3 council seat. He does not intend to run for the mayor’s position.

title: Tipoff today BHSU in Elite 8
byline: By Jason Gross Black Hills Pioneer
description: Black Hills State University’s Tristan Hurdle passes through the defense during a recent men’s basketball game. Hurdle and the Yellow Jackets will face Nova Southeastern in their first NCAA Division II Elite 8 contest today. Pioneer file photo
_full_text:
Tipoff today BHSU in Elite 8
Black Hills State University’s Tristan Hurdle passes through the defense during a recent men’s basketball game. Hurdle and the Yellow Jackets will face Nova Southeastern in their first NCAA Division II Elite 8 contest today. Pioneer file photo 

PITTSBURGH, Pa. — Black Hills State University will tip off against Nova Southeastern at 4 p.m. today as the NCAA Division II Elite 8 men’s basketball tournament continues its opening day of games.

The eighth-seeded Yellow Jackets bring a 30-4 record and 13-game winning streak into their game against the defending NCAA Division II national champion and top seed Nova Southeastern. 

Black Hills State defeated Dallas Baptist 76-62 on March 17 to claim the South Central Region tournament title.

Nova Southeastern has won its last 24 games and brings a 30-1 record into today’s game. The Sharks (based in Davie, Florida) defeated The University of Alabama in Huntsville 79-63 on March 17 to earn the South Region tournament title.

Wednesday’s first game pits third-seeded Gannon facing number 6 seed Michigan Tech at 10 a.m. Gannon is located in Erie, Pennsylvania; Michigan Tech is based in Houghton.

Gannon brings a 31-3 record into the Elite 8 and defeated Indiana University (Pennsylvania) 81-74 in the Atlantic Region championship game on March 17. The Golden Knights have won their last 3 games.

Michigan Tech has posted a 29-6 record so far this season. The Huskies conquered Walsh (Ohio) 87-70 to secure the Midwest Region title and win their 3rd straight game.

Cal State East Bay is the tournament’s number 2 seed and faces number 7 Oklahoma Baptist in Wednesday’s second game set to begin at 12:30 p.m.

The Cal State East Bay Pioneers are based in Hayward, Calif., and are 33-0 this season. They downed Point Loma 73-58 for the West Region championship on Marcy 17.

Oklahoma Baptist defeated Minnesota-Duluth 80-76 on March 17 to win the Central Region tournament crown. The Oklahoma team (30-3 on the season, with 3 straight wins) is based in Shawnee.

Wednesday’s final game will pit the number 4 seed from Damaen against the fifth seed from Lander. Daemen is located in Amherst, N.Y.; Lander is in Greenwood, S.C.

Damaen boasts a 33-1 record and 13-game winning streak. The Wildcats advanced to the Elite 8 by defeating Saint Anselm 94-73 in the East Region title game played March 17.

Lander (28-5) earned its Elite 8 berth on March 17 when it sidelined Lenior-Rhyne 86-62 in the Southeast Region title game. The Bearcats have won their last 13 games.

The winners of Wednesday’s four games against to the Final 4 set for Friday in Pittsburgh.

Indianapolis, Indiana, will host the national championship game on Sunday, April 5.

title: Lead applies for funding  to spruce up Prospect Park
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: LEAD — When members of Lead Beautification cleaned out the garden at Prospect Park last spring, they discovered rotting timbers and various levels of disrepair, in the park that overlooks the mouth of Lead’s Main Street.
_full_text:
LEAD — When members of Lead Beautification cleaned out the garden at Prospect Park last spring, they discovered rotting timbers and various levels of disrepair, in the park that overlooks the mouth of Lead’s Main Street. 

Since then, in accordance with their mission to spruce up the Lead community, beautification committee members have been working with Tallgrass Landscape Architecture, of Custer, on a plan to revitalize the park. On Monday, Beautification Committee President Linda Phillips and Matt Fridell, of Tallgrass, presented a plan to the city commission. 

“Our goal is to turn the highly visible park into a garden showcase,” Phillips said. “Prospect Park is the entrance to our Main Street, and we would like to create a space that will be a primary public destination next to our downtown.” 

Prospect Park is located next to Miner’s and Merchant’s Trading Post, on a hill on Main Street. The park includes several steps that lead up to an old stamp mill piece of equipment, and an overlook view of the Open Cut.  

Fridell presented ideas for making the park more ADA accessible, fixing retaining walls, and creating a circle garden with benches, and a small gathering space for programs. 

The price tag for the improvements could be between $250,000 to $400,000, Fridell said, due to the expensive retaining walls and concrete that would be involved with fixing the park. However, he said there are federal and state grants that could help pay for the project. One land and water conservation fund grant from the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and the S.D. Game, Fish, and Parks, could pay for 80% of the project, with 20% coming from in-kind volunteer contributions, he said. 

On Monday, members of the Lead City Commission authorized City Administrator Robin Lucero to work with Phillips and Fridell to apply for grants for the project.
