title: Nerds unite!:  LeadCon to draw gamers, writers, cosplay artists, and more
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: LeadCon will feature a weekend of fun games, discussion panels, a cosplay contest, and more, April 10-11. Courtesy graphic
_full_text:
Nerds unite!  LeadCon to draw gamers, writers, cosplay artists, and more
LeadCon will feature a weekend of fun games, discussion panels, a cosplay contest, and more, April 10-11. Courtesy graphic 

LEAD — Nerds from throughout the Northern Hills and beyond will unite April 10-11 for LeadCon, a convention of gamers, cosplay, fandom, film, and fun. 

The Lead Area Chamber of Commerce will sponsor the event that is modeled after ComicCon events, which are conventions of like-minded creators and gamers that have exploded in popularity across the globe. Organized by a local group called the Lead Council of Nerds, Levi Wilson, one of the organizers, said it will be filled with variety.

“It’s a gathering of nerds and geeks of their favorite hobbies,” Wilson said of ComicCon events across the globe. “LeadCon is going to be Lead-sized.” 

The ticketed weekend event will start at Lewie’s Burgers and Brews with nerd trivia, April 10, where Wilson said the grand prize winner will walk away with a Dungeons and Dragons 3-book starter that includes a master guide, the players handbook, and the monster manual — a $150 value. 

On April 11 the Historic Homestake Opera House will host a haven for gamers, writers, cosplay, and “nerdom.” The venue will include hobby and art vendors, a board game gallery, and more. Opening ceremonies for the event will begin at 9:30 a.m., and then at 10 a.m. games will start, with some requiring pre-registration and an additional fee. A Magic: The Gathering tournament and a Warhammer 40K King of the Colloseum Tournament will both require participants to sign up on a first-come-first-served basis, with separate fees to participate. A Smash Bros tournament will also be a separate fee, and will start at 10 a.m., with a championship game held in the opera house theater at 3 p.m.  

At 10 a.m., Wilson said LeadCon organizers will host Dungeons and Dragons games for those who have not played before. 

“It’s just kind of a one-off experience for people who have never gotten to play dungeons and dragons before,” he said. “We’re trying to grow the hobby.” 

In addition to the introductory game, Wilson said LeadCon will include a discussion panel of veteran and new Dungeons and Dragons dungeon masters. 

“It’s going to be a discussion of hteir experiences and trying to get more people to be DMs, because that’s a common choke point in growing the hobby,” Wilson said. 

Another panel discussion will include local writers who will discuss the many different types of writing and opportunities that are available. Among the panelists will be Sean Covel, who is the producer of Napolean Dynamite, and a representative from Henry’s Books in Spearfish. 

Starting at noon, there will be a showing of the Napolean Dynamite movie in the opera house theater, with a question and answer session with producer Sean Covel. 

Following the movie, participants can participate in Blood on the Clocktower and Dungeon and Dragons games. Participants are encouraged to sign up for the games ahead of time, as participation will be first-come-first-served. 

Cosplay costumes are a major part of ComicCon events, and LeadCon will be no different, Wilson said. Participants are encouraged to dress up as their favorite television or movie characters, or to create their own character costumes. At 2 p.m., there will be a cosplay costume panel. Then at 4 p.m. there will be a cosplay contest, with prizes that include gift cards to The Knothole craft and sewing store in Spearfish, gift baskets, and more. 

The day will conclude with closing ceremonies at 5:30 p.m., with a showing of the Princess Bride afterward. 

While Wilson said tickets for the event will allow admission into all activities, participants who want to attend the April 10 nerd trivia, or the Princess Bride movie will be able to purchase individual tickets for those events. 

Pre-tickets are on sale for a discount, and Wilson said so far they have already sold 30 tickets. His goal is to draw 200, and he said the Chamber has already gotten calls from as far away as Nebraska for the event. 

LeadCon, he said, is strategically scheduled to be a fun, indoor activity during Lead’s shoulder tourism season. When the ski hills and snowmobile trails close for the winter, and before it warms up enough for spring and summer outdoor recreation, Wilson said the event is a great way to attract people to Lead.

title: Highway 85 and Crook City Rd.  turn lane project scheduled to begin
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: Courtesy photo
_full_text:
Highway 85 and Crook City Rd.  turn lane project scheduled to begin
Courtesy photo 

SPEARFISH — Weather permitting, the South Dakota Department of Transportation (DOT) will begin construction on left-turn lanes at US Highway 85 and Crook County Road Tuesday. 

South of Interstate 90 and Exit 17 in Spearfish, the project will aim to maintain 2-way traffic while it is underway. 

 A 10-foot width restriction will be in place for both lanes, however.

Signs noting an over-width detour will be placed on U.S. Highway 14A and I-90, according to the DOT. 

The prime contractor on this $1.4 million project is Complete Concrete, Inc., based in Rapid City. Rapid City, SD. The overall project completion date is set for Friday, Oct. 16.

title: Local educator joins forces with Navy Veteran thriller authors for unique writing event
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: Dave Bruns is a Navy veteran and the author of thriller novels. Courtesy image
_full_text:
DBruns.jpg
Dave Bruns is a Navy veteran and the author of thriller novels. Courtesy image 

 MollyB.jpg
Molly Barari, pictured, of Heirlooms Creative Writing has partnered with two experienced novelists to host a free writing workshop in Rapid City. Courtesy image 

RAPID CITY — Rapid City educator and writing instructor Molly Barari of Heirlooms Creative Writing is teaming up with U.S. Navy veterans and co-authors David Bruns and J.R. Olson to present a free writing workshop.

 The Writer’s Life & Fiction Writing Workshop” will take place April 11 from 9- 11 a.m. at the Minneluzahan Center. Designed for writers of all ages and skill levels, the workshop brings together local instruction and nationally published experience. 

 Bruns and Olson will lead the first hour, offering an inside look at the professional writing life. Drawing on their backgrounds as Navy veterans and bestselling thriller authors, they will discuss storytelling, publishing, and how real-world military experience informs their fiction.

 Barari will lead the second hour, guiding participants through a live fiction writing exercise and offering a supportive environment for sharing work and receiving feedback.

 Over their decade-long partnership, Bruns and Olson have published with traditional publishing houses, released a series through a mid-sized press, crowd-funded novels, and independently published their work. Their latest release, “Weapons Free,” is a modern-day submarine thriller in the style of “The Hunt for Red October.”

 Bruns and Olson will also appear at the Rapid City Public Library on Saturday, April 1 from 3 to 4 p.m. for a free talk, Real-World Threats to Page-Turning Fiction. The event will include a discussion of their military thrillers and a behind-the-scenes look at their long-running creative partnership.

 Both events are free and open to the public. RSVPs are encouraged for the April 11 writing workshop at Minneluzahan Center.

title: Greenpeace seeks new trial, claiming jury pool biased in case over Dakota Access Pipeline
byline: By Mary Steurer North Dakota Monitor
description: BISMARCK, N.D. — Greenpeace has asked for a second trial after a judge entered a $345 million judgment against the organization in a landmark case brought by the developer of the Dakota Access Pipeline.
_full_text:
NorthDakotaMonitorLogo

BISMARCK, N.D. — Greenpeace has asked for a second trial after a judge entered a $345 million judgment against the organization in a landmark case brought by the developer of the Dakota Access Pipeline.

The case “threatens to result in one of the largest miscarriages of justice in North Dakota’s history,” attorneys for the environmental group wrote in a brief filed last week.

After a three-week trial roughly a year ago, a Morton County jury directed Greenpeace to pay Energy Transfer about $667 million, finding the environmental group at fault for inciting illegal acts against the company during anti-pipeline protests in North Dakota in 2016 and 2017 and for publishing false statements that harmed Energy Transfer’s reputation. 

Greenpeace denies Energy Transfer’s claims and maintains that it brought the lawsuit to hurt the environmental movement.

Southwest Judicial District Judge James Gion in October slashed the jury’s award to $345 million, though he didn’t finalize the award until late February.

Greenpeace is now taking steps to fight the judgment, which includes its motion for a new trial.

The environmental group’s reasons for the request include claims that the jury instructions and verdict form contained errors, and that Energy Transfer was allowed to present unfair and  irrelevant evidence to jurors. The group also alleges the jury pool was biased.

Greenpeace says the jury’s award assumes that Greenpeace was entirely responsible for any injury Energy Transfer sustained related to the protests. Jurors were not given the opportunity to consider whether Greenpeace was only at fault for a portion of the damages, the organization wrote in its brief.

Attorneys for Greenpeace also referenced the mailers and other media circulated to Mandan and Bismarck residents before the trial that contained anti-Dakota Access Pipeline protest and pro-energy industry content. 

The environmental group seeks a new trial in Cass County, arguing in part that the jury pool in the Fargo area would be more fair because its residents did not directly experience the Dakota Access Pipeline protests and because the local economy is less dependent on the energy industry.

If Greenpeace’s request for a new trial is denied, it plans to appeal the case to the North Dakota Supreme Court, the organization has said.

Greenpeace previously asked for the trial to be moved from Morton County to Cass County in early 2025, which Gion and the North Dakota Supreme Court denied. 

The lawsuit is against three separate Greenpeace organizations — Greenpeace USA, Greenpeace International and Greenpeace Fund.

Energy Transfer as of Wednesday morning had not submitted a response to Greenpeace’s motion for a new trial. Previously, the company has defended the jury’s verdict and disputed Greenpeace’s claims that the court proceedings were not fair.

Energy Transfer has indicated it may appeal Gion’s decision to reduce the award to $345 million.

Greenpeace will not have to pay any of the $345 million judgment for at least a couple of months, Gion ruled Tuesday.

Court documents indicate that the organization could have to pay a bond of up to $25 million while appeals proceed, though the environmental group has asked the judge to waive or reduce this amount. Gion has not decided on this motion.

He noted that obtaining such a large bond will be challenging.

“The magnitude of this matter defies simple decisions,” Gion wrote.

Energy Transfer in court filings urged the judge to require Greenpeace to post the full $25 million.

Any bond money Greenpeace provides would be held by a third party while the appeals proceed, according to Greenpeace USA.

Greenpeace International has filed a separate lawsuit in the Netherlands that accuses Energy Transfer of weaponizing the U.S. legal system against the environmental group. Energy Transfer asked Gion to order that the overseas suit be paused while the North Dakota case is still active, which Gion denied. The company appealed his ruling to the North Dakota Supreme Court, which has yet to make a decision on the matter.

title: Quick Picks: Bestsellers
description: It’s a new month, and that means there is a new lineup of books rising to the top of bestseller charts. This week we bring you to the top 6 sellers on Apple’s book charts.
_full_text:
It’s a new month, and that means there is a new lineup of books rising to the top of bestseller charts. This week we bring you to the top 6 sellers on Apple’s book charts. 

1. “Project Hail Mary” by Andy Weir

Genre: Science Fiction

Ryland Grace is the sole survivor on a desperate, last-chance mission – and if he fails, humanity and the earth itself will perish. Except that right now, he doesn’t know that. He can’t even remember his own name, let alone the nature of his assignment or how to complete it.

2. “The Night We Met” by Abby Jimenez

Genre: Romance

In everyone’s life, there’s a split-second decision that can change everything.

For Larissa, it came when choosing which guy to ride home with after a concert. That night, she had no idea she’d met the perfect man. She and Chris are great together, co-parenting a slightly unhinged rescue Yorkie, sharing their favorite books, and judging bread (pumpernickel for the win!). For the first time amid all her side hustles to scrape by, things finally feel easy.

3. “Theo of Golden” by Allen Levi 

Genre: Fiction

One spring morning, a stranger arrives in the small southern city of Golden. No one knows where he has come from or why.

His name is Theo. And he asks a lot more questions than he answers.

4. “Judge Stone” by Viola Davis and James Patterson

Genre: Thriller

All rise for Judge Stone.

The most respected citizen in Union Springs, Alabama (population 3,314), is Judge Mary Stone. She holds 2 responsibilities running her family farm and presiding over her courtroom. It’s there she draws the most controversial case in the history of the South. 

5. “Exit Strategy” by Martha Wells

Genre: Science Fiction

Murderbot wasn’t programmed to care. So, its decision to help the only human who ever showed it respect must be a system glitch, right?

6. “The Correspondent” 

Genre: Fiction

At 73, Sybil has used her correspondence – witty and wise – to make sense of the world. But beyond the page, she has spent the last 30 years keeping the people who love her at arms’ length, until letters from someone in her past force her to examine one of the most painful periods of her life.

title: Public retirees in SD face potential sixth year of less-than-inflation benefit changes
byline: By Joshua Haiar Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: PIERRE — The officials who manage South Dakota’s public employee retirement system said Wednesday that if current investment trends continue, retirees are likely to see a July 2027 cost-of-living benefit adjustment similar to the 1.56% increase they will get this July.
_full_text:
SearchlightLogo

PIERRE — The officials who manage South Dakota’s public employee retirement system said Wednesday that if current investment trends continue, retirees are likely to see a July 2027 cost-of-living benefit adjustment similar to the 1.56% increase they will get this July.

If inflation stays above 2% as it has lately, that could mean a sixth straight year of increases less than inflation. 

Senior actuary Doug Fiddler noted that inflation jumped from 1.28% in fiscal year 2021 to 5.92% in 2022, then nearly 9% in 2023, and has remained above 2% since. 

Most state and local public employees, including teachers and state workers, contribute 6% of their pay to the retirement system with a 6% employer match, although some classes of employees contribute more. There are more than 105,000 members in the system.

“There is no way on 6% contributions we can afford to pay a 9% COLA,” Fiddler said, referring to cost-of-living adjustments, “when our return was pretty low that year as well.” 

He told the retirement system board during a virtual meeting that, given recent headlines affecting the stock market, he does not expect the trend to change. 

“We are going to have to balance our benefits with the money we have, the resources we have,” Fiddler said. “And those are based on fixed contributions.”

With one quarter left to go in the current fiscal year, the retirement system’s investments are expected to achieve an annual return of 6% to 6.5%. Fiddler said that’s despite “pretty volatile markets.” 

Fiddler shared a chart showing the retirement system could only have afforded its maximum 3.5% cost-of-living adjustment this year if it achieved returns of about 13.5% or higher.

The board will set the July 2027 cost-of-living increase later this year.

title: News and Notes
description: Handley Center raises $18K
_full_text:
Handley Center raises $18K

LEAD — The Handley Recreation Center raised about $18,000 with its annual Mile High Basketball Tournament, held March 13-15 in Lead. Handley Center board president Darla Auld reported that the event drew about 50 teams, with their families and friends to play basketball, eat, shop, and stay in Lead for the weekend. 

— Wendy Pitlick  

Digger purchase 

LEAD — The city of Lead will spend $4,000 to purchase a digger that will help the parks department with a multitude of jobs. Jerry Mertesedorf told the Lead City Commission Monday that the equipment will help with planting the 16 trees the city ordered with a federal tree grant, as well as help improve drainage at Manuel Brothers Park, fix water leaks, and help install the tent for the Fourth of July celebrations. “This is something I’ve needed for years, and it really became important this year with the tree grant,” he said. “The problem with the park is there’s so much rock in there. I have half a dozen-plus water leaks every year in the sprinkler system that we have to find. This year we’ve got 16 trees to plant that are going to be relatively large and will require big holes. We try to do improvements every year and we saw a need for it. It’s a swamp down there, so we’ll try to get some drainage. I expect if everything goes well and the weather continues like it is, that will be completed well before the Fourth, and the grass will be put back in good shape.”  

— Wendy Pitlick

title: ‘The Best’ takes the stage Saturday at LDHS
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: LEAD — Lead-Deadwood High School Theater Department is set to present their spring play, The Best, at 7 p.m. Saturday in the high school auditorium.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

LEAD — Lead-Deadwood High School Theater Department is set to present their spring play, The Best, at 7 p.m. Saturday in the high school auditorium. 

The play is a compilation of monologues and scenes from the perspective of man’s best friend: dogs. Each actor speaks from the viewpoint of a different dog and reveals their inner emotions and thoughts or, depending on the breed, lack of thoughts. 

“The audience will get a taste of a variety of emotions or backstories from the characters that they’ll meet,” said director Shaley Lensegrav. “Some of the characters are young and excited; some are older dogs reflecting on their families; some have great lives; some are in shelters waiting for a home. We’ve got a full variety of comedic to sentimental scenes.” 

“I feel like this performance really challenges our young actors to step outside of their comfort zone by trying monologues where they don’t have anyone else to depend on,” said director Greg Watson. “It helps them gain more confidence in their stage presence by directing their conversations to the audience rather than to a fellow actor.” 

This show will be the final performance for several of the actors that have been a part of the Lead-Deadwood program for many years. 

“It is always sad to lose our seniors, but we couldn’t be more grateful for all of the hard work that they’ve put into our program,” Lensegrav said. 

Cost of admission is $5 for adults $3 for students. Separate from admission, the theater department is collecting donations of pet food, treats, toys, or bedding that will then be donated to the local humane societies. 

“We hope to use this show as a way to give back to our local humane societies. Maybe it could lead to some shelter adoptions or at least make the audience think about what their dog at home might be thinking/saying,” Lensegrav said.

title: SD’s state-owned shooting range exceeds annual visitation estimate in  first five months
byline: By Joshua Haiar Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: The Pete Lien & Sons Shooting Sports Complex, about 10 miles north of Rapid City, exceeded its projected visitation. Photo by Seth Tupper, South Dakota Searchlight
_full_text:
SD’s state-owned shooting range exceeds annual visitation estimate in  first five months
The Pete Lien & Sons Shooting Sports Complex, about 10 miles north of Rapid City, exceeded its projected visitation. Photo by Seth Tupper, South Dakota Searchlight 

RAPID CITY — South Dakota’s new state-owned West River outdoor shooting range has had as many visits in five months as officials anticipated for its entire first year, according to an update Thursday. 

The Pete Lien & Sons Shooting Sports Complex has logged more than 30,000 total visits since opening in early November. 

In 2024, officials with Game, Fish and Parks guessed the site would see that many visits annually. 

“We’re there in 5 months of operation,” the agency’s John Kanta said during a meeting of the state’s Game, Fish and Parks Commission.

Kanta said a rather mild winter helped. He said the complex has also sold 700 memberships and brought in nearly $100,000 in total sales. 

The 400-acre complex is roughly 15 miles north of Rapid City and 25 miles southeast of Sturgis. It includes areas for long-range shooting, clay target shooting and tactical training. 

Shooting competitions are filling the calendar, officials said, with some events expected to draw hundreds of shooters, and law enforcement agencies regularly use the site for training.

Rising use will likely require more staffing, according to Wildlife Division Director Tom Kirschenmann. 

The strong early turnout comes after years of scrutiny over the project’s funding and development. After tribal artifacts were found during initial land surveys, Game, Fish and Parks opted not to use federal money to avoid stricter requirements for studying the site’s significance to Native Americans. Former Gov. Kristi Noem directed $13.5 million in governor-controlled Future Fund money to the project after lawmakers repeatedly declined to fund it. 

Gun-industry companies and groups contributed $2 million toward construction, and some Democrats raised concerns about whether that would give donors leverage over the state.  

About $6 million of the project’s total funding has been given or pledged by donors.

The Department of Game, Fish and Parks initially said in 2021 that the project would cost about $9.9 million. That grew to $12 million in 2022. The state paused the project when calls for bids drew only one bid. The price tag had ballooned to about $20 million by the time the state broke ground in December 2023. 

The facility’s summer hours run from 8:30 a.m. to 7:30 p.m. from May through August.

title: What makes a successful  chip seal project?
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: Chip seal operations require certain conditions to work. The city of Spearfish and the asphalt company are both trying to determine why the Colorado Boulevard Main Street project last fall was not successful.
_full_text:
Chip seal.JPG
Chip seal operations require certain conditions to work. The city of Spearfish and the asphalt company are both trying to determine why the Colorado Boulevard Main Street project last fall was not successful.  

 Colo Blvd Main chip seal.JPG
Pioneer photo by Sierra Ferguson 

SPEARFISH — Drivers on Main Street and Colorado Boulevard from the mouth of Spearfish Canyon to the intersection with Jackson Boulevard, have observed slick, black, pock-marked roads with confusing or fading lane markings after a fall 2025 chip seal project failed. Neither the contractor on the project, Dakota Asphalt, nor city leadership dispute that the project failed and Spearfish Public Works Director Adam McMahon said both parties are committed to finding a solution. McMahon said he intends to discuss the issue at every city council and study session until the issue is resolved. A representative of Dakota Asphalt declined to offer comment at this time, but was amendable to providing more information and context to the Pioneer at a later date. 

Chip seal is a cost effective way to apply a hard surface to protect asphalt roads from the sun’s rays. The oil in asphalt has sulfur in it that, in direct sunlight, is replaced with oxygen. That oxygen breaks up the oil that binds the road surface, and can eventually cause it to dissolve back into gravel. 

The surfacing method involves using oil to adhere crushed stone, often called cover aggregate or chips, to the road surface. Pressure and heat allow the chips to set, protecting and improving the road surface. 

The state department of transportation (DOT) provides standards specifications, requirements, for these kinds of projects. In section 360.3 of the DOT standard specs manual, surface treatment operations, like chip seal are described as “permitted during daylight hours when conditions are dry, when wind does not adversely affect the spraying operation, and when overnight low air temperatures within 24 hours of the planned application are forecasted to be at least 45 degrees fahrenheit.” 

Did You Know?
Both the emulsifier, or oil, used in chip seal and the chips themselves have an atomic charge? Thinking back to science class, recall that 2 atoms with a negative charge repel one another. Two atoms with a positive charge do the same. In order for chips and oil to adhere properly, the atomic charges of the oil and aggregate have to be opposite — on positive, one negative. That’s why the DOT requires contractors to set aside their chips and send samples of both materials to a state lab before highway projects move forward. The Pioneer is working to confirm if a lab test was used in Spearfish’s recent chip seal project. 

Air and surface temperatures should be no lower than 60 degrees fahrenheit in the shade, according to the specifications. 

However, Mike Carlson, a Rapid City area engineer with the DOT said, in order for chips to cure air temperatures should be closer to 70 degrees. 

The recommended timeframe for all chip seal projects is May 15 through Aug. 31. Depending on the type of cover material, the recommended timeframe extends to Sept. 15.

“Due to the late bidding for this project, an addendum was added to the bid to give the contractor the option of completing the project after the season for this type of work had closed provided that the minimum temperatures for the necessary cure time were met,” McMahon said. “Alternatively, the contractor was given the option to wait until the spring of 2026 to perform the work after the new season began. The contractor elected to perform the project in the fall.”

September 2025 was relatively warm and dry. According to climatological data from the National Weather Service in Rapid City, the average high temperature in September was 74.7 degrees, and the average low temperature was 49.3 degrees. The average high temperature in October was 61.7 degrees. The average low, during the month when the majority of work was done, was 39.4 degrees. In the early parts of the month, recorded highs stretched to 85 degrees. The coldest days were Oct. 13 and Oct. 29, where the lows dipped to 28 degrees.  

In addition to temperature, one of the most important elements of a successful chip seal project is the quality and quantity of the aggregate material, or chips, contractors use. 

“The goal is to get the chips to stick in the oil.  Too few chips the road turns black and slippery.  Too many chips is not a problem, the loose chips need to be picked up.  The main issue with chips is that they are clean,” Carlson said. 

The DOT performs tests on chips before its highway projects — in part to make sure that the oil and aggregate the contractor intends to use are compatible. 

It is not clear if chip conditions were a factor in the Main Street Colorado Boulevard project. McMahon said the cause of the failure is still subject to evaluation. 

Carlson said failures happen. 

“I’ve been around several of these projects where you wonder what happens,” Carlson said. “Afterward you go through a forensic process to determine what went wrong, and then we write the specs so that it doesn’t happen again.” 

On Monday, the city council asked to prioritize getting a project started to fix the roadway. 

McMahon said the bid process might not be complete until this fall, if the city would like to hire a new contractor.  

“Next spring would be more realistic,” McMahon said.

title: City leaders wait for legal decision  before enacting mine regulations
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: Seven of 8 property owners submitted a petition to voluntarily annex about 130 acres of this land into the city of Piedmont, to try and avoid development of a limestone mine near their property. One property owner has taken legal action to contest the annexation. Pioneer file photo
_full_text:
City leaders wait for legal decision  before enacting mine regulations
Seven of 8 property owners submitted a petition to voluntarily annex about 130 acres of this land into the city of Piedmont, to try and avoid development of a limestone mine near their property. One property owner has taken legal action to contest the annexation. Pioneer file photo  

PIEDMONT — Members of the Piedmont Board of Trustees will wait to make any decisions that could affect a limestone mine near city limits, until recent legal action is resolved. 

On March 2, P5, LLC, owned and operated by the Policky family that holds property and business interests in Piedmont, filed a petition for a writ of certiorari and request for a temporary restraining order against the city of Piedmont and 7 landowners. The petition is a request for a higher court to review the city of Piedmont’s decision to accept a voluntary annexation for about 130 acres. Zachary Policky, a manager of P5, LLC, owns 59 acres of the annexed land, but he was the only property owner that did not sign off on the annexation. The remaining 7 property owners said Policky’s signature was not needed, since their signatures satisfied state law requirements of at least 75% of the land valuation to be represented on the petition. Policky disagreed, and he argues in the lawsuit that his property is worth more than the officially recorded assessment. 

On Tuesday, the Piedmont Board of Trustees listed an agenda item to discuss an ordinance placing a one-mile jurisdiction around the city to prevent mining. Phil Anderson, president of the board of trustees said that action, and any other actions relating to zoning the voluntarily annexed property or governing or prohibiting the limestone mine, have been put on hold pending a judicial decision about whether the annexation petition was legal. 

The issue started when Simon Contractors, a Wyoming-based firm announced approval of its state permit to develop a limestone quarry, in the Black Hills Pioneer legal notices. The quarry is permitted for 300 acres. Simon officials have since reported they do not intend to mine that much land, but repeated calls to the company to clarify the operation have not been returned. 

Maps for the quarry show the mine boundaries are immediately adjacent to several residential properties, and is very close to Piedmont city limits. In an effort to prevent development of the mine, seven property owners who live right next to the mine property submitted the voluntary annexation petition to the city of Piedmont, which has ordinances prohibiting mining within the city limits. 

Policky, who has a deal for Simon Contractors to operate part of its mine on his 59 acres of land, opposed the voluntary annexation. In court papers, he argues that the annexation will interfere with his business interest to lease his land to Simon Contractors for the mine, and that his land is worth more than the assessment used for the voluntary annexation.

title: L-D Arts Center LEGO Contest,  LDHS science fair set for Friday
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: The Lead-Deadwood Arts Center LEGO contest typically draws about 60-70 children of all ages, from as far away as Wyoming. This year’s event will be held Friday, and will include a Lead-Deadwood High School science fair. Pioneer file photo
_full_text:
L-D Arts Center LEGO Contest,  LDHS science fair set for Friday
The Lead-Deadwood Arts Center LEGO contest typically draws about 60-70 children of all ages, from as far away as Wyoming. This year’s event will be held Friday, and will include a Lead-Deadwood High School science fair. Pioneer file photo 

LEAD — LEGOS and STEAM go hand in hand. 

That’s why this year’s 22nd annual Lead-Deadwood Arts Center LEGO contest will once again coincide with Lead-Deadwood High School Science, Technology, Engineering, Arts, and Mathematics (STEAM) fair. The event will be held at 6 p.m., Friday, at Lead-Deadwood High School Gym 1. 

Karen Everett, executive director of the Lead-Deadwood Arts Center said every year, children of all ages and from as far away as Wyoming, bring in artfully crafted and engineered creations. Because of that, the event is perfectly paired with the annual science fair at the high school. 

In the past, LEGO creations have included a playable violin, spaceships, football stadiums, a rooftop garden, and more.

Student science fair creations this year will include a potato battery, making slime, elephant toothpaste, launching balloons with baking soda and vinegar, popsicle stick bridges, and putting marshmallows in a vacuum chamber. 

All ages, from preschool up to adults, are invited to enter the LEGO contest, and the contest typically attracts between 60-70 entries.

“It’s amazing the creativity and the excitement the kids have,” Everett said. “It’s hard to pick the winners in each category because everyone does so good. The kids are usually really supportive of other entries. Kids get super excited to see what other kids bring. It’s more about sharing your love of LEGOS and being creative. We tie it in with the STEAM movement because it’s a combination of all those things. It’s a perfect STEAM project.”

title: BHSU Center for American Indian Studies  to host annual wacipi
description: The Black Hills State University Center for American Indian Studies will host its 41st annual Lakota Omniciye Wacipi in the Donald E. Young Center April 18 at noon to conclude its celebration of American Indian Awareness Week taking place April 13-17. BHSU photo by Juli Teasley
_full_text:
BHSU Center for American Indian Studies to host annual wacipi.jpeg
The Black Hills State University Center for American Indian Studies will host its 41st annual Lakota Omniciye Wacipi in the Donald E. Young Center April 18 at noon to conclude its celebration of American Indian Awareness Week taking place April 13-17. BHSU photo by Juli Teasley 

SPEARFISH — The Black Hills State University Center for American Indian Studies will host its 41st annual Lakota Omniciye Wacipi in the Donald E. Young Center April 18 at noon to conclude its celebration of American Indian Awareness Week taking place April 13-17.  

 “American Indian Awareness Week, highlighted by the 41st Annual Lakota Omniciye Wacipi, is a powerful reminder of the importance of cultural visibility and collective care,” said Jordon Mendoza, assistant director of the Center for American Indian Studies at BHSU.    

The annual Wacipi is dedicated to educating the community about Native American culture, celebrating the rich cultural heritage of South Dakota, and features Indigenous dancers, singers, and drummers from all over the state.  

This year’s American Indian Awareness Week theme is “Elevating Native Voices: Embracing Our Collective Impact.” 

Presentations throughout American Indian Awareness Week will be given by Cante Heart, Bobbie Koch, Dew Bad Warrior-Gange, Marty Two Bulls, Dr. Brittany Bearstail, Bobby Pourier, and Tasha Peltier. Topics include Indigenous peacekeeping, Lakota public artworks, intergenerational hearing, and more. The speaking sessions will be held in Jonas Academic Room 107 and are free and open to the public. 

“Through this year’s theme, emphasizing community, health, and education calls us all to listen, learn, and stand in support of Indigenous knowledge and leadership,” Mendoza said. 

General admission to the Wacipi is $8. BHSU students, faculty, staff, children under 5, and seniors 55 and older may attend the event for free. For a full schedule of events and more information on American Indian Awareness Week or the Wacipi, please visit BHSU.edu/CAIS or contact Jordon Mendoza at Jordon.Mendoza@BHSU.edu.

title: Stage Run set to get  street lighting,  residents voice opposition
byline: By Sara Morgan Black Hills Pioneer
description: The roads of the first and second phases of Stage Run, constructed as early as 2009, have been without streetlights since construction. The development is now set to have streetlights installed by the end of the year, bringing it into compliance with city ordinances. Pioneer photo by Sara Morgan
_full_text:
20260410_102328.jpg
The roads of the first and second phases of Stage Run, constructed as early as 2009, have been without streetlights since construction. The development is now set to have streetlights installed by the end of the year, bringing it into compliance with city ordinances. Pioneer photo by Sara Morgan 

 20260410_102136.jpg
Pioneer photo by Sara Morgan 

DEADWOOD — In spite of resounding opposition from Stage Run residents in Deadwood, city officials are looking to install streetlighting in the first 2 phases of the development.

Street lighting in the first 2 phases of Stage Run Development in Deadwood has been placed in the city’s capital improvement plan, with city officials hoping to get lighting installed before the end of the year. Many houses along the streets were built nearly 15 years ago and roadways have been without the feature the whole time, despite an ordinance adopted by the city around 2010 requiring them. Many residents spoke out against the plan to install street lighting at the Monday meeting, but city officials say the installation is “very likely.”

Pat Dringham, representative of the development’s block club, spoke at the meeting to voice collective opposition to the streetlights from some of the residents. 

“The responses on the streets and summarized to us were strong opposition against the installation of streetlights,” said Dringham. “The first question asked at our block club meeting was, ‘Can we stop it?’ That’s when I met with Lornie (Stalder, director of public works). We kind of talked about it and he said that everything was really to be determined yet as far as design and style. It would be a major change to our neighborhood.”

Dringham said that members of the block club met with developer Bill Pearson and came away with the impression that street lighting was, “optional, not mandated.”

Construction of the development began in 2009. Stalder said that Deadwood adopted Rapid City’s standards and ordinances some time around 2010. Those standards claim that roadway lighting is “intended to provide minimum lighting levels in the roadway for vehicular and pedestrian safety.” The ordinance requires that all electrical work comply with the National Electrical Code and the Requirements and Standards for the South Dakota Department of Transportation for roadway lighting. 

According to ordinance 2.19.2, “streetlights shall, whenever possible, be installed on a property line, which runs perpendicular to the street. … On residential streets including lane / place, local, and collector streets, lights shall be placed at all intersections and shall be located between intersections, essentially at curves, with a non-staggered pattern and not more than 400 feet apart.”

Other residents of the development spoke up at the meeting, voicing concerns of established lawns being disturbed during construction and the necessity of lights on a narrow road. 

Stalder said that residents will “likely” lose some part of their yards once the streetlights are installed, noting that the city has a right of way that can be used for the purpose of infrastructure. If the concrete pads of the lights encroach further into private property, he said an easement would be necessary. 

Stalder also noted that the development would likely require no more than 9 total streetlights. He said that solar lights would be less likely to disturb property and roads during construction and would cost significantly less to install and run.

“Everybody’s aware that Black Hills Energy is seeking a 25% increase, and the city spends about $363,000 a year in electricity,” Stalder said at the Monday meeting. “So, we’re going to see, say, a $90,000 increase if we choose not to go with solar. It’s going to be a big cost to bore underneath the power, as well as putting in the lights. That would be way more invasive.”

Lexi Lux, resident of Stage Run, spoke online during the meeting, voicing safety concerns for pedestrians in the neighborhood.

“Our neighborhood is extremely dark at night, and I do have concerns about safety — whether it’s wildlife, people walking, or my own children,” Lux said. “I can see where yes, it’s nice not having lights in the neighborhood. But I mean, frankly, we live in city limits, and I think that there is certain things that we need to follow, being that we are in city limits. One of those things being having streetlights in the neighborhood.”

Stalder said that a document from 2012 shows that the city agreed with Pearson that streetlights would be installed at the end of the Mystery Wagon One section of development, signed by Pearson and Francis Toscana, the mayor at that time. Street lights have been installed in phase 3 of the development, which is currently vacant.

“They would be installed at the end of, basically Mystery Wagon One in the development, and they would come back and put them in Stage Run and Mystery Wagon One when they were done. That was done before I got here. They moved on to finish the second part of Mystery Wagon without, I’m assuming, any knowledge of this document sitting out there.”

Stalder said that the developers of the newly finished Deadwood Ridge Apartments were required to comply with all city ordinances during construction at a cost to the developer, including storm drainage, sidewalk, curb, gutter, and streetlighting. 

“We required the Ridge to follow our ordinances, I don’t know how we can really backtrack,” Stalder said. “It’s a contentious issue. It’s change, and not everybody likes change. There’s people out there that want the street lights, and there’s more people that don’t want the streetlights. I think as a city, we have to have pedestrian safety and vehicle safety. If you ignore that, you’re not protecting your citizens.” 

He said he is unsure if the cost of installing streetlighting in the development will come at a cost to Stage Run’s developer or the city, saying that the LLC the city first worked with while planning Stage Run has since been dissolved. 

A task force is expected to be formed involving Stage Run residents and city officials to determine the aesthetics, type, and number of poles to be installed in the development.

title: Calendar quirk and law change have SD election officials worried about deadlines
byline: By Makenzie Huber Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: PIERRE — Election officials in South Dakota are worried about a possible delayed start for early and absentee voting as they rush to prepare ballots for the June 2 primary, and they’re also navigating changes to registration requirements signed into law by Gov. Larry Rhoden last month.
_full_text:
SearchlightLogo

PIERRE — Election officials in South Dakota are worried about a possible delayed start for early and absentee voting as they rush to prepare ballots for the June 2 primary, and they’re also navigating changes to registration requirements signed into law by Gov. Larry Rhoden last month.

Lincoln County Auditor Sheri Lund said South Dakota voters should check their registration status and party affiliation as soon as possible. New voters should register soon as well, in case there are processing delays because of missing documentation required by a new law.

The new law, which went into effect on March 26, requires new voters to provide documentation of their U.S. citizenship with a driver’s license or identification card issued after July 1, 2025, a tribal ID, or a legible photocopy of a birth certificate, U.S. passport, consular report of birth abroad from the U.S. Department of State, or certificate of naturalization.

“If you’re a first-time registering voter in South Dakota, do not procrastinate,” Lund said. “Do it now, and make sure it goes through the right way.”

South Dakotans can check their registration status on the Secretary of State’s Office website. The voter registration deadline for the primary election is May 18.

Kayla Delfs, election coordinator with the Lincoln County Auditor’s Office, said the office had received 10 voter registration applications as of March 7 that were missing the necessary documentation to prove U.S. citizenship as required by the new law.

One person emailed proof of citizenship, but Delfs said she has not received documentation yet from the others after mailing notification of the problem to them. Those voters will be registered as federal only voters until they provide proper documentation — a new voter status signed into law in 2025. Federal only voters can vote in presidential and congressional races, not state and local races.

Pennington County Auditor Sabrina Green expects problems with missing U.S. citizenship documentation to dwindle over the next several years, as South Dakota driver’s licenses and identification cards expire and get renewed. Last July, the state began requiring newly issued cards to display the licensee’s citizenship status.

As county auditors and their staffs work to inform new voters of the changes, they’re also grappling with a quickly approaching start to early and absentee voting.

Prospective candidates from political parties had to file nominating petitions by the last Tuesday in March to make the ballot for the June 2 primary. Through a quirk of the calendar, that fell on March 31 this year, which shortened the time between the filing of those petitions and the printing of ballots for early and absentee voting. 

Lawmakers this year reacted to complaints about that by changing the nominating petition deadline to the third Tuesday in March. But they were concerned about changing the deadline for a petition process that was already underway, so they made the change effective for future elections, not the current one.

Early and absentee voting for the primary election begins on April 17. On Tuesday, the Secretary of State’s Office drew the order in which candidates will appear on ballots, giving county auditors eight business days to prepare, proofread and order ballots.

Green said her office will alert county residents if early voting is delayed, adding that she is “nervous” about the approaching deadline.

“I would rather our ballots are done correctly, proofed and without errors instead of rushing to meet the deadline,” Green said.

Early and absentee voting is required by state statute to begin “no later” than 46 days before the election, and ballots must be printed 48 days before. Auditors considered allowing voters to use sample ballots, like they’re allowed to on Election Day if a polling place runs out of ballots, but auditors decided against it, Delfs said.

“We’ve got our hands tied,” Delfs said. “What law do we break here?”

Lund said it’ll be a “hard push” to ensure early voting starts on time, but she is hopeful it will. The Secretary of State’s Office is allowing auditors to order early and absentee ballots separately from their Election Day ballots, which will speed up the process because there will be fewer ballots to print and ship so soon.

“I have my fingers crossed,” Lund said.

title: Homestake 150th celebration  draws standing-room only crowd
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: More than 350 people attended the 150th anniversary of Homestake celebration at the Historic Homestake Opera House, Thursday. The event featured speakers, hors d’oeuvres, cake, and a champagne toast. Pioneer photo by Wendy Pitlick
_full_text:
Homestake 150th celebration  draws standing-room only crowd
More than 350 people attended the 150th anniversary of Homestake celebration at the Historic Homestake Opera House, Thursday. The event featured speakers, hors d’oeuvres, cake, and a champagne toast. Pioneer photo by Wendy Pitlick 

LEAD — The Historic Homestake Opera House was completely packed Thursday night, as more than 350 Lead residents and visitors gathered to celebrate the 150th anniversary of the Homestake Gold Mine. 

The event drew a very special guest — Moses Manuel’s great-granddaughter, Stevie Neuman. Fred and Moses Manuel are credited with discovering the first gold that would become the Homestake Mine. 

Attendees enjoyed presentations from David Wolff, who discussed the history of the Homestake Mine; Ann Delicate, who discussed what it was like to live in the Homestake mansion as the daughter of a mine manager; and Jerry Aberle, who discussed the employee culture at Homestake. Afterward, Jacque Fuller, who served as Lead’s first and only female mayor and who has been heavily involved in historic preservation and Lead development since the 1970s, lead the crowd in a champagne toast. 

The event was the first of many events that will be held to commemorate the 150th anniversaries of Lead and Deadwood.

title: Spearfish man arrested  on drug charges in drug free zone
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: SPEARFISH — South Dakota’s Unified Narcotic Enforcement Team-North (UNET-N) announced Friday of the arrest of a Spearfish businessman for 2 felony drug charges, both occurring in a drug free zone.
_full_text:
SPEARFISH — South Dakota’s Unified Narcotic Enforcement Team-North (UNET-N) announced Friday of the arrest of a Spearfish businessman for 2 felony drug charges, both occurring in a drug free zone.

Christopher Lee Miller, 41, was arrested March 31 and was charged with one felony count of a distribution of a schedule I substance in a drug free zone and one felony count of possession with the intent to distribute in a drug free zone. The substance has allegedly been identified as THCA + HXY 9-THC + Delta 8-THC.

Spearfish police received a tip about the suspect, who owns HotHead Vapor in Spearfish and the investigation ensued. 

Recovered in the investigation allegedly were approximately 97 THCA + HXY 9-THC + Delta 8-THC live resin vape pins, Delta 8 infused gummies, and more than $1,600 in U.S. currency.

Miller was released on a $5,000 bond and his arraignment is scheduled for April 16. 

The Unified Narcotic Enforcement Team – North (UNET-N) consisting of the South Dakota Division of Criminal Investigation (DCI), Spearfish Police Department, Belle Fourche Police Department, Sturgis Police Department and the Meade County Sheriff’s Office, initiated the investigation.

title: Black Hills Pioneer at 150: The Railroads
description: Pioneer file graphic
_full_text:
Black Hills Pioneer at 150 years
Pioneer file graphic 

Black Hills Pioneer
The Black Hills Pioneer is the oldest continuously operating business in West River Dakota Territory; and 2026 marks our 150th year of telling the stories of the Black Hills. Over time our newspaper has absorbed the titles of numerous other publications, and we would like to share some of the more compelling stories from those early editions.
 Every Saturday we will be publishing stories from our archives, focusing heavily on the gold rush era of the 1800s, starting with our very first publication on June 8, 1876. These stories are re-printed exactly as they were first published, leaving in place the historic typos, grammar, style and terminology – politically correct or otherwise. It is our belief that history is sacrosanct and should never be edited to reflect the societal norms of the present.
 We hope you enjoy this historic journey. It is an honor and a privilege to be a trusted permanent record keeper of the Black Hills community; we look forward to the next 150 years of history in the making in the magnificent Black Hills.

 

~ November 23, 1890 • Deadwood Daily Pioneer ~

The Railroads

Railroad Paragraphs

The second line of piles for the Elkhorn roadbed on lower Main Street is nearly finished. Only about four piles remained to be driven when the men quit work at dark last night.

J. E. Ainsworth and Dr. Louthan, of Spearfish, are now at Powderville, Mont. Their mission is in connection with the Elkhorn extension north, though it is not known exactly what is intended.

The Deadwood Central Round House will have two stalls for engines besides a machine shop. The walls are now nearly completed and all the timbers in place. Flooring and roofing will be begun in a few days.

The grade of the B. & M. from Gold Run up Whitewood gulch to the railroad crossing is nearly all completed. No bridges have yet been put in, but all the bulkheads and heavy grading have been finished, and only surfacing now remains to be done. Beyond Ten-Mile ranch several heavy cuts and some rock work are uncompleted.

The Elkhorn will ship in a steam engine within the next few days with which to hoist the hammer of the pile driver. Billy Richards, formerly engineer at the Uncle Sam mill, will take charge of it. As soon as the pile driving for the roadbed below the Gem is completed, a line of piles will be driven up the creek to Lee Street, for a bulkhead.

Excavation for a culvert on the B. & M. to drain the flow of water from the White Rocks hill, was completed yesterday. Stone work has been going on for some time and a line of masonry is now built, about fifty feet long. The rocks are placed on each other without any mortar between, as the water would wash out everything of that kind.

The Deadwood Central yesterday threw its track from the old narrow-gauge bed on the flat above Cleveland, onto the new B. & M. grade. The small rails were nailed down to the broad-gauge ties, and last night the Lead City train ran over the new road. Peter O’Neill owns the placer claim on which the change was made, and objects to the B. & M. and D. C. running over his ground. He was too late however, to get an injunction, and will probably begin legal proceedings for ejection.

~ November 23, 1890 • Deadwood Daily Pioneer ~

Still Dancing

The Indians at Pine Ridge Continue to Dance and Threaten.

The Nebraska Militia Notified to Be Ready to March Against the Reds.

The Situation Uncertain.

Special to the Pioneer:

Pine Ridge Agency, Nov. 22— The situation here is in status quo. No one can tell what the next hour will bring forth. The least trouble between the soldiers or civilians and the Indians will precipitate a conflict. General Brooke has occupied the agency quarters since his arrival, and has been busily engaged in receiving and replying to communications relative to the situation. He is anxiously awaiting the instructions from the department at Washington, which were due before he left Omaha, regarding whether or not he shall interfere with the ghost dance. Two of the best scouts in the government’s employ reported to General Brooke last night at 9 o’clock, that 150 lodges of the Wounded Knee fanatics, including some of the most desperate and treacherous redskins in this part of the country, had moved to White River, twenty miles north of here, and had again begun that horrible and excitable ceremony, the ghost dance, in a wilder manner that had been known so far. The scouts said they talked with several of the leaders, and that the latter used the most threatening language. They reviewed all their wrongs at the hands of the whitemen and asserted that now was the time for the Indians to regain their lost supremacy. They declared that they and their associates had fully determined to shoot any and all government officials or soldiers who attempted to interfere with, or suppress the dance.

All the Indians in these lodges are well armed. Their arms consist of Winchester rifles, navy revolvers and knifes. They have large quantities of ammunition and provisions, and are receiving reinforcements hourly. The present trouble has brought to the surface about 250 of the carbines that were carried by the soldiers at the time of the Custer massacre. When the Indians surrendered, about 1,200 guns were given up also, but it was noticed at the time, that not one of the modern rifles that were taken from the dead bodies of Custer’s soldiers were among the number of guns surrendered. They were secreted for use at just such a time as this.

The total number of soldiers now stationed at this reservation is about 1,000. Should a conflict occur, this force would have to contend with five or six thousand Indians, armed with Winchester rifles, familiar with the country, and desperate with excitement and fanatic zeal. The reservations and the number of Indians affected by this strange Messiah craze, are as follows:

Cheyenne River Agency……………………. 2,846

Crow Creek and Lower Brule Agency.. 2,171

Pine Ridge Agency……………………………. 5,611

Rosebud Agency……………………………….. 7,586

Standing Rock Agency………………………. 4,110

The reservations could put in the field 8,000 warriors fully armed and equipped as completely as the handful of soldiers they would have to oppose. What the result of a conflict would be, can be easily surmised. 

General Brooke received a telegram from General Miles last night, giving him power to call as many more troops to this point as he may deem necessary.

Some few scouts and Indian police, who were sent out Thursday to notify the non-dancing faction to move into the agency precincts until the present trouble is settled, returned last night and reported that the Indians to whom they were sent, signified their perfect willingness to do as requested. A number of these immediately put out their tires, folded their tepees and accompanied the scouts and police into the agency. It is expected that these friendly Indians can be gathered in by Sunday night, and if this can be accomplished more dangerous work— that bringing the disturbers to time— will begin Monday morning.

All the available troops from the Department of the Platte are mustered here. A telegraph line has been constructed from the agency to Rushville, Neb., for the purpose of sending and receiving government messages. It is also used by the mass of newspaper correspondents who have flocked hither.

Calling Out the Militia

Special to the Pioneer:

Beatrice, Neb., Nov. 22— General Calby, commanding the first brigade of the Nebraska Nation Guards, issued orders for the men composing this brigade to prepare for marching orders.

title: Exit 14 Phillips 66 gas station to be rebuilt
byline: By Mark Watson Black Hills Pioneer
description: The former Exit 14 Phillips 66 gas station building was demolished this week. A new building will open in its place. Pioneer photo by Mark Watson
_full_text:
Exit 14 Phillips 66 gas station to be rebuilt
The former Exit 14 Phillips 66 gas station building was demolished this week. A new building will open in its place. Pioneer photo by Mark Watson 

SPEARFISH — The Exit 14 Phillips 66 gas station that has been closed since a Nov. 25 fire has been demolished. 

Demolition began Tuesday and the last part of it came down Friday.

Kelly Deis, manager of the gas station, said the exact timeline of the reconstruction is not known.

“A new structure will be going in sooner than later,” she told the Pioneer Friday as she watched the last piece of the building hit the ground. “They still have to clean this up and get the concrete out. I hate to speculate, but if the stars align, the doors will be open mid to late September.”

The fire, the exact cause is unknown she said, began in a storage room on the east side of the building.

“We heard some very loud popping noises going on. And then the place just started filling up with smoke. We had some fire in the way back room, and we just told everybody they needed to evacuate immediately, and that’s when everybody evacuated, staff included,” Deis told the Pioneer the day after the fire.

The building suffered extensive smoke and water damage.

While no people were injured, the store’s beloved black cat Panther was lost in the fire.

title: Phase 2 of Spearfish Creek Preservation Plan moves forward
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: City council green-lit phase 2 of the Spearfish Creek Preservation Plan, marking the beginning of a public engagement oriented chapter in process. Pioneer photo by Sierra Ferguson
_full_text:
SPF Creek alt.jpeg
City council green-lit phase 2 of the Spearfish Creek Preservation Plan, marking the beginning of a public engagement oriented chapter in process. Pioneer photo by Sierra Ferguson 

 SPF Creek 1.jpeg
Pioneer photo by Sierra Ferguson 

SPEARFISH — Phase one of the Spearfish Creek Preservation Plan was completed in July 2025. Prepared by ISG, the report identifies, “the greatest threats to the creek and find feasible steps that could be used to restore, enhance and protect the beneficial habitat in the recreational watershed,” according to Spearfish Parks and Forestry Superintendent Rex McDonald. 

On Aug. 4, the city council adopted the first phase of the plan. Then, on Monday, it voted unanimously to approve a $62,980 proposal from ISG Engineering — the same group that completed phase one — to start phase 2. 

“This is $7,000 and change under the $70,000 that was approved for the project in the 2026 budget,” McDonald said. 

Where phase one looked at threats to the stability of Spearfish Creek (like lost of a riparian buffer and bank erosion and the conversion of green spaces to imperious surfaces that contribute to runoff), phase 2 will look at the community’s desires and hopes for its future. 

 “This will provide the city with a practical and visionary road map for enhancing Spearfish Creek in a way that balances recreation, ecology, and long-term community benefit,” McDonald said. 

ISG plants to use an interactive website, social pinpoint, for 2 months to gather public input digitally. Then, the contractor will hold 2 public open houses to solicit in-person feedback. The group also plans to hold a technical advisory group workshop that will bring together “key local and state agency representatives” in order to gauge expert and stakeholder opinions on necessary restoration projects. 

Once research and data gathering wraps up, ISG has agreed to prepare “detailed conceptual sketches to identify priority sites along the creek.”

Those sketches will be used in phase 3, which will implement the projects recommended under the first 2 phases of the plan.

title: Army Corps warns of hydropower and recreation impacts as drought persists in Missouri River basin
byline: By Joshua Haiar Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: Ryan Larsen, with the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, gives a presentation Wednesday in Sioux City, Iowa, on the status of the Missouri River basin. Photo by Joshua Haiar, South Dakota Searchlight
_full_text:
Army Corps warns of hydropower and recreation impacts as drought persists in Missouri River basin
Ryan Larsen, with the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, gives a presentation Wednesday in Sioux City, Iowa, on the status of the Missouri River basin. Photo by Joshua Haiar, South Dakota Searchlight 

SIOUX CITY, Iowa — The Missouri River basin is in its fourth year of drought, and federal officials say that could affect hydropower production and recreation in the upper basin states.

John Remus is the chief of the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers’ Missouri River Basin Water Management Division.

“Through this winter, we saw very little Plains snowpack, and that’s pretty much all gone and melted,” he said Wednesday. “Our mountain snowpack is well below average. And we just haven’t had any precipitation.”

The Missouri River basin includes about 530,000 square miles of land that drain into the river in multiple states, including South Dakota, as well as Canada.

Corps officials presented data at a public meeting showing 83% of the basin is abnormally dry and 63% is in drought, and they said drought conditions are likely to persist. Hydropower generation from dams on the river this year is expected to be roughly 20% below average due to low water levels.

Remus said the situation is serious, but not alarming. He said the system of dams and reservoirs managed by the Corps on the river was designed with long droughts in mind.

“It’s not as bad as we’ve seen,” Remus said. “We had a long drought in the early 2000s, where we saw, actually, much lower reservoir levels.”

Remus said the basin also experiences major swings in conditions, including floods. He pointed to the 2024 Big Sioux River flood as an example of how extreme rainfall and flooding can occur even during a span of years with below-average precipitation.

For the general public, Remus said one of the clearest drought effects on the river could be reduced access for recreation. Lower water levels could leave some boat ramps dry and create access problems at marinas.

“I don’t want to call it critical or make it look like we’re in some desperate situation, because we’re not,” Remus said. 

The South Dakota Game, Fish and Parks Commission, which manages many access points along the river, plans to hear a Missouri River outlook presentation during its meeting Thursday and Friday in Pierre.

title: Artemis II astronauts describe  their lunar voyage as surreal and profound ahead of Earth return
description: HOUSTON (AP) — Drawing ever closer to Earth, the Artemis II astronauts tidied up their lunar cruiser for its upcoming “fireball” return and reflected on their historic journey around the moon, describing it as surreal and profound.
_full_text:
HOUSTON (AP) — Drawing ever closer to Earth, the Artemis II astronauts tidied up their lunar cruiser for its upcoming “fireball” return and reflected on their historic journey around the moon, describing it as surreal and profound.

As the next-to-last day of their flight dawned Thursday, humanity’s first lunar explorers in more than half a century were less than 150,000 miles (240,000 kilometers) from home with the odometer clicking down.

“We have to get back. There’s so much data that you’ve seen already, but all the good stuff is coming back with us. There are so many more pictures, so many more stories,” said pilot Victor Glover, adding that “riding a fireball through the atmosphere is profound as well.”

Being cut off from all of humanity for nearly an hour while behind the moon was especially “surreal,” according to commander Reid Wiseman.

“There’s a lot that our brains have to process ... and it is a true gift,” Wiseman said late Wednesday during the crew’s first news conference since before liftoff.

While out of contact behind the moon Monday, Wiseman, Glover, Christina Koch and Canada’s Jeremy Hansen became the most distant humans ever, clocking in at a record 252,756 miles from Earth before heading back. As they emerged from behind the moon, they experienced a wondrous total solar eclipse as the orb blocked the sun from their perspective.

Launching from Florida on April 1 diminished the amount of illumination on the lunar far side, Glover noted, but the eclipse was the consolation prize “and it was one of the greatest gifts.”

Friday’s reentry and Pacific splashdown off the coast of San Diego — as dynamic and dangerous as liftoff — now topped everyone’s minds. The recovery ship, USS John P. Murtha, was already at sea, with a squadron of military planes and helicopters poised to join the operation.

It’s the first time that NASA and the Defense Department have teamed up for a lunar crew’s reentry since Apollo 17 in 1972. Their Orion capsule will come screaming back, hitting the atmosphere at a predicted 34,965 feet per second — or 23,840 mph — not a record but still mind-bogglingly fast.

Mission Control will be paying close attention to how the capsule’s heat shield holds up. During the only other Orion test flight to the moon — in 2022 without a crew — the heat shield suffered considerably more damage than expected from the 5,000 degrees Fahrenheit of reentry.

Instead of replacing Artemis II’s heat shield, which would have forced another lengthy delay, NASA tweaked the capsule’s descent through the atmosphere to reduce the blisteringly hot exposure. Next year’s Artemis III and beyond will fly with redesigned heat shields.

Artemis III will see astronauts practice docking their capsule with a lunar lander or two in orbit around Earth. Artemis IV in 2028 will attempt to land two astronauts near the moon’s south pole, setting the stage for what NASA hopes will be a sustainable lunar base.

NASA officials have been loath to provide their risk assessment numbers for the nearly 10-day mission, acknowledging launch and entry as the biggest threats.

“We’re down to the wire now,” said NASA’s Lakiesha Hawkins. “We’re down to the end of the mission, and obviously getting the crew back home and getting them landed safely, is a significant part of the risk that’s still in front of us.”

title: Navigating teenage heartaches: a journey through love and loss
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: “Growing Pains” was published by Palmetto Publishing April 1. It’s a poetic exploration of teen heartbreak by debut author Kay Fisher. Image courtesy Palmetto Publishing
_full_text:
Navigating teenage heartaches: a journey through love and loss
“Growing Pains” was published by Palmetto Publishing April 1. It’s a poetic exploration of teen heartbreak by debut author Kay Fisher. Image courtesy Palmetto Publishing 

Charleston, S.C. (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — “Growing Pains,” a debut book of poetry by Kay Fisher, delves into the intricate emotions of teenage heartache, love, and loneliness. 

Out April 1 from Pametto Publishing, this poignant narrative captures the essence of adolescence through personal reflections on infatuation, loss, and the determination to heal. Fisher’s journey is a heartfelt exploration of navigating the tumultuous waters of young love, where moments of joy intertwine with profound sorrow. 

Each chapter invites readers to connect with their own experiences in order to foster empathy and understanding. With vivid details, such as the bittersweet memory of a first kiss under the stars, “Growing Pains” is poised to resonate deeply with anyone who has faced the complexities of growing up. 

Fisher structures the narrative with poetic prose, encouraging readers to recognize that healing is a journey filled with ups and downs. Even in the darkest moments, there is always a glimmer of hope, she reflects, capturing the essence of her work. “Growing Pains” is not just a collection of heartaches; it is a celebration of resilience and the beauty of finding oneself.

title: Quick Picks: Poetry Month
description: Courtesy photo
_full_text:
Quick Picks: Poetry month
Courtesy photo 

April is national poetry month. In celebration of the power of rhyme and verse, we reached out to the Piedmont Public Library for poetry collection recommendations. 

1. “Devotions” by Mary Oliver

Genre: Poetry, non-fiction

Pulitzer Prize-winning poet Mary Oliver presents a personal selection of her best work in this definitive collection spanning more than five decades of her esteemed literary career.

2. “Here: Poems for the Planet” edited by Elizabeth J. Coleman

Genre: Poetry, environment

“Here: Poems for the Planet” is a love song to a planet in crisis. Summoning a chorus of over 125 diverse poetic voices, this anthology approaches the impending environmental crisis with a sense of urgency and hopefulness.

3. “A Beautiful Day in the Neighborhood: The Poetry of Mister Rogers” by Fred Rogers, illustrated by Luke Flowers

Genre: Poetry, picture book

“Mister Rogers’ Neighborhood” had a revolutionary impact on children’s television, and on millions of children themselves. Through songs, puppets, and frank conversations, Mister Rogers instilled the values of kindness, patience, and self-esteem in his viewers, and most of all, taught children how loved they were, just by being themselves. “A Beautiful Day in the Neighborhood” reimagines the songs from the show as poetry, ranging from the iconic “Won’t You Be My Neighbor?” to the forgotten gems. The poems deal with topics such as difficult feelings, new siblings, everyday routines, imagination, and more.

4. “A World of Wonders: Geographic Travels in Verse and Rhyme” by J. Patrick Lewis 

Genre: Poetry, picture books

Could anyone somersault across the San Andreas fault? Why was Hawaii named after a Sandwich? Of longitude and latitude, which has more “flatitude”?

You’ll travel the globe (and some of the sky) discovering answers to these and other questions you never knew to ask. There are all sorts of amazing things to know about thousands of “spaces and places to be” within the fantastical world of GE-OG-RA-PHY. Dive in, come aboard, zip along, take it slow-traveling by book is a great way to go.

5. “Runny Babbit” by Shel Silverstein

Genre: Poetry, humor

From the legendary creator of  Where the Sidewalk Ends, A Light in the Attic, Falling Up,  and  The Giving Tree  comes an unforgettable new character in children’s literature: Runny Babbit. Runny Babbit is Shel Silverstein’s hilarious and New York Times- bestselling book of spoonerisms — words or phrases with letters or syllables swapped: bunny rabbit becomes Runny Babbit. Welcome to the world of Runny Babbit and his friends Toe Jurtle, Skertie Gunk, Rirty Dat, Dungry Hog, Snerry Jake, and many others who speak a topsy-turvy language all their own. 

6. “Oh Theodore! Guinea Pig Poems” by Sasan Katz 

Genre: Poetry, picture books

Come meet a plump, fuzzy guinea pig with a big appetite, a lot to say, and a personality all his own. As you, and his new owner, get to know him, you’ll find out what he eats and how he speaks. You’ll also discover the work involved in caring for a feeding, cleaning, and taking him out for exercise. But it hardly seems like work once your pet becomes your best friend.

With the popularity of guinea pigs as family and classroom pets, Theodore’s antics are sure to ring true to many readers. And for those who haven’t had a guinea pig of their own, these short, funny, and accessible poems will create a vivid first impression.
