title: Sturgis VFW Auxiliary presents  250th anniversary flags to local entities
byline: By Tim Potts Black Hills Pioneer
description: The Sturgis VFW Auxiliary presented flags to the city of Sturgis, Meade County Commission, Sturgis Chamber of Commerce, and the Sturgis VFW Club Tuesday at the Vets Club. Pictured from left are: Sean Henderson, veteran service officer, Nancy Worth, Jim Thompson, city of Sturgis, Sharon Vinson, Christina Koates, Doreen Creed and Gary Deering, Meade County commissioners, Travis Bartels, Veronica Grosek, Slater Tople, Mike Pauling, and Jessie Dufner, President of the chamber, board. Pioneer photo by Tim Potts
_full_text:
Sturgis VFW Auxiliary presents  250th anniversary flags to local entities
The Sturgis VFW Auxiliary presented flags to the city of Sturgis, Meade County Commission, Sturgis Chamber of Commerce, and the Sturgis VFW Club Tuesday at the Vets Club. Pictured from left are: Sean Henderson, veteran service officer, Nancy Worth, Jim Thompson, city of Sturgis, Sharon Vinson, Christina Koates, Doreen Creed and Gary Deering, Meade County commissioners, Travis Bartels, Veronica Grosek, Slater Tople, Mike Pauling, and Jessie Dufner, President of the chamber, board. Pioneer photo by Tim Potts 

STURGIS — The Sturgis VFW Post 2730 and its auxiliary presented commemorative 250th anniversary flags to several local organizations Tuesday evening at the Sturgis Veterans Club.  

Flags were presented to Meade County, the city of Sturgis, the Sturgis Area Chamber of Commerce, and the Sturgis Veterans Club as part of a statewide effort to recognize the upcoming 250th anniversary of the United States.

Nancy Worth, past president of the Sturgis VFW Auxiliary, said the project was inspired by a challenge issued to auxiliaries across South Dakota.

“We have been challenged across the state to do something for the 250th anniversary of the United States,” Worth said. “So, what we’ve done is join forces with our local post and purchase four 250th anniversary flags.”  Each of the four flags will be displayed at a designated location in the community during the nation’s 250th anniversary celebration, particularly around the Fourth of July.

“One will be given to Meade County, one to the city of Sturgis, one to the Sturgis Chamber of Commerce, and one to the Sturgis Veterans Club, to be flown during the time frame of celebrating our 250th birthday,” Worth said.

Worth said the idea came after she discovered information about the commemorative flags online. 

“I saw the information online, and these flags are actually South Dakota-made, they came out of Sioux Falls,” she said. “I brought it up to our auxiliary, and they said yes, we’ll do it. The post went along with it, and we split the cost.”

During the presentation, Worth emphasized the auxiliary’s appreciation for the community partners receiving the flags.

Mike Pauling, commander of VFW Post 2730, said the event reflects the organization’s commitment to both honoring the nation’s history and supporting the local community.

“I’m here to show my support for the greater Sturgis community and this dedication to make South Dakota stronger,” Pauling said. “It’s also in remembrance of everything that has been sacrificed to get us where we are today in the land of freedom.”

title: Spearfish discusses next steps to address Main St., Colorado Blvd. chip seal failure
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: SPEARFISH — On Monday, Spearfish city staff fielded questions from the public and the city council about the Colorado Boulevard and Main Street Chip Seal failure.
_full_text:
SPEARFISH — On Monday, Spearfish city staff fielded questions from the public and the city council about the Colorado Boulevard and Main Street Chip Seal failure.

On Sept. 2, 2025, the city awarded the project to Dakota Asphalt for $475,385.56. Simon Contractors of SD, Inc. and Bituminous Paving, Inc. also initially bid on the project. At the time, engineers estimated the total project cost at $519,385.56, including construction, consultant design, and construction administration. The budget for the chip seal project was $200,000, so the council voted to pass a supplement of $319,385.56.

The project got underway in the fall, and almost immediately, it became clear that the attempt to preserve the pavement through downtown and along Colorado Boulevard had failed, according to city staff at Monday’s meeting. 

“We have been and are still working on evaluating the cause of the failure,” Spearfish Public Works Director Adam McMahon said.

In recent weeks, community members and business owners have contacted city leaders with complaints about the state of the road and the dust and loose chips stirred up by the project.

“It is easily recognizable, whether it’s by a citizen, or an elected official, or business owner that the downtown project contractor did not meet any of the standards as intended,” said Spearfish Mayor John Senden. “The business owners with outdoor seating, this has caused additional work (for them), so we’re sorry indeed for any challenges that have been presented.”

On Monday, John Williams, who owns 2 businesses on Main Street, asked the council to treat the road repair as an infrastructure emergency.

“I would caution council as you are making a decision on this, time is of the essence. We are a tourism community,” said Spearfish resident Nathan Hoogshagen.

Councilman Larry Klarenbeek said it might be time to stop working on determining the reason for the chip seal failure and focus on a speedy repair.

“We really need a date or dates,” Klarenbeek said. “We don’t really need to determine cause. It seems to me we need to get it done.”

Councilwoman Ana Rath offered her agreement.

“We are evaluating our options,” McMahon said. “Some of the options that are being considered are: a refund of the project, a reapplication of the chip seal, a mill and overlay, a combination of a chip seal and mill and overlay, and concrete surfacing downtown.”

Try chip seal again

Dakota Asphalt representatives have acknowledged that the result of the chip seal project is unacceptable. They’ve also offered to re-perform the service they were hired to do, under warranty.

“They’re not any happier about it than anyone else. They are not trying to make the city accept this as it is,” said Spearfish Attorney David Knox. The current contract with Dakota Asphalt still stands. Until that agreement is dissolved, the city can’t bring in another contractor for the work under the document’s terms.

“Personally, I think that it is better to go ahead and proceed with the current contract,” Knox said. “I think we should give them the opportunity to perform like they are supposed to and then, at a minimum, give us additional time to decide longer term what we want to do.”

McMahon said the state offered to send support from the department of transportation to help the company calibrate its equipment, with the hope of increasing the likelihood of a successful round 2 result.

“One could build a case that says, ‘They didn’t do a very good job this time, and now we are going to ask them to do it again,’” said Councilman Dean Myers. Myers asked Knox to clarify if the city has a performance bond it could call in.

“I believe we do. If they fail or refuse to perform. At this point, though, they are offering to perform. They are offering to come in and make it right,” Knox said.

The city could negotiate to dissolve its contract with Dakota Asphalt and bring in another company to finish the job. That would require putting the project back out to bid, which could further delay the start of actual road work.

Even with Dakota Asphalt redoing the job, timing will remain an issue. Chip sealing requires ground temperatures to be high enough for the oil laid down by crews to bind with asphalt chips and “cure.” The job can’t be done in the winter. 

“We can’t, or shouldn’t start work until at least May 15, whether it is asphalt or chip seal. Those are the seasonal limitations for his work,” McMahon said. “Asphalt plants aren’t even open yet. They’re kind of forecasting that they’ll open late April or early May at this point.”

“If they are saying they are going to come back and fix it, let’s get it fixed. I don’t think we need to sit and wait for a cause, because they’ve already offered and said, ‘Now, we are going to fix it.’ Have a date,” Rath said.

Mill and overlay instead

Mill and overlay generally involves milling or grinding down the top of the road surface to lay down new asphalt. In answer to a question from Spearfish resident Doug Markworth, McMahon said the lifespan of chip seal is, “5-10 years versus 10-20 for asphalt.”

Mill and overlay on Main Street and Colorado Boulevard could be more expensive, and would also require either extensive contract renegotiations or the dissolution of the city’s contract with Dakota Asphalt. A mill and overlay project would need to go through the bid process.

Markworth said there is a citizen petition circulating to see at least a portion of the road surface asphalted with a mill and overlay rather than chip sealed a second time.

Apply a combination of chip seal, mill, and overlay

The petition asks that the city consider recovering funds from the fall 2025 chip seal project and applying them to a mill and overlay on the 500, 600, and 700 blocks of Main Street as a “long term solution.” The petition further asks that asphalt be applied to Colorado Boulevard “as feasible up to where infrastructure improvements are required.” 

“I know the combination of chip seal and overlay has been the heavily discussed option in the public, kind of the rough cost estimate for that would be in the $100,000-200,000 range,” McMahon said.

Hoogshagen asked in person if it might make the most sense to mill and overlay the downtown core and Main Street, then proceed with chip seal on Colorado Boulevard.

“Is there an opportunity to go in and perform some sort of adjustment to the contract and say, okay, we would like you to come and chip seal portions of Colorado further south, but then maybe have some sort of change to the contract to have them do mill and overlay on Main Street and that would still abide by the terms of the contract so that we are not going over that?” Hoogshagen asked.

Knox said that could be achieved through an amendment of contract of the change order process.

“There is a change order process, but both parties have to agree. I can’t guarantee or comment on whether the contractor would agree to that change. That would be one of the first steps in the process,” McMahon said. “There are limitations on change order, certain (dollar) amounts we can go up to on that to determine to what extent we can accomplish that.”

Explore concrete surfacing downtown

In a move that would require similar contractual renegotiations, McMahon noted the city had the option to try applying concrete downtown on the roadway. Little further discussion was had around that option at Monday’s meeting.

‘Time is of the essence’

“Our goal at this point is to make a determination sometime in April, with work starting in June,” McMahon said.

No matter what the council decides moving forward, timing in an issue. The work can’t start too early, or it might not be successful. Start too late, and roadwork runs up against peak tourist season and community events that help support Spearfish’s downtown businesses.

“(Mayor) John (Senden), I want to commend you for going and talking to all the businesses downtown. I want to thank you for that. If the decision is made to do something in June, please include downtown building and business owners in that before you pull the trigger on it,” Hoogshagen said. “That is when those folks make their money for the year.”

McMahon said Downtown Friday nights — a concert and festival on Main Street — poses a particular challenge.

“The city’s goal is to coordinate projects that impact events before they are scheduled so that other accommodations can be made,” McMahon said. Those kinds of accommodations are usually made at least a year in advance.

“You said we wouldn’t want to get a project started this year because of our downtown Friday night events,” said Councilwoman Jamie Hafner. “Is there not an option to move them?”

“Potentially,” McMahon replied. “There are a lot of factors to work out on that. Relocating can be challenging because of the power we need for the stage and other accommodations.”

Senden suggested that the event could be moved to 5th Street.

title: Fyffe, Pesicka, and Hintz elected to join Belle Fourche city council
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: Left: John Fyffe was elected to the Belle Fourche City Council in Ward 1 Tuesday, according to preliminary election results.
_full_text:
Fyffe, Pesicka, and Hintz elected to join Belle Fourche city council
Left: John Fyffe was elected to the Belle Fourche City Council in Ward 1 Tuesday, according to preliminary election results. 

UPDATED -

Pioneer staff reports 

BELLE FOURCHE— The unofficial results of Tuesday’s special municipal election in Belle Fourche are in. According to preliminary numbers provided by the city of Belle Fourche, John Fyffe claimed the Ward 1 council seat with 79 votes. Wilma Goehring trailed with 25 votes.

“I think this will be a unique opportunity for Belle Fourche, with three new people in leadership,” Fyffe told the Pioneer. “It’s an exiting time in Belle Fourche, we’ve got a lot of big projects coming up.” 

Fyffe noted that the city is looking at relocating or expanding the landfill, upgrading its water treatment facility and other 100-year-old water infrastructure. He also referenced the new police department project in describing some of the reasons he’s looking forward to joining the council and the discussion on topics that are important to voters. 

In Ward 2, the race was tight. Gene “Geno” Pesicka is set to be sworn in as alderman after claiming 84 votes — just 2 more than Falina Selchert. 

“To everybody who has ever said that your vote does not matter, let this election be evidence that it does,” Pesicka said. “I won by 2 votes. One vote fewer and it would have been a tie, 2 votes fewer and I would be spending more nights with my family. The process is important, and you vote does count.” 

There were 4 candidates vying for the Ward 2 seat, including Faith Kari-Melanson with 28 votes and Aydalou “Ida” Caser with 42.

Pesicka said he’s looking forward to taking on his new role in Belle Fourche. 

“There are a lot of things that the city council handles. I will step in where I am needed most,” Pesicka said. “I am eager to do my part and get to work.” 

Vern Hintz ran unopposed in Ward 3 and is expected to be sworn in as alderman.

The Pioneer reached out to each of the candidates in this week’s race for further comment. Only Fyffe responded by press time.

ORIGINAL -

BELLE FOURCHE — The unofficial results of Tuesday’s special municipal election in Belle Fourche are in. According to preliminary numbers provided by the city of Belle Fourche, John Fyffe claimed the Ward 1 council seat with 79 votes. Wilma Goehring trailed with 25 votes.

In Ward 2, the race was tighter. Gene “Geno” Pesicka is set to be sworn in as alderman after claiming 84 votes — just 2 more than Falina Selchert. There were 4 candidates vying for the Ward 2 seat, including Faith Kari-Melanson with 28 votes and Aydalou “Ida” Caser with 42.

Vern Hintz ran unopposed in Ward 3 and is expected to be sworn in as alderman.

title: Handley Center asks for  task force to assess city takeover plan, recreation need
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: In August, the city of Lead agreed to fund the Handley Recreation Center through May, and held the remaining funds for recreation in the city budget to possibly fund a takeover plan. On Monday, Handley Center President Darla Auld asked the city to start moving forward with a plan that can be successfully implemented by the end of May. Pioneer file photo
_full_text:
Handley outside.jpg
In August, the city of Lead agreed to fund the Handley Recreation Center through May, and held the remaining funds for recreation in the city budget to possibly fund a takeover plan. On Monday, Handley Center President Darla Auld asked the city to start moving forward with a plan that can be successfully implemented by the end of May. Pioneer file photo 

LEAD — The Handley Center board wants the city to establish a task force that will assess recreation in the community and the best ways to serve citizen’s recreational needs. 

On Monday, Darla Auld, president of the Handley Center board, proposed the task force to move the process forward for the city’s consideration of taking over the recreation center that is currently run by the nonprofit board. She asked for the independent task force to focus on analysis, programming needs, childcare needs, and whether a new facility or the existing building is the most efficient model for the future. 

For many years the city of Lead has allocated $125,000 to the Handley Recreation Center, to run recreation needs for the city. When the city did its budget in August 2025, it assigned $52,000 to the Handley Center nonprofit board to run recreation programs and a preschool through May 2026, and it set aside the remaining $73,000 into a separate city fund. The idea at that time was that the city would explore the possibility of taking over the Handley Center as a city-run facility, and the remaining funds would pay for staff and upkeep. 

But since that time, Auld said there has been no movement toward the city taking over. 

“The Handley board and membership has been patient and cooperative,” Auld said. “We agreed to a May 2026 transition when the city stipend ends for the Handley because this commission voted last summer to hold budgeted funds for that purpose. But with less than 2 months left until the current funding model expires, the time for waiting and seeing has passed. Anyone who has ever started a business knows it takes at least a year of hands-on operation just to understand the mechanics and service flow. The city has never managed this facility or any recreation other than parks. One cannot fully grasp the complexity of the service we offer, without stepping up to the plate.”

In February, Mayor Dustin Heupel announced that the city would likely vote on a resolution to take over management of the Handley Center at its March 2 meeting. But to date, there has not been a resolution brought to the city commission for consideration.  

Whether the city agrees to take on the Handley Center, Auld said city funding is imperative to prevent total closure. 

“The imminent need is to launch a transition plan from the city by May 29,” she said. 

One of the sticking points in negotiations has been the Handley board’s insistence on deed restrictions, which would require the city to turn the building back over to the nonprofit board if it ever decides it does not want to run it. In the past Heupel has said he will not tie future commissions to that agreement. But Auld said it’s a safeguard for the city and for the citizens, to ensure the building is not lost to a private developer and recreation opportunities are preserved for the future. 

“It’s a clean ‘out’ for the city,” she said. “If a better location is eventually found, or if the city determines it can no longer manage the facility, the community doesn’t lose this asset to a private developer. It protects our residents regardless of the building’s future.” 

But some residents have long questioned the structural integrity of the building, and have repeatedly asked the city to get an engineering report about needed repairs, before committing to taking it over. City officials said they have not ordered such a report.  

On Monday Rose Burns asked the city whether they have an engineering report. 

“If you’re looking at a deadline of May 30, one would think that would be something you’d want to review,” Burns said. “A task force should have been part of this discussion when this was brought up last summer. There are questions that need to be asked.” 

Opponents to the idea of the city taking over the Handley Center say those recreation funds should be re-directed to pay for public infrastructure repairs and improvements, and many point to the building’s need for extensive repair. The building needs a new roof, new heating units, a new elevator, and other costly repairs. 

Burns asked about a possible selection process for the proposed task force, but members of the city commission did not address the matter. Auld’s proposal was made during a time when visitors can bring items to the commission that are not on the official agenda. However, the city cannot legally take action on items that are not listed on the agenda, so the proposal was viewed as informational. 

During her presentation to the commission, Auld also addressed the difference between the Handley Center and the Deadwood Recreation Center. The 2 facilities complement each other, with the Handley Center offering services that Deadwood does not. Local baseball, archery, hockey, and other teams rely on the Handley’s multipurpose room for indoor practices, the facility offers a weight room and walking track with 24-7 access, and it operates a preschool center, among other programming. 

“While we work alongside the Deadwood Rec, we offer different, essential services that complement one another,” she said. “One cannot simply replace the other. The Handley is the last remaining venue for the specific indoor programming our residents and the surrounding area rely on. We provide the only infrastructure for many of our youth sports teams, and we have done it in the most cost-effective way possible for local citizens.” 

Auld urged the city commission to move forward with a plan for the recreation center, so services are not lost. 

“Lead is a community that builds things rather than watching them fade,” she said. “Citizens wait for a plan, we have the public support for a plan, and we have the facility. We ask the commission for a firm commitment to the future and the immediate seating of this task force, to ensure no lapse in service for our residents this summer.”

title: Deadwood prepares for  return of annual foodie event
byline: By Sara Morgan Black Hills Pioneer
description: Foodies are invited to Deadwood’s annual Forks, Corks, and Kegs event, where they can sample exclusive appetizers and high-end beers and wines. Pioneer file photo
_full_text:
Deadwood prepares for  return of annual foodie event
Foodies are invited to Deadwood’s annual Forks, Corks, and Kegs event, where they can sample exclusive appetizers and high-end beers and wines. Pioneer file photo 

DEADWOOD — Deadwood Main Street will flow with the finest brews and vintages this weekend with the return of Forks, Corks, and Kegs, the city’s annual ticketed foodie event.

The event is scheduled to kick off Friday with event registration at the Deadwood Mountain Grand from 2-8 p.m. The Wine Around Deadwood will run from 5-7 p.m. at various locations around Deadwood, where representatives from major beer and wine distributors around the area will be present to answer questions. 

The Appetizer Crawl is scheduled to take place from 7-9 p.m., where ticketholders are invited to sample dishes created by local chefs and paired with a beer or wine sample.

“The appetizer crawl is a favorite of the event, and it’s a great chance for chefs to make creative appetizers,” said Jesse Allen, events coordinator for the Deadwood Chamber of Commerce. “It allows them to be a little creative, try something different, and get out of the box.”

The event continues into Saturday with the Deadwood Dine Around taking place from 11 a.m. to 3 p.m., offering visitors another chance to taste dishes, beers, and wines created for the event.

“That’s the same thing, it really gives chefs the chance to feature some of their higher-end products, some things you might not have seen on the menu before,” said Allen. “It’s also a great opportunity for people to get out and see what their favorite restaurants have to offer and maybe try a place you haven’t been to beforehand.”

The Grand Tasting will take place from 4-6 p.m. at the Deadwood Mountain Grand, where visitors are invited to pick up their commemorative glass and sample major distributors newest and greatest products.

Allen said 15 businesses have signed up to participate this year. Each location is set to feature a culinary creation paired with a complementary beer or wine. To taste the carefully curated creations, participants must be 21 or older and purchase either a general admission or VIP ticket. 

The VIP experience includes an exclusive tasting event at the Deadwood Social Club Friday night, where representatives will be onsite to talk about wines and answer questions.

 Smashed Grapes, from 2-3 p.m. on Saturday, is a brand-new VIP experience this year. The event buys ticketholders a chance to learn from 4 top wine representatives from around the country, who will blindly try to sell taster on their top tier products. Participants will vote on their favorite wine for a chance to crown the Smashed Grapes Champion. 

VIP ticketholders also gain early entry into the grand tasting event on Saturday and complimentary access to the reserve room, where high-end wines will be available for tasting.

All tickets include a commemorative glass and free trolley rides. 

Allen said the event is a great opportunity to get out and experience everything Deadwood has to offer. 

“Deadwood, of course, is known for their gaming and food and beer and wine, and this is a great way to get out and have a great time,” said Allen. 

To purchase tickets visit Deadwood.com or purchase tickets at registration from 2-8 p.m. on Friday or 9 a.m. to noon on Saturday at the Deadwood Mountain Grand.

title: Engineering the amenities at America’s Underground Lab
byline: By Mike Ray Sanford Underground Research Facility
description: Nate Sanchez, a plumbing contractor with Harrison Western, works to install a bathroom pod on the 4,850-foot level of SURF. Photo by Stephen Kenny/SURF
_full_text:
20251006-BathroomInstall_Stephen Kenny_LR-3.jpg
Nate Sanchez, a plumbing contractor with Harrison Western, works to install a bathroom pod on the 4,850-foot level of SURF. Photo by Stephen Kenny/SURF 

 20251006-BathroomInstall_Stephen Kenny_LR-7.jpg
Andrew Brosnahan, a professional engineer at SURF, looks through a set of pipes on the 4,850-foot level.  Photo by Stephen Kenny/SURF 

 20251006-BathroomInstall_Stephen Kenny_LR-8.jpg
One of the new restroom pods at SURF provided by a local company, B&T Manufacturing, located at the 4,850-foot level Ross Campus. Photo by Stephen Kenny/SURF 

LEAD — Installing a world-class laboratory nearly a mile underground in a former gold mine that must accommodate 250 scientists, engineers and technicians daily requires a few basic amenities — including restrooms. 

But engineering even the simplest amenities 4,850 feet underground is not as easy as it sounds.

Andrew Brosnahan, a professional engineer at SURF, led the engineering and design efforts for the new bathrooms at the 4,850-foot level in the Ross Campus.

“It’s so different down here for installing anything. Being 4,850 feet below the surface is the most challenging part of it,” Brosnahan said. 

He noted the limited space in the drifts (or tunnels), and the uneven rock walls that pose additional hurdles when doing something simple, like running water or electric lines. 

“Just unspooling all the pipe coils, and then adding in all the anchors, there’s a lot of work just to get all the piping installed. You just don’t have nice square geometry to run things like you would in a building on the surface,” he said.

Brosnahan said technicians at SURF completed much of the work for the project. Their vast experience working at the unique site proved essential to the project’s success.

“The SDSTA electrical and operation crews were instrumental in helping on this project. The electrical group here at SURF did an amazing job completing this portion of the project,” he said.

Brosnahan said SURF was fortunate to partner with a local Rapid City company, B&T Manufacturing, which builds ready-made modular bathroom pods that are basically plug-and-play. The modular bathrooms also fit into the cage — the conveyance used to transport people and equipment from the surface underground — making it easy to deliver them a mile underground.

“The restrooms look great and we are thankful that B&T Manufacturing is so good to work with and has the capabilities to fabricate these custom restrooms with such proximity to SURF. Usually something like this would be procured somewhere halfway across the country,” he said.

Brosnahan also credited Harrison Western, which helped with the installation and hook-up of the utilities to the pods.

“Harrison Western is great to work with. Their crews are always hard working and are ready to work with us to overcome challenges on a project like this,” Brosnahan said.

Running water and electricity to the modular bathrooms was just one challenge. Engineers and technicians also had to figure out how to treat the wastewater. On the surface this would be as simple as plumbing into a municipal or private septic system. But a mile underground, all water needs to be treated on-site before it’s discharged.

“This is the intake to our 2-stage treatment system designed by Orenco,” Brosnahan said while placing his hand on a large tank sitting at the 4,850-foot level of the Ross Campus. “Stage 1 is the septic tank and is just like in your house, same kind of concept. The next tank is the second stage and provides the actual water treatment. This tank is a multiple-pass, packed-bed aerobic wastewater treatment system for processing residential strength wastewater.  At a high level the incoming waste is cycled multiple times over filter curtains prior to being discharged.” 

Brosnahan says the water is also run through an ultraviolet process before being sent as treated water into the deep pool at SURF. This water is eventually pumped to the surface by the SDSTA dewatering system to be treated again as mine wastewater, before being sent down Gold Run Creek.

The underground amenities at SURF have come a long way since the historic mining days. Before the laboratory, miners had a toilet seat on a railcar tank they called the “Red Wagon.” Dan Regan, who worked for the former Homestake Mine and then SURF for more than 50 years, recalls the process.

“So, they’d place that Red Wagon on the rails strategically in an area, and when you needed the services you used it, flipped the lid. And of course, they wouldn’t just ship them up when they were used once. They’d wait until they were near plumb-full. My goodness, it was a real task. No one wanted to be the person assigned to ship them up the shaft and replace them.”

The research in the vicinity of SURF’s 4,850-foot level Ross Campus includes the deep underground neutrino experiment. This and other experiments at SURF will require hundreds of personnel underground daily through this century. This makes flushing out the engineering challenges needed to upgrade the Red Wagon worth all the effort.

title: Spearfish passes resolution forming new ‘affordable’ housing TIF near Exit 14
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: SPEARFISH — On Monday, the Spearfish City Council unanimously resolved to form a new Tax Increment Finance District (TIF 9) to support Sioux Falls-based Lloyd Companies, LLC, in financing the development of Stone Ridge Commons, a 94-unit multi-family rental housing project.
_full_text:
SPEARFISH — On Monday, the Spearfish City Council unanimously resolved to form a new Tax Increment Finance District (TIF 9) to support Sioux Falls-based Lloyd Companies, LLC, in financing the development of Stone Ridge Commons, a 94-unit multi-family rental housing project. 

Those 94 rental units will be distributed across 5 buildings — some will be townhome-style with attached garages. Others will be flat-style apartments, according to representatives of Lloyd Companies, who presented to the city council.

Stone Ridge Commons is designed to offer 22, one-bedroom units, 46, 2-bedroom units, and 26, 3-bedroom units, all with rent capped at 80% of Area Median Income (AMI) for 10 years. AMI is currently $102,200, as set by the state. That figure caps rent for a one-bedroom apartment at $1,635, rent for a 2-bedroom at $1,840, and a 3-bedroom unit at $2,044.

As presented in the project plan, the site to the west of the Spearfish Holiday Inn and Convention Center will be equipped with 23 garage stalls, a management office, a play area for children, a dog park, and open green space. The development plan also includes the connection of Platinum Drive (where Culver’s is located) and Paramount Drive (which runs north of the Holiday Inn Convention Center).

“The site and surrounding parcels within the Paramount and Platinum Drive corridor remain largely undeveloped due to a lack of public infrastructure necessary to support development, including adequate roadway connectivity as well as water and sewer improvements,” the TIF project plan, which was submitted to the city council, reads. “As a result, the property and surrounding parcels are underutilized and do not contribute meaningfully to the city’s tax base in its current condition.”

In addition to unanimously resolving to establish TIF 9, the council also unanimously approved the project plan for the Stone Ridge Commons — both moves were recommended by Spearfish Planning Commission. Those decisions were not made without community members expressing some apprehension.

Jeremy Smith, following Mayor John Senden’s new questions policy, queried city staff via email. He then presented his questions in person on Monday.

Rental properties, Smith said, did not appear to qualify for funding through an affordable housing TIF under state law or city policy.

“Housing expected to be provided is owner-occupied, single-family homes or attached homes. That is in our city’s TIF plan, talking about affordable housing,” Smith said. “I don’t think this comes anywhere close to that as rental housing.”

City Attorney David Knox said that rental housing wasn’t explicitly included in the chapter of South Dakota Law Smith referenced.

“In 2018, the legislature adopted affordable housing as an acceptable type of TIF. The key requirements are that if they are sold to individual purchasers, they are priced below the first-time home buyer limit put out by South Dakota HUD (Housing and Urban Development), or if they are rented, they are rented at a maximum percentage,” Knox said. “I understand the confusion, but Title 13 does allow for affordable housing to be a part of a TIF.”

“It is consistent with the city’s comprehensive plan as well as the city’s tax increment financing guideline recommendations,” Spearfish Community Development Director Peter Wysocki said. “Affordable housing is one of the many principles for use of tax increment financing. … The policy doesn’t restrict affordable housing’s definition to only affordable housing purchased in the city.”

Tax increment financing is a public financing system that uses future increases in property taxes to reimburse the costs of public improvements built within a designated TIF boundary. The value of all the properties inside the district is assessed or calculated, and the total amount of property tax generated by all those properties is noted — the base amount of property tax revenues. All property revenue above this base amount, for the life of the TIF, is captured by the TIF to refund the costs of the public improvements or project. This amount — the amount over the base — is called the “tax increment.” Over time, property values are presumed to increase in part due to infrastructure improvements brought about by the TIF project itself. When the TIF is retired, the total taxation values revert to the original taxing entities.

“Our site (base) valuation that we received from the Lawrence County Board of Equalization is $152,700. Our projected year 20 valuation for the project would be about $18.3 million,” Lloyd Companies Development Project Manager Ricky Quintana said. “That is based off of actual assessed values of comparable properties here in Spearfish.”

The city council approved a 20-year life span for TIF 9. Councilman Scott Hourigan asked, with the housing demand in Spearfish, if the developer thought the TIF might be paid off early. The company’s senior financial analyst, Kellen Erpenbach, said that would be tough to estimate.

“We hope it would for you guys, but I don’t have any numbers I can commit to,” Erpenbach said.

Smith asked the city what the financial impact would be on all of the taxing entities that would not receive levies based on the full tax valuation of the property as it is developed over the lifetime of the TIF.

“As we look at the fiscal impact, the entire TIF district debt that (Lloyd Companies) is requesting is the $3,233,000. That is going to be the total impact to our taxing entities. Because we don’t know the future mil levies for those taxing entities, we can’t give an exact dollar amount,” said Spearfish Finance Officer Michelle DeNeui. “They do provide our current mil levy. If you take our mil levy, the city would look at property tax and not receiving that for 20 years, a total of $471,690 based on our mill levy of $1.93.4 per taxable valuation. The county’s portions would be $676,805, and then the school district, because this is an affordable housing TIF, the school doesn’t lose out on anything.”

The state aid formula, DeNeui said, should cover about $2,084,558 that would have been deferred from the school district.

“So this TIF is an indirect way of utilizing state aid formula funds to pay for school district mill levies for projects in Spearfish. So part of the financing of this road and infrastructure, $2.4 million, is being redirected from education funds,” Smith said. “I’m concerned that they (TIFs) hinder our state education as a whole.”

With the TIF and the project approved, Lloyd Companies can move forward with developing Stone Ridge Commons.

“We anticipate going through site plans and specs around this spring,” Quintana said. “Construction of Paramount and Platinum Drive would kick off at the same time as the construction of the multi-family project. They will both kick off tentatively in June of 2026.” 

Quintana said he expects the majority of the project to be completed by the fall of 2027.

title: Artemis II astronauts channel Apollo 8  with a striking Earthset photo
description: In this image provided by NASA, The Artemis II crew captured this view of an Earthset on Monday, as they flew around the Moon. Photo courtesy of NASA
_full_text:
Artemis II astronauts channel Apollo 8  with a striking Earthset photo
In this image provided by NASA, The Artemis II crew captured this view of an Earthset on Monday, as they flew around the Moon. Photo courtesy of NASA 

HOUSTON (AP) — The Artemis II astronauts are now forever intertwined with Apollo 8.

A day after the historic lunar flyaround, NASA on Tuesday released striking new photos taken by the U.S.-Canadian crew.

The four astronauts channeled Apollo 8’s famous Earthrise shot from 1968 with their own: Earthset, showing our planet setting behind the gray, pockmarked moon. Another photo captures the total solar eclipse that occurred when the moon blocked the sun from the crew’s perspective.

The three Americans and one Canadian are now headed home, with a splashdown in the Pacific set for Friday. In the meantime, scientists at Houston’s Mission Control are poring over the stream of moon photos beaming down.

Apollo 8’s three astronauts became the world’s first lunar visitors, orbiting the moon on Christmas Eve 1968. Their Earthrise shot became a symbol of the modern-day environmental movement.

Artemis II marks NASA’s first return to the moon with astronauts — a critical step toward a lunar landing by another crew in two years.

title: Historical, cultural, and environmental tensions mount ahead of uranium exploration hearing
byline: By Meghan O’Brien Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: The walls of Craven Canyon, in the southern Black Hills of South Dakota, are marked with ancient Native American rock art. A proposal to drill exploratory holes in search of uranium near the canyon has sparked controversy. Photo courtesy of Lilias Jarding, Black Hills Clean Water Alliance
_full_text:
Historical, cultural, and environmental tensions mount ahead of uranium exploration hearing
The walls of Craven Canyon, in the southern Black Hills of South Dakota, are marked with ancient Native American rock art. A proposal to drill exploratory holes in search of uranium near the canyon has sparked controversy. Photo courtesy of Lilias Jarding, Black Hills Clean Water Alliance 

A plan to drill exploratory holes in search of uranium near a southern Black Hills canyon lined with ancient Native American rock art has raised tensions ahead of a hearing next week to determine the project’s fate.

At a procedural hearing in January, a tense exchange with project opponents led to an outburst by a member of the board considering the project’s permit application.

“Your goal in life is to be a jerk,” board member Bob Morris told a member of the public attending the hearing.

The comment was directed toward Taylor Gunhammer. He’s an Oglala Lakota community organizer.

“It was just profoundly unprofessional,” Gunhammer said afterward, reacting to the exchange.

Gunhammer and other project opponents were arguing for the relocation of a permit hearing from the state capital city of Pierre, in central South Dakota, to a location closer to the proposed project in the southwestern part of the state. Morris’ fellow board members ultimately agreed to the move and scheduled a five-day hearing to begin Monday in the city of Hot Springs.

The hearing will be a continuation of a long-running struggle over proposed uranium mining in the southern Black Hills.

Project scope

Uranium is a metallic, radioactive element used as fuel in nuclear weapons and nuclear power plants.

Uranium mining became common in the 1950s to supply the United States with materials for Cold War nuclear weapons. That included the southern Black Hills of South Dakota, where mining and milling occurred in the Edgemont area until the 1970s. Much of that mining took the form of open pits, some of which were never reclaimed to a natural condition.

In the early 2000s, a new proposal arose to mine uranium near the southern Black Hills map dots of Dewey and Burdock. That plan, from Texas-based enCore Energy, has met staunch opposition from Native Americans and environmental activists and has been in regulatory and legal limbo for two decades. The project would utilize the “in situ” method of drilling wells and injecting a solvent to loosen underground uranium deposits and draw them to the surface.

Last year, the federal government’s Permitting Council selected enCore’s project for inclusion in FAST-41, a process intended to improve coordination among permitting agencies and hold them accountable to deadlines.

Interest in uranium exploration and mining has risen recently, in response to nuclear energy’s potential to meet the growing electricity demands of data centers handling the computing needs of artificial intelligence.

In March 2024, Clean Nuclear Energy Corporation and its Canada-based parent company Nexus Uranium filed an application to drill exploratory holes in search of uranium near Craven Canyon in the southern Black Hills, about 3 miles southeast of the proposed Dewey-Burdock mine.

Nexus says it has claims on a total of nearly 10 square miles of land along the southern edge of the Black Hills. Its proposed exploration project, called Chord, spans a mix of federal and state land totaling nearly 6 square miles. The company has a lease on 1 square mile of state land in Fall River County, which is the portion of the project under consideration by the state Board of Minerals and Environment.

Clean Nuclear Energy plans to use 50 drilling sites to drill holes up to 700 feet deep on the state land, according to its 2024 application. Each hole will take approximately two weeks to drill. It’s estimated that the operation will use up to 10,000 gallons of water daily, drawn from nearby municipal and private water sources, to help lubricate drills.

A part of the Chord exploration project would also take place on U.S. Forest Service land, where the agency is determining whether an environmental assessment is necessary. It would use an additional 17 drill sites. The federal agency has fielded nearly 1,000 public comments about the project, including opposition from six tribal nations.

The citizens of Fall River County, where much of the historical mining took place and new mining is proposed, voted in 2022 to declare mining for uranium a public nuisance. But Clean Nuclear Energy attorneys argue that state law supersedes county ordinances, and the Minerals and Environment Board agreed.

Morris, appointed by the board to rule on motions, said the decision hinged on the difference in South Dakota’s legal definitions of a “mining operation” and a “uranium exploration operation,” which “constitute separate actions.”

Water and rock art concerns

More than 40 people and organizations have formally raised concerns about the drilling on state land. The area holds cultural and religious significance to Indigenous people, for whom the Black Hills is a sacred site in their spiritual traditions. The drilling sites are near Craven Canyon, which is home to ancient rock art, including petroglyphs and pictographs.

Some of the rock art is thought to be about 7,000 years old, according to NDN Collective, an Indigenous rights organization based in Rapid City. The canyon itself has been there for “generations immemorial,” said Duane Two Bulls, a lead organizer with Black Hills Clean Water Alliance.

“It predates our people,” Two Bulls said. “It was one of the first spots that, in our creation stories and in our history, it was always there.”

Some of the rock art depicts ceremonies and creation stories, Two Bulls said.

“The main concern that would come to my mind right away is that whenever they start drilling, like on top of the canyon, then it’s going to affect the soundscape. And it’s going to cause like an internal vibration where that drilling, that drilling may possibly cause the pictographs on the canyon, the petroglyphs to pretty much shale off the canyon.

Gunhammer said the drilling proposal is an affront to Indigenous people.

“You would never drill for uranium in the lawn of the White House,” Gunhammer said. “You would never mine for lithium at Arlington National Cemetery.”

A report from the State Historic Preservation Office found that vibratory effects from drilling will be minimal and that the “the risk of damage to these sites is low.”

Other opponents worry that further drilling in the area could disrupt holes left from past exploration projects, leaving underground water deposits known as aquifers vulnerable to possible contamination.

Lilias Jarding is the executive director of the Black Hills Clean Water Alliance.

“It’s a multi-faceted set of problems,” she said. “All the things that drive our economy, our health, our ability to have communities here — because we need water — are all things that are being impacted by the mining rush.”

The drilling will take place in Fall River County, the tenth most impoverished county in the state, according to the National Institute of Minority Health and Disparities. It neighbors the Pine Ridge Reservation and Oglala Lakota County, the state’s most impoverished county.

“We are the people who live downstream,” said Mashanaposhe Camp, from Porcupine, South Dakota. “Whenever the water is affected, anything downstream, we’re going to be the ones to drink this water.”

Native Americans also have treaty claims to the area. All of western South Dakota was set aside as a Great Sioux Reservation by the 1868 Treaty of Fort Laramie. The United States broke that treaty during the 1870s Black Hills gold rush and subsequently forced tribes onto smaller reservations.

“Every single cultural resource in that one square mile of the project area, the area of potential impact, belongs at least to the Sioux Nation, and possibly to others who’ve also traversed that particular sacred site area known as Craven Canyon,” said Chase Iron Eyes, an attorney for the Great Plains Tribal Water Alliance.

Company responds

Jeremy Poirier, CEO of Nexus Uranium, said the company’s project would not impact Craven Canyon.

“This idea that we’re going to be doing exploration anywhere near or in Craven Canyon is false and factually incorrect,” Poirier said. “We’re not doing any exploration in Craven Canyon or near it.”

In permitting materials, maps of the proposed drill sites show them to be above and back from the western rim of the canyon. A letter from the Board of Minerals and Environment says the proposed operation is located within a quarter-mile of the canyon’s edge.

About 160 acres of the canyon are protected from mining by the U.S. Forest Service, but Two Bulls said there are still many scientifically and culturally important sites throughout the area that are unprotected.

The nearest protected site is a little more than 500 feet from a drill site, according to a letter from the State Historic Preservation Office.

That letter also found that the proposed exploration “will not encroach upon, damage, or destroy a historic property which is included in the National and State Registers of Historic Places.”

“There will be temporary effects,” according to the letter, including one drill pad “within the boundaries of a suspected burial site.” That drill pad has been removed, Poirier said, and the state Department of Agriculture and Natural Resources has approved all other proposed drill pad sites.

Poirier, of Nexus Uranium, said he’s “never, ever experienced” this kind of delay on a permit for exploratory uranium drilling.

“We’ve been having those conversations, we spent hundreds and thousands of dollars on studies to follow the state guidelines and the permitting process,” he said. “We’re doing that, and we’ll continue to do that.”

Poirier knew the project would raise concerns.

“We knew that it was going to be a little bit of a longer process than maybe some other processes that I’ve been involved with,” he said. “We’ve gone above and beyond in what we’ve done as far as spending all this money before we even drill a hole.”

The Board of Minerals and Environment can deny an application to explore for uranium for several reasons, including negative impacts on historical, archaeological or recreational aspects of an area, if those impacts outweigh the benefits of the exploration. Members can also deny the permit if it will negatively affect the productivity of aquifers.

title: Sturgis will not appoint mayor Dargatz to fill position until special election, June 2
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: STURGIS — The city of Sturgis will not appoint a mayor to serve until a special election is held, June 2, to fill the vacancy.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

STURGIS — The city of Sturgis will not appoint a mayor to serve until a special election is held, June 2, to fill the vacancy. 

On Monday, the Sturgis City Council approved a resolution, which states that appointing a mayor for 2 months is not practicable. Instead, it states that the city council president, Tony Dargatz, will serve as “Mayor Pro Tempore,” until citizens elect a new mayor. 

The decision was necessary after former Mayor Kevin Forrester resigned March 23, amidst health issues related to pancreatic cancer. Forrester died from the illness on Sunday.

“I think given the history of appointing mayors it would be unsuccessful,” said Councilwoman Ruth Nichols. “I think we have to take into consideration the public’s opinion and give the public the right to vote for mayor.” 

In his resignation letter, Forrester named Rod Bradley as his preferred successor. Before the city council’s vote on Monday, Councilman Jim Thompson said the city should consider Forrester’s wishes. When the city council held a special meeting March 25 to set a special election to fill the mayor’s seat, Thompson asked the council to consider honoring Forrester’s request to appoint Bradley. The council did not respond to Thompson at that time. 

“We have not had that discussion, and when I brought it up last time all I had was silence in what I considered to be very rude to Kevin, who made that request,” Thompson said. “I think that whatever we have done in the past is not the issue now. I would like us to at least discuss that as to why it wasn’t even brought up. There has to be a reason why he made that recommendation. I’m not against elections. I would like that to happen.” 

City Administrator Aaron Jordan said he had many conversations with Forrester about the city’s transition to a new mayor. While Jordan was not with Forrester when he wrote his resignation letter, he said the subject of an appointment was never part of their discussions over the course of about five months, until they discovered a state law, which appeared to require an appointment. Jordan indicated that could be the reason Forrester named Bradley as his choice for an appointment.  

South Dakota Codified Law 9-8-2, states that a mayoral vacancy shall be filled by appointment of a majority vote by the council, as soon as practicable after the vacancy occurs, but if it is not practicable, the council president should serve as mayor pro tempore. 

According to the Sturgis resolution passed on Monday, the council interprets the word “practicable” to mean what is “reasonably capable of being carried out in a workable, sensible, and public interest serving manner.” 

“We discussed it quite often, because we knew that it was a matter of time when he would probably resign and what that transition would look like,” Jordan said. “Also, kind of going back on our experience when we were both on the council, when Mayor (Mark) Carstensen resigned, we had those series of appointments and the consternation that created for our city. So, I think we were always operating under the impression that there was a special election. I feel pretty confident that over that time we had that discussion, and particularly that Friday, we talked specifically about the special election and who would be running, and those types of things. Never once did the appointment come up.” 

The city resolution states that appointing a sitting alderman to fill Forrester’s seat would cause that alderman to vacate the council seat, creating an additional vacancy to be filled. It also states appointing someone for just two months would not be in the best interest of government. 

“Appointing someone who does not presently hold elected municipal office to serve as Mayor for only a short interim period would not materially improve continuity of government and would not serve the public interest as well as permitting the Mayor Pro Tempore to continue performing the duties of the office temporarily under existing law,” according to the resolution. 

The council voted 7-1 to approve the resolution, with Thompson as the lone dissenting vote. Petitions for the mayor’s seat and a Ward 1 council seat are available at city hall, and are due back by 5 p.m. May 1. Residents of Sturgis must obtain signatures from at least 50 registered voters who live inside the city limits, to be placed on the ballot for the mayor’s position.

title: Remix Room brings splashy new art experience to Spearfish
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: SPEARFISH — It’s a rage room, with paint. A place for unbridled curiosity, creativity, and play. Despite its humble, warehouse-like appearance, the Remix Room in Spearfish is determined to make a neon splash.
_full_text:
SPEARFISH — It’s a rage room, with paint. A place for unbridled curiosity, creativity, and play. Despite its humble, warehouse-like appearance, the Remix Room in Spearfish is determined to make a neon splash.

Owner Tiffany Zubrod has always loved crafts and creative activities. As a single mom, she’s bonded with her 10-year-old son over DIY paint nights. The drawback to at-home artistic pursuits? Each activity comes with cleanup.

When Zubrod was diagnosed with breast cancer and was undergoing treatment, her priorities shifted. She wanted her son to have more activities. More ways to express himself and let loose.

So, piece-by-piece, element-by-element, she imagined the Remix Room into being.

“From the outside, it is kind of like ‘what are we getting into here?’” Zubrod said.

Inside an industrial-looking building on Fairgrounds Loop, there is a dark space — lit almost exclusively by black lights. The walls are sprayed and splattered with neon paint, and the padded floor wears the evidence of hours of flying, flinging, and spraying liquid paint.

Patrons book sessions online. When they arrive, they receive a basket packed with liquid paint, brushes, and an ever-evolving variety of accessories.

“Then you get your protective gear. A full suit, hairnets, shin-high booties, and goggles. Once you’re all suited up, you go inside and make what you want of it,” Zubrod said. “It is an invitation to let loose.”

There are paint balloons meant for breaking, and a landscape littered with objects that are meant to get messy.

“Kids go in there, and there is paint flying, screaming, and running. It just varies based on the group what is going to happen,” Zubrod said. “It is common, adults aren’t like, ‘Let’s go destroy things.’ That is not how our brains work, but I have noticed that with adults, we need a little more permission.”

Zubrod said some people enjoy throwing paint at their friends and making “paint angels” out of colorful spills on the floor.

“We have this new feature, it’s kind of like a dunk tank,” Zubrod said. “You throw a foam ball at a thing, and there is a bucket full of paint, someone stands under it and gets covered in paint.”

There’s Jenga, Tic-Tac-Toe, and a handful of other games that patrons can use while they frolic in neon paint. Zubro suggests using the paint “dunk tank” as a way to increase the stakes for adults during board game night.

“Maybe the loser at the end has to go under there, but the kids will be like ‘I want to go under there’ but just like a way for adults to go under there,” Zubrod said. “With adults, you’re not all going to be like, ‘COVER ME IN PAINT.’ Some are. It varies.”

Sometimes, the Remix Room is at its best when it’s chaotic.

Zubrod recalled her experience using the space with her son the first time.

“It was crazy. He and his best friend were my first little trial. It was a few weeks before I even opened,” Zubrod said. “They lost their minds. They had so much fun, but it was so hard for me. Watching your 10-year-old throw paint and run and scream, your instinct is to be like, ‘Hold on. Slow down.’ Then it’s like, ‘Wait, don’t hold on. Have fun. Have fun.’

Zubrod said the magic of the Remix Room is in its desperation from the rest of reality. Where else are you invited to make a mess, all materials included?

“I like the ability for people to come in and forget everything. It is like paint therapy. You can come in here and forget all of the nonsense out there,” Zubrod said.

Conversely, Zubrod said Remix Room can also foster a sense of calm.

“It doesn’t have to be chaos. It doesn’t have to be a disaster. You can rent the space the same, get all of the same gear, but you can sit and turn on whatever music you want, and just have an hour to unwind. You don’t have to clean up the mess. You don’t have to bring the stuff. It is all ready for you, and you can just chill out for an hour,” Zubrod said.

The Remix Room is open by appointment only. More information can be found at www.entertheremix.com.

title: Lead to celebrate Homestake 150th anniversary, Thursday
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: A bond from the Homestake Mining Company, which was the largest gold mine in the western hemisphere for 125 years. Pioneer file photo
_full_text:
Share bond:1879.tif
A bond from the Homestake Mining Company, which was the largest gold mine in the western hemisphere for 125 years. Pioneer file photo 

 0407 fred manuel:1876(RGB).tif
Fred Manuel, with his brother Moses, staked the claim that would become the Homestake 150 years ago.  Pioneer file photo 

 LeadCity:1876.tif
The city of Lead was settled in 1876. Courtesy of Deadwood History, Inc., Homestake Mining Company collection 

LEAD — It’s been 150 years since Fred and Moses Manuel staked the claim that would eventually become the largest gold mine in the western hemisphere — the Homestake Gold Mine. 

The iconic event occurred on April 9, 1876, and on Thursday the city of Lead and other sponsors will hold a special 150th anniversary of Homestake celebration. The event will be held at 7 p.m., at the Historic Homestake Opera House, and will feature 3, 25-minute presentations about the history of Homestake, as well as a gourmet charcuterie board, cake, and a champagne toast. 

Historian David Wolff will kick off the event and discuss what made the Homestake a success. Wolff said it will be a broad-brush overview of key moments in the gold mine’s history, including innovations early superintendents introduced that lead to the cyanide process, building the slime plant, railroad technology, hydroelectric plants, and lumber operations. He will also discuss the importance of the rise in gold prices in the 1930s, and then again in 1970. That will lead to a brief discussion about the Homestake closure and subsequent transfer of the mine to the state of South Dakota, for underground science. 

“I’ll talk about all of those auxiliary enterprises as key elements to the Homestake,” Wolff said. “But also we’ll talk about the workers and some of the evetns that affected the workers. It’s a lot of ground to cover in the time I have, but we’re gonna try to pull it off.” 

After Wolff’s presentation, Ann Delicate Thompson will discuss “Stories from the daughter of a Homestake Mine manager.” Jerry Aberle will discuss the employee culture at Homestake. 

In addition to the presentations, Todd Jones and volunteers with the Historic Homestake Opera House will also be on-hand to give guided tours of the opera house building. 

Lead City Administrator Robin Lucero said the event will kick off the city of Lead’s 150th anniversary celebration, and it is free to the public.

title: Second Black Hills  mining proposal selected for federal  fast-track permitting
byline: By Makenzie Huber Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: Geologists examine an exploration trench in the Bear Lodge rare earth elements deposit, within the Wyoming portion of the Black Hills. Photo courtesy of Wyoming State Geological Survey
_full_text:
Second Black Hills  mining proposal selected for federal  fast-track permitting
Geologists examine an exploration trench in the Bear Lodge rare earth elements deposit, within the Wyoming portion of the Black Hills. Photo courtesy of Wyoming State Geological Survey 

UPTON, Wyo. — Federal officials have selected a second Black Hills mining proposal for an expedited permitting process. 

The Bear Lodge Rare Earth Mining Project is on the Wyoming side of the Black Hills, and it’s expected to produce rare earth elements critical to alloy and magnet production.

It will be included in FAST-41, a federal process meant to improve coordination among permitting agencies and hold them accountable to deadlines. The 25-person federal Permitting Council says it can shave 18 months off a project’s review time.

The project has been stalled for several years by permitting, market saturation from China and challenges raising capital, according to reporting from the Cowboy State Daily. China processes a majority of the world’s rare earth elements.

Wyoming Republican U.S. Sen. Cynthia Lummis said the proposed mine will encourage American mineral independence.

“The Bear Lodge Project in northeastern Wyoming is on track to become an important domestic source of critical elements like neodymium and praseodymium, which our defense systems and high-tech manufacturers depend on,” Lummis said in a news release.

Rare earth elements have multiple uses, ranging from batteries and lasers to radiation shielding for nuclear reactions. A specific element identified for the Bear Lodge project would be used to make magnets for electric vehicle motors, wind turbines and other applications. 

The first fast-tracked Black Hills project — the long-lingering Dewey-Burdock uranium mining proposal in southwestern South Dakota — remains in progress on multiple permitting items, according to the federal infrastructure permitting dashboard.

The Dewey-Burdock project is named for rural locations near Edgemont along the southwestern edge of the Black Hills. It has been in the works for nearly 2 decades. It requires numerous federal, state and local permits, and has been mired in administrative and court appeals for years. The Black Hills Clean Water Alliance, NDN Collective and Oglala Sioux Tribe are among the project’s opponents.

If the Dewey-Burdock permits are approved at the federal level, the state can begin its own permitting process. A spokeswoman with the company leading the project said she hopes it will begin development within the next couple of years.

The Bear Lodge Rare Earth project is proposed by Rare Element Resources, which would operate the mining and processing arms of the project. The company completed construction of a $60 million rare earth processing and demonstration plan in Upton, Wyo., late last year.

Rare Element Resources did not respond to requests for comment from South Dakota Searchlight.

The FAST-41 process was created by Title 41 of the “Fixing America’s Surface Transportation Act,” signed into law by President Barack Obama in 2015. It originally focused on transportation infrastructure. It was expanded to mining projects during the final days of Donald Trump’s first presidential administration in 2021.

Trump signed an executive order last year directing federal officials to accelerate domestic critical mineral production, including copper, zinc, titanium and uranium. His administration is increasingly using FAST-41 to carry out the order.

title: 40% of GF&P, DOT partnership  funds used in Northern Hills
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: PIERRE — A partnership between the South Dakota Game, Fish, and Parks, and the state Department of Transportation has resulted in more than $1 million in road maintenance and repairs in the Northern Hills in 2025.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

PIERRE — A partnership between the South Dakota Game, Fish, and Parks, and the state Department of Transportation has resulted in more than $1 million in road maintenance and repairs in the Northern Hills in 2025. 

Adam Kulesa, planning and development administrator told the GF&P Commission recently that the department’s 5-year agreement with the DOT provides $2.8 million annually to use for collector roads. Collector roads are roads that lead from a state or local government road into state parks. About 40% of the 2025 allocation was spent on roads in Custer State Park, Shep’s Canyon and Angostura Reservoir, the Pete Lien and Sons Shooting Complex, and Shadehill Reservoir. Some projects have included $200,000 for an asphalt lift on all 18 miles of the Wildlife Loop Road in Custer State Park, and $400,000 for access roads to the Pete Lien and Sons Shooting Complex. 

“That’s a significant expenses that we would have had to take out of license dollars if we didn’t have this program,” he said. 

June will mark the end of the current agreement between the GF&P and DOT, and Kulesa said the GF&P will present its plan for 2027 projects in late April or early May, for DOT approval. That list includes $156,000 in road maintenance , Custer State Park, Shep’s Canyon, and Spearfish Canyon. Additionally, it includes $700,000 in alternate projects, including replacing a bridge in Custer Stat Park and applying $200,000 for road maintenance at the Pete Lien and Sons Shooting Complex.

title: Custer State Park leads state  in parks program participation
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: PIERRE — Custer State Park led the state in 2025 for participants in parks programming, officials with the South Dakota Game, Fish, and Parks said recently.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

PIERRE — Custer State Park led the state in 2025 for participants in parks programming, officials with the South Dakota Game, Fish, and Parks said recently. 

GF&P volunteer coordinator Rachel Comes told the GF&P Commission that state parks had a total of 2,236 programs that drew 92,252 participants in 2025. Of those, 51% of participants were youth, and 49% were adults. 

According to the report, Custer State Park drew 25 percent of the total number of program participants, with 1,164 programs drawing 23,034 people. That compares to the other top 6 parks in the state, with 14,689 participants for 204 programs at Good Earth State Park, southeast of Sioux Falls; 12,965 participants in 93 programs at Fort Sisseton Historic State Park, in Lake City; 8,582 participants at 118 programs at Adams Homestead and Nature Area, at McCook Lake; 7,758 participants in 69 programs at Newton Hills State Park in Canton; 3,515 participants in 71 programs at Pelican Lake Recreation Area in Watertown; and 3,158 participants in 75 programs at Lewis and Clark Recreation Area in Yankton. 

The totals, Comes said, don’t include the Custer State Park Buffalo Roundup, which she said is the most popular event, with more than 23,000 attendees. 

During her presentation, Comes also highlighted the success of 3 different programs the GF&P facilitates to boost attendance and participation in state parks. The Park Rx program works with the state Department of Health, to issue park passes to qualifying physicians, who can write a prescription and distribute the passes to patients who may benefit from being outdoors. 

“The programs goal is to improve physical and mental health by encouraging increased outdoor activitiers in state parks by going through health care providers,” Comes said. “It aims to normalize prescribing nature to treat and prevent chronic disease, allowing patients to get a free or discounted park pass.” 

Comes said more than 500 healthcare providers participated in the Park Rx prescription program in 2025, making it a record year for participation. More than 471 park pass prescriptions were redeemed, with 391 of those being annual passes sold at a discounted price, and 80 free daily passes. Overall, she said 1,581 people enjoyed the parks with the prescription program. That compares to 1,205 people in 2024, and 958 people in 2023. 

Go Fourth is another partnership program the GF&P has with the Department of Health since 2015. It aligns with a statewide initiative to promote health through physical activity, Comes said.  

“The program aims to introduce students to South Dakota’s incredible outdoor spaces while encouraging health, active lifestyles, and a lifelong love of nature,” Comes said. 

Through the Go Fourth program, all fourth graders in South Dakota receive a form each spring that offers them the opportunity to obtain a one-day park pass, a half price annual pass, or a 3-year subscription to Conservation Digest. The GF&P also offers the students a one-hour equipment rental, which can include paddle boats, cross country skis, kayaks, and more. 

In 2025, Comes said there were 572 Go Fourth passes redeemed. Of those, she said 516 were discounted annual passes, 56 daily passes were redeemed, and 52 subscriptions to Conservation Digest were given out. Participants also redeemed 20 free equipment passes. Overall, the program attracted 2,453 people to the state parks. 

South Dakota residents who want to check out the state parks system, but who are unable to purchase a pass, can check out a park pass from participating libraries. The Check out SD State Parks program, which GF&P offers in partnership with the S.D. Department of Education, offers 3-day park passes at participating public libraries, which is valid for one passenger vehicle. In 2025, Comes said 86 public and academic libraries participated in the program, offering more than 156 passes that were checked out more than 2,216 times. 

New programming for South Dakota state parks this year includes a nationwide initiative known as First Day Hikes, which provides park staff and volunteer-led naturalist hikes. The hikes were held in 14 parks, including the Mickelson Trail and Custer State Park, and attracted more than 430 participants. 

“These provided a successful, meaningful stsart to the new year, celebrating outdoor recreation, local history and connections across the state,” she said. 

This year’s state parks theme, Adventure 250: Make Your History in South Dakota Parks, ties in with the nation’s 250th anniversary. Comes said the theme encourages visitors to explore multiple parks through fun and accessible adventures. 

“As participants complete adventures, they’re building their own park history while earning points and, of course, prizes,” she said, adding that more information about the state parks theme activities will be given during the GF&P Commission’s April meeting, scheduled for April 9-10.

title: Opponents sue over Forest Service’s approval of drilling near Lakota sacred site in Black Hills
byline: By Joshua Haiar Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: Pe’ Sla, or Reynolds Prairie, is a high-elevation Black Hills meadow of sacred importance to Lakota people. Photo by Seth Tupper, South Dakota Searchlight
_full_text:
Oppenents sue over Forest Service's approval.jpg
Pe’ Sla, or Reynolds Prairie, is a high-elevation Black Hills meadow of sacred importance to Lakota people. Photo by Seth Tupper, South Dakota Searchlight 

 Pe-Sla-graphite-drilling-map-e1775244780220.jpeg
A map included in court documents shows the high mountain meadow known as Pe’ Sla in South Dakota’s Black Hills, and the locations of proposed exploratory graphite drilling. Courtesy illustration 

RAPID CITY — Opponents filed a lawsuit this week challenging the U.S. Forest Service’s approval of a graphite drilling project near Pe’ Sla, a site in the Black Hills of western South Dakota that holds cultural and spiritual significance for Native Americans.  

The plaintiffs are the Indigenous rights group known as NDN Collective and the Black Hills Clean Water Alliance, both based in Rapid City, and Earthworks in Washington, D.C. 

The lawsuit challenges the Forest Service’s decision allowing Rapid City-based Pete Lien & Sons to move forward with exploratory drilling for graphite. Graphite is used in electric vehicle batteries, lubricants, pencils and other products.

Pe’ Sla, also known as Reynolds Prairie, is a sacred site used for prayer, ceremony and cultural activities. The area is a high-elevation meadow surrounded by forested mountains in the central Black Hills. Pe’ Sla is one of the sites in the Black Hills that corresponds with celestial features in traditional Lakota spirituality.

The lawsuit says the Forest Service improperly used a process known as a “categorical exclusion” to bypass environmental and cultural reviews. 

“We will not stand for the desecration of our sacred sites, land, and water by extractive mining companies like Pete Lien,” said Wizipan Garriott, president of NDN Collective, in a press release. “The laws of the United States are often rigged against us, but we are confident this categorical exclusion was unlawful.”

The lawsuit alleges a categorical exclusion was improper because the project includes drilling, road work and other activity near Pe’ Sla that goes beyond what a categorical exclusion allows. The plaintiffs also argue that Pe’ Sla’s religious and cultural importance should have triggered a fuller review rather than the abbreviated process.

Neither Pete Lien & Sons nor the Forest Service immediately responded to requests for comment.

A representative of Pete Lien & Sons previously told Searchlight the company was reviewing the plan’s potential impact on sites of cultural and historical significance in the proposed project area, and questions and concerns should be directed to the Forest Service.
