title: American Legion Post 164 celebrates 107th birthday
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: SPEARFISH — The American Legion Post 164 celebrated its 107th birthday and annual awards dinner Saturday, honoring individuals and organizations whose service, leadership, and compassion reflect the Legion’s core values.
_full_text:
SPEARFISH — The American Legion Post 164 celebrated its 107th birthday and annual awards dinner Saturday, honoring individuals and organizations whose service, leadership, and compassion reflect the Legion’s core values.

The evening highlighted excellence in education, community service, and veteran advocacy, while also recognizing outstanding members across the Legion Family. Several honorees will advance to Department (state) level competition.

Educator of the Year: Anton Peterson

Anton Peterson was recognized as Educator of the Year at both the Post and District levels.

A veteran of more than 20 years in the United States Air Force, Peterson now serves as a seventh-grade math teacher at Spearfish Middle School. He inspires students through hands-on learning, high expectations, and genuine care.

Beyond the classroom, he mentors students through Student Council, archery, and community-based projects, helping shape confident, engaged citizens. He will now advance to state-level competition.

Good Samaritan of the Year: Auxiliary Unit 164

The Spearfish American Legion Auxiliary Unit 164 was honored as Good Samaritan of the Year at both the Post and District levels.

The unit provides year-round service to veterans, families, and the community through outreach programs, youth support, and partnerships with local organizations.

In the past year, Unit 164 assisted 1,620 veterans, supported 2,048 children, contributed 1,651 volunteer hours, and donated more than $13,000 to community causes. They will now advance to the state level.

Legionnaire of the Year: John Herweh

John Herweh was named Legionnaire of the Year at both the Post and District levels.

A dedicated Legionnaire, Herweh consistently steps forward to volunteer and bring new ideas to the Post. Despite the demands of full-time work and raising a family, he remains actively engaged in supporting Legion programs.

He led the post’s inagural 2.2 Mile Walk for Heroes – Be the One, coordinating fundraising efforts and organizing a successful event that brought together more than 70 participants.

“Be the One” is the American Legion’s national initiative focused on preventing veteran suicide, encouraging individuals to reach out and support veterans in need.

Herweh will now advance to state-level competition.

Legion Family Members of the Year

The evening also recognized outstanding individuals across the Legion Family for their dedication and service:

Hope Herweh, Auxiliary Unit 164 Junior Member of the Year, for her compassion, positive spirit, and commitment to helping others.

Geney Holley, Auxiliary Unit Member of the Year, for her leadership as Unit Chaplain and her meaningful contributions across numerous programs and events.

Doug Henwood, Sons of the American Legion Squadron 164 Member of the Year, for his continued leadership and dedication to the Legion’s mission.

Buck DeWeese, American Legion Riders-Black Hills Chapter Rider of the Year, for his unwavering support of veterans, including funeral escorts, outreach, and community service.

The annual awards dinner serves as a powerful reminder of the strength and character found within the Spearfish community. Through their dedication to service, these individuals and organizations continue to make a lasting impact on veterans, families, and future generations.

As several honorees advance to state-level competition, they carry with them the pride and support of the entire community.

title: Windstorm, fire recovery efforts continue in Custer State Park
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: CUSTER — Recovery efforts continue in Custer State Park, after a December windstorm and a major fire in March caused significant damage.
_full_text:
CUSTER — Recovery efforts continue in Custer State Park, after a December windstorm and a major fire in March caused significant damage.   

Matt Snyder, regional park supervisor for the South Dakota Game, Fish, and Parks, told members of the GF&P Commission recently that the park is still working with loggers to clear out debris from a major windstorm that hit the park, Dec. 18. The storm with 48 hours of wind gusts of 94 to 122 miles per hour, caused extensive damage throughout the park, and knocked all power out for about 6 days. The South Dakota Department of Transportation, Department of Corrections, and South Dakota Wildland Fire assisted with recovery efforts. Statewide, Snyder said park staff came from outside of Custer State Park, and from mid-January through February they averaged about 78 full time staff, who logged more than 10,000 hours cleaning up. 

Snyder said the staff hauled 1,400 tons of material to Sylvan and Center lakes, and to the State Game Lodge. The GF&P contracted with Baker Timber to grind the remains and to haul it away. 

Snyder said there are about 12 units in the park where timber crews could operate to clear brush and debris, but there are many areas that are inoperable because crews won’t be able to get equipment in for work. Of those 12 units, Snyder said the GF&P has sold 5 for timber sales — 4 near the Wildlife Loop Road and one behind the Coolidge store. So far, he said, crews have taken about 2,900 tons, with 139 loads of merchantable chips. Much of the timber is not useable though, Snyder said, and the park has had to bear the cost of that removal. 

On the southern side of Custer State Park, near Highway 87, Snyder said Morgan Logging has been clearing the area for firewood. To date, he said they have removed about 180 tons. 

Snyder said there are still about 7 logging units in the park that are not under contract, because loggers are hard to find. 

“A major change over the last 5 to 7 years with the closing of Neiman’s mill in Hill City, we’ve lost a lot of our loggers,” Snyder said. “We had loggers galore when we were in our pine beetle era. Now, it’s a lost business. They’re just not there anymore.” 

Part of the problem, he said, are that the logs from the wind storm are twisted and broken, making them unusable for planks. The only thing they can be used for, he said, is firewood or wood chips. 

“You have one contractor that does chipping in the Black Hills, and you got a few firewood vendors,” he said. “But there’s wood galore wherever you want to go.” 

To put it into perspective, Snyder said logging companies paid $1.50 to $2 per ton for salvageable logs after the Legion Lake fire in 2017. After the December windstorm, he said Baker is paying 25 cents a ton and Morgan is paying the GF&P 2 cents a ton for the logs. 

“It wasn’t about the money,” he said. “It was about trying to get this stuff off the landscape. So we’re going to continually work with them to see if they’ll continue to maybe look at taking some more areas off our hands and continually clean things out for us.” 

So far, Snyder said Custer State Park has spent about $186,000 in direct recovery efforts from that windstorm. Equipment and in-kind costs that were submitted to the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) are at nearly $700,000. Custer State Park Resort Company reported it had 82 facilities that sustained more than $510,000 in damage. 

Much of that damage has been repaired already, Snyder said. 

“We’re pretty much back in shape,” he said. “There are a couple of things we’re waiting on for a little better weather for shingling, but otherwise we’re going to be in good shape for the summer.” 

The Qury Fire, which started March 13, burned a total of 9,100 acres east of Custer, with about 1,200 acres burned in the park. No park structures were lost in the fire, and Snyder said geography and thinning activities helped the fire on Custer State Park lands stay close to the ground. At one point, he said the fire raged around Mount Coolidge, but because of the structure’s height and geography, winds blew the fire around it and over a 13-acre slope over U.S. Highway 87. 

“if the head of the fire would have come more south of Mount Coolidge, the winds would have drawn it over Highway 87 more and we would have seen more fire in the park,” he said. “We had done some thinning in years past, so that helped it stay on the ground. There are some places on forest service land and private areas, where the fire burned very intense.” 

Overall, Snyder reported there were about 319 personnel working the Qury Fire, with crews from the U.S. Forest Service, GF&P personnel, and volunteer fire departments from across the state and the nation. 

Despite the fire, Snyder said Custer State Park trails are open, and resort facilities are set to open May 1. 

“We are preparing just like a normal season,” he said. “I’d be lying if I didn’t say the staff are tired. But the staff in the park area are rebuilding. We’ve had to do stuff like this before. It’s just a matter of tying your shoe a little tighter, and out you go. Everybody just pitched in and did what they needed to do.”

title: Meade County passes burn ban changes
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: STURGIS — Major changes to the Meade County burn ban ordinance mean the chairman of the county commission can declare an emergency ban, and fires are automatically prohibited during the Sturgis Motorcycle Rally.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

 

STURGIS — Major changes to the Meade County burn ban ordinance mean the chairman of the county commission can declare an emergency ban, and fires are automatically prohibited during the Sturgis Motorcycle Rally. 

On Tuesday, the Meade County Commission gave its first nod to the new ordinance, which states that no person shall set an open fire when the Grassland Fire Danger Index is at the high, very high, or extreme category. Changes to the ordinance give the chairman of the Meade County Commission the power to declare an emergency burn ban on open fires, that will remain effective until the next regularly scheduled county commission meeting. The ordinance requires the commission board chairman to notify the sheriff and Meade County dispatch about the emergency ban. 

The change comes after high winds and temperatures have raised concerns about severe fire danger in Meade County. This year there have been multiple small fires, and at least one large grass fire that burned 60 acres near Bear Butte State Park on Gravel Road. 

In previous years, the Meade County Commission has issued a resolution, banning open fires during the Sturgis Motorcycle Rally. However, with the new ordinance, the ban is set in place to automatically ban all fires in the county for 14 days before, and 7 days after the Rally.

title: Lawrence Co. Board of Equalization  delivers annual report, shows  housing market may be leveling out
byline: By Sara Morgan Black Hills Pioneer
description: The Lawrence County Board of Equalization delivered an annual report Tuesday, showing that the county continues to increase in overall growth and assessed value. The report hints that the discrepancy between assessed value and market value could be closing. Pioneer photo by Sara Morgan
_full_text:
BoardEqualization.jpg
The Lawrence County Board of Equalization delivered an annual report Tuesday, showing that the county continues to increase in overall growth and assessed value. The report hints that the discrepancy between assessed value and market value could be closing. Pioneer photo by Sara Morgan 

DEADWOOD — The housing market could be leveling out after the upheaval lasting from 2021 to 2025 according to the Lawrence County Board of Equalization’s annual review, hinting that the gap between assessed value and market values of property could be closing.

According to the report, the preliminary sales ratio, determining the difference between assessed value and taxable value, continues to climb from 86.2% in 2025 to 86.96% this year. Brett Runge, director of equalization, said that the county has not received a factor from the state determining what value a property will be taxed at, but her office’s preliminary guess stands at 0.977. If the factor granted by the state is accurate to Runge’s office’s guess, she said a property with an assessed value of $100,000 would be taxed at a value of $97,700.

Runge said that the gap is closing, in part, because sales prices and market values are beginning to stabilize. 

“Last year, we were at 86.2% and it took a larger increase in assessment values to get to that percentage,” she said. “Our overall county value increased by 9.9%. This year, our overall county value increased only 5% to get a preliminary sales ration of 86.96%.”

Runge reported 625 “good sales” on all properties excluding agriculture last year, up from 573 in last year’s report. 

Runge said that a good sale, “requires that buyer and seller are informed and knowledgeable about the property, the property is offered on the open market for a reasonable amount of time, a willing buyer and a willing seller, and not sold under duress or with creative financing terms.”

“The state wants us and all counties to be at 100% of market value.  We perform a sales analysis of all the good sales in the county,” said Runge.  “We research the sales to make sure it is a good sale, see if we have missed anything about the property (if they finished a basement or remodeled the house) and call buyers or sellers to get more information.  We look at the sales to see if we need to raise or lower an entire area/neighborhood, so it is important that the sales are good sales, and the property information is correct.  Also, the State audits our sales and then calculates a factor from our median sales ratio (how close we are to market value).  The assessed value is multiplied by the state factor to come up with the taxable value. That state factor adjusts our full and true assessed values down or up to 85% of market value.  All counties in the state have an initial sales ratio calculated from their sales and the state gives them a factor to adjust their taxable values to 85% of market.  The taxable value is the amount that the mill levy is multiplied against to come up with the tax amount.”

Runge said that new legislation in 2025 changed the way her office was able to increase overall owner-occupied value last year and add value from building permit growth. Overall owner-occupied value in the county can only increase by 3% due to reappraisal. Growth is not included in that percentage. Runge reported that last year, owner occupied properties across the county increased by approximately 2.2% after reappraisal.

“When we go out to look at building permits, if an owner-occupied property, for example, has a deck put on, unless the value of that deck is 40% of the total value of the property, we can’t count it as growth. Which, before, we could have,” said Runge. “So, we’re going to have a lot less growth because of that. Decks, driveways, even maybe small additions or porches that won’t be 40% of the whole value so we won’t be able to count it.”

The overall assessment in the county increased by 5%, Runge said, increasing by $297,095,270 from 2024. Runge said that value has been larger in previous years, and attributes the lower number to the change in legislation.

After 4 tax increment financing (TIF) districts were dissolved between Spearfish and Deadwood in 2025, preliminary growth in the county jumped from $129,995,866 to $238,528,616. Runge said $130,089,798 of last year’s growth can be contributed to the dissolution of the TIF districts. 

“When they build houses or commercial buildings in these TIF districts, it doesn’t get added (to the preliminary growth) until that TIF district is dissolved,” said Runge. “So, the cities and the county doesn’t get any growth from that for years and then all of a sudden, boom, they dissolve, and they get it.”

Runge reported that the total number of parcels increased from 21,188 to 21,422 across the county and building permits continue to rise. She reported that between Aug. 1, 2024, to July 31, 2025, the county issued 919 building permits. That number is up 14 from the same period last year. 

“I was actually surprised there were that many compared to the year before,” said Runge. “I thought it might have slowed down a little bit, but we have so many houses being built out at Powder House and some of the new developments out by Whitewood and Spearfish. So, we’re still pretty strong as far as building permits.”

Runge said her office reappraised 1,118 parcels last year, most of which were located in the city of Lead and hadn’t been reappraised since approximately 2005. Last year, Runge and her team created a new 10-year cycle, planning to decrease the amount of time between reappraisals with new technology and more concentrated efforts.

title: A lifetime of writing and reflection comes together in LaVerne LaVake’s ‘Through the Wilderness’
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: “Through the Wilderness: Prose, Poetry, and Sourdough” published in November 2025. It’s the subject of author talks in regional libraries throughout the northern Hills. Courtesy image
_full_text:
A lifetime of writing and reflection comes together in LaVerne LaVake’s ‘Through the Wilderness’
“Through the Wilderness: Prose, Poetry, and Sourdough” published in November 2025. It’s the subject of author talks in regional libraries throughout the northern Hills. Courtesy image 

SPEARFISH — Decades before author LaVerne LaVake published “Through the Wilderness: Prose, Poetry, and Sourdough,” his children grew up with scraps of his writing scattered all around the house — there were song lyrics scribbled on the back of candy bar wrappers, and notebooks storing ideas and chord progressions that might eventually coalesce into a song. Some of those scraps have been stitched together into his new book.  

LaVake, a South Dakotan, used his singing voice for years to express himself. He started singing in his school’s choir when he was 13. By the time he was in his teens, he was part of a garage band, performing covers of Grand Funk Railroad and Creedence Clearwater Revival. He competed in all-state music competitions.

He continued to write songs and stories while serving in the Navy for four years. Later, he came to maintain a file on his computer titled “possibilities” where he stored anecdotes, stories, and phrases that were sticky enough to potentially turn into a memorable poem, melody or prose.

Publishing his work — short stories and lyrics turned poems — didn’t occur to LaVake until recently, after the author survived a life altering experience.

“In (19)97, I think it was, I had a construction accident. Basically, all of the writings stopped. It caused a bleed, a stroke, and everything was shut off,” LaVake said. “I started putting what I had together because, after something like that, you’re kind of worried that your life is over. I had another stroke on April 15 last year, and suddenly I could write again. I’ve been writing ever since.”

LaVake suffered a third stroke that robbed him of his singing voice, elevating writing as a form of expression to even greater importance in his life.

So his book is about music. It is about growing up in South Dakota. It is about sourdough bread.

The stories that inspired and were added to “Through the Wilderness” reflect the building blocks of LaVake’s life.

“I grew up in a bakery,” LaVake said. “My dad was a baker, and I have memories of going in with him on a Friday night in the bakery. I thought I was helping, but mostly I was just sleeping on the flour sacks as a grade school boy.”

LaVake remembers running scales at his father’s Bowman bakery, and drew inspiration from his father’s love of time spent in the kitchen.

“I still do a lot of cooking and make a lot of sourdough recipes. That, I thought that was kind of part of my own history. I thought I would put it in the book,” LaVake said.

LaVake said he’s written about everything from a fishing trip with his dad to a canoe trip with his son.

“It would be like reminiscing on something. I would just write, then as time went by, sometimes you realize, ‘Well, that’s just junk,’ and you throw it away,” LaVake said. “When I was using notebooks instead of the computer, some of that stuff I just thought, ‘That isn’t really worth keeping.’ Some people might think what is in the book is not worth keeping, but that’s OK, it’s all that I consider part of me.”

LaVake maintains an active blog and hopes that all of his work inspires readers.

“I usually end up with every story having a look at the cross as it were, or a look at yourself that you don’t measure up, but there is a way you can. We aren’t stuck here alone. There is a God out there,” LaVake said. “We can find hope when it seems hopeless. I hope that my stories end up with that kind of feel. There is a light at the end of the tunnel that hopefully is not a train.”

LaVake is scheduled for a free author talk at the Whitewood Public Library on Tuesday at 10 a.m.

title: National Library Week  is April 19-25: Here’s how area libraries are  highlighting their hard work
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: Next week is National Library Week, first celebrated in 1958 by the American Library Association. Pioneer photo by Sierra Ferguson
_full_text:
National Library Week  is April 19-25 Here’s how area libraries are  highlighting their hard work
Next week is National Library Week, first celebrated in 1958 by the American Library Association. Pioneer photo by Sierra Ferguson 

SPEARFISH — Peppered across the region are a series of unique, dedicated local libraries. Each one has its own character and its own carefully planned programming. Next week is National Library Week, and each regional library is gearing up in its own way — aiming to draw community members in and celebrate their achievements throughout the year.

In Whitewood, Library Director Deb Terhune is working on building up her library’s social media presence — shouting out the 8 volunteers who keep the small, but mighty library running. 

“For example, when we have story time, we’ll have a class from the elementary school ask them to join us, and we could have 40-plus kids at a specific story time, plus adults. It takes many hands and many volunteers to make things go smoothly with that many kids,” Terhune said. “We always do a craft, we do a snack, if it’s nice, we’ll go outside and chase bubbles. It takes quite a few of us to have things operate smoothly.”

The Whitewood Public Library plays an important role in its town.

“There are really no organized activities for kids other than what the school might have, or every Wednesday there’s an after-school program at one of the churches,” Terhune said. “We have multitudes of programs for kids and adults.”

Whitewood library cards are available free to all Lawrence County residents.

“In addition to checking out books, DVDs, and audiobooks, you can check out our 3-day state park pass, so that if you want to go check out Custer and you have a library card, you can come in and you actually check out the park pass. It’s a hang tag for your mirror, for 3 days,” Terhune said. “Even without a library card, our faxing, printing, and scanning services are available. We have 4 computers for the public to use as long as they are over 18. We have Zoom cameras set up so that we can help people with Zoom meetings.”

In Deadwood, Library Director Kennedy Penk said one of their most underutilized resources is the three day state park pass.

Her library in a historic landmark, marking 150 of its home community.

“Deadwood is celebrating 150 years, so the library is doing a 150th reading challenge. We are challenging all of our patrons to read 150,000 minutes,” Penk said.

Reading logs are available to pick up at the Deadwood library.

Deadwood’s library also hosts after-school programs for kids in kindergarten through 5th grade.

“We get a group of kids that come over after school, and we do activities, snacks, and free time. We learn about the library. We explore the library. We just embrace creativity, and the kids are absolutely fantastic. That is probably one of my favorite programs we have,” Penk said. 

Penk said the building itself is special in Deadwood, but the patrons are what contribute to the character of the library — from the books her team selects to the programs they curate.

“The community is what makes the library,” Penk said.

Belle Fourche is marking National Library Week with giveaways and drawings for prizes. To be entered to win prizes, patrons can check out materials from the library. At checkout, they’ll be entered in a drawing.

Librarian Wanda Nelson said one of the things that makes the Belle Fourche library special is its South Dakota Room, which celebrates books about the state or books written by South Dakota authors.

In Sturgis, the week is packed with events: Monday is “Right to Read Day,” where patrons are encouraged to check out banned books. Tuesday is “National Library Workers Day” marked by special treats after toddler story time, Wednesday there will be preschool storytime, followed by coding club, then Thursday is “World Book Day” which will be marked by a special “by the book” session of chess club and a 6 p.m. “Books on Tap” book club meeting at the Knuckle Saloon. 

“National Library Week … is a time to celebrate the many ways libraries bring people together, spark imagination, and support lifelong learning. From books and digital resources to job assistance and creative programming, libraries are essential to thriving communities,” Assistant Library Director Sierra Frazier-Riggs said. “At Sturgis Public Library, we invite everyone to join the celebration! Whether you’re a longtime patron, a new neighbor, or haven’t visited in a while, now is the perfect time to explore all the library has to offer. Our library provides free Wi-Fi, public access computers, digital collections, genealogy workshops, storytime, STEAM programs, and much more, ensuring that everyone has access to resources that educate, inspire, and connect.”

Grace Balloch Memorial Library in Spearfish also wants to highlight its programming this National Library Week.

“We really try to be a library for the whole community for all ages, all walks of life, bringing everybody together and making a community space where everyone feels comfortable,” said Library Director Amber Wilde.

The library runs a game club for teens, regular youth storytimes, and twice monthly Lego club and fiber arts clubs. In the month of May, the library will hold early voting as well, Wilde said.

“I would just encourage everybody to stop into their local library. We’d love for it to be in Whitewood, but stop in at any library, especially if you haven’t been in a while. You may not realize all of the wonderful things that they have, the changes that they’ve made. We get a lot of people that stop in and say, ‘It’s been 10 years, and things have totally changed,” Terhune said.

title: Ballots not ready for April 17 absentee voting
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: STURGIS — Ballots will not be available in time for absentee voting to start on schedule.
_full_text:
STURGIS — Ballots will not be available in time for absentee voting to start on schedule. 

Meade County Auditor Dawn Christenson told county commissioners Monday that the state did not complete its ballot drawing order for candidates until late afternoon on April 7, and her staff stayed in the office until late into the evening to finish creating ballots. In Meade County, she said there are more than 50 different kinds of ballots. There are 41 precincts, and some of those precincts are split, since some has school district or other types of elections, and others do not. Because of the primary election, Christenson said the county will also have a Republican primary ballot and a non-political ballot, as well as a federal ballot. Additionally, she said this year the county is combining its election with Piedmont, which will also have a couple of different ballots. 

All of those ballots were submitted to the printer April 7, and the county is now awaiting proofs. Once the proofs arrive, she said, the state needs to approve the ballots before they will be ready for voters. 

With absentee voting set to start on Friday, Christenson said it’s not likely the ballots will be completed in time.

Christenson said voters who come to cast their votes before the county receives ballots will have to put their name and phone number on a list, and the auditor will call them when ballots are available. Voters can also fill out an application to have ballots mailed to them when they are available. 

Lawrence Count Auditor Brenda McGruder said she is also confident her ballots will not be ready by Friday, but she hopes to have them available by early next week. Voters will have to wait until ballots arrive before casting their votes, she said. Once ballots arrive, McGruder said she plans to let voters know through the Black Hills Pioneer newspaper, social media, and by posting on the county website. 

Meade and Lawrence counties are not alone in the ballot dilemma. Across the state, auditors have reported scrambling to get ballots finished for early voting. According to state law, the deadline to file petitions to be included on the June 2 ballot was the last Tuesday in March, but with 5 weeks in March this year that deadline landed on March 31. That shortened the time between the filing of those petitions and the printing of ballots for early and absentee voting. 

The Legislature addressed the problem with House Bill 1095, which changes the deadline for filing to the third Tuesday in March, but that bill will not go into effect until July 1. 

Early and absentee voting is required by state statute to begin no later than 46 days before the election, and ballots are required to be printed 48 days before.

title: $4 million for gunsmithing program coming to Rapid City
byline: By Seth Tupper Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: RAPID CITY — South Dakota’s governor, who’s in the midst of an election campaign, awarded another grant Tuesday from an economic development fund he controls.
_full_text:
RAPID CITY — South Dakota’s governor, who’s in the midst of an election campaign, awarded another grant Tuesday from an economic development fund he controls.

Republican Gov. Larry Rhoden announced a $4 million Future Fund award for Western Dakota Technical College in Rapid City. The college will use the money to move a gunsmithing school from Colorado and incorporate it into Western Dakota’s offerings for students.

Rhoden, who attended Western Dakota decades ago but didn’t graduate, made the announcement at the college. 

“It’s fitting to me that South Dakota would be home for a school like this,” Rhoden said. “South Dakota is the most Second Amendment-friendly state in the nation.”

Last week, Rhoden granted $6 million from the Future Fund to establish a South Dakota Defense Institute in Rapid City that will help companies in the state earn federal military contracts. That grant brought the unobligated balance of the Future Fund down to $13 million, a spokesperson for the Governor’s Office of Economic Development said at the time.

A similar spurt of Future Fund grants from Rhoden last year sparked criticism from the three Republicans who are running for his job. Aberdeen businessman Toby Doeden called the grants an “attempt to buy votes,” state House Speaker Jon Hansen said the Future Fund was “funding the governor’s political future,” and U.S. Rep. Dusty Johnson said although Future Fund grants are an important economic development tool, “they aren’t meant to help someone’s campaign.” Meanwhile, Johnson has pledged that if he’s elected, he’ll use $2 million from the Future Fund to create a new local business startup initiative.

Upon being reminded of that criticism Tuesday by South Dakota Searchlight, Rhoden called it “ridiculous” and said he’s awarding Future Fund grants to projects and ideas that will improve the state’s economy and workforce.

“I am doing my job,” Rhoden said. “If you look at the opportunities that I have as a governor, as far as an appropriate use for Future Funds, this is picture perfect.”

The state gets money for the Future Fund by charging a fee to employers. They pay the fee when they submit payroll taxes that support unemployment benefits.

The late Republican Gov. George Mickelson convinced lawmakers to create the fund in 1987. They placed it under the governor’s exclusive control to enable quick responses to economic opportunities.

State law says only that the fund “must be used for purposes related to research and economic development for the state,” but that’s about to change. Lawmakers approved new restrictions earlier this year, in response to past uses of the fund by Rhoden’s predecessor, former Gov. Kristi Noem. Rhoden was elevated from lieutenant governor after Noem resigned in January 2025 to become secretary of the federal Department of Homeland Security.

Noem’s controversial uses of the Future Fund included a fireworks show at Mount Rushmore, the construction of a state-owned shooting range near Rapid City that legislators refused to fund, a rodeo in Sioux Falls where Noem carried the American flag into the arena on horseback, and a workforce recruitment advertising campaign that featured Noem as the star.

Rhoden signed the legislative Future Fund reforms into law last month, but they won’t take effect until July 1 — after the June 2 primary election pitting Rhoden against three opponents for the Republican nomination. 

The reforms add legal definitions for acceptable uses of the fund, mandate more reporting to legislators about awards, specify the information required of applicants, direct the Governor’s Office of Economic Development to formulate rules for the fund’s use, and require the office to make recommendations to the governor about potential awards.

The latest grant from Rhoden will help move the equipment, faculty and curriculum of the Colorado School of Trades, which operates solely as a gunsmithing school, to Western Dakota Tech. 

The president of the Colorado school, Ryan Lishner, said Tuesday that gun policies in Colorado “are starting to impede what firearms dealers and firearms manufacturers can do, and that’s getting to where it’s impacting our educational process.” He views South Dakota as willing to support the gunsmithing program “at a much higher level than Colorado was going to do.”

The Colorado school can accommodate up to 20 faculty members and 140 students. After completing the 14-month program, students go on to professions ranging from self-employed gunsmiths to employees of major firearms manufacturers. 

“Western Dakota Tech is going to have the ability to grow that program to a much higher level,” Lishner said.

title: Rapid City lab is on closure list
byline: South Dakota Searchlight
description: RAPID CITY — A Forest and Grassland Research Laboratory in South Dakota’s Black Hills will close and potentially relocate to another state as part of a national reorganization, according to the U.S. Forest Service.
_full_text:
RAPID CITY — A Forest and Grassland Research Laboratory in South Dakota’s Black Hills will close and potentially relocate to another state as part of a national reorganization, according to the U.S. Forest Service.

The lab, housed in the Black Hills National Forest’s Mystic Ranger District office at Rapid City, does research for forest and grassland managers dealing with a changing climate, invasive species, fire, habitat fragmentation and threatened species. The lab houses scientists associated with the Maintaining Resilient Dryland Ecosystems Program, which investigates the biology, use, management and restoration of grasses and shrublands. 

No information was immediately available about the number of employees affected by the closure of the Rapid City lab. South Dakota Searchlight contacted the Black Hills National Forest and the Rocky Mountain Research Station headquarters. Both told Searchlight to direct questions to the national press office via email, but the office has not responded.  

The Forest Service’s reorganization website is unclear about the ultimate fate of research labs on the closure list. “In many locations, ‘closure’ refers only to individual buildings currently housing small teams,” the website says. “Staff and programs will continue their work, relocated into fewer facilities while maintaining research presence across the country.” But the website does not specifically say if the Rapid City lab will move after its closure, or where it will go.

Dave Mertz, who retired from the Black Hills National Forest in 2017 as a natural resource officer, said he worries the change could negatively affect the Forest Service. 

“What will happen is these people will get reassigned, but do not want to move. They will be gone,” he said. “They haven’t really explained why they’re doing this.”

title: Forest Service shake-up that includes SD lab will boost states’ role, but even supporters have concerns
byline: By Alex Brown Stateline
description: WASHINGTON, D.C. — A sweeping reorganization of the U.S. Forest Service signals that the agency is planning to lean heavily on states to help manage millions of acres of federal land, foresters across the West say.
_full_text:
WASHINGTON, D.C. — A sweeping reorganization of the U.S. Forest Service signals that the agency is planning to lean heavily on states to help manage millions of acres of federal land, foresters across the West say.

State officials and timber industry leaders say they’ve been given scant details about the plan, which will move the agency’s headquarters from Washington, D.C., to Salt Lake City, restructure its regional management, and close scores of research stations in dozens of states.

While they wait for the dust to settle, they’re preparing for the Forest Service — with its workforce slashed by the Trump administration — to ask more of its partners under the new model.

“The Forest Service itself is unable to uphold its mission and cannot alone manage the many challenges on these landscapes,” said Nick Smith, public affairs director with the American Forest Resource Council, a timber industry group. “The transition from regional offices to more state-level offices is a recognition that partnerships are the future for the Forest Service.”

But many forestry veterans fear the shake-up will cause more attrition in an agency that’s already shrunk because of Trump’s cuts to the federal workforce. Some see a clear sign that moving the headquarters to Utah — a state whose leaders are often hostile to federal land ownership — is designed to undermine the Forest Service’s management of its lands.

The closure of 57 research stations, some agency partners fear, will threaten critical science that states and other forest managers rely on to learn about wildfire behavior, timber production and a host of other issues.

Some observers noted that the agency is required to seek congressional approval to relocate offices, which could trigger legal challenges to the plan if lawmakers do not weigh in.

Meanwhile, some foresters feel the uncertainty swirling over the agency will cause chaos as the West heads into a dangerous fire season amid record temperatures and drought.

The plan announced on March 31 will relocate Forest Service Chief Tom Schultz and his headquarters staff to Salt Lake City. The agency will close its nine regional offices, each of which oversee national forests across multiple states. Replacing those offices will be 15 state directors, mostly in Western states.

Many state leaders, from both conservative and liberal states, say they welcome the opportunity to deepen their partnerships with the Forest Service and play a greater role on federal lands. But they’re still anxious to see more details about the agency’s new structure and concerned that national forests remain deeply understaffed.

“There are definitely a lot of vacancies in key positions that need to be filled,” said Jon Songster, federal lands bureau chief with the Idaho Department of Lands. “I hope that a lot of that remaining expertise is not lost, but shifted to the forest level where it’s desperately needed. Hopefully with all these changes there will be opportunities to put more people in some of those key gaps.”

Scarce details

The Forest Service manages nearly 200 million acres of land, mostly in Western states. With a mandate to manage the land for multiple uses, the agency oversees timber harvests, livestock grazing, outdoor recreation and wildlife habitat.

Under President Donald Trump, the Forest Service has lost about 16% of its workforce — nearly 5,900 employees — through buyouts, layoffs and early retirements. Trump’s proposed budget for 2027 would cut billions of dollars from the agency’s funding.

Many observers view the reorganization plan as an effort to force out more longtime agency leaders. The moves are expected to affect about 5,000 employees across the various offices that are relocating.

“If this were a stand-alone proposal where the American public and the public agency employees had trust in the administration, a lot of it makes sense,” said Mike Dombeck, who served as chief of the Forest Service under President Bill Clinton and remains a vocal conservation advocate. “But the level of trust is at rock bottom.”

In its announcement, the agency said that the new state-based model will bring decision-making closer to the forest level and reduce bureaucracy. The Forest Service did not grant a Stateline interview request.

State foresters, who are responsible for managing the forests in their states, say they’ve been given few details other than the new office maps released by the agency. They don’t know when the transitions will happen, which officials will be staffing the new offices or what authority they will have.

“They’ve made the statement that they need to rely more on states,” said Washington State Forester George Geissler. “If you’re going to lean on us, it might help us to know what that means.”

States’ role

In recent years, the Forest Service has increasingly partnered with states, tribes, counties and nonprofits to carry out projects on federal lands. Foresters say agreements such as the Good Neighbor Authority have become a critical tool, allowing more work to happen in national forests even as the feds’ own capacity shrinks.

“We’ve seen some of that institutional knowledge (at the Forest Service) dwindle a little bit,” said Utah State Forester Jamie Barnes. “Building these partnerships, if you do see a decline on one side or the other, you can bridge that loss. We’re working together, making joint decisions so we can get timber off the landscape here in Utah.”

Some foresters said they welcome the chance to work more closely with the Forest Service, but they’re concerned that the agency has not recovered from Trump’s workforce cuts. Reassigning hundreds of employees to new locations could lead to more attrition.

In Wyoming, state officials are excited to have Forest Service leaders working in close proximity. But State Forester Kelly Norris acknowledged that the move could be “bumpy,” given the lack of details and ongoing workforce shortages in the agency.

“The logistics of this may be a lot harder implemented than said,” she said. “We see this as a positive for us, but I do think that this is going to be a real long transition.”

Idaho, Utah and Wyoming are among the Western states that share the Trump administration’s goal of increasing timber production on federal lands. Trump has moved to limit environmental reviews and protections for endangered species to speed up logging projects.

Some Forest Service veterans feel the move to increase states’ role will prove destructive in some parts of the West.

“We’re putting the governance of the forests more subject to states’ interests,” said Kevin Hood, executive director of Forest Service Employees for Environmental Ethics, a nonprofit that advocates for civil employees. “I would be concerned that the values that don’t have strong lobbying groups, such as watershed integrity, may be subjugated to extractive values like timber, mining and grazing.”

Several agency veterans stressed that the Forest Service’s state directors should be career professionals, not political appointees.

HQ move

By relocating its headquarters to Salt Lake City, the Forest Service said in its announcement, the agency is moving leaders closer to the forests they manage.

But some are skeptical the move will bring stronger management to the West. During Trump’s first term, he moved the Bureau of Land Management headquarters to Grand Junction, Colorado. Only 41 of the 328 employees subject to the transition actually relocated.

“Shaking things up is going to get people to abandon their positions, and that’s the intent,” said Chandra Rosenthal, Western lands and Rocky Mountain advocate with Public Employees for Environmental Responsibility, a group that defends whistleblowers in the federal service. “It’s a long-term dismantling of the scientific backbone and staff. The theory is that the federal government will abandon a lot of the public lands and then states will be forced to fill in those gaps.”

Rosenthal and others noted that Utah’s political leaders are hostile to federal land ownership. U.S. Sen. Mike Lee, a Republican, led an effort last year to sell off millions of acres of federal land, which drew widespread backlash before it was withdrawn. Utah’s state government has also sued the federal government, seeking to claim control of 18.5 million acres of federal land managed by the Bureau of Land Management.

“Why would you move the headquarters of a public lands management agency to the state that is the most anti-public lands in the country?” said Dombeck, the former Forest Service chief.

Dombeck also noted that the Forest Service chief frequently reports to the White House, testifies in congressional hearings and coordinates national policy with other agency leaders. Moving the position out of D.C., he said, makes little sense.

In a webpage set up to respond to news coverage of the move, the Forest Service said it is a “myth” that the transition is designed to reduce its workforce or transfer federal lands to the states.

But some agency veterans are skeptical.

“It’s hard not to reach the conclusion that this is an effort to weaken federal agencies and federal management of these lands,” said Robert Bonnie, who served as undersecretary of agriculture for natural resources and environment during the Obama administration. “You’re going to lose some good staff as part of the reorganization, as they move chairs across the deck of the Titanic.”

Meanwhile, some state leaders are concerned that the uncertainty caused by the reorganization and Trump’s staffing cuts could lead to chaos as wildfire season approaches. With record temperatures and drought drying out much of the West, foresters expect a challenging fire season this summer. The Forest Service remains the nation’s largest wildland firefighting agency, even as the Trump administration seeks to consolidate wildland fire operations into a separate service under the U.S. Department of the Interior.

“I’ve got federal firefighters, fire managers, and all they’re talking about is what’s happening at (the Forest Service),” said Geissler, the Washington state forester. “I don’t feel like having a bunch of distracted firefighters on my hands going into a summer fire season.”

title: Hey Kids! Learn how NASA already has  next Artemis flight in its sights
description: HOUSTON (AP) — Never-before-glimpsed views of the moon’s far side. Check. Total solar eclipse gracing the lunar scene. Check. New distance record for humanity. Check.
_full_text:
HOUSTON (AP) — Never-before-glimpsed views of the moon’s far side. Check. Total solar eclipse gracing the lunar scene. Check. New distance record for humanity. Check.

With NASA’s lunar comeback a galactic-sized smash thanks to Artemis II, the world is wondering: What’s next? And how do you top that?

“To people all around the world who look up and dream about what is possible, the long wait is over,” NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman said as he introduced Artemis II commander Reid Wiseman, pilot Victor Glover, Christina Koch and Canada’s Jeremy Hansen at Saturday’s jubilant homecoming celebration.

Now that the first lunar travelers in more than a half-century are safely back in Houston with their families, NASA has Artemis III in its sights.

“The next mission’s right around the corner,” entry flight director Rick Henfling observed following the crew’s Pacific splashdown on Friday.

In a mission recently added to the docket for next year, Artemis III’s yet-to-be -named astronauts will practice docking their Orion capsule with a lunar lander or two in orbit around Earth. Elon Musk’s SpaceX and Jeff Bezos’ Blue Origin are racing to have their company’s lander ready first.

Musk’s Starship and Bezos’ Blue Moon are vying for the all-important Artemis IV moon landing in 2028. Two astronauts will aim for the south polar region, the preferred location for Isaacman’s envisioned $20 billion to $30 billion moon base. Vast amounts of ice are almost certainly hidden in permanently shadowed craters there — ice that could provide water and rocket fuel.

The docking mechanism for Artemis III’s close-to-home trial run is already at Florida’s Kennedy Space Center. The latest model Starship is close to launching on a test flight from South Texas, and a scaled-down version of Blue Moon will attempt a lunar landing later this year.

NASA promises to announce the Artemis III crew “soon.” Like 1969’s Apollo 9, Artemis III aims to reduce risk for the moon landings that follow.

Apollo 9 astronaut Rusty Schweickart loved flying the lunar module in low-Earth orbit — “a test pilot’s dream.” But there’s no question, he noted, that “the real astronauts” at least in the public’s mind were the ones who walked on the moon.

Wiseman and his crew put their passion and feelings on full display as they flew around the moon and back, choking up over lost loved ones as well as those left behind on Earth.

During the their nearly 10-day journey, they tearfully requested that a fresh, bright lunar crater be named after Wiseman’s late wife, Carroll, who died of cancer in 2020. They also openly shared their love for one another and Planet Earth, an exquisite yet delicate oasis in the black void that they said needs better care.

Artemis II included the first woman, the first person of color and the first non-U.S. citizen to fly to the moon.

“Wonderful communicators, almost poets,” Isaacman said from the recovery ship while awaiting their return.

Apollo’s manly, all-business moon crews of the 1960s and 1970s certainly did not do group hugs.

For those old enough to remember Apollo, Artemis — Apollo’s twin sister in Greek mythology — couldn’t come fast enough.

Author Andy Chaikin said he felt like Rip Van Winkle awakening from a nearly 54-year nap. His 1994 biography “A Man on the Moon” led to the HBO miniseries “From the Earth to the Moon.”

“It’s amazing how far we’ve come and how different this experience is from back then,” Chaikin said from Johnson Space Center late last week.

The hardest part, according to NASA Associate Administrator Amit Kshatriya, is becoming so close to the crews and their families and then blasting them to the moon. He anxiously monitored Friday’s reentry alongside the astronauts’ spouses and children.

“You know what’s at stake,” Kshatriya confided afterward. “It’s going to take risk to explore, but you have to make sure you find the right line between being paralyzed by it and being able to manage it.”

Calling it “mission complete” only after being reunited with his two daughters, Wiseman issued a rallying cry to the rows of blue-flight-suited astronauts at Saturday’s celebration.

“It is time to go and be ready,” he said, pointing at them, “because it takes courage. It takes determination, and you all are freaking going and we are going to be standing there supporting you every single step of the way in every possible way possible.”

title: Belle Fourche heavy equipment students  dig into real-world experience
byline: By Austin Bishop Ag education educator and FFA advisor; CTE director
description: BELLE FOURCHE — On Thursday, March 26 the Belle Fourche High School Heavy Equipment Operation (HEO) second semester students were able to showcase their heavy equipment operation skills on an actual worksite. Our industry partnerships with local companies site works specialist and Scull Construction service provided the opportunity for our second annual worksite field day.
_full_text:
BELLE FOURCHE — On Thursday, March 26 the Belle Fourche High School Heavy Equipment Operation (HEO) second semester students were able to showcase their heavy equipment operation skills on an actual worksite. Our industry partnerships with local companies site works specialist and Scull Construction service provided the opportunity for our second annual worksite field day. 

Students enrolled in the HEO class work through industry theory and machinery operation in a controlled environment, operating equipment simulators in the classroom. Equipment highlighted in the class includes an excavator, forklift, skid steer, wheel loader, and bulldozer. Students advance through the course work based on industry standard performance indicators. These advancements are then demonstrated by their proficient use of the actual equipment. Belle Fourche High School educator Jim Hartwell explains, “This is an integrated part of the course curriculum. Hands-on experiences, like these, allow our students opportunity to take classroom fundamental skills and put them to action.”

Steven Uttecht, a senior site superintendent for Scull Construction Service states, “Scull Construction and Site Works Specialists appreciate the opportunity to spend time with the area students.  The time we get to spend with the future young minds of our industries through the strategic partnership with the Belle Fourche School District and their CTE program is invaluable to us.  Community investment is one of the cornerstones of our companies and we look forward to these opportunities to give back and possibly create some interest in construction with the students along the way. “

The Belle Fourche School District Career and Technical Education (CTE) program remain committed to embedding real-world experiences into its curriculum — closing the gap between classroom learning and industry, one piece of heavy equipment at a time.

title: BHSU sustainability department to host Earth Day Festival Saturday
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: SPEARFISH —Black Hills State University’s sustainability department is hosting an Earth Day Festival on the campus green, featuring presentations, food trucks, tours, and interactive activities. Students, faculty, staff, and communities are invited to participate. The theme this year is “think global, act local.”
_full_text:
SPEARFISH —Black Hills State University’s sustainability department is hosting an Earth Day Festival on the campus green, featuring presentations, food trucks, tours, and interactive activities. Students, faculty, staff, and communities are invited to participate. The theme this year is “think global, act local.”   

Saturday events will kick off with a social hour at 9:30 a.m. followed by a presentation by the Spearfish Trails Coalition, this year’s keynote presenter, in Meier Hall from 10 to 11 a.m. The coalition will give a presentation on their mission of creating enriching trail experiences to foster connection, stewardship, and engagement across the community.  

The Earth Day Festival will start after the presentation at 11 a.m., with educational booths, student and community organization tables, food trucks, solar panel tours, a free plant giveaway, and interactive activities on campus green.

Heathen Dream, Chuck Will’s Widow, and Neon City will play live music throughout the day.  

“Our goal is to provide over 300 BHSU students, faculty, staff, and community members with an encouraging and informative experience at the Earth Day Festival,” said Taryn Davis, project lead for the festival. “We hope attendees leave inspired to take local action and contribute to a healthier, thriving world.”

title: Lawmakers approve pay and bonus package for investment manager, despite concerns about returns
byline: By Meghan O’Brien Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: PIERRE — South Dakota’s state investment officer will receive a salary raise and an opportunity for an incentive bonus that could push his total pay beyond $1 million for the next fiscal year, despite concerns from some lawmakers about underperformance.
_full_text:
PIERRE — South Dakota’s state investment officer will receive a salary raise and an opportunity for an incentive bonus that could push his total pay beyond $1 million for the next fiscal year, despite concerns from some lawmakers about underperformance.

Members of the legislative Executive Board voted 12-3 on Tuesday to approve the pay and bonus package, after the state’s Investment Council previously recommended it.

The Investment Council manages nearly $21 billion worth of total assets that fund the retirement system for public employees and also support the state budget, including health care and education trust funds. Clark told lawmakers he believes in a conservative approach to investing.

“We’re only going to be conservative, versus others, when the markets are above our estimates of fair value,” he said.

Clark compared his investment strategy to famed investor Warren Buffet: “buy low, sell high.”

Investment returns for the State Retirement System have been below its benchmark each of the last three years, and the cumulative returns over the past 10 years have also underperformed the benchmark. That’s been a factor in the system’s retirees receiving cost-of-living benefit adjustments less than inflation for five straight years. The system is still beating its benchmark returns over the longer term of 20 years and beyond.

Clark said the council has been avoiding investments in major tech companies and artificial intelligence, which have become “expensive and overvalued,” he said.

The council acted similarly in the late 1990s to avoid the worst effects of the “dot-com” bubble, Clark told lawmakers.

House Majority Leader Scott Odenbach, R-Spearfish, cast a “no” vote Tuesday on Clark’s compensation package.

“At some point we are going to get questions from state employees, and from people who have a significant amount of their retirement invested in this,” Odenbach said. “Is that the wisest way forward? Can we as the policymakers answer to those who are looking to us and say, ‘Are you doing your job to make sure we’re getting the returns?’”

Clark currently makes a salary of $647,723. His new salary will begin July 1, the start of the 2027 fiscal year. He will receive an increase of 1.4% with all other state employees as budgeted by the Legislature, which will bring his salary to $656,791.

The investment performance incentive plan approved by the Executive Board could provide Clark up to 200% of his salary as a bonus, after the performance of the State Retirement System fund is audited and other performance indicators are taken into account. The council weighs its performance against a Capital Markets Benchmark, which is an index that can be used as a comparison to measure an investment portfolio’s results.

The average bonus for Clark is about 80%, he said. That would be a little more than $525,000 on top of his new salary. At 200%, the maximum allowed bonus, Clark could see an additional $1.3 million, “which is almost impossible to achieve,” he said.

Last fall, Clark received a little more than $125,000 as a bonus for performance during the 2025 fiscal year, on top of a salary from the same fiscal year of about $640,000, resulting in total compensation of more than $765,000.

title: Largest neutrino experiment in the U.S. wins Project of the Year award
byline: By Tracy Marc Fermilab Media Relations
description: LEAD — The Underground Construction Association (UCA) has awarded the Long-Baseline Neutrino Facility/Deep Underground Neutrino Experiment (LBNF/DUNE) at the Sanford Underground Research Facility (SURF), the prestigious 2026 Project of the Year Award. Hosted by the U.S. Department of Energy’s Fermi National Accelerator Laboratory, DUNE is a cutting-edge neutrino experiment comprised of 3 massive caverns located a mile below the surface. The space underground will house massive detectors and an entire laboratory system dedicated to neutrino research.
_full_text:
LEAD — The Underground Construction Association (UCA) has awarded the Long-Baseline Neutrino Facility/Deep Underground Neutrino Experiment (LBNF/DUNE) at the Sanford Underground Research Facility (SURF), the prestigious 2026 Project of the Year Award. Hosted by the U.S. Department of Energy’s Fermi National Accelerator Laboratory, DUNE is a cutting-edge neutrino experiment comprised of 3 massive caverns located a mile below the surface. The space underground will house massive detectors and an entire laboratory system dedicated to neutrino research.

“The dedicated engineering teams who designed, excavated and constructed the colossal caverns in South Dakota completed the project successfully and with an impeccable safety record,” said Fermilab Director Norbert Holtkamp. “Congratulations to the design and construction teams who achieved this important milestone. Construction of a project like this has never been done before in the U.S. They have my deep appreciation as we move to the next phase of making the underground laboratory a reality.”

The LBNF won the award for a project in the $100 million to $500 million category. The construction teams of the LBNF/DUNE project included engineers from Arup, Delve Underground, Fermilab, Kiewit-Alberici Joint Venture, SURF, and Thyssen Mining, Inc. Together, they pushed the limits of geotechnical engineering with the formation of two massive caverns, each measuring 65-feet wide, 92-feet tall, and 495-feet long. The three caverns span an underground area close to the size of eight soccer fields.

For Mike Headley, the executive director of the South Dakota Science and Technology Authority and laboratory director at SURF, this award was made possible thanks to more than two decades of visionary leadership, generous philanthropy and dedicated labor.

“I think this UCA award is tremendous in that it recognizes the monumental scale of this project,” Headley said. “Building something like this on the surface would be challenging enough. It’s so impressive that through the cooperation of multiple partners, we have this enormous accomplishment nearly a mile underground,” Headley said of the project’s safety record; SURF logged more than one million hours during construction without a lost-time incident.

“The UCA Project of the Year Awards are presented to a project team or group that demonstrates insight and understanding of underground construction or a significant project, which may include practices, developing concepts, theories or technologies to overcome unusual problems within a project, resulting in little to no outstanding issues.”

Excavation work at the far site began in early 2019 and was completed in February 2024. During that time, the underground spaces were prepared for the DUNE project with the restoration and expansion of historic rock-handling systems that removed more than 800,000 tons of rock from 4,850 feet below ground. The rock traveled up the renovated mile-deep Ross shaft at SURF, continuing along an above-ground, three-quarter-mile-long conveyor to the Open Cut. 

LBNF consists of three long cut-out caverns; two of the caverns will house two far detector modules each, placed end-to-end, while the third will house cryogenics equipment and other utilities that keep the powerful detectors running.

Excavation successfully concluded with the stellar safety record of more than 1 million hours worked without any lost-time injury.  

DUNE scientists will study the behavior of mysterious particles known as neutrinos, to solve some of the biggest questions about our universe. Why is our universe composed of matter? How does an exploding star create a black hole? Are neutrinos connected to dark matter or other undiscovered particles?

The project is the largest neutrino collaboration in history and consists of more than 1,500 scientists and engineers from over 35 countries.

title: Strong El Niño predicted to bring warmer, drier conditions to Black Hills
byline: By Sara Morgan Black Hills Pioneer
description: DEADWOOD — Sub-surface waters in the eastern Pacific Ocean near the equator are expected to be warmer than average this summer, pushing warmer winds across the northern hemisphere this summer. For the Black Hills, that means there is a strong likelihood that warm and dry weather patterns could persist through the summer and into the fall.
_full_text:
DEADWOOD — Sub-surface waters in the eastern Pacific Ocean near the equator are expected to be warmer than average this summer, pushing warmer winds across the northern hemisphere this summer. For the Black Hills, that means there is a strong likelihood that warm and dry weather patterns could persist through the summer and into the fall. 

Melissa Smith, a hydrologist and meteorologist at the National Weather Service in Rapid City, said that the warmer waters in the eastern region of the Pacific Ocean near the equator will create an El Niño, pushing warmer weather patterns across the northern hemisphere. As a result of the warmer conditions and increased water vapors in the air, westerly winds will push storm systems across the United States. A strong El Niño, a result of sub-surface water temperatures measuring approximately 2 degrees Celsius warmer than average, means that the storm systems are likely to be pushed farther south than the Black Hills, creating warmer, drier conditions through the summer and fall. 

“What (an El Niño) does, is it helps determine the position of the jet streams, so where the storms are going to track, where warmer weather air ends up settling over the continental United States,” said Smith. “So, if we were to go into a strong El Niño, which the possibilities this year are above average, that means that we are probably looking at greater conditions for warmer conditions this summer and great chances for drier conditions this summer.”

Smith said that if the predictions of the National Weather Service and Climate Prediction Center are correct, the conditions could be compounded by the results of last year’s warm winter. 

“Typically, in the winter we see anywhere from about 8-15% of our annual moisture, so we don’t have a lot of precipitation that we tend to get during the winter,” Smith said. “But usually, we get our spring snow storms and our spring precipitation which can then help us carry on for the year. With such a warm winter this year, there was a lot of areas where the ground didn’t freeze very deep. By the time we got even a little bit of snow, the ground was already thawed. Therefore, we didn’t see very much run off this season. Without that runoff, we have a lot of dry stock ponds, and a lot of the lakes are all lower than average because of it.”

Smith said the lower levels of stock ponds and lakes, existing fire dangers, and predicted dry and warm conditions could mean that the fire risk will remain high in the Black Hills region. 

“Right now we have very high danger across the entire area, especially in the Southern Black Hills,” Smith said. “They’ve been having a lot of wind, and with the hot and dry conditions, it has increased the fire danger. There are areas in the Southern Black Hills and even into portions of the Central Black Hills that are in extreme, D3-level drought, along with most of Southwestern South Dakota. Conditions tend to change pretty much north of Highway 212, they start to improve as you get into Northwestern South Dakota. But on hot and windy days, the wind can carry fire. We still have a lot of dead grasses out there.” 

Smith said to prepare for the possibility of a strong El Niño creating a warm and dry growing season, people with lawns and gardens should brace themselves for seasonal water limitations. 

To know if your area is under a seasonal water restriction, check with your local municipal government. 

Smith said that historically the Black Hills region cycles through wet and dry seasons lasting an average of 5-15 years. The last dry period lasted from 2000-2008 before transitioning to a wet period that lasted from 2008-2021.

“That was a longer wet period than we will typically see, but it looks like we’re likely going into a drier period,” said Smith. “How bad that dry period will be and how long it will last, that’s still to be seen.”

title: More than a meat counter: Black Hills Butcher Block expands offerings,  aims to deliver fine, fresh cuts and friendly service
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: SPEARFISH — When the Black Hills Butcher Block opened a sleek new storefront in Spearfish, the family-owned business brought its mantra from the shop on Fairgrounds Loop to the heart of town.
_full_text:
SPEARFISH — When the Black Hills Butcher Block opened a sleek new storefront in Spearfish, the family-owned business brought its mantra from the shop on Fairgrounds Loop to the heart of town.

“If you wouldn’t take it to your grandma’s house for Sunday dinner, don’t let it walk out the front door of our butcher shop. Every piece of meat that comes in the back end of our business goes out the front end, cut by a butcher, with the intent of being served at a dinner table,” said Butcher Block owner Brian Mehmen.

He has a degree in butchering and meat science, which has helped to set the Black Hills business apart. Expertise, at the Butcher Block, is an intergenerational affair and the guiding light for what is fast becoming a boutique, experience-based meat marketplace. 

“We wanted to provide a superior product, with a superior experience, with an amazing service level,” Brian said. “People can buy meat from anywhere, but if they bought meat and an experience from us and it was fresh and friendly, it would beat everybody else out.”

Originally from a farm in Iowa, Brian graduated from college in 1989 and pivoted, improbably, from fine meats to high finance.

“I went to work for a place in Northern Wisconsin called Louie’s Fine Meats. It was very similar to this. It was a full-service meat market-type establishment. Literally, that is how I became a butcher. I fell in love with it day one, everything about it,” Brian said. “I never thought I would ever leave the industry, but then 5 years later, I was recruited into financial services and left.”

So, what brought Brian from Wall Street to Ryan Road in Spearfish? The answer is family. 

Brian’s son, Noah, grew up in Spearfish. He graduated first from Spearfish High School then Black Hills State University.

“I graduated during COVID. I walked out of college during COVID. No one was hiring. No one was open. It really was a thing; corporate-level hiring was non-existent,” Noah said. “I always liked working with my hands. I am a tinkerer.”

So, Noah decided to try butchering and was hired by the Butcher Block’s previous owners. As a trade or a culinary art form, cutting meat captured his imagination.

“Every night he would come home and be like, ‘Did you know? Have you done this?’ I would be like, ‘Yeah, yeah, yes.’ He was like, ‘Dude, I love this,’” Brian said.

When the shop came up for sale, the family purchased it.

Noah told the Pioneer he enjoys cutting meat, but he especially loves building relationships with customers.

“I like having our consistent regulars who come in every single day, and you know their story. You know their lives,” Noah said.

The decision to expand beyond the location at Fairgrounds Loop was made, in part, out of necessity.

“The reason we had to move out of the other place was that we couldn’t handle one more client. There were lines out the door every day, and people would say, ‘I would love to have stopped, but you guys were so busy I couldn’t get in,’” Brian said.

The new white steel building at 320 Ryan Road offers more than 4,800 square feet of space. The Butcher Block now has 120 linear feet of meat case to stock with award-winning sausages, thick-cut pork chops, and other offerings folks can’t find anywhere else in town.

House-smoked bacon is a bestseller. Ground beef and ribeye steaks are popular, too. The Butcher Block also stocks whole chickens, flank steaks, skirt steaks, tomahawks, short loins, T-bones, porter houses, tri-tips — the list of offerings is extensive.

“We know what to do with a short plate on a beef cow. We break that down and turn it into beef short ribs for home chefs,” Brian said. “We take custom cut orders too. People will be like, ‘Have you ever heard of this cut?’ We’ll be like, ‘Yes, we have, and we can do it for you.’”

If a customer spots something in the case they’re not sure how to prepare, the Butcher Block has a plan for that, too. They have an in-house executive chef on hand to offer guidance.

Wesley Castle has roots in fine dining in Arizona.

Castle runs the Butcher Block’s sister dutch-oven catering business, and now, he prepares 3 breakfast items and 3 lunch items daily — served at the front counter of the new market.

“The menu will start to get more and more intricate, too,” Castle said. “I get bored very quickly, and I have to start changing things.”

In addition to prepared food, the Butcher Block aims to elevate other local businesses. They serve pastries from the Rustic Nook. Shoppers can take home bread and buns from Hannah’s Twist. Bags of Sawyer Coffee line one shelf in the market.

“My wife Lynley, she was the one who designed this. She said, I want a localized market. I want a place where people can come and have an experience,” Brian said. “I think we have delivered.”

In the heart of cattle country, there is also pressure to deliver an excellent product.

“The biggest compliment to us is when the beef producers come and buy the beef from the butcher and the meat market that they trust and love,” Brian said.

Many of the regulars at the butcher shop are local ranchers. That, Brian said, is the ultimate compliment.

“These boys should be super proud,” Brian said. “Fresh and friendly, only the finest cutes in the Black Hills: that is what these two do.”

Noah does take great pride in his work. That pride is translating into a desire to nurture career opportunities in his chosen trade.

“We are interested in hiring the next generation of young people who want to get into a trade, who are willing to get into a career that isn’t construction or moving away,” Noah said. “We want folks to stay in Spearfish. Work for a local business. Really enjoy it. We will give you a career. We have a positive environment around here. We have a smiley place.”

title: Black Hills uranium permit hearing postponed to May
byline: By Meghan O’Brien Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: HOT SPRINGS — A public hearing about an application to explore underground for uranium in the Black Hills has been rescheduled to May 18-22.
_full_text:
HOT SPRINGS — A public hearing about an application to explore underground for uranium in the Black Hills has been rescheduled to May 18-22.

The hearing will take place in Hot Springs at the Mueller Civic Center. It had previously been scheduled for this week but was postponed.

In March 2024, Clean Nuclear Energy Corporation and its Canada-based parent company Nexus Uranium filed an application to drill exploratory holes in search of uranium near Craven Canyon in the southern Black Hills.

The company plans to use 50 sites to drill holes as deep as 700 feet on state-owned land, according to its application. Each hole will take approximately 2 weeks to drill. The company has additional drilling plans on federally owned land, which are under review by the U.S. Forest Service.

Dozens of individuals and groups have raised formal complaints to the state’s Board of Minerals and Environment about the potential adverse effects of the proposed drilling, including concerns for cultural resources and historic sites, such as ancient Native American petroglyphs in the canyon, and water quality.

Uranium is a metallic, radioactive element used as fuel in nuclear weapons and nuclear power plants. Interest in uranium exploration and mining has risen recently in response to nuclear energy’s potential to meet the growing electricity demands of data centers, which handle the computing needs of artificial intelligence.

The state Board of Minerals and Environment can deny an application to explore for uranium for several reasons, including negative impacts on historical, archaeological or recreational aspects of an area, if those impacts outweigh the benefits of the exploration. The board can also deny the permit if it will negatively affect the productivity of aquifers.
