title: City council and town board violated  open meeting laws, commission finds
byline: By Meghan O’Brien Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: Members of the South Dakota Open Meetings Commission meet Monday in Pierre, including, from left, Katelynn Hoffman, Michael Smith, Austin Hoffman and Emily Sovell. Photo courtesy of South Dakota Attorney General’s Office
_full_text:
City council and town board violated  open meeting laws, commission finds
Members of the South Dakota Open Meetings Commission meet Monday in Pierre, including, from left, Katelynn Hoffman, Michael Smith, Austin Hoffman and Emily Sovell. Photo courtesy of South Dakota Attorney General’s Office 

The Gary City Council and the Hermosa Town Board of Trustees will be reprimanded after failing to follow requirements for public meetings, South Dakota’s Open Meetings Commission decided Monday in Pierre.

The Gary City Council failed to allow members of the public to participate in an open meeting by asking 3 attendees, who are also city employees, to leave the meeting. Gary is a city of 240 people in northeastern South Dakota.

“They are still members of the public,” said Austin Hoffman, vice chair of the commission and McPherson County state’s attorney. “They certainly would have had the right to be there.”

Another complaint alleged the Gary City Council entered an executive session with 3 non-board members, who were also city employees, without a vote to authorize their being in executive session. Public board members can exclude the public from executive sessions with a majority vote to discuss legal matters, performance of public employees and a limited list of other topics.

The commission will hold off deciding on that complaint until its next meeting, where members anticipate hearing from Gary’s city attorney as well as the person who filed the complaint.

The commission found the Hermosa Town Board of Trustees violated open meetings laws by discussing city business an hour before the official start of meetings.

The complaint alleged that board members in the town of 382 in western South Dakota were running “work sessions” and not properly alerting the public.

“It’s truly worrisome to me if we have a council, committee, commission, whatever it may be, out there that is holding a ‘work session’ beforehand without any kind of notice and the public not being able to participate,” Hoffman said. “I am hopeful it will not happen again.”

Other complaints and final reprimands

The commission has fielded a complaint about the City of Egan Board of Trustees, which alleges multiple violations of the state’s open meeting laws, including the board’s failure to publish agendas 24 hours prior to a public meeting and failure to publish meeting minutes in a timely manner.

The commission delayed action, Hoffman said, because there wasn’t enough information in an investigatory file and some of the allegations deal with matters outside of the commission’s jurisdiction.

The commission also reviewed written findings and conclusions for a pair of decisions made in November.

In one of those decisions, the commission determined that the Rapid City Area School Board did not violate transparency laws in its handling of a former superintendent’s contract termination.

In the other decision, the commission reprimanded the Green Valley Sanitary District Board of Trustees for failing to properly cite a legal reason before entering executive session and improperly stopping a citizen from recording a public meeting.

The Open Meetings Commission’s 5 members are county prosecutors, known as state’s attorneys, and are appointed to the commission by the state attorney general.

title: A year of comebacks
byline: By Sara Morgan Black Hills Pioneer
description: Bryan Hencke, Sara Linn, and Greg Glodt, all participated in the Comeback Strong event on Saturday at the Sturgis CrossFit and Flex Fitness gym in Sturgis.  Pioneer photo by Tim Potts
_full_text:
A year of comebacks 2.JPG
Bryan Hencke, Sara Linn, and Greg Glodt, all participated in the Comeback Strong event on Saturday at the Sturgis CrossFit and Flex Fitness gym in Sturgis.  Pioneer photo by Tim Potts 

 A year of comebacks 1.JPG
 A number of teams from the community entered the Comeback Strong event showing community support.  Terry Anderson, on team “old and broken’ does the wall balls during the event.  Pioneer photo by Tim Potts 

STURGIS — For just more than a year, Emily Meyer and Josh Wilson created a curriculum to teach people struggling with addiction to treat their whole body, not just their minds. To celebrate, Meyer and Wilson hosted a fundraising event Saturday, where 3 clients gave testimonials, pairs did circuit workouts, and breakfast burritos, t-shirts, and raffle tickets were sold. With a surprise donation from First Interstate Bank of $25,000 the event raised almost $30,000 total. 

“I don’t even have words to describe it,” said Meyer. “It was this full circle moment of the community really coming together, our community being affected by our clients and our clients being affected by the community, which is what we’re working for. I was wildly surprised.”

The comeback program

A year ago, Meyer and Wilson created a 48-session program, where clients being treated for substance abuse would visit Flex Fitness, Sturgis CrossFit, twice a week for 24 weeks. For the first hour of the meeting, clients worked through a CrossFit workout program as a group. For the second hour of the meeting, clients worked through group therapy sessions, where they shared their experiences, learned new skills, and were challenged to treat their minds and their bodies with a more wholistic, healthy approach. The program is run and funded primarily by Compass Point, an addiction treatment center in Spearfish. 

Each client of Compass Point, whether they’re enrolled in the comeback or not, is given free, 24-hour access to Flex Fitness. 

“All of our clients have access to that gym and to the coaching available there because of the contract that we signed with them,” said Meyer. “I don’t know any other treatment facility that offers their clients a free gym membership, but we do.”

Over the course of the year, Meyer said that the program has seen approximately 30% of the clients complete the program.

“In that number of people that haven’t completed (the program), several of them have come back,” Meyer said. “They’ve circled back through the program because they needed a higher level of care at that moment.”

Meyer said that of the approximately 70% of the people that have completed the program, most of them have come back for a second round.

“There’s no other program that we run at Compass Point that has people asking to stay in it,” she said. “I think that’s really the thing that’s different, is that people don’t want to complete it, they want to stay in it. We have a couple people that have been in it for the entire time we’ve been running it, because they don’t want to complete it. If you look at all the research and the studies that they’ve done on people in treatment for substance use, the longer a person is engaged with treatment, the better their outcome. So, the longer these people are involved in the program, the more likely they are to gain that long-term sobriety and recovery.”

Meyer, a graduate of DUI Court who now works full-time at Compass Point as an addiction counselor trainee, part-time at Sturgis Coffee, and attends college full-time to become a certified counselor, said that one of the goals of the comeback was to get “treatment out of the treatment center,” where she hoped clients would have a chance to see themselves in a different light. 

 Fundraising event raises $30,000 for addiction treatment.JPG
For just more than a year, Emily Meyer, of Compass Point treatment center in Spearfish, and Josh Wilson of Flex Fitness, Sturgis CrossFit, have created an addiction treatment curriculum that combines CrossFit with traditional group therapy. The team hosted a fundraising event Saturday to raise funds for another year of treatment. Pioneer photo by Tim Potts 

Compass Point receives $25,000 donation from First Interstate Bank’s Believe in Local Campaign
By Tim Potts
 Black Hills Pioneer
 STURGIS – Northern Hills Alcohol and Drug Services’ Compass Point program has received a significant boost in its mission to support individuals in recovery, thanks to a $25,000 donation from First Interstate Bank and the First Interstate BancSystem Foundation through their fifth annual Believe in Local campaign.
 The funding will directly support Compass Point’s Comeback Strong program, an innovative approach to substance abuse recovery that combines physical fitness with counseling to help participants rebuild both physically and emotionally.
 At Compass Point, recovery is approached as a deeply personal journey. Staff emphasize individualized treatment, education, and community support, with a strong focus on dignity and respect. Many counselors bring lived experience alongside professional training, helping create an environment where clients feel understood and supported.
 “The Comeback Strong program has already changed lives, and this grant helps ensure it continues to do so,” said Executive Director Hilary Schwab. “Every dollar goes directly toward giving our clients access to the structure, support, and community they need to truly heal.”
 First Interstate Bank Commercial Relationship Manager Bryan Hencke nominated Compass Point for the award after witnessing the program’s impact firsthand.
 “Believe in Local has been a cornerstone of who we are for 5 years now,” Hencke said. “It never gets old to celebrate nonprofit organizations like Compass Point that are making a real difference in the communities we serve. Sturgis is better because of the work they do, and we’re proud to invest in that mission.”
 Hencke said he has seen “true miracles” through the program’s transformation of participants. “I’ve watched this program grow from the start, and the changes in people’s lives are incredible,” he said. “That’s why I wanted to make sure Compass Point was nominated.”
 The Believe in Local campaign, launched in 2022, awards $1 million annually to nonprofits across First Interstate’s 10-state footprint, with 40 organizations each receiving $25,000 grants. Compass Point was selected from more than 640 nominations submitted by bank employees.
 Compass Point Finance and Human Resources Director Grace Stien said the donation came as a welcome surprise. “We’ve had so many blessings through this program, but without funding, we can’t continue to grow,” Stien said. “This helps us take the next step forward, reaching more people and changing more lives. It truly means more recovery and more miracles.”

“When they walk into that gym, nobody necessarily knows that they have 4 felonies or that they are on probation or parole,” Meyer said. “Nobody knows that. All they know is that this person is at the gym to work out today. So, we’re giving them the opportunity to see themselves in a different light and to maybe be a person that, maybe, they never thought they would be.”

In giving these clients that opportunity, and by teaching them how to care for their bodies as well as their minds, Meyer said she’s seen clients learn to nurture their souls. 

“When we’re challenging them to stick to a diet or to try and eat different foods, they’re really learning that they can do difficult things,” she said. “Through regular exercise and better nutrition, they’re regulating their nervous system. They’re having less moments of anxiety, less moments of feeling triggered to use, they’re less angry, less aggressive, because they have an outlet for that stress and for all the energy.”

The result, Meyer said, has been a culture of inclusivity and camaraderie that clients don’t want to leave behind. 

“We have a daily topic for each session, but we’re always really adamant about addressing issues as they come up,” she said. “If one of our clients comes in and they’re really struggling with something, or they have a problem that they really need to sort out in that moment, we have a tendency to kind of push that curriculum to the back of the session and be present with our clients with what they need at that moment. Because that’s part of it, is meeting them where they are. I think that’s the reason people don’t want to leave, because the culture we’ve created in this group is such that there’s a deep camaraderie that we’re in this together and they want to stay in that unit. They feel like they belong and they feel like they’re surrounded by support.”

Measured growth

Meyer said that when the comeback program first started, they designed the curriculum to be a closed group. To enroll in the program, clients would have to wait for the 24-week session to begin again. 

“The idea was to try and keep the same people together the whole time,” said Meyer. “But, we had so many people that wanted to join that we really felt like we were doing a disservice by saying, ‘No, you have to wait.’”

Meyer said she and Wilson transitioned the group into an open group, enrolling new clients as they expressed interest. Additionally, as the program grew, the pair expanded the program into 4 classes a week.

“We had to add in a second group,” she said. “Now we’re running a group at 1 p.m. and 6 in the evening on Tuesdays and Thursdays. That was kind of a stretch to our schedules and figuring out the logistics of that.”

Meyer said she and Wilson have been working at creating a concrete, developed curriculum that facilitators can stay consistent with. From there, she said they’ve partnered with Black Hills State University and South Dakota University in Vermillion, who will help them collect and organize data from the comeback program over the next year to create an evidence-based program.

“Our goal is to get this program and this curriculum basically certified through NAADAC (National Association for Alcoholism and Drug Abuse Counselors),” Meyer said. “Giving us that evidence-based stamp would allow other treatment agencies to employ this program. We want to see the comeback, this program, with nutrition and fitness integrated into treatment, we want to see that in every treatment facility around the world. Because, really, it works.”

Lifesaving testimonials

Three clients stood up and gave testimonials Saturday, where they spoke about how the comeback program helped them regain control of their lives while struggling with the early stages of sobriety. The first speaker, Courtney Ring, was one of the first people enrolled in the comeback program when it made its debut on April 22, 2025. 

“Hi, my name is Courtney. I’m an alcoholic and an addict,” Ring said. “I’m a daughter, a sister, a mother, a friend, and member of this community. But 20 months ago, I was also unhealthy, lost, broken, embarrassed, guilty, ashamed, and alone.”

Ring said that she used alcohol as a way to cope, celebrate, and get through her daily life. 

“Celebration, drink. Sad, drink. Rough Tuesday, I deserve a drink,” said Ring. “For a while, that worked. Until it didn’t.”

Ring said that as she tried to gain control of her drinking, that control always managed to slip out of her grasp.

“Only weekends, only a few, only once a week. But it never lasted,” Ring said. “It always ended up the same way: every day, all day. Eventually, that was the trouble, and I had to ace the truth that I had no control over alcohol or drugs. I surrendered. Then, when I removed the substances, I was still empty, still depressed, still hurting. That’s when I realized alcohol and drugs were never the real problem. They were just a Band-Aid covering much deeper wounds. When removing the Band-Aid didn’t heal me, I started doing the real work. I went to therapy, I got involved with Compass Point, and they introduced me to a new class.”

Ring said that the first hour of the comeback, working out, was the easy part. The second part, the discussion, was what she said she found challenging. 

“Still, I made a challenge to myself. If I was going to take sobriety seriously, I would use whatever tools were available to me,” Ring said. “So, I showed up even when I didn’t want to — especially when I didn’t want to. Because I know what happens when I don’t. Somewhere along the way, things started changing. I started healing. I started regulating my nervous system, I started understanding myself, I started writing new chapters of my story. So, let me reintroduce myself. Hi, my name is Courtney. I am a daughter, a sister, a mother, a friend, and a member of this community. I am strong, resilient, courageous, intelligent, healthy, loved, valued, beautiful, grounded, proud, and still learning how to be vulnerable. Because I’ve learned that vulnerability isn’t weakness, it’s the courage to admit when something’s wrong and ask for help. And that’s exactly what I did. I needed help, a lot of it.”

Ring said that she was grateful for Meyer and Wilson and the comeback program, who showed her that she was “worth showing up for.”

“But I can’t give you all the credit, because I did the work,” Ring said. “I continued to show up. I stayed consistent. I kept going, especially on the days I didn’t want to. I’ve learned from this. I wasn’t weak when I was lost, I wasn’t broken when I was hurt, and my past does not define me, but it is a part of my story. Over the past 20 months, I’ve turned my pain into wisdom. I didn’t erase my past; I learned how to carry it differently. I put my pieces back together, but this time they’re not held together by glue — they’re mended by gold.”

If you or someone you know is struggling with addiction, please reach out to Compass Point at (605) 642-7093 or call the substance abuse and addiction hotline at (844) 289-0879.

title: 1,213 South Dakotans could lose Medicaid as federal work requirements take effect, department says
byline: By Makenzie Huber Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: PIERRE — The South Dakota Department of Social Services estimates 1,213 people on expanded Medicaid, about 4% of the group, could be disenrolled after federal work requirements are implemented in 2027.
_full_text:
SearchlightLogo

PIERRE — The South Dakota Department of Social Services estimates 1,213 people on expanded Medicaid, about 4% of the group, could be disenrolled after federal work requirements are implemented in 2027.

Medicaid is government-funded health insurance for people with low incomes. South Dakotans voted in 2022 to expand Medicaid to adults with incomes up to 138% of the poverty level, a decision that allowed the state to capitalize on a 90% federal funding match.

Congress passed a law, signed by President Donald Trump, last summer to implement federal work requirements for expanded Medicaid. 

The federal work requirements will mandate that enrollees from ages 19 to 64 work, volunteer or go to school 80 hours a month. Participants will have to meet those requirements a month before they enroll, and Medicaid renewal will be moved from an annual basis to every 6 months.

The federal government allows exceptions for people who are disabled, pregnant, eligible for the Indian Health Service, in foster care, were formerly in foster care and are younger than 26 or were released from incarceration in the last 90 days, among others.

Division Chief of Children and Family Services Tiffany Wolfgang shared the assessment and estimate with the Board of Social Services at its Tuesday meeting in Pierre.

Of the 29,504 patients enrolled in expanded Medicaid at the end of 2025, the department identified 6,066 patients, or about 20%, who “could not be determined” as meeting exemptions, work requirements or community engagement requirements outlined by the federal law.

“We do not yet collect information on volunteer service, medical frailty or veteran disability status, as these are not current factors of eligibility,” Wolfgang said in an emailed statement.

The assessment found that 39% of participants are already enrolled and compliant with other federal programs with work requirements and another 29% already meet federal work requirements. About 37% meet tribal membership exemptions and 29% meet caretaker exemptions. Recipients could be counted in multiple categories.

Of the remaining 6,066 people, Wolfgang estimates 20% will not meet the requirements by implementation, resulting in possible disenrollment. That estimate is based on what other states experienced after implementing state-level work requirements, she told South Dakota Searchlight. Previous estimates from health policy organization KFF projected 13,000 disenrollments when the One Big Beautiful Bill Act passed the House of Representatives.

Wolfgang told board members the department is working on communicating the change with tribal representatives, the department’s Medicaid advisory council, and medical providers to ensure recipients aren’t surprised by the change.

Department Secretary Matt Althoff told members of the board that the estimated number of potential disenrollments is “if we do nothing.”

“We don’t intend to do nothing,” Althoff said. “We want to make sure they understand and ultimately help put them in a position to make informed decisions that you can choose not to do the community engagement, but you won’t be eligible for Medicaid.”

The disenrollment would occur over the course of 2027, as recipients are up for Medicaid renewal.

title: ‘Dances With Wolves’ actor Nathan Chasing Horse sentenced to life in prison for sexual assault
description: LAS VEGAS (AP) — A Nevada judge sentenced “Dances With Wolves” actor Nathan Chasing Horse on Monday to life in prison for sexually assaulting Indigenous women and girls.
_full_text:
APLogo

LAS VEGAS (AP) — A Nevada judge sentenced “Dances With Wolves” actor Nathan Chasing Horse on Monday to life in prison for sexually assaulting Indigenous women and girls.

A jury had previously convicted him of 13 charges, mostly related to sexual assault of 3 women.

Accusers and their families told Judge Jessica Peterson they continue to suffer from the trauma caused by Chasing Horse, 49, and struggle with their faith after he exploited his position as a spiritual leader.

“There is no way to get back the youth, the childhood loss, my first time, my first kiss, the graduation I never got to have,” said Corena Leone-LaCroix, who was 14 when Chasing Horse assaulted her. “The life that little girl could have lived has been taken from me forever.”

The Associated Press typically does not use the name of alleged sexual assault victims unless they come forward publicly, as Leone-LaCroix has.

Chasing Horse, wearing his navy blue Clark County Detention Center uniform, stared straight ahead as victims read their statements and remained quiet as he was escorted out of the courtroom. He’ll be eligible for parole after serving for 37 years, and has continued to deny the charges against him.

“This is a miscarriage of justice,” he told the judge on Monday.

Peterson said she was struck by his continued denial of the charges despite the evidence shown in trial.

“You preyed on these women’s trusts and their spirituality, and you manipulated them for your own personal gratification,” she said before she announced his sentence. When the hearing adjourned, more than a dozen people in the courtroom clapped.

Other charges in Canada are still pending

The sentencing wraps a yearslong effort to prosecute the former actor after he was first arrested and indicted in 2023. That initial arrest reverberated around Indian Country, with law enforcement in other states and Canada following up with more criminal charges. Those charges are still pending.

The British Columbia Prosecution Service said Chasing Horse was charged with sexual assault in February 2023, though the date of the alleged offense took place in September 2018 near Keremeos, a village about four hours east of Vancouver. In November 2023, the case paused due to Chasing Horse’s charges in the United States, but resumed the following year.

After all of Chasing Horse’s appeals have been exhausted, British Columbia prosecutors will assess next steps, Damienne Darby, communications counsel for the British Columbia Prosecution Service, said in an email.

A warrant against Chasing Horse remains outstanding in Alberta, the Tsuut’ina Nation Police Service in Alberta said in a statement following Chasing Horse’s conviction in January. The Tsuut’ina Nation Police Service said that it is in contact with the Alberta Crown Prosecutors Office regarding the warrant.

January trial focused on his role as spiritual leader

Chasing Horse was born on the Rosebud Reservation in South Dakota, which is home to the Sicangu Sioux, one of the 7 tribes of the Lakota nation. Following his appearance as the young Sioux tribe member Smiles a Lot in Kevin Costner’s Oscar-winning film “Dances With Wolves,” Chasing Horse traveled across Indian Country to attend powwows and perform healing ceremonies.

During his trial, Nevada prosecutors said Chasing Horse used his reputation as a Lakota medicine man to prey on Indigenous women and girls.

Deputy District Attorney Bianca Pucci told the jury that for almost 20 years, Chasing Horse “spun a web of abuse” that ensnared many women.

Jurors heard from 3 women who said Chasing Horse sexually assaulted them. The jury returned guilty verdicts on some charges. He was acquitted on others.

Needing medical help

Multiple victims described how they participated in his ceremonies or went to Chasing Horse for medical help.

Chasing Horse allegedly told Leone-LaCroix when she was 14 that the spirits wanted her to give up her virginity to save her mother, who was diagnosed with cancer. He then sexually assaulted her and told her that if she told anyone, her mother would die, according to Pucci. The sexual assaults continued for years, Pucci said.

Chasing Horse denied the allegations and his attorney questioned the main accuser’s credibility, calling her a “scorned woman.” His attorney had filed a motion for a new trial, arguing that a witness was not qualified to talk about grooming and that the statute of limitations had expired. That motion was denied.

Victims and their family members testified that they struggle with their faith as a result of Chasing Horse’s actions. The mothers of the victims said Chasing Horse betrayed their trust and abused sacred traditions.

“Even to this day I struggle to regain my faith and spirituality,” said Lynnette Adams, the mother of Siera Begaye, one of the other victims.

The AP typically does not use the name of alleged sexual assault victims unless they come forward publicly or approved the use of their names, as Begaye has.

Begaye said she still faces complications after suffering an ectopic pregnancy as a result of the assault and being forced to undergo surgery.

“I am choosing to see this moment as a fresh start,” Begaye said. “I will rebuild my life, reclaim my voice and continue fighting for the future I deserve.”

title: Opera house to host community discussion of assault survivor’s memoir
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: SPEARFISH — Sexual assault is a tough topic to tackle. People are reticent to discuss it, and that makes building awareness, talking through prevention, and planning ways to support victims a challenge.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

SPEARFISH — Sexual assault is a tough topic to tackle. People are reticent to discuss it, and that makes building awareness, talking through prevention, and planning ways to support victims a challenge.

“We really wanted to engage the community in the conversation about sexual assault, so we looked for a memoir, or any book, that would tell the story of a survivor of sexual assault,” said Renae Servaty, the director of the Victims of Violence Intervention Program (VVIP), which operates Artemis House. “Reading a book is more of an individual journey, where people could engage as much as they wanted.”

The memoir VVIP selected is “Know My Name” by Chanel Miller. Miller was attacked by Brock Turner, a Sanford University Athlete, while she was unconscious following a fraternity party. Miller was known as “Emily Doe” while her case was prosecuted.

“The book kind of takes you on her journey through her time when she was originally assaulted, through the trauma — through the ups and downs, and the court process,” Servaty said. “We decided to take that book, do the community book read, and then kind of cap it off or culminate it with an expert panel, expert question and answer session that will be open to the community.”

With support from Henry’s Books Foundation and the Zonta Club of Spearfish, VVIP was able to provide 40 free copies of the book for members of the public to pick up and read. People took home all 40 copies, Servaty said.

On Thursday, a panel of experts will gather at the Matthews Opera House to discuss the book and raise awareness about sexual assault in Spearfish.

“We wanted to have a broad perspective from individuals who would be involved in the process if someone were to report a sexual assault,” Servaty said. The panel will include a licensed counselor, an attorney consultant, a Spearfish Police detective, a victim services provider, and an advocate from Artemis House.

“Some of the things that we want to talk about and want to bring to the forefront are just ways that anyone can be involved in preventing sexual assault,” Servaty said.

The free discussion is scheduled at 6:30 p.m.

title: Local students shine at state FFA convention
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: Courtesy of FFA.org
_full_text:
Local students shine at state FFA convention
Courtesy of FFA.org 

BROOKINGS — Local students shined among the 2,500 South Dakota Future Farmers of America (FFA) members and guests during the second day of the state convention April 21. 

Local students were honored for their accomplishments in agriscience the Star Pageant, District Star Greenhands, Proficiency Awards, and received the State FFA Degree, the highest degree awarded at the state level. 

Agriscience awards

More than 200 FFA members showcased 196 projects during the agriscience fair on April 8. Winners were recognized April 20-22.

Research Supervised Agricultural Experiences programs offer a deep dive into the scientific aspects of agriculture. These projects include investigation of materials, processes, and information to generate new knowledge or validate existing research. Through these projects, students collaborate with peers, educators, university researchers, and industry specialists. 

Local students honored for agriscience accomplishments are as follows: Atlee Olson of Belle Fourche, first place in animal systems, Division 5; Kasey Dobesh of Belle Fourche, first place in plant systems, Division 3; Branson Brink of Belle Fourche, first place in power, structural, and technical systems, Division 3; Aaliyah Lennon of Belle Fourche, first place in social systems, Division 3.

Proficiency awards

Students were recognized in excellence in developing career-specific skills through their Supervised Agricultural Experience (SAE) proficiency projects at the convention. The awards recognize members who have developed their skills and abilities through employment, entrepreneurship, and placement experiences in agriculture-related fields. Winners from each area will advance to compete at the national level this October. 

Atlee Olson of Belle Fourche was awarded first place in agriscience research and animal systems and first place in veterinary science.

Greenhand recipients

Each year, one member of each district is recognized for their accomplishments in demonstrating outstanding achievements in their SAE. These members demonstrated their skills by advancing beyond the chapter level through an application and interview process, where they were selected based on their accomplishments and growth during their first year in FFA. 

Kate Blair, of Belle Fourche, was honored as the winner for District 5.

FFA members receive State Degree

The State FFA Degree is the highest honor the association can bestow upon its members. Recipients have dedicated several years to developing their SAE programs, building leadership skills, and contributing to their communities through service and involvement. 

Local students who received the degree are as follows: Tucker Hughes, Atlee Olson, Jazlyn Olson, Andrew Pruitt, and Kate Williamson of Belle Fourche; Chloe Cammack, Jackson Cammack, Alexis Cermak, and Elizabeth Storm of Sturgis.

Star pageant

State Star finalists are South Dakota FFA members recognized for earning their state degrees and demonstrating top SAE.

The Star in Agriscience Award recognizes FFA members that demonstrate the top agriscience-based supervised agricultural experience in the state. Members from across the state engage in conducting research to better the agricultural industry using scientific methods.

Atlee Olson, of Belle Fourche, was honored as a State Star in agriscience.

title: Deadwood gaming shows  positive first quarter in 2026
byline: By Sara Morgan Black Hills Pioneer
description: Courtesy photo
_full_text:
Deadwood gaming shows  positive first quarter in 2026
Courtesy photo 

DEADWOOD — The South Dakota Commission on Gaming announced April 17 that gaming increased by 8.51% in March compared to the same month last year, continuing a winning streak for the first quarter of the year. 

Overall, gaming activity was up 11.44% over the same 3 months last year. Slot machine revenue drove the growth with an 8.9% rise over March 2025, but table games and sports wagering increased as well, showing 2.79% and 3.46% gains respectively.

“It is really encouraging to see the first quarter of 2026 ahead of last year,” said George Milos, director of the Deadwood Gaming Association. “As we inch closer to summer, we are happy to see inquiries increasing and are optimistic that the trend will continue.”

In March, $2,095,542.22 was given out in “free play,” resulting in taxable gross revenues of $12,589,894.02 for the month. 

A total of $1,133,090.46 was collected in gaming taxes. Those monies were allocated as follows: $125,898.94 to the state general fund, $402,876.61 to South Dakota Tourism, and $100,719.15 to Lawrence County.

title: Water to surge into drought-depleted  Lake Powell but at costs elsewhere
description: Low water levels at Wahweap Bay at Lake Powell, along the Upper Colorado River Basin are shown, June 9, 2021, at the Utah and Arizona border at Wahweap, Ariz. (AP Photo/Ross D. Franklin, File)
_full_text:
Water to surge into drought-depleted  Lake Powell but at costs elsewhere
Low water levels at Wahweap Bay at Lake Powell, along the Upper Colorado River Basin are shown, June 9, 2021, at the Utah and Arizona border at Wahweap, Ariz. (AP Photo/Ross D. Franklin, File) 

FORT COLLINS, Colo. (AP) — Canyons in eastern Utah will churn this spring with huge volumes of water — as much as 50,000 toilets flushing constantly at the same time — in a desperate attempt to maintain electricity generation for thousands of homes across much of the Western U.S.

The Green and Colorado river flows might seem like a bounty of moisture in a parched desert of sandstone arches and prickly cacti, but in fact it’s just the opposite.

After the driest winter on record, officials this spring want to raise the level of badly depleted Lake Powell on the Colorado River to keep its hydropower humming. To do so, they plan to eventually let out as much as a third of the water in Flaming Gorge Reservoir upstream on the Green River in Wyoming and Utah, which would exceed a record 2022 surge that kept electricity flowing.

Lake Powell, held back by Glen Canyon Dam, supplies inexpensive and carbon-free electricity to more than 350,000 homes. But it comes at a growing cost elsewhere in a contested river basin relied upon heavily by ranchers, industries and some 40 million residential water customers.

At Flaming Gorge in southwestern Wyoming, Buckboard Marina owners Tony and Jen Valdez are eyeing water levels expected to decline by 10 feet by late summer because of the releases. It will mean an ever-longer drive to the water’s edge to launch boats.

“Of course we’re concerned,” Jen Valdez said. “And it will probably get to a point where we’ll need to be more concerned.”

A balancing act to keep up power production

If everything goes to plan — and with no relief from the weather — Flaming Gorge will fall by as much as 27 feet a year from now, leaving Buckboard Marina even more high and dry.

Though it’s likely only a temporary solution amid long-term drought, there will be effects downstream, too, as U.S. Bureau of Reclamation water managers plan to keep more water than usual from flowing out of Lake Powell on the Arizona-Utah line.

Downstream, Lake Mead near Las Vegas is on track to resemble lows four years ago that revealed formerly submerged boats and human remains.

The drastic measures are necessary to keep the Powell waterline high enough to run the power-generation turbines without air getting into the system and causing damage, federal officials say.

Hydropower is a renewable resource — when there’s water

From cities and tribes to rural electric cooperatives and public utility districts, some 155 customers receive hydropower electricity from Glen Canyon Dam and other federal generators. None relies 100% on hydropower.

Many are in disadvantaged communities and all are not-for-profit entities that pay for, among other things, the costs to operate and maintain the dam and the federal government’s investments in it.

The federal Western Area Power Administration has contractual obligations to provide a certain amount of electricity to its customers. A loss of hydropower would require WAPA to seek power elsewhere that likely would be more expensive and not renewable, said Leslie James, executive director of the nonprofit Colorado River Energy Distributors Association.

“If Glen Canyon hydropower is reduced to zero or a low amount, it will have different impacts on what they charge communities,” James said.

It’s a situation James said she hasn’t seen in her 48 years helping electricity customers in Arizona, Colorado, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming.

Replacing federal hydropower with market purchases has forced rate increases for the past five years at Heber Light & Power southeast of Salt Lake City, with the latest increase hitting 13%, said Emily Brandt, the utility’s energy resource manager.

Keeping Lake Powell up could bring environmental costs

Increasingly frequent drought, evaporation and water demand — especially to irrigate alfalfa for the cattle industry — have shrunk the level of Lake Powell to 3,526 feet above sea level — just 23% of full capacity.

To keep generating power, the reservoir can’t fall below 3,490 feet, which is the level of the water intakes for Glen Canyon Dam’s electricity generators.

That has never happened since the 710-foot dam was completed in 1963 and Lake Powell was gradually filled to full capacity in 1980.

In 2022, the Bureau of Reclamation released an unprecedented 500,000 acre-feet of water from Flaming Gorge to raise Lake Powell. The latest Flaming Gorge releases to maintain Lake Powell’s power generation could eventually total double that amount.

Meanwhile, the plan to hold back 1.5 million acre-feet in Lake Powell will result in the Hoover Dam producing 40% less electricity at an even lower Lake Mead downstream.

Another downside: Warm water from Lake Powell’s surface could encourage the spread of smallmouth bass, an invasive fish that competes with a threatened native species, the humpback chub, in the Colorado River downstream from Glen Canyon Dam. Groups including the Grand Canyon Trust urge water managers to mix in deeper, cooler water to keep the Grand Canyon inhospitable to smallmouth bass.

A decades-long trend of worsening drought

The strongest releases from Flaming Gorge in the days and weeks ahead will be calibrated to help native fish in the Green River, a Colorado River tributary.

Eventually Flaming Gorge will dip from 83% full to an estimated 59% full. The 2022 releases from Flaming Gorge were followed by a wet winter, which alleviated water worries across the region for a time.

“We kind of got saved by Mother Nature,” said Valdez, the Buckboard Marina owner.

A wet year or two won’t be enough to reverse a quarter-century-long “ megadrought “ resulting at least in part from human-caused climate change. But Valdez is optimistic that wet weather will return like before.

“Hopefully we can expand into doing some other things,” Valdez said. “Because it’s going to come back eventually.”

title: East Colorado Boulevard closure begins
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: This map shows the closure area, and detour route for projects that are scheduled to close Colorado Boulevard until August 31. Map courtesy city of Spearfish
_full_text:
East Colorado Boulevard closure begins
This map shows the closure area, and detour route for projects that are scheduled to close Colorado Boulevard until August 31. Map courtesy city of Spearfish 

SPEARFISH — Two separate public works projects are set to close Colorado Boulevard from Rainbow Road to Aurora Avenue, now until Aug. 31.

The construction schedule includes opening the road to traffic from Aug. 1- 16 for the Sturgis Motorcycle Rally, before closing again for final improvements.

All access to the Keating Resources SportsPlex, Sky Ridge neighborhood, SoDak Storage Facility, and Cenex gas station will be from Highway 85/Exit 17 during the road closure.

Through-traffic on East Colorado Boulevard will be detoured to Interstate 90 between Exits 14 and 17.

Spearfish’s contractor for the Colorado Boulevard Widening Project, Dakota Redi-Mix, Inc. of Black Hawk, and the Eastside Acres developer’s contractor, Woelber Excavating of Belle Fourche, are coordinating to perform work necessitating the road closure.

title: Rep. Mary Fitzgerald to receive SDNA open government award
byline: South Dakota NewsMedia Association
description: Rep. Mary Fitzgerald
_full_text:
Rep. Mary Fitzgerald to receive SDNA open government award
Rep. Mary Fitzgerald 

BROOKINGS — Rep. Mary J. Fitzgerald’s work to improve South Dakota’s open government laws has earned her recognition as the 2026 recipient of the South Dakota NewsMedia Association’s “Eagle Award.”

The award is given by SDNA to an individual or group that has demonstrated outstanding efforts to protect and enhance open government and the public’s right to know.

Fitzgerald, a St. Onge Republican representing District 31, has sponsored various bills during her time in the legislature that have strengthened open government. This year, she sponsored a bill that helped clarify language in the open records laws to mirror similar language in the state’s open meetings laws. It becomes law on July 1.

In 2025, she sponsored a bill that requires governing boards to annually review the open meetings laws and a bill that clarified teleconferencing in the open meetings laws. Both bills became law.

“Rep. Fitzgerald has been a true advocate for open government issues during her time in the Legislature,” SDNA Executive Director David Bordewyk said. “She is always willing to work for solutions that are reasonable, balanced and ultimately protect the public’s right to know. We are appreciative of her commitment to these issues and are proud to honor her with the SDNA Eagle Award.”

Fitzgerald is completing her third term as a state representative. She is a member of the House Judiciary Committee and the House Local Government Committee. 

Fitzgerald will receive the Eagle Award during the association’s annual convention Friday in Deadwood.

SDNA started the Eagle Award in 2002 to honor those who support and advocate for the First Amendment and open government issues in South Dakota. The first recipients were the 2002 state Supreme Court justices for their work to bring cameras into their courtroom proceedings. Among other recipients over the years have been the late journalist and educator Chuck Baldwin (2024), Rep. Tim Reed (2022), Sen. Arthur Rusch (2019), Gov. Dennis Daugaard (2018), Sioux Falls resident Gordon Heber (2010) and Attorney General Larry Long (2004).

South Dakota NewsMedia Association, based in Brookings, represents the state’s newspapers and digital news outlets.

title: Gov. Rhoden appoints Callahan as  Fourth Circuit Judge
byline: Pioneer Staff Reports
description: Judge Chad Callahan. Courtesy photo
_full_text:
Judge Chad Callahan .jpg
Judge Chad Callahan. Courtesy photo 

PIERRE — Gov. Larry Rhoden announced Friday the appointment of Judge Chad Callahan as a circuit judge in the Fourth Circuit, a vacancy created by the retirement of Judge Mike Day.

“Judge Callahan is a man of integrity who communicates clearly, calmly, and professionally,” said Rhoden. “I have no doubt that he will uphold the rule of law, show care to everyone in his courtroom, and keep our state strong, safe, and free.”

Callahan grew up in Johnstown, Pa. He graduated from the University of Pittsburgh Johnstown in 1994 with a bachelor’s degree in political science and graduated from Ohio Northern University in 1998 with a juris doctorate. Callahan began his career in private practice and later served as a public defender in Pennington County. From 2013-2017, he served as an assistant attorney general under Attorney General Marty Jackley. He currently serves as a Magistrate Judge in the Fourth Circuit.

“I am truly honored and grateful that Governor Rhoden has chosen me. I will do my best to continue to earn his faith in me,” said Callahan. “I’d also like to acknowledge the judge that I will replace, Michael Day, who has served the 4th Circuit as an attorney since 1983 and as a Circuit Judge for the last 13 years. He will be greatly missed as he begins his well-earned retirement.”

Callahan and his wife live in Black Hawk. They have 3 children.

title: Absentee voting  starts in Northern Hills
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: NORTHERN HILLS — Absentee voting in Lawrence, Meade, and Butte counties are open for the June 2 election.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

NORTHERN HILLS — Absentee voting in Lawrence, Meade, and Butte counties are open for the June 2 election. 

In Butte and Meade County, absentee voting began on Friday. In Lawrence County absentee voting started on Monday. Officials from the Lawrence County auditor’s office said they received ballots over the weekend, which caused the slight delay.

Registered voters in all counties can cast their ballots at the auditor’s offices during regular office hours, from 8 a.m. to 5 p.m., Monday through Friday. 

Early and absentee voting is required by state statute to begin no later than 46 days before the election, and ballots are required to be printed 48 days before. According to that schedule, absentee voting was scheduled to start April 17. 

But according to state law, the deadline to file petitions to be included on the June 2 ballot was the last Tuesday in March, and with 5 weeks in March this year, that deadline landed on March 31. That shortened the time between the filing of those petitions and the printing of ballots for early and absentee voting. 

The Legislature addressed the problem with House Bill 1095, which changes the deadline for filing to the third Tuesday in March, but that bill will not go into effect until July 1. 

Voters must bring required identification to the auditor’s office to cast an absentee ballot. Acceptable forms of identification include a South Dakota driver’s license or non-driver identification card, a United States passport or other United States government issued photo identification card, a student identification card from a South Dakota high school or college, or a tribal photo identification card. 

In-person, absentee voting will be open until 5 p.m. June 1, the day before the election. Absentee ballots returned by mail must be received by the auditor’s office no later than 7 p.m. June 2.

title: The numbers behind the latest poll
byline: By Alexander Rifaat South Dakota News Watch
description: PIERRE — What did the South Dakota News Watch recent polls find.
_full_text:
SDNewsWatchLogo

PIERRE — What did the South Dakota News Watch recent polls find.

Last week, a series of stories examined the results of the latest poll sponsored by South Dakota News Watch and the Chiesman Center for Democracy at the University of South Dakota. We look back at the main takeaways and provide an explanation on the methodology.

Key findings

Here is a recap on the latest poll results:

U.S. House Rep. Dusty Johnson has pulled ahead in the race to secure the GOP nomination for governor, while the three other candidates - Gov. Larry Rhoden, state House Speaker Jon Hansen, and Aberdeen businessman Toby Doeden are in a statistical dead heat for second place. Meanwhile, state Attorney General Marty Jackley and Sen. Mike Rounds are on course to win their contests for the GOP nominations for U.S. House and U.S. Senate, respectively.

A majority of South Dakotans approve of President Donald Trump’s decision to fire former Gov. Kristi Noem from her role as secretary for the Department of Homeland Security. While most South Dakotans approve of the job performance of Rhoden and Rounds, U.S. Senate Majority Leader John Thune saw his approval rating drop.

Almost half of respondents disapproved of new tax legislation that will allow counties the option to implement a slight increase in sales taxes in exchange for the lowering of property taxes. A majority of respondents supported local law enforcement cooperation with the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement, new state voter ID laws, the U.S. Supreme Court’s decision to strike down Trump’s tariff policy, and for limits to be placed on data centers.

Most South Dakotans are to some degree unsatisfied with the current state of democracy in the United States. However, a majority believe state public officials are being held accountable at some level and about half of respondents said lawmakers spend either enough time or a great deal of time working on the most pressing issues for the state.

Methodology

The polling, conducted by Mason-Dixon Polling and Strategy, ranked as one of the least-biased and most-factual pollsters by Media Bias/Fact Check, was split into two separate scientific surveys.

For the poll on the GOP gubernatorial, U.S. House, and U.S. Senate nomination races, Mason-Dixon surveyed 500 registered Republicans between April 7-11. For the polling on job performance, voter sentiment on a number of issues, and thoughts on democracy and accountability, Mason-Dixon surveyed 500 South Dakotans across party affiliation between April 7-9.

The interviews were selected at random from a South Dakota voter registration list that included both land line and cell phone numbers. Quotas were assigned to reflect voter registration by county. For the poll on the GOP races, all respondents indicated they were likely to vote in the upcoming June 2 primary.

“It went smoothly and efficiently, which allowed for it to be completed within an optimum window,” Brad Coker, founder of Mason-Dixon, told News Watch. “South Dakota voters are poll friendly.”

The process for deciding the topics and drafting the survey questions was a collaborative effort between South Dakota News Watch, the Chiesman Center, and Mason-Dixon.

We attempted to cover as many topics as possible without overwhelming respondents and ourselves.

Inevitably,  there were some poll questions readers might have wanted that we were unable to get to, such as the job performance of Johnson as congressman or voter sentiment towards the current military conflict with Iran.

We hope you have found the poll results insightful and stay tuned for our next voter survey this fall.

South Dakota News Watch is an independent nonprofit. Read, donate and subscribe for free at sdnewswatch.org.

title: Trump’s budget would gut local libraries and museums. Congress is not on board
byline: By Shauneen Miranda States Newsroom
description: WASHINGTON — President Donald Trump is looking to eliminate funding in fiscal 2027 for the agency that serves as the primary federal funding source for libraries and museums nationwide.
_full_text:
StatesnewsroomLogo

WASHINGTON — President Donald Trump is looking to eliminate funding in fiscal 2027 for the agency that serves as the primary federal funding source for libraries and museums nationwide.

But congressional appropriators — who rebuffed similar efforts to gut the agency in fiscal 2026 — expressed little enthusiasm for the proposed cut in interviews with States Newsroom. Groups representing museums and libraries across the country also blasted the president’s proposal. 

The administration is requesting $6 million in fiscal 2027 for the agency, known as the Institute of Museum and Library Services, “for necessary expenses to carry out (its) closure.”

U.S. Sen. Shelley Moore Capito, chair of the Senate Appropriations Subcommittee on Labor, Health and Human Services, Education and Related Agencies, noted that her panel did not agree to the same Trump request in fiscal 2026 to eliminate funding for the agency. 

“I personally have always been a fan of libraries, and it does a lot for local communities,” said Capito, a West Virginia Republican whose panel writes the annual bill to fund the Institute of Museum and Library Services. 

“So, that’s what he does, he proposes, and then we look at it and make our own decisions,” she said. 

Last year’s request turned down

The spending package signed into law by Trump in February provides roughly $292 million for the agency this fiscal year — a sharp rejection of Trump’s efforts. 

Capito said that though her committee will consider the president’s fiscal 2027 request, “if you look at what we did last year, it shows that we kind of rejected that premise.” 

Rep. Robert Aderholt, an Alabama Republican and chair of the corresponding Appropriations subcommittee in the House, appeared noncommittal about pursuing Trump’s fiscal 2027 request to gut the agency.

In response to States Newsroom’s request for a phone interview, Aderholt provided a written statement. 

“We are reviewing the request from the Administration and the requests from every member of the House,” Aderholt said, adding that “this is a member-driven process, and we look forward to working with our colleagues in putting together a strong bill for the American taxpayers.” 

Legal battles

The agency was created by Congress in 1996 and has a mission to “advance, support, and empower America’s museums, libraries, and related organizations through grantmaking, research, and policy development.”

The administration has taken major steps to try to dismantle the agency, including through a March 2025 executive order. 

However, Trump’s Department of Justice reached a settlement earlier in April with the American Library Association — the nation’s largest library association — and the American Federation of State, County and Municipal Employees — the country’s largest union of cultural workers — that protects the agency and guarantees it will continue issuing grants and program operations. 

In another setback for the administration, the DOJ dropped its appeal this month in a case brought by 21 attorneys general, who challenged the administration’s efforts to dismantle the agency and had secured a major court victory in November. 

‘The barbarians are at the door’

Meanwhile, leading Democrats on the House and Senate appropriations panels dealing with the agency’s spending were quick to lambaste Trump’s proposal in interviews with States Newsroom. 

Sen. Tammy Baldwin, ranking member of the Senate subcommittee and a Wisconsin Democrat, described the agency as “such an incredibly valuable entity” and vowed to fight “tooth and nail” to protect it. 

Rep. Rosa DeLauro, ranking member of the full House Appropriations Committee and the spending subcommittee with jurisdiction over the agency, said the administration’s request is “just neanderthal.”

The Connecticut Democrat said “we’ll work to restore like we try to do every time,” while adding that Trump’s request indicates that “the barbarians are at the door.” 

Library, museum organizations push back

Leading library and museum organizations fiercely opposed Trump’s request and called on Congress to reject the proposal. 

In a statement, Sam Helmick, president of the American Library Association, said Trump’s “continued attack” on the agency in the budget request and the March 2025 executive order to shutter it “shows the extent to which the administration is tone deaf to the needs of millions of Americans who rely on libraries every day: older adults and veterans who use library telehealth spaces; unemployed people who use library resources to find a new job or learn new skills; families who count on story time; and students and faculty who do research in school and academic libraries.”

John Chrastka, founder and executive director of EveryLibrary, said Trump’s proposal is “a direct threat to the infrastructure that millions of Americans rely on every day,” in a statement. 

Chrastka, whose organization is dedicated to building support for libraries, said “libraries are not optional,” but instead represent “essential public resources that support literacy, workforce development, and community connection in every state.”

The American Alliance of Museums blasted the proposal as “misguided and out of step with the American public and Congress,” noting that similar efforts in fiscal 2026 and prior budget cycles to yank funding for the agency were rejected due to “strong bipartisan, bicameral support in Congress and sustained advocacy from the museum community.” 

The Institute of Museum and Library Services declined to comment on Trump’s fiscal 2027 budget request.

title: Sturgis issues first approval for residential camping ordinance
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: The city of Sturgis continues to work on revising its residential camping ordinance. Residents have been renting out their lawns for Rally campers for decades. Pioneer file photo
_full_text:
Sturgis issues first approval for residential camping ordinance
The city of Sturgis continues to work on revising its residential camping ordinance. Residents have been renting out their lawns for Rally campers for decades. Pioneer file photo 

STURGIS — Following months of public input, and several different drafts, the Sturgis City Council has given its first nod to what the city attorney has described as the most liberal residential camping ordinance in the state. 

The new ordinance allows property owners to have 3 camping units, with up to 10 camping guests by right. If more camping units and guests are expected, the property owner must obtain a free permit from the city, and the ordinance does not apply a maximum cap on how many units are allowed. The permit issuance is based upon lot size, lot layout, the property owners’ relationship to neighbors, the owners’ camping plan, and compliance with health and safety standards such as fire extinguishers and sanitary measures. 

While the revised ordinance presented on Monday defined Rally camping as 3 days before the Sturgis Motorcycle Rally, 10 days during the Rally, and 3 days after, residents said that’s not enough. Many Rally attendees come a full week before the Rally starts, they said. 

Due to public comment, first reading approval for the ordinance came with a change to allow Rally residential camping 7 days before the Sturgis Motorcycle Rally starts, 10 days during the Rally, and 3 days after the Rally ends. The council voted 5-1, with Councilman Mark Chaplin as the lone dissenting vote. 

“I’m not overly thrilled with the additional 7 days,” Chaplin said. “I think that’s a little too long. We denied motorcycle only parking downtown, and all of a sudden we’re going to open it up so we can have camping in all of our yards?” 

The residential camping ordinance allows camping for 27 days a year — 20 days during the Rally, and 7 days for the rest of the year. Non-rally camping is limited to 3 camping units and 10 guests. But some residents want more, than that. 

Joy Nichols, who rents spots on her property for Rally camping, told the council she appreciates all the work and public input that has gone into crafting the new ordinance. She also asked the council to consider permits for non-Rally residential camping. 

“We have a large family and we often have 50-60 people, so I would assume that if they approve this I would have 2 separate permits — one for Rally and then another for our reunion,” Nichols said. 

Sturgis City Attorney Eric Davis said the ordinance is the most liberal in the state, and many municipalities don’t allow residential camping at all. 

“The way the ordinance is drafted right now, if Sturgis adopts this ordinance, it will have, we believe, significantly, by far, the most liberal residential camping ordinance of any community that we’ve located in South Dakota,” Davis said. “Most communities don’t allow it at all, with the idea being that if you want to have an event like a large family reunion, the best place to do that is in a commercial campground and not at your neighborhood parcel. But recognizing that some parcels would obviously be better suited for that than other parcels, that’s why it was left the way it is, with the idea of an RV coming with maybe 2 tents for the kids. Or 2 RVs. That’s the kind of event that people would expect to see around a neighborhood. There is certainly no categorical opposition to the idea of having a permit for such a thing. The question just becomes about neighborhood compatibility on a land use/rezoning issue unrelated to the Rally.” 

Some council members expressed concern about allowing a non-Rally camping permit for more than the 7 days that the proposed ordinance allows. 

“How are we keeping a residence from becoming year-round camping? I don’t think that’s what anybody’s wanting,” said Councilman Jesse Blakeman. “I don’t think my neighbors probably care if I had some camping during the Rally. But they’re certainly going to care if I have people coming in and out, 6 months a year, for 2 weeks at a time.” 

Following further discussion and approval of the first reading, Davis said he will bring the ordinance back with the proposed change to allow Rally camping 7 days before the start of the Sturgis Motorcycle Rally, to be considered for second reading at the next regularly scheduled meeting, May 4. Additionally, Davis said he will propose language for a permit that can be used for non-rally camping.

title: Lost season for some winter sports leads to adaptation in Black Hills visitor industry
byline: By Makenzie Huber Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: Terry Peak Ski Area’s snowmaking system in South Dakota’s northern Black Hills operates during the 2025-2026 winter season, while surrounded by dry conditions. Photo courtesy of Terry Peak
_full_text:
Lost season for some winter sports leads to adaptation in Black Hills visitor industry
Terry Peak Ski Area’s snowmaking system in South Dakota’s northern Black Hills operates during the 2025-2026 winter season, while surrounded by dry conditions. Photo courtesy of Terry Peak 

LEAD — Dry, dormant grass surrounded Terry Peak this winter. The ski area was a patch of artificial white amid miles of brown.

The Black Hills experienced one of its driest, warmest winters on record, according to state climatologist Laura Edwards. 

“You see a lot of top 10s across South Dakota, essentially,” Edwards said. “I think that’s the story of the winter.”

In the Black Hills, winter weather is an economic lifeline during tourism’s offseason. The visitor industry of the northern Black Hills, in particular, is built around snowy winters.

From October through early April, snowfall in the Northern Hills was about 8 feet less than average. Other parts of the Black Hills saw deficits of 2-3 feet.

That lack of snow impacted businesses that depend on winter recreation, such as downhill skiing, snowmobiling and cross-country skiing. Owners and industry workers expect warmer winters to continue, forcing them to adapt.

Winter precipitation amounts are trending downward and average winter temperatures are trending upward in Lawrence County, which encompasses the northern Black Hills, according to data from the National Centers for Environmental Information. The county is trending an inch less in precipitation between December and March compared to the late 1800s, and it’s trending 4 degrees Fahrenheit higher.

$6.5 million investment keeps ski area open

This winter was among the “most challenging” in Linda Derosier’s 38 years at Terry Peak.

“It’s tough to remind people or get them to understand that there was snow at Terry Peak when they’re looking at brown grass in their backyard,” said Derosier, the ski area’s marketing director. 

Winters have become warmer in the last few decades, Derosier said, and snowfall in the northern Black Hills varies widely year to year.

The resort spent $6.5 million over the past two years to update its snowmaking system, replacing water lines, adding snowmaking locations and installing permanent snow guns. 

The investment more than tripled snowmaking capacity and helped sustain operations this season. Ski resorts across the United States closed early — or didn’t open at all — because temperatures were too warm to make snow.

Derosier said employees often made snow overnight when temperatures were cool enough on the mountain, which is one of South Dakota’s highest with a summit above 7,000 feet. She added that the visitors who came this year are more likely to return and buy season passes because they saw the resort was able to maintain reliable conditions.

Cross-country skiing, snowmobiling nearly nonexistent

No snow means no business for Recreational Springs Resort, said owner Brent Eslinger. In addition to lodging, the resort offers a restaurant, bar and snowmobile rentals.

Snowmobiling relies entirely on natural snowfall, and riders were largely unable to get out in the Black Hills this winter.

The annual Deadwood Snocross National was canceled in January because of lack of snow. Eslinger still hosted an annual vintage snowmobile club gathering, though members couldn’t take their usual ride.

Cross-country skiing was nearly nonexistent, said Eric Anderson, volunteer grooming coordinator for the Black Hills Nordic Ski Club. The nonprofit maintains cross-country skiing and snowshoe trails in the Northern Hills.

The club typically grooms trails 30 to 40 times a year. This winter, Anderson said the club groomed 3 times.

“When skiing isn’t available, people pivot to other things to do,” Anderson said. “It makes it tough to maintain your user group, community and interest level when you don’t have snow every year and it isn’t as predictable.”

Without winter recreation, Eslinger has to work harder to bring locals and visitors into his business. Recreational Springs Resort also rents all-terrain vehicles and hosts events and private parties, such as weddings and reunions.

“You need to have that winter traffic to help make it through the rest of the year,” Eslinger said. “It’s either that or become a seasonal operation. Which, even then, I have fixed costs I have to pay for and that winter income helps offset a lot of that.”

Economic, visitor impact

Other Black Hills winter events were canceled due to warm, dry conditions. Chinook Days in Spearfish and the Burning of the Beetle in Custer were canceled, the former due to lack of snow and the latter due to high fire danger.

Visitor spending varied across the region this winter. Meade County, which includes Sturgis, experienced declines in winter visitor spending, according to the South Dakota Tourism dashboard, while Pennington County, which includes Rapid City, posted gains.

Custer County visitor spending fluctuated — down 23.8% in December compared with the previous year, up 22.6% in January and down 25.5% in February. Lawrence County, which includes Spearfish and Deadwood, had modest gains in December and January before a 7% drop in February.

Michelle Thompson, president of the Black Hills and Badlands Tourism Association, said snow-dependent businesses struggled, but other businesses benefited from the milder weather. Visitors had easier access to hiking trails, parks and museums.

The Black Hills Stock Show and Rodeo in Rapid City, an annual indoor winter event, “had a great year,” Thompson said, with record crowds and sold-out performances.

Thompson said visitor numbers throughout the Black Hills and Badlands region remained comparable to years past — total visitor trips were down 0.8% compared with last winter, while visitor spending increased 1.7%. The winter season accounts for just under a quarter of annual visitation.

“We feel for the businesses that rely on winter sports, and we know it wasn’t great for them,” Thompson said. “When the weather is good, we draw in visitors for other experiences we have.”

title: Sturgis election petitions due May 4
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: STURGIS — Petitions for the Sturgis mayor and Ward 1 council seats are not due until May 4.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

STURGIS — Petitions for the Sturgis mayor and Ward 1 council seats are not due until May 4. 

Previously, Sturgis city officials reported the deadline for petitions was May 1. However, on Monday Councilman Jim Thompson announced that prospective candidates will have a little extra time to collect signatures. 

South Dakota Codified Law 9-13-14.2 requires that a petition for a municipal vacancy election be turned in at least 30 days before the election date. The election is scheduled for June 2, which is 29 days after the set petition deadline of May 4. 

However, South Dakota Codified Law 12-1-17 is an election specific time computation statute, which states that if the 30 day deadline for petitions falls on a weekend, or on a legal holiday, that deadline will be moved to the next business day at 5 p.m. Therefore, Sturgis City Attorney Eric Davis said the deadline should be moved to Monday. 

The petitions are for a special municipal election that was prompted when former Mayor Kevin Forrester resigned shortly before succumbing to cancer. Shortly after Forrester resigned, Ward 1 Councilman Nick Jones announced that he accepted opportunity out of state, and his resignation from the council will be effective May 19. 

Any Sturgis citizens who live within the city limits may submit a petition for mayor. Only those who live in Ward 1 in Sturgis can submit a petition for the city council. Petitions must include 50 signatures from registered voters who reside in the city of Sturgis.

title: Meade Co. Senior Center receives generous donation to purchase commercial oven
byline: By Tim Potts Black Hills Pioneer
description: Noreen Suniga, director of the Meade County Senior Center, stands by the new commercial-grade oven that was recently purchased with funds from a generous gift from Albert and Laverne Elliot. Pioneer photo by Tim Potts
_full_text:
Meade Co. Senior Center receives generous donation to purchase commercial oven
Noreen Suniga, director of the Meade County Senior Center, stands by the new commercial-grade oven that was recently purchased with funds from a generous gift from Albert and Laverne Elliot. Pioneer photo by Tim Potts 

STURGIS — A meaningful and deeply appreciated gift from Albert and Laverne Elliot is helping the Meade County Senior Citizens Center continue its long-standing mission of serving older adults in the community. Their generous donation, specifically designated for the purchase of a commercial-grade oven, represents not only a financial contribution but a lasting investment in the health, safety, and experiences of its members.

The Elliots’ gift comes at a critical time, as the center works to modernize its kitchen equipment. The current equipment has served the senior center for many years, but aging appliances have made it more challenging to prepare food efficiently and consistently. With the addition of a new commercial oven, the center will be able to improve food preparation, expand menu options, and create a safer, more reliable kitchen environment.

Executive Director Noreen Suniga emphasized that the donation will have a lasting impact on future programming while also creating new opportunities for facility rentals, helping to strengthen the Senior Center’s long-term financial sustainability.

“This is more than just a piece of equipment,” Suniga said. “It directly supports the meals and gatherings that bring people together. The Elliots’ generosity will help us better serve our members while also supporting our staff and volunteers in the kitchen.”

Caramel rolls and biscuits and gravy are served on Mondays at 8 a.m., providing a warm and welcoming start to the week. The senior center currently offers popular weekly meal gatherings that are a highlight for many members. Lunch is served on Thursdays at 11:30 a.m., allowing members and guests to enjoy a home-style meal while connecting with friends and neighbors. For many members, these weekly meals are about more than food, they are an opportunity to connect, share stories, and build friendships. By investing in the kitchen, the Elliots are helping preserve that sense of community.

The new oven will enhance the quality and efficiency of these offerings, allowing the senior center to continue providing fresh, well-prepared meals in a safe and inviting environment. It also supports special events and fundraisers where food plays an important role in bringing the community together.

Beyond its immediate benefits, the donation helps position the senior center for the future. As the older adult population in Meade County continues to grow, the need for reliable gathering spaces and meal opportunities becomes increasingly important. The new equipment will help ensure the Senior Center can continue to meet those needs for years to come.

The Elliots’ contribution reflects a strong spirit of community generosity. Their support underscores how vital local partnerships are in maintaining and enhancing services that improve the quality of life for older adults.

The center in Sturgis remains dedicated to fostering a safe and inclusive space where older adults can thrive through social activities, wellness programs, and increase quality of life. Membership at the center is available for $30 per year and includes access to programs, classes, social activities, and special events, along with opportunities to build friendships. To learn more about membership or ways to support the Senior Center, visit www.mcseniorcenter.org.

title: Sturgis to consider  election law changes
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: STURGIS — Changes in state law mean the city of Sturgis must adjust its election ordinances.
_full_text:
STURGIS — Changes in state law mean the city of Sturgis must adjust its election ordinances. 

On Monday, City Attorney Eric Davis alerted the Sturgis City Council to an ordinance he plans to bring to the next council meeting, which will change how the city handles its elections. 

In 2025, the state legislature passed House Bill 1130, which requires a municipality’s regular election to be held on the first Tuesday after the first Monday in June, or the first Tuesday after the first Monday in November. This year, the legislature passed House Bill 1030, which allows cities to only hold elections in even-numbered years. House Bill 1095 revises municipal nominating petition timing for June and November elections, and Senate Bill 165 distinguishes between a temporary absence or temporary incapacitation of the mayor, and an actual vacancy in the office. 

Changes to state law mean the council will have to decide how it wants to move forward with its election cycles. First, Davis said the council will need to decide whether to hold municipal elections in June or November. Historically, municipal elections were held in April. 

Council members told Davis they prefer to see elections in June, as November pushes up against year-end reports and budgets. 

Additionally, Davis said the council will have to decide whether it wants to shift to holding elections during even-numbered years. If the council chooses that option, it will need to extend certain council terms. 

“The principal upside of holding elections in even-numbered years would be administrative simplicity, cost savings, and alignment with higher profile election dates,” Davis said. “The principal downside would be that the city must manage a term-extension transition and will have fewer, but more concentrated, municipal election cycles.” 

The new ordinance will also address vacancies in the city council president and vice president seats. It expressly states that in the case of a mayoral vacancy the council president will step in as mayor pro tempore, and the vice president will move into the president’s position. It also further clarifies policies and procedures for special elections. 

The Sturgis City Council is scheduled to discuss the ordinance at its next regularly scheduled meeting, May 4. 

There will be an election in Sturgis on June 2, but it is a special election resulting from the resignation of former Mayor Kevin Forrester and Ward 1 Council member Nick Jones. Candidate petitions for the positions are due to city hall by 5 p.m. May 4.
