title: Out of the box thinking on culvert project saves city and airport money
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: North of the existing executive hangars at the Black Hills Airport/Clyde Ice Field, the ground is prepped for the installation of new hangar buildings. Pioneer photo by Sierra Ferguson
_full_text:
Out of the box thinking on culvert project saves city and airport money
North of the existing executive hangars at the Black Hills Airport/Clyde Ice Field, the ground is prepped for the installation of new hangar buildings. Pioneer photo by Sierra Ferguson 

SPEARFISH — A willingness to collaborate, and think outside the box on the part of Spearfish City employees, surplus material from one project was used to bolster another — saving time, money, and resources. 

When North Star Construction installed a new box culvert on Rainbow Road in Spearfish, the contractor was left was almost 13,000 cubic yards of surplus material. That dirt would have needed to have been hauled away and stockpiled. At a later date, if the city wanted to use that dirt to grade a different job site, the contractor would need to be paid again to move that material. 

Instead of hauling 1,300 truck loads of dirt from Rainbow Road to a stockpile location, North Star Construction President Matt Polich said, the dirt went straight to work. 

“We were able to go through the airport and not have all of the traffic, all of the Jake brakes, all of the wear on the road,” said North Star Construction President Matt Polich.

But the dirt didn’t just move through the airport. It was used there — to level ground were new executive hangars can be constructed. 

“It motivates potential builders, when they want to build new units, new hangars, it is essentially ready to go with minimal dirt work,” Channing Wagner, president of Eagle Aviation, the fixed base operator at the Black Hills/Clyde Ice Airport. “It really provokes that person who is thinking about building.”

That turns around into a benefit for the city, who stand to bring in earnings from hangar ground leases and higher property tax revenues. 

Spearfish Public Works Director Adam McMahon and Sarah Caron, the assistant public works director for the city, both worked to make the pivot possible. Polich said the ordinary, expected thing for them to do, would have been to require North Star to fulfill their contract on the Rainbow Road project and not reach beyond that. 

“I would like to highlight city engineering. They had to be willing to operate outside of the safe thing,” Polich said. “The safe thing for Adam to do would have been to put the stock pile out there, let the next contractor do whatever. He went out on a limb to say, ‘Yes, you guys can build this, that is great,’ then work with Mead and Hunt (engineers) and work with Channing.”

Wagner said the dirt work was done well, at very little cost to the city. 

“I get paid for the seeding and some erosion control, which translates to maybe $5,000 and there are several hundred thousand dollars worth of improvements that we completed,” Polich said. “It is a no-brainer. It is an absolute grand slam home run for the city.”

Wagner said there are 12 lots available for construction of private T hangars. Ten were spoken for before the dirt work. 

“I have 12 lots spoken for now,” Wagner said. “The economic impact is a huge thing.”

Wagner said people fly into the airport, then rent cars, buy fuel, groceries locally. They also book hotel rooms and eat at local restaurants, Wagner said. 

“That economic impact is, you truly can’t quantify, but it is there,” Wagner said.

title: Monument Health Sturgis Hospital Auxiliary to host Spring Fling and Tea Party
byline: By Tim Potts Black Hills Pioneer
description: The Monument Health Sturgis Hospital Auxiliary prepare for a St. Patrick’s days party at the elderly care center, one of the many things they do for the facility.  The Auxiliary members started planning for the Spring Fling and Tea Party that will be held today at the Methodist Church in Sturgis.  From left:  Paula Schmeltzer, Patty Kennedy, Cindy Voyles, Jan Clites, Sheri Johnson.
_full_text:
Monument Health Sturgis Hospital Auxiliary to host Spring Fling and Tea Party
The Monument Health Sturgis Hospital Auxiliary prepare for a St. Patrick’s days party at the elderly care center, one of the many things they do for the facility.  The Auxiliary members started planning for the Spring Fling and Tea Party that will be held today at the Methodist Church in Sturgis.  From left:  Paula Schmeltzer, Patty Kennedy, Cindy Voyles, Jan Clites, Sheri Johnson.   

STURGIS — The Monument Health Sturgis Hospital Auxiliary will welcome the community to its annual Spring Fling and Tea Party at 1:30 p.m. today at the United Methodist Church, located at 1755 Ballpark Rd., in Sturgis.

The popular springtime event will feature a variety of local business displays, a showcase of spring fashions, a silent auction, and door prizes. Guests will also enjoy an afternoon of coffee, tea, sandwiches, and sweet treats in a relaxed and festive atmosphere.  Admission is $10 for adults and $5 for children ages 5–12, and cash or check will only be accepted.  

This year’s grand prize is a membership to the Sturgis Community Center, adding to the excitement of the afternoon.

Cindy Voyles, chairperson of the event said the Spring Fling and Tea Party continues to be a meaningful way for the community to come together while supporting local healthcare initiatives.

Proceeds from the event will support the auxiliary’s fundraising efforts to purchase a podiatry chair for the hospital clinic and long-term care facility, helping enhance patient care in the Sturgis area.

“The Auxiliary provides quality time with the long-term care residents attached to the hospital,” said Patty Kennedy, an auxiliary member.  “We play bingo with them. We have a monthly birthday party with them and we have special treats to celebrate with them.”

title: Lawyer: Igloo lease dispute not  over despite Supreme Court ruling
byline: Bart Pfankuch South Dakota News Watch
description: PIERRE — Legal challenges to the lease used by the owner of a survivalist complex south of Edgemont will continue despite a ruling by the South Dakota Supreme Court on Thursday that favored the ownership group of Vivos xPoint, a lawyer for the plaintiff said.
_full_text:
SDNewsWatchLogo

PIERRE — Legal challenges to the lease used by the owner of a survivalist complex south of Edgemont will continue despite a ruling by the South Dakota Supreme Court on Thursday that favored the ownership group of Vivos xPoint, a lawyer for the plaintiff said.

The high court ruled Thursday that the lease is not “illusory,” or illegal simply because it was changed after it was signed by a tenant who was evicted from the complex in 2023.

The court ruled in favor of Vivos xPoint Investment Group, which owns the complex of hundreds of former Army munitions bunkers that have been converted to residences for so-called preppers who intend to survive a global catastrophe.

The high court ruling released on Thursday overturned a 2025 circuit court judge’s opinion that the lease used by Vivos was illegal and that the subsequent eviction was also therefore improper. The court remanded the case back to circuit court “for further proceedings consistent with this opinion.”

As such, the battle to have the lease declared illegal will continue, though with different arguments than the one considered by the Supreme Court, said Matthew Hays McCoy, a Custer attorney who represents the plaintiff in the eviction and who argued on his behalf before the justices.

“Now that this has been kicked back, we cannot use the illusory argument to say the lease is illegal, but we can use other arguments to overturn the eviction,” McCoy told News Watch. “In some ways it is kind of irrelevant to the larger issues.”

McCoy said Vivos would now have to proceed with a formal eviction process in order to formally kick his client out of the Vivos complex.

He said his client has strong arguments that he did not violate terms of the lease as it was amended by Vivos. McCoy also said he expects that a $17 million class action lawsuit filed in 2025 by several Vivos tenants arguing that the lease is invalid can still move forward.

Eric Schlimgen of Spearfish, attorney for Vivos, was traveling and unavailable for comment on Thursday, according to his office.

The current legal entanglement is part of a highly litigious atmosphere surrounding the Vivos xPoint bunker complex, which has been the source of numerous tenant complaints to the state Attorney General’s Office, lawsuits on both sides and a near-fatal shooting in 2024, as previously reported by News Watch.

The sprawling Vivos site includes 575 above-ground, earth-covered concrete bunkers that were used by the U.S. military from 1942 to 1967 to store conventional and chemical munitions at the Black Hills Ordinance Depot in a town once known as Igloo.

The bunkers were purchased by California businessman Robert Vicino in 2016 and have since been rented to survivalists who sign a 99-year lease to live in bunkers they do not own.

The lease governs their residency and includes a list of rules on behavior within the complex. The legal question centered on whether the lease and rules list were legal because Vivos changed the language after the lease was signed.

The 14-page lease and an extensive list of rules and regulations have become the subject of a handful of lawsuits after they were used as the basis to evict several bunker residents. 

Evicted tenants then lose the right to occupy the bunkers despite paying up to $55,000 in upfront fees, a monthly service charge and for improvements to make the bunkers more livable.

The high court case centered around the eviction of Daniel Sindorf, a former Vivos resident who signed a lease and paid $35,000 up front in 2020. The eviction came after a July 2023 incident in which Sindorf pulled out a gun after he said he felt threatened by a Vivos employee’s dogs.

Vivos states that several months prior to the confrontation, the company added an addendum to the rules included within the lease that made it a violation to “brandish” a weapon outside of designated shooting areas within the complex. 

Sindorf’s attorneys argued that the lease and, therefore, the eviction were both illegal because it is not lawful to make changes to a signed lease without consent of both parties or without recourse by the tenant.

In a 3-page ruling in April 2025, Circuit Judge Scott A. Roetzel noted that “the 99-year lease is an illusory contract that plaintiff (Vivos) can unilaterally modify the terms of at any time with no resource for defendant (Sindorf).”

But in its ruling Thursday, the high court said that “Vivos’ discretion was not unqualified or absolute, as Sindorf contends,” and that Vivos’ modification of the lease was “reasonable, made in good faith, equally enforced and not adopted for the purpose of evading the loser’s obligations.”

title: Nelsen named associate vice president for University Advancement at BHSU
description: Melissa Nelsen
_full_text:
Nelsen named associate vice president for University Advancement at BHSU
Melissa Nelsen 

SPEARFISH — Black Hills State University has announced the appointment of Melissa Nelsen as associate vice president for University Advancement.

Nelsen brings extensive experience in fundraising, strategic planning, and donor relations within both higher education and healthcare organizations. Throughout her career, she has contributed to annual campaign planning, supported capital campaigns, and cultivated strong relationships with donors, securing gifts at all levels, including major and planned gifts. 

In her new role, Nelsen will help guide comprehensive fundraising strategies, oversee major gift initiatives, and strengthen engagement with alumni and donors. The position plays a critical role in advancing the university’s mission through philanthropic support and collaboration with university leadership and the BHSU Foundation. 

“Melissa’s proven leadership and passion for building meaningful relationships make her an outstanding addition to our advancement team,” said Steve Meeker, vice president of University Advancement. “Her experience and vision will help elevate philanthropic support and further strengthen connections across the BHSU community.”

Nelsen currently serves as the director of major gifts/development for the Wayne State Foundation at Wayne State University in Wayne, Neb. Her past experience includes serving in leadership roles that provided broad oversight of development operations, including the position as an interim CEO, where she gained valuable insight into strategic advancement initiatives and team leadership. 

“I am honored to join Black Hills State University and contribute to its important mission,” said Nelsen. “I look forward to working collaboratively to build relationships and advance opportunities that support students, faculty, staff, and the future of BHSU.” 

Nelsen will begin her role at BHSU on June 22.

title: BHSU to spotlight resilience and  community through Fukushima Stories
description: SPEARFISH — Black Hills State University’s International Office will host “Fukushima Stories: 15 Years of Resilience and Community” from 4-6 p.m. today at the Joy Center. This event invites the community to learn more about the resilient recovery and unique culture of Fukushima after being stricken by the Great East Japan Earthquake in 2011, a story rarely told beyond Japan’s borders.
_full_text:
NEWBHSUBee

SPEARFISH — Black Hills State University’s International Office will host “Fukushima Stories: 15 Years of Resilience and Community” from 4-6 p.m. today at the Joy Center. This event invites the community to learn more about the resilient recovery and unique culture of Fukushima after being stricken by the Great East Japan Earthquake in 2011, a story rarely told beyond Japan’s borders. 

This event allows attendees to immerse themselves in the culture of the region by making Daiyamaki, a giant sushi roll, as well as sampling sake and local foods found in Fukushima.   

“Events like this give our community the opportunity to experience another culture in a meaningful and memorable way,” said Kaitlin Palmer, director of international relations and global engagement at BHSU. “Through food, conversation, and cultural exchange, participants can learn more about Japan while building connections and appreciation across cultures.” 

The event will also welcome guest speaker Jake Price, a filmmaker, photographer, and educator who is focused on climate change and the impact it has on communities worldwide. Price has documented Fukushima’s post-disaster recovery, and his work has been featured in “The New York Times,” “The New Yorker,” “BBC,” and “Rolling Stone,” while also receiving top honors from World Press Photo. Price’s documentary, “The Invisible Season,” will be shown at 6 p.m. in Jonas 110 on Wednesday, April 29.  

“Fukushima Stories brings voices and experiences from Fukushima to the forefront, sharing resilience, hope, and the power of community. Join us on April 25 to listen, learn, and be inspired,” said Yukari Tadano, the Japan Outreach Initiative coordinator at BHSU. 

Admission to the event is $10 for attendees over the age of 21 and $5 for those under the age of 21. Tickets can be purchased at https://partiful.com/e/NbGsZ9mLFQsUVwXPN1Rz. 

For more information, please contact Yukari Tadano at Yukari.Tadano@BHSU.edu.

title: Supreme Court rules officer  names can be kept secret
byline: By Bart Pfankuch South Dakota News Watch
description: PIERRE — Two police officers involved in a shootout with a suspect in 2024 can have their names protected from public view to prevent someone from potentially locating or harassing the officer or their families, according to a ruling Thursday by the South Dakota Supreme Court that reversed a lower court’s decision.
_full_text:
SDNewsWatchLogo

PIERRE — Two police officers involved in a shootout with a suspect in 2024 can have their names protected from public view to prevent someone from potentially locating or harassing the officer or their families, according to a ruling Thursday by the South Dakota Supreme Court that reversed a lower court’s decision.

Justices ruled that Marsy’s Law, a state constitutional amendment approved by South Dakota voters to protect victims of crimes, applies to police officers.

“Whether a victim’s name, initials or other information should be redacted will depend on the unique circumstances of each case and the asserted interests that should be balanced by the court,” the ruling states.

The justices heard oral arguments in the case during a traveling court session held in March at the Hamre Recital Hall on the campus of Augustana University in Sioux Falls.

The case “State versus Albaidhani” centers around an April 3, 2024, incident in which 2 Sioux Falls officers stopped a vehicle and then exchanged gunfire with Samir Albaidhani. One officer and Albaidhani were shot but survived.

As the criminal case progressed, the two officers’ names were published in court documents, but they later asked a court to redact their names from future documents related to the case.

Marsy’s Law is a constitutional amendment passed by South Dakota voters in 2016 that provides crime victims with a variety of rights, including being treated with fairness and respect and to be free from intimidation or harassment.

The law includes, “The right, upon request, to prevent the disclosure to the public, or the defendant or anyone acting on behalf of the defendant in the criminal case, of information or records that could be used to locate or harass the victim or the victim’s family.”

A circuit judge ruled that the 2 officer names or other identifying information could not be redacted from documents. The court noted that, “a name alone does not provide location details about the individual. Nothing in the plain language of Marsy’s Law prevents the disclosure of a victim’s name.”

That ruling prompted the appeal.

The officers were represented by Jeffrey R. Beck, an attorney representing the Fraternal Order of Police.

Beck told the court that releasing names of victims, including police officers, makes it easy for someone to find out where they live. Beck said he searched his own name online and found extensive information about himself, including everywhere he had lived back to his childhood.

Attorneys for Albaidhani included Kylie Beck and Emily Herbert of the Minnehaha County Public Defender’s Office. Kylie Beck argued that keeping people’s names out of public records could make it difficult for lawyers to contact them or subpoena them for interviews. She also said redacting names could interfere with a defendant’s right to due process.

The case will now go back to circuit court “for further proceedings consistent with this opinion,” the Supreme Court ruling states.

title: Wyoming communities want  time to consider embracing nuclear energy,  as feds rush to expand industry
byline: By Dustin Bleizeffer WyoFile
description: KEMMERER, Wyo. — With one nuclear power plant already under construction, Wyoming will soon be home to high-level nuclear waste storage unless the federal government builds a centralized facility.
_full_text:
WyoFileLogo

KEMMERER, Wyo. — With one nuclear power plant already under construction, Wyoming will soon be home to high-level nuclear waste storage unless the federal government builds a centralized facility.

When TerraPower proposed building its first advanced, liquid sodium-cooled Natrium power plant outside Kemmerer, lawmakers quickly carved out an exception in the state’s otherwise blanket storage ban to allow spent nuclear fuel that comes from any in-state nuclear power plant.

But the conversation about nuclear waste storage in the Cowboy State is far from over.

The industry is gearing up for what advocates say is a global nuclear energy revival, and some in Wyoming — including Gov. Mark Gordon, the Wyoming Energy Authority and Wyoming Business Council — are actively recruiting developers. TerraPower, backed by Microsoft billionaire Bill Gates, is considering more Natrium power plants in the state. Other players have considered setting up nuclear microreactor manufacturing facilities here, which could include storing spent fuel from portable units deployed around the world and returned to the state.

Building on those ambitions, state officials are eager to connect Wyoming’s reawakening uranium mining industry with the U.S. Department of Energy’s Nuclear Lifecycle Innovation Campuses initiative. Though it is “not an expression of interest in nuclear waste storage,” Wyoming “welcomes the opportunity to partner with DOE, national laboratories and private industry to strengthen the domestic nuclear fuel cycle,” the Energy Authority recently told the Department of Energy.

But do Wyoming communities want to crack the door open wider to the industry and, potentially, its radioactive waste?

“There’s all this risk, and we’re trying to make sure that those risks are minimized,” Big Wind Carpenter, a member of the Northern Arapaho tribe, said, adding, “What is the benefit for the community? I think those are good discussions to start to happen.”

Carpenter, who serves as tribal engagement coordinator for the Lander-based Wyoming Outdoor Council, took part in a nuclear energy forum this week hosted by the University of Wyoming’s School of Energy Resources, Ruckelshaus Institute and the Wyoming Energy Authority. A major focus was on how to educate and empower communities to engage with the industry, with federal officials and, ultimately, to decide whether or not they want the industry in their backyards.

That conversation happened very quickly in Kemmerer — from TerraPower announcing its site selection in November 2021 to lawmakers amending the state’s waste storage ban a few months later in 2022. There’s been a series of legislative attempts since to further amend Wyoming’s ban on nuclear waste storage or do away with it completely — all have failed amid intense disagreement that has divided some communities.

Radiant Industries withdrew its proposed nuclear microreactor manufacturing facility from Natrona County in November after a contentious year of trying and failing to get assurances that the state might further loosen its waste storage ban. The episode spurred Gordon to dub opponents — and the far-right Freedom Caucus, in particular — “ Club No,” describing “a new culture of no matter who began or who commenced it, we’re against it.”

Such high tensions are a sign of moving too quickly, said Jennifer Richter, who has studied why some communities have chosen to welcome nuclear waste storage and other aspects of the industry while many others have not. It’s a years-long process that cannot be rushed, Richter said, noting that the federal government has tried for more than 40 years to get buy-in from communities for a central U.S. nuclear waste repository, “and that has not gone particularly well.”

Why can’t the U.S. get comfortable with nuclear waste storage, like Finland or Sweden?

“It’s because we have a much longer and more complicated history with nuclear,” said Richter, an associate professor at Arizona State University’s School for the Future of Innovation in Society.

Lax regulations led to radioactive contamination of soils and water throughout the American West that resulted in increased cancer rates — pollution and repercussions that remain unresolved in many cases, including on the Wind River Reservation. The boom-and-bust nature of uranium mining also built many ghost towns, adding to skepticism of the industry.

“How to reckon with that history is actually what a lot of communities are asking,” Richter said, “rather than just going forward.”

Technology, safety and trust in oversight

Every type of “advanced” nuclear reactor technology being pursued today is not entirely new. It’s all been tried and tested — at least in a controlled research setting, said Christine King, director of the Idaho National Laboratory’s Gateway for Accelerated Innovation program.

“We’ve tested over 52 experimental reactors over the years at INL,” King said, adding that cooling systems such as liquid sodium and new fuel types such as tri-structural isotropic pellets, or TRISO, that encase enriched uranium are designed to add layers of safety.

But whatever the fate of spent nuclear fuel — whether it’s eventually processed for reuse, stored onsite or in a central repository — and whatever the nuclear power generation type proposed, “It’s not up to anybody to tell you whether nuclear power or how much or how little is right for Wyoming,” King said. “These are 100-year relationships you’re going to have with these projects.”

Whether the federal government has the patience that most communities require is another question.

Confident in human health and safety technology advancements, the Trump administration has ordered a major overhaul of the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission, primarily to speed up permitting. The “wholesale revision” of regulations and permitting timelines is moving quickly, said Tison Campbell, a partner at industry market analysis company K&L Gates who previously worked for more than 19 years at the Nuclear Regulatory Commission.

“That’s in process right now,” Campbell said. “Only a few of those proposed rules (changes) have been issued. Seventeen or 18 more are going to come out in the next few weeks for a 30-day comment period each.

“They’re planning to finalize everything by the end of November,” Campbell said, “so there could be a new regulatory regime in place.”

At the same time, Wyoming’s boom-and-bust uranium mining industry is on the upswing with previously idle operations coming back online and new mining projects in the works. For its part, the Wyoming Department of Environmental Quality says it is equipped with the staff and institutional knowledge to usher the mining industry’s expansion.

In fact, both the Department of Environmental Quality and its permittees go above and beyond federal standards, including Nuclear Regulatory Commission regulation of milling facilities that distill raw uranium ore to uranium oxide, or “yellowcake,” Wyoming’s Land Quality Administrator Brandi O’Brien said. The Department of Environmental Quality assumed primacy of that federal oversight in 2018.

A Converse County resident, however, quizzed O’Brien about a wastewater pond at the Smith Ranch-Highland uranium facility that is allegedly not up to standards and leaking. Maria Katherman also alleged that the company, for years, has avoided fixing the problem despite state regulators being made aware of the problem.

O’Brien acknowledged the problem, and noted “we also did inherit sites that (were previously) licensed by the Nuclear Regulatory Commission. Not that they did anything wrong, but it was a different regulatory requirement for the time they were licensed.”

“If our industry is going to expand,” Katherman said, “and you talk about, ‘How can we convince the public?’ Well, in Converse County, I’m the public, and no amount of regulations on paper are going to convince me.”

title: Poll: Majority of South Dakotans unsatisfied with current state of democracy
byline: By Alexander Rifaat South Dakota News Watch
description: PIERRE — A majority of South Dakotans are unhappy with democracy in America but believe state lawmakers are largely being held accountable, according to a new poll sponsored by South Dakota News Watch and the Chiesman Center for Democracy at the University of South Dakota.
_full_text:
SDNewsWatchLogo

PIERRE — A majority of South Dakotans are unhappy with democracy in America but believe state lawmakers are largely being held accountable, according to a new poll sponsored by South Dakota News Watch and the Chiesman Center for Democracy at the University of South Dakota.

The survey of 500 registered voters across party affiliation conducted April 7-9 by Mason-Dixon Polling and Strategy, ranked as one of the least-biased and most-factual pollsters by Media Bias/Fact Check, found 32% of respondents were very unsatisfied with the current state of democracy and 22% saying they were somewhat unsatisfied. 

Meanwhile, 30% of those polled said they were somewhat satisfied and 14% said they were very satisfied. Roughly 3% of respondents were unsure. The margin of error is plus-or-minus 4.5%.

The discontent with democracy was largely fueled by Democrats and Independents.

Three in 4 Democrats polled said they were either very or somewhat unsatisfied with the state of democracy, while 70% of Independents said they were either very or somewhat unsatisfied. On the flip side, 62% of Republicans said they were either very or somewhat satisfied.

Women were also more likely than men to be unsatisfied with democracy in America.

While 57% of women said they were either very or somewhat unsatisfied, men were roughly evenly split at 49% between those that were either very or somewhat satisfied and those who were either very or somewhat unsatisfied.

SD public officials are being held accountable, voters said

Despite their skepticism over national politics, the respondents largely believe South Dakota public officials are being held accountable to residents.

According to the poll, 45% of voters believe South Dakota public officials are somewhat accountable, with 12% saying they are very accountable.

However, as with the question on the current state of democracy, Democrats and Republicans have vastly different views on the issue.

While 64% of Democrats believe public officials in South Dakota are either somewhat unaccountable or not accountable, 74% of Republicans believe the officials are either somewhat accountable or very accountable.

Meanwhile, 31% of Independents believe officials are not being held accountable and 19% said they were somewhat unaccountable. 

Hellwege said the seemingly contradictory sentiments of feeling unsatisfied with democracy but also believing they are holding officials accountable could be down to the so-called Fenno’s Paradox in which voters may have a low opinion of a legislative body but continually re-elect their representative out of a belief in their own ability to hold their elected official in check.

Do lawmakers spend enough time on issues that matter?

Nearly half of respondents believe lawmakers either spent enough time or a great deal of time on what they thought were the most pressing issues during the legislative session that ended last month.

In total, 41% of those polled thought the South Dakota Legislature dedicated a fair amount of time to pertinent issues and 7% said a great deal of time was spent. Conversely, 25% said not much time was spent on pertinent issues and 13% said almost no time. Approximately 14% of respondents were not sure. 

As with the other two survey questions, the responses varied along party lines, with a majority of Democrats and Independents believing lawmakers either spent not much time or almost no time on what they deemed to be the most important issues and a majority of Republicans believing the opposite.

Hellwege highlighted that as most Republicans continue to have a rosy picture of the current political environment, Independents are increasingly diverging from the GOP on issues and, in many cases, aligning with Democrats, which wasn’t the case 2 years ago.

title: One size doesn’t fit all
byline: By Sara Morgan Black Hills Pioneer
description: DEADWOOD — The Lead-Deadwood School District is looking at offering an alternative education program at the high school as a way to boost enrollment and bring students back to the building after they left over the years to homeschooling and other educational opportunities.
_full_text:
DEADWOOD — The Lead-Deadwood School District is looking at offering an alternative education program at the high school as a way to boost enrollment and bring students back to the building after they left over the years to homeschooling and other educational opportunities.

Superintendent Erik Person said that he and a team of other school administrators recently toured 3 alternative schools, including Box Elder Job Corps, Second Chance High School in Mitchell, and the alternative school in Sturgis. Person said that in their visits, they saw children excited about getting their education and a career with a level of personal investment from the students. 

“Just simply looking at the number of students who have gone from attending our traditional high school to seeking an alternative instruction or homeschooling in the last 5 years, it’s significant enough to have us asking if there’s a way we can serve those kids better, meet their needs better,” said Person. “Some of it is obviously that some of those kids just have trouble following rules. But all of those problems are not necessarily unsolvable. Sometimes with the right supports, those kids can be successful. I think we’ve seen enough numbers to justify taking a second look. We don’t want to see any kids fail.”

A handbook and possible budgetary impact is expected at the May 12 school board meeting. 

Enrollment process

According to a draft document distributed at the April 14 school board meeting, students applying for the alternative education program must have demonstrated a failure to thrive in a traditional high school setting. Failure could include dropping out of school the preceding year, completing 4 years of high school without earning a diploma, becoming pregnant or having to assume the care of minor children, excessive absences, or returning from homeschooling. 

To be accepted, students must be enrolled at Lead-Deadwood High School or have been accepted through open enrollment. Person said that students applying to the alternative education program through open enrollment should submit their open enrollment application at the same time as their application to the alternative school. 

“We want your open enrollment application and your alternative school application at the same time so we can just be upfront with people,” said Person. “Because if they’re open enrolling to get into this, like if they’re not going to be a good fit because let’s say it’s a kid that’s just straight up behavioral problems and we just don’t see where they’re going to be overcoming that, we would just tell them upfront. We’re not accepting your application to the alternative school; do you still want to go through with your open enrollment application? Maybe they would, maybe they wouldn’t.” 

 Students applying to the open enrollment program cannot be currently suspended or expelled from any public district and must state their desire to earn a high school diploma in their application while agreeing to commit to a custom education plan. 

A personal learning plan will include “employment, and/or an internship (paid or unpaid), and/or a courseload with significant CTE (career technical education) focus, and/or significant participation in L-DSD (Lead-Deadwood School District) cocurricular activities.”

A completed application to the alternative program must include 2 reference letters from employers, adult members of the community, or educators.

“We want to establish that they could be successful here,” said Person. “We’re realizing that we’re dealing with kids that probably have not experienced a lot of success in our traditional high school. But what we’re looking for is people that are like, if we give you the right supports, if we do some of the right things with you, you’re motivated to make this second chance work.” 

Person said applicants will be put through a screening process, including an interview with staff and school administrators, before being accepted. 

“Some of the misconceptions about alternative schools are that it’s just kind of a place where any struggling student would go if they had a behavior problem or they’re a kid that has a chronic absenteeism, ship them to the alternative school and they’ll deal with it,” said Person. “That’s not really the model we’re looking for. If a kid is truant or chronically absent, what we probably will say is if you don’t show up to regular school, you’re probably not going to show up here. But if you’re motivated to get into this (program), what we want you to do is, say, over the next 9 weeks cut your absences in half. … Can we see some measured progress? That would be the kinds of things we’d be looking for on some of those that maybe we didn’t accept.”

Person said in the first year the goal is to accept between 7 and 10 students.

Hybrid classroom model

Person said that he plans to introduce an online curriculum through either Edgenuity or Acellum and will have an open classroom in the high school where one full-time employee, who is not a certified teacher, will be available to monitor the classroom, assist if able, and monitor behaviors. 

Edgenuity and Acellum both require an “instructor of record,” who submits grades, monitors progress and can provide instruction if needed. Person said one instructor per core subject would be assigned a period with an office hour where they would be available to students. 

“The kid is doing the work online and is going through the learning modules online, but then we’ve got a certified teacher in our building who is going to be checking in with that kid, maybe distance communicating with them, monitoring their grades, their progress on assignments, that kind of thing,” said Person. “Those teachers will have office hours, where if a kid is a little stumped in this online thing they’re doing, they can go to a certified teacher right here in our building during a certain period of the day and sit down and get help with that class.”

Person said in order for the model to work, classes will be condensed to create the extra period for the teachers assigned to the alternative program. 

“With our declining enrollment, we have some pretty small class sizes. We’ve got some classes with 6 or 8 kids,” said Person. “So, maybe instead of teaching 2 sections of 8, you might have a section of 16 kids and then boom, you have an open period.”

Person said the small class sizes of have been the result of the slow trickle of declining enrollment the district has faced.

Person said he has not researched the effectiveness of Edgenuity or Acellum, something he said is “almost irrelevant” under the proposed model. 

“Where it becomes irrelevant is our intention to not just take that program off the shelf and just say, ‘OK, here’s Edgenuity, it says this is an algebra one class,’” he said. “These platforms are customizable, so our teachers can make sure that they are aligned to South Dakota content standards, they can make some tweaks in there.”

Person said the intention is to support students in the program, not “throw them to the wolves.”

“Every kid will have the opportunity to take advantage of, in addition to their online platform, we have a person in the building that’s supporting that,” he said. “It’s not just turning them loose on the program.”

To graduate, students will be required to complete a capstone project, a South Dakota requirement for a high school diploma.

Bringing kids back to school

Person said students accepted into the alternative program will be encouraged to enroll in athletics, cocurricular classes like band and choir, and career and technical education classes, and even lunch.

“We want to encourage a pretty strong internship, or work-related educational path for these kids,” Person said. “We might have some kids that maybe they’re not in traditional wood shop class, but if they have an interest in pursuing building construction, maybe part of the day we put them into an internship or just out in the workforce. We have kids that stop attending here and one of the big drivers is they want to be able to make money to contribute to the family or whatever reasons they may have. They want to be out in the world of work, and they don’t want to wait until graduation to do that. Well, maybe there’s a model that fits that.”

Person said the alternative program will function mostly within the normal hours of a school day, but exceptions could be made to tailor the program to a student’s individual needs.

As well as accommodating non-traditional student’s needs, Person said the goal for these students is the same as every other high school student: help them set goals and find a path to success after high school.

“What we really want to see is not just get through and get your credits, we also want to help these kids find their passion,” said Person. “A lot of times you have to step into a CTE class or a cocurricular class, whatever it may be, to find your passion and what really drives you Some kids, their passion is athletics. So, if we can find ways you can be more successful academically while pursuing that passion, great.”

Budgetary impact

Person said that he is preparing an estimated budgetary impact on the district to present at the next school board meeting scheduled for May 12. 

“It’s going to be a fairly low budgetary impact. The total cost of this thing will be less than a certified FTE (full time employee),” he said. “That might sound like, well, gee, for only 10 kids that’s still kind of high. But when you’re taking a second run at things and giving kids a second chance at being successful, it’s not going to be probably the most efficient. But what is one kid worth? What’s 10 kid’s success worth? I think it’s worth quite a bit. It’s an investment in our future.”

title: Belle Fourche  Middle School celebrates Arbor Day
byline: By Brandon Woltkamp Black Hills Pioneer
description: BELLE FOURCHE — The Belle Fourche Middle School on Thursday celebrated the 154th anniversary of Arbor Day with the planting of an Autumn Blaze maple tree.
_full_text:
BELLE FOURCHE — The Belle Fourche Middle School on Thursday celebrated the 154th anniversary of Arbor Day with the planting of an Autumn Blaze maple tree.

Ann Anderson, a fifth grade teacher at the middle school, has celebrated Arbor Day with her classes for 20 years.

“I see the kids writing essays every year, and now doing poster contests,” said Anderson. “(The students) are excited about trees.”

The essay winners received cash rewards depending on where they placed. Third place got $10, second got $15, and first got $25. 

Anderson said she loved reading this year’s entries of essays written by her students.

The first-place essay winner was Jadah Adamson, second was Breklyn Montgomery, and third was Nova Ripley-Wall. All students are fifth graders.

Fifth grader Greta Abraham won the Arbor Day poster contest and will have her work submitted to the South Dakota Department of Agriculture and Natural Resources where 12 posters will be selected state-wide to create a calendar for 2027. 

All 4 students contributed and planted the tree in between Ziebach Street and the parking lot by the gymnasium.

“When they do this, they will come back and this tree will be their tree. This tree will be here forever,” said Anderson.

On the importance of Arbor Day, Anderson said, “Taking care of the Earth, restoring what might have been.”

title: Black Hills Pioneer at 150: 1892
description: Pioneer file graphic
_full_text:
Black Hills Pioneer at 150 years
Pioneer file graphic 

Pioneer at 150
The Black Hills Pioneer is the oldest continuously operating business in West River Dakota Territory; and 2026 marks our 150th year of telling the stories of the Black Hills. Over time our newspaper has absorbed the titles of numerous other publications, and we would like to share some of the more compelling stories from those early editions.
 Every Saturday we will be publishing stories from our archives, focusing heavily on the gold rush era of the 1800s, starting with our very first publication on June 8, 1876. These stories are re-printed exactly as they were first published, leaving in place the historic typos, grammar, style and terminology – politically correct or otherwise. It is our belief that history is sacrosanct and should never be edited to reflect the societal norms of the present.
 We hope you enjoy this historic journey. It is an honor and a privilege to be a trusted permanent record keeper of the Black Hills community; we look forward to the next 150 years of history in the making in the magnificent Black Hills.

~ March 23, 1892 • Queen City Mail ~

It Resulted In A Murder.

Eddie Thurlow Murdered By Al Carroll in a Dispute Over a Game of Cards.A Quarrel Arises Between Them and Carroll Uses a Knife With Fatal Effect.Ed Owens and Miller McKenzie Instantly Killed by Giant Powder in the Highland.

A terrible murder was committed last night at the Vincellette place, on Centennial prairie.

News was brought to the city this morning that last night Eddie Thurlow, aged about nineteen, and Al Carroll, another young man, were playing cards at Vincellette’s place, when a dispute arose, one of the boys accusing the other of cheating at the game.

From words they came to blows, and young Thurlow staggered backwards, dropping into a chair. His only words were, “Boys, my arm is broken,” and in two minutes he was a corpse.

On examination one of his hands was found to be severely cut, and a fearful gash across the neck, which severed the windpipe and jugular vein.

Carroll has fled, and at 8 o’clock this morning had not been apprehended.

The Killing was done about 1 o’clock this morning. The murderer will scarcely be able to escape.

Killed by Giant Powder.

Early this morning W. J. Bryant of Lead City arrived in Spearfish, on the way to Redwater to notify the Murrays of the death of their friends, Ed Owens and Miller McKenzie.

The two men were working in the 400-foot level of the Highland mine, one of the Homestake groups. They went into the mine yesterday afternoon, on the day shift, and fired a blast, which “blew out” without doing any execution.

They procured more giant powder and went down again, which was the last time they were seen alive. Six p.m. was their quitting time, and failing to come up for supper, it was feared some accident had befallen them. No night shift was running on that level, consequently no information could be obtained except by going down into the mine.

Two men descended to the level, and in about twenty minutes came up with the report that both men were dead—literally blown to atoms. A coroner’s inquest will be held today, which may develop some further particulars, but this was all that was known when Mr. Bryant left Lead at an early hour this morning.

Miller McKenzie is well known in this city, having at one time resided here.

~ March 30, 1892 • Queen City Mail ~

The Murder of Thurlow

The Crime for Which Alfred Carroll Will Sooner or
Later Be Called to Justice.
The Remains of the Murdered Man Laid to Rest in the Spearfish Cemetery.

Thursday the remains of young Eddie Thurlow were deposited in the Spearfish cemetery, followed by a large procession of neighbors and friends.

The coroner’s inquest developed the fact the deceased was cut or stabbed in five places. The blow that caused death was a deep stab in the neck, close to the collarbone, which severed the subclavian artery, probably penetrating to the chest or cavity of the lungs, which accounts for so little blood flowing outwardly.

As nearly as can be learned the facts are about as follows: Eddie Thurlow was the son of a widow, and was the main support of the family; and he is represented as a boy of good disposition, no way inclined to be aggressive or quarrelsome. Alfred Carroll, the murderer, was about the age of young Thurlow, and in all that goes to make up a manly character was the very opposite of his victim. The boys met at the house of Charles Vincillette Tuesday night to pass the evening playing cards with the two sons of Mr. Vincillette. Carroll was detected in “turning a jack” from the bottom of the deck, which caused some dispute, but was passed over till finally Thurlow chanced to turn a jack, when Carroll at once accuses him of cheating.

This was followed by hard words and a challenge from Carroll to go out of doors and fight. Thurlow at first declined that method of settling the matter, and tried to dissuade Carroll from such a proposition, but finally went out, when Carroll struck him in the face. Thurlow sought to ward off the blows of his assailant, but showed no desire to fight. Carroll then rushed on him, striking several vicious blows, and when Thurlow turned to enter the house, Carroll made several slashes at his back. Thurlow stepped inside the door, saying, “Boys, my arm is broken,” which were the last words he ever uttered.

He sank into a chair and expired. The Vincellette boys appealed to Carroll to help them hold up the wounded boy, and received for a reply, “Let the son of a —— die,” and mounting his horse rode away.

Sheriff McDonald was notified by wire and started post haste after the murderer. At Belle Fourche he found that Carroll had passed there some hours ahead. The sheriff and posse pursued, but found no further trace of him. Knowing that the father of Carroll has a cattle ranch at Slim Buttes, and believing that Carroll had gone in that direction, the sheriff sent a party of determined men in pursuit. He will probably be run down and captured, though his knowledge of the country on the ranges may enable him to elude his pursuers for several days.

~ June 1, 1892 • Queen City Mail ~

Another Fatal Wreck.

One Man Killed and Eight Injured by a Wreck on the Deadwood Central.
The Engineer Lost Control of the Train, Owing to Defective Sand Pipes.

The Independent brings news of a fatal wreck on the Deadwood Central yesterday morning. It says that as the Owl trail, which leaves Lead City at 3:20 a.m., was coming down the grade below the settling dam, the engineer lost control of the train by reason of the failure of the sand pipes to work.

There were twelve persons on the train— Engineer Mannahan, Fireman Harding, Conductor Crist, and the following passengers: J. W. Dodge, B. & M. conductor; J. F. Goldrick, B. & M. brakeman; Walter Simpson, wife and child; Jim Scott, an employee in the D. C. round house; J. M. Kyde, a clerk in Bloom’s clothing store; Will Thomas, a jeweler; T. L. Munroe, a Lead miner; Mrs. S. Hanson of Deadwood.

As soon as it was ascertained by the momentum of the train that the engineer had lost control, the passengers began to jump off. Walter Simpson grabbed his child and jumped, at the same time calling to his wife to follow him. Conductor Crist, with rare forethought, seized Mrs. Simpson and prevented her from following the direction of her husband. Simpson and his child were both seriously injured in the fall.

Pearl Harding, the fireman, jumped from the engine without receiving other injuries than bad bruises upon the face, head and body.

Strange to say, Engineer Mannahan stayed by the wreck with his hand upon the throttle, and although the cab of his engine entirely demolished and broken into kindling wood, he escaped dangerous injuries. He was caught in the back by the toolbox behind him, pinned in the wreck, and was extracted by those less injured.

Jim Scott jumped and received internal injuries, which were not at first thought to be serious. He was taken to his home on Miller Street where he died at 8 o’clock this morning.

Will Thomas jumped from the rear end of the car and struck his head on the corner of a tie, crushing his skull like an eggshell. He was taken to the residence of Dr. Paddock, where a trepanning operation was preformed and the skull raised from the brain. At this writing he has regained consciousness, and the prospects for his recovery are more favorable.

T. L.  Munroe, the Lead miner, sustained very serious injuries to the left leg.

J. M. Kyde escaped with a badly bruised leg.

J. F. Goldrick was standing on the front platform of the car, and remained on the car until the engine struck the rocky bank, which demolished it. He was thrown several somersaults into the creek below, but only received slight injuries about the hips and back.

Mrs. Hanson was seized by Conductor Dodge, and prevented from jumping from the flying train. Those who remained in the car were not hurt, while everyone who jumped was seriously injured.

~ August 17, 1892 • Queen City Mail ~

Drugged And Slugged.

That Is What Happened to Ed Dotson, According to All Appearances.
A Deadwood Female Said To Be a Prominent Actor in the Mystery.

Quite a ripple of excitement was created here at an early hour last Friday by the report that a dead man had been found in the streets near the residence of Mrs. Jenks. A reporter made haste to the spot and found a young man stretched upon the grass beside the road, and apparently unconscious. Several persons had gathered around, but no one recognized the man. The Jenks boys alleged that at about the hour of midnight a team passed the house, drove a few rods west, and there dumped the man out of the wagon or buggy, turned around and drove off in the direction of Deadwood. This statement was confirmed by the wagon tracks.

The reporter went for James Rogers, one of the city council, and about the same time J. F. Summers and Sam Rice arrived, all of whom recognized the man as Ed Dotson. He was immediately removed to the residence of Mrs. Wendels, and soon afterwards removed to the residence of his parents in this city.

Young Dotson has for several years resided in Deadwood and has become sadly dissipated. Thursday he drove over from Deadwood with a single horse and buggy, accompanied by a woman, said to be of unsavory reputation, but as to that the BULLETIN has no knowledge beyond the street talk. His relatives here say he left with the woman in the buggy about midnight, and at that time had between $70 and $80 cash in his pocket. When found, his coat and vest were off and no money or other valuables could be found. Being still unconscious, no particulars of the affair can be obtained from him. The woman, the horse and buggy have not been seen here since Thursday. His symptoms indicate heavy drugging, and in connection with other circumstances point to the probability of drugging and robbery, though there is no positive proof to sustain this theory. He has been drinking freely before leaving here, and the disappearance of the woman, the money, the horse and carriage naturally lead to the conclusion that these four items made their way back to Deadwood together.

title: Pass the baton: Spearfish’s Aidan O’Shea to compete  in elite undergrad conducting competition
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: SPEARFISH — Name 3 art forms. Maybe you think first of visual art: painting or its modern counterpart, graphic design. Maybe you think of music: of playing instruments and composing symphonies. Perhaps your mind shifts to the stage, and you think of dance, of theater.
_full_text:
SPEARFISH — Name 3 art forms. Maybe you think first of visual art: painting or its modern counterpart, graphic design. Maybe you think of music: of playing instruments and composing symphonies. Perhaps your mind shifts to the stage, and you think of dance, of theater.

For 19-year-old conductor Aidan O’Shea, leading an orchestra involves much more than commanding a team of musicians, baton in hand.

“You get the most unique job. You get to do all of the arts, all at once, when you are a conductor,” O’Shea said. “In some ways you are dancing. In some ways you’re singing. In some ways you’re playing an instrument. … Then, as a conductor, you also get to design what you want the stage or lights to look like for your orchestra. You get some visual arts in there. You design the program and how you want the program to flow and what type of literature you want to share, and the overarching story of your concert or show — I like that word better, more fun.”

Conducting, for him, is the ultimate amalgamation of art forms, and he should know. He is one of only 5 finalists, selected from a pool of hundreds of young people from around the country, to compete in the Symphony of the Americas Pass the Baton National Collegiate Conducting Competition in Fort Lauderdale, Fla.

O’Shea grew up in Spearfish. His mother is a vocal instructor and runs O’Shea Vocal Studio.

“I think it runs in my blood,” O’Shea said of his dedication to music.

O’Shea first tried his hand at conducting a youth orchestra under the mentorship of Mary Pochop.

 Aidan 2.JPG
Spearfish’s Aidan O’Shea is one of 5 finalists in the Symphony of the America’s Pass the Baton Competition for conductors. Photo courtesy Concordia College 

“I’ve always loved leading music in rehearsals, whether it was in middle school band or choir or high school,” O’Shea said. “We were very thankful that Mary, I think she has been doing it for over 20 years, she has this string program. I was able to be part of it starting in middle school. In my sophomore year (of high school), she allowed me to conduct a couple of the orchestra pieces at the concert. I started that in my sophomore year, and she let me do it all through my senior year.”

O’Shea’s skills have grown significantly since then. Now, he’s a freshman at Concordia College in Moorhead, Minn. — one of a small few institutions with programs tailored to suit the people hoping to become pedagogues and tack the moniker of “Maestro” in front of their name.

O’Shea has proved to be a standout pupil. After submitting a video to Symphony of the Americas’ undergraduate conducting competition, he was chosen to take part in the inaugural Pass the Baton competition.

Hundreds of conducting students applied. Only 5 were selected.

Pass the Baton allows student-conductors like O’Shea to step onto the podium and lead professional orchestras — a new kind of pressure for young people who may have only thought through arrangements and prepared programs for younger students or their peers.

“With the professionals, you have less time, so you want to make sure you have your gestures well prepared. You also want to treat it more as a collaboration, rather than an education,” O’Shea said. “Often, for high school orchestra or middle school orchestra, whatever it was, you’re teaching the students things. You can act as an educator and a pedagogue, whereas when you’re working with professionals, now you’re collaborating. They have their own vision, their own idea of the music.”

There are 3 layers, 3 core elements of conducting, O’Shea said.

The first is gestures. This is the part audiences recognize. The waving batons. The hair flips. The dramatic motions that convey dynamics and mood to concertgoers and musicians alike.

“You are portraying the music to the players so that they are together, so that there is clarity. The first important thing about that is that there is clarity. Then, I would say, equally as important as clarity is expressiveness. You want to express the music to them in the way that you want them to play the music,” O’Shea said.

Working with the pros, gestures might be especially important.

“You speak a lot less, and your gestures need to be so solid that they communicate everything necessary with the orchestra,” O’Shea said. “That is the difference.”

The second job of a conductor is score analysis — understanding the music.

“That is the time that you spend studying the score, learning the music, learning what the trouble parts are going to be for different players in the orchestra if you are working with a more amateur orchestra. You become very familiar with the form and the overarching story and just all of the details that you need to know about the music before walking into rehearsal,” O’Shea said.

The third part is running rehearsals. Collaborating with other musicians and helping players who may need it through challenging elements of a score.

O’Shea, and the other competitors from the University of Houston, Manhattan School of Music, Alabama A&M, and the University of Washington, will work under the tutelage of Maestro Luke Frazier, the new music and artistic director of Symphony of the Americas. Then, the best among them will be selected to earn a cash prize and the accolade of having conquered Pass the Baton in 2026.

“I’m so thankful and honored. I honestly was not expecting it. As a freshman, I am going up against seniors who are auditioning for their graduate schools and all of that. I was like, there is no way that this will happen,” O’Shea said. 

Today, O’Shea planned to fly out to the competition. He’s prepared music by American composers — including pops pieces from “Our Towns” and “Sway,” which was recorded by Michael Bublé — and is ready to showcase his style and skill. Tuesday is competition day.

Regardless of the outcome, he looks forward to a future conducting and designing programs, digesting music history, preparing to instruct, conduct, sing, dance, and raise his baton in pursuit of delivering live, shimmering performances to classical-music listeners for years to come.

“I think I am probably most looking forward to the concert itself. That is going to be awesome. I am so blessed to have this opportunity. Then, I am also just really looking forward to learning from Maestro Luke as well as just getting connections in the industry,” O’Shea said.

title: SEDC works to bring 15 new businesses to Sturgis in 2025
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: STURGIS — Business formation and support continues to be strong at Sturgis Economic Development Corporation (SEDC), Executive Director Amanda Anglin said Monday.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

STURGIS — Business formation and support continues to be strong at Sturgis Economic Development Corporation (SEDC), Executive Director Amanda Anglin said Monday. 

Anglin gave a year-end report about SEDC activities during the Sturgis City Council meeting. In 2025, she said taxable sales were at $256.4 million, and 15 new businesses opened, including 4 new businesses in the Sturgis Industrial Park. Of those new businesses, she said there were 25 new jobs created, and even more jobs were retained in town. 

“Sturgis continues to be the economic engine of Meade County, driving business growth, sales activity, and investment growth across the region,” Anglin said. 

She added that the new businesses in the industrial park highlight the importance of having ready-to-use buildings and sites available. 

In addition to new businesses, Anglin said last year SEDC worked with 11 active business prospects, and connected with more than 75 businesses through for retention and expansion, project assistance, and resource coordination. The organization also worked with 15 businesses on active expansion projects, 5 of which have been completed, she said. 

One of the successful projects last year, Anglin said, was providing revolving loan funds to support Belle Joli Winery in a 10,000 square foot expansion. 

Throughout 2025, Anglin said SEDC worked on a major project to bring business to a parcel of land the organization owns at Interstate 90, Exit 32, near the Scooptown Car Wash. Unfortunately, she said, that project did not materialize, and SEDC is working with a realtor to advertise the property for sale across the Black Hills and the region. 

Housing and development is also a major focus for SEDC, Anglin said. In 2025, she reported there were 269 building permits issued, and of those 27 were for single family homes, with $9.4 million in residential construction value. Building permits and housing activity have stabilized in Sturgis after declining during the COVID-19 pandemic, she said. However, she said multi-family housing remains limited, and housing and infrastructure limitations continue to be barriers to future economic development.

“Housing continues to be a challenge and an opportunity, not just in Sturgis but across the Black Hills,” she said. “When new houses are built they fill immediately, showing consistent demand. Housing availability remains a constraint to growth, with strong demand and limited new supply, particularly in multi-family units.” 

Anglin reported that SEDC continues to plan through the Sturgis Strategic Housing Trust Fund, and they are waiting for funds to become available to work on more affordable housing projects.  

Anglin said much of the work SEDC does is behind the scenes, with forming business relationships and developing infrastructure. But all of that hard work pays off, with job creation and development, she said. 

“Overall, it directly impacts our jobs, our tax base, and our community vitality,” she said. “So we continue to remain focused on business attraction, entrepreneur support, housing, infrastructure, storytelling, marketing, and community advocacy.”

title: Arrest made ending 2-day manhunt
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: PIEDMONT — A Rapid City man is in custody Friday afternoon following a manhunt that began Thursday.
_full_text:
PIEDMONT — A Rapid City man is in custody Friday afternoon following a manhunt that began Thursday.

According to the Meade County Sheriff’s Office, on Thursday evening, law enforcement officers from multiple agencies were in the area of Meadow Retreat Road and Erickson Ranch Road as they were searching for Deja Guthmiller, 22, who was allegedly wanted for a parole violation. He was also wanted in connection with a burglary and armed robbery in Pennington County.

Then, early this morning, the Meade County Sheriff’s Office received information indicating that Guthmiller may have returned to a family residence located on Meadow Retreat Road in Piedmont.

Officers were able to confirm Guthmiller’s presence inside the residence. Based on this information, deputies obtained a search warrant, and South Dakota Parole Services issued an absconder warrant for his arrest. Due to credible threats previously allegedly made by Guthmiller, combined with a recent history of violence, he was considered a significant risk to public safety.

Given the potential danger to the community and responding personnel, the special response team, comprised of members from the Rapid City Police Department and the Pennington County Sheriff’s Office, was requested to assist.

Through coordinated efforts among multiple law enforcement agencies, Guthmiller was safely taken into custody without further incident. He was transported to the Pennington County Jail. Additional charges are pending.

Meade County Sheriff Pat West emphasized the seriousness of the situation and the importance of collaboration in bringing it to a safe resolution:

“This was a dangerous and rapidly evolving situation involving an individual with a demonstrated history of violence,” West said. “The coordinated response by our partner agencies ensured that this threat was addressed quickly and safely, minimizing risk to our community. We are fortunate to have strong working relationships across jurisdictions that allow us to respond effectively in situations like this.”

West also highlighted the broader team effort involved:

“While this incident required significant resources, other agencies stepped in to assist with unrelated calls for service throughout Meade County. This level of cooperation is critical in rural areas and reflects a shared commitment to public safety across western South Dakota.”

The Meade County Sheriff’s Office extends its appreciation to the following agencies for their assistance:

South Dakota Highway Patrol

Rapid City Police Department

Pennington County Sheriff’s Office

Sturgis Police Department

South Dakota Game, Fish and Parks

South Dakota Parole Services

United States Marshals Service

Meade County 911 Communications

Pennington County 911 Communications

No further information will be released at this time.

All suspects are presumed innocent until proven guilty in a court of law.

title: Sturgis city administrator, city attorney resign
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: STURGIS — Sturgis City Administrator Aaron Jordan’s role was always meant to be temporary, as the retired Army colonel indicated when he took the job at city hall.
_full_text:
Aaron Jordan.jpg

 Eric Davis.jpg

STURGIS — Sturgis City Administrator Aaron Jordan’s role was always meant to be temporary, as the retired Army colonel indicated when he took the job at city hall. 

Jordan and Sturgis City Attorney Eric Davis announced their resignations Thursday morning. Davis’ resignation is effective May 31, while Jordan plans to leave Aug. 23. 

Jordan said he previously spoke with former Mayor Kevin Forrester about leaving the city administrator post at some point this year. Those conversations happened well before Forrester was diagnosed with the cancer that claimed his life on April 5. 

“When I came on as the city administrator, we both knew that my time was a transitional period,” Jordan said. 

Jordan, an amateur historian whose interest is primarily in genealogy said he and his wife plan to travel and research their family history together. Additionally, Jordan said he recently became a grandfather for the first time, and he plans to spend more time with his family. 

Forrester recruited Jordan for the city administrator job in January 2025, at a time when the city of Sturgis was in disarray. From April 2023 to October 2024, the city had suffered 11 resignations of elected officials and key staff leadership positions, Sturgis residents had voted to change from a city manager-led form of government to a strong mayor government with a city administrator, and the city council was struggling with balancing and creating a workable budget. Forrester asked Jordan to apply his experience in leadership management roles to help get the city back on track. 

Under Jordan’s leadership, the city of Sturgis went from operating at a $4 million loss from 2021-2023, to operating with a surplus and improving revenues in 2025. 

“As we look into the future with our comprehensive plan and we identify projects that we would like to do, we are starting to have the resources to make some of that a reality.”

One of Jordan’s priorities when he took the job in February 2025 was to build a resiliency and morale for city staff. On Thursday, he said he is very confident in the team at city hall. 

“There will always be continuous change in any organization, and the organization has to be able to accommodate that,” Jordan said. “We’ve done a good job creating a resilient force that when individuals leave we still have a strong team that continues to move things forward. This team is much greater than the sum of a few individuals. Our department heads have a lot of continuity, experience, and competencies. They care about the people of Sturgis and are committed to serving the city of Sturgis. That whole network is still in place. The things Kevin Forrester and I have wanted to do, we have moved the football and made it better.” 

Jordan said the Sturgis City Council has already started the process to recruit a new city administrator and city attorney. The city will “cast a wide net” to find the right people for the jobs, he said, and Jordan said he is committed to working with the city to ensure the transition is smooth, even if that means he has to stay a little longer than originally planned. Jordan said he believes the city may even be able to recruit a city administrator from South Dakota. 

Davis said if needed, he will provided interim legal services, after he resigns as a full time employee, to ensure a smooth transition. Davis was appointed as city attorney in October 2025, and before that he served the city as a contracted attorney.  He noted that his role was always intended to be transitional. 

“It was always understood by the elected leadership that my service with the city would be temporary and transitional,” Davis said. “I accepted the appointment to help Kevin, Aaron, and the Council during an important transition period for the city. The mayor and council understood for several months that I was intending to conclude my service this summer, and the special mayoral election seemed like the appropriate point for my departure, given the new mayor’s role in selecting key officers such as the city attorney.” 

“Eric brought valuable legal expertise and perspective during an important time for the city,” said Mayor Pro Tempore Tony Dargatz. “His work helped support the mayor, council, and staff in advancing key initiatives and improving overall governance.”

During Davis’ time with the city, he worked to complete a comprehensive recodification project to revise all city ordinances to reflect Sturgis’ new form of government, and to provide updates to ensure city codes aligned with changed state laws. He has also been working with city leaders to re-structure Rally sponsorship contracts, in an effort to provide more government transparency.  

Davis said it has been a pleasure to work with the city of Sturgis. 

“Sturgis has a very capable and committed team of department heads and staff who carry the important work of the city forward every day,” he said. “it has been a real pleasure to work with and support that team in tehri respective roles. I am proud of the work we did together, grateful for the opportunity to have contributed to it, and feel confident matters are being handed over to the next administration in a better position.” 

Davis said after he leaves the city, he will take some time off this summer and then return to private practice. 

Jordan said he is confident that Sturgis city staff will do a good job continuing city services. 

“I think the key thing is that the sum of the team is much greater than any individual part,” Jordan said. “We’ve got a great team. We have a very experienced, committed team. I am confident we will get great people into those positions, and from the outside looking in, people probably won’t see much difference.” 

Dargatz said the city council members are committed to identifying next steps for both positions, with a focus on maintaining stability and continuing the progress that has been made.

“We are confident in our staff and leadership team and remain committed to serving the residents of Sturgis with professionalism and integrity throughout this transition,” Dargatz said.

In 2023-2024 the city of Sturgis suffered a series of key resignations, starting when former city manager Daniel Ainslie resigned in April 2023. Longtime Mayor Mark Carstensen resigned in February 2024, shortly after the state supreme court ruled that voters should have the opportunity to decide the city’s form of government. Councilman Preston Williams resigned in June 2024. In September 2024, longtime city attorney Mark Marshall resigned, and then in October 2024 Mayor Angela Wilkerson, and Councilmen Don Lanpher and Mike Bachand resigned. Before the resignations started, longtime Sturgis Finance Officer Fay Bueno had planned to retire from her post in February 2024. Sturgis Human Resources Director Lisa Katzenstein, and Bueno’s finance officer replacement, Adam Hosch, both resigned in August 2024.
