title: Lawrence Co. commissioners speak out at candidate forum
byline: By Sara Morgan Black Hills Pioneer
description: Incumbent Timm Comer and newcomer Mike Davies, pictured right, spoke at the Lawrence County Commission candidate forum Saturday, where they discussed issues such as conditional use permits, property tax relief, and conflicts of interest. Pioneer photo by Sara Morgan
_full_text:
1 Lawrence County candidate forum.jpg
Incumbent Timm Comer and newcomer Mike Davies, pictured right, spoke at the Lawrence County Commission candidate forum Saturday, where they discussed issues such as conditional use permits, property tax relief, and conflicts of interest. Pioneer photo by Sara Morgan 

SPEARFISH — Lawrence County residents gathered at the Heritage Center in Spearfish, Saturday, to hear from the five candidates for the 3 open, 4-year county commission terms.

Candidates answered questions gathered from constituents, including how to monitor conditional use permits, declarations of conflicts of interest, and property tax relief for owner occupied properties.

Incumbents Timm Comer, Brandon Flanagan, and Eric Jennings were present alongside challengers Mike Davies and Robert Haiwick to answer questions received from constituents. 

Monitoring conditional use permits

Candidates were asked how they think conditional use permits should be monitored in the county, who holds the responsibility for ensuring the conditions of the permits are being followed, and who is responsible for following up on complaints the county receives about the permits. 

Flanagan said the process starts with the public, who should file complaints when they see that conditions of the permits aren’t being met with the director of planning and zoning. 

“The planning and zoning director then does an investigation, they come to the county commission, and then the board does a review,” said Flanagan. “If the conditional use permit is found to be in violation, there’s a couple of options. They’re given a chance for remediation, and if they don’t fix the problem then we can revoke the conditional use permit. That’s exactly the process in a nutshell. I’ve been involved on both sides of that, so I’m probably the best one to have to ask that question.”

Haiwick said that he is not part of any commission and does not know the rules and regulations of conditional use permits. 

Jennings agreed with Flanagan’s response, who he said that he did an excellent job describing the process and he didn’t have anything more to add. 

Comer said that he also had nothing more to add to Flanagan’s response. 

Davies said that laws should be enforced by law enforcement, and conditional use permits should be enforced by the people who issue them. 

“We’re a problem-based commission right now,” said Davies. “Somebody reports a problem and that problem gets investigated. So, we’re reacting to things that come up, we’re not proactive on preventing things that could come up. So, my approach would be, why can’t we have some review of the 500 or so, I understand you can check that number that may not be right, conditional use permits that are out there. How do we know that all these conditional use permits are really complying with their conditions? I know people have come to me with complaints about conditional use permit grantees next to them and complain that there’s nobody to enforce it. Maybe it’s gone through that process, I don’t know, but I would say my instinct is we need some effort to make sure that the existing permits are continuing to operate within the rules that they were given at the beginning. It just doesn’t seem right to me that we have police out there enforcing the laws, but we have nobody essentially out there enforcing the conditional use permits unless there’s a problem that is brought to the attention of planning and zoning.”

 2 Lawrence County candidate forum.jpg
Pictured from left, incumbent Brandon Flanagan, challenger Robert Haiwick, and incumbent Eric Jennings spoke at the Lawrence County commission candidate forum Saturday. Pioneer photo by Sara Morgan 

Jennings added that conditional use permits are annually reviewed. 

“We do an inspection there and make sure that the rules are being followed and there have been no complaints,” Jennings said.

Conflict of interest

Candidates were asked how they would identify a conflict of interest during their service as a county commissioner and if they would recuse themselves from voting if they thought a conflict of interest was present. 

Jennings said that a conflict of interest is clearly defined by state law.

“In the legal sense, a conflict of interest exists when, in this case a commissioner, has the opportunity to vote on an issue that he or she would financially benefit from. When that arises, the commissioner will declare a conflict of interest and recuse him or herself and sit in the gallery until the issue is decided,” Jennings said. “A perceived conflict of interest is a much harder target to hit. Everyone has their own definition of that. In a relatively small county like Lawrence County, if you select a commissioner who has lived here for a lengthy time and knows the county well, he or she may have a non-financial relationship with people bringing business before the county. Lawrence County Commission has stringently followed the legal definition of conflict of interest and will probably never be able to satisfy the perceived conflict of interest concerns. Me, personally, I operate I’m the sole proprietor of a ranch. I’ve not had any conflicts of interest arise in the county because I don’t have that many — my financial interests aren’t that spread out. I don’t think I can come up with an example that I can give you a conflict of interest and I’ve never had to do any business, selling the county anything or anything that I would have benefited from. 

Comer said that identifying conflicts of interest is an important part of every meeting. 

“We have the opportunity to declare if we think there is a conflict of interest and then others determine that based off of state law,” said Comer. “If I get into it where I’m going to matter an issue I didn’t think about, I would immediately declare I think there’s a conflict of interest and recuse myself if needed. I think that’s important. But again, small communities, you know a lot of people, you know a lot of businesses, and it’s a very different situations if we were in bigger communities where you didn’t know a lot of people. But growing up here, working in this area, running around this area, you get to learn and know a lot of people.”

Davies said that his experience working as an executive in the Department of Veterans Affairs, where he served as the head physician for the Fort Meade VA hospital in Sturgis, granted him exceptional experience in the dangers of ignoring conflicts of interest. 

“A conflict of interest occurs when there’s split loyalties,” said Davies. “When a person has loyalty to themselves and their job, their employer, and a loyalty to all of the people in the county, for example. In my past as an executive in the Department of Veteran’s Affairs, we were very careful, very careful about conflicts of interest. We could not have lunch with a vendor unless we paid for it ourselves. We could not take any gift from a vendor at all, and finally they said unless it’s less than $25. I have a former colleague, you can look it up, John Wyndham, who did violate that law, and now he’s facing somewhere between 20 and 25 years in prison for violation of conflict of interest laws on the federal level.”

Davies said that he thinks the county commission has been “loose” with their declarations of conflicts of interest. 

“I think if we’re working for an employer that owns more than 10% of the land in the county, or has privileges on those lands to mine, and we’re working for the people in the county? That’s kind of a built-in problem,” said Davies. “Yeah, there’s a legal definition, but conflict of interest is kind of like pornography. You know it when you see it. There’s a lot of people that, I think, push the edges of that around here. I’m a little concerned about this. In my own situation, I don’t think I have a conflict of interest. I’m retired from the VA, we live out in the country, I don’t do business with the county, I think I could represent you fairly. At the same time, if there was a conflict of interest that did come up, I know what it is, I would declare it and be honest about it.”

Comer spoke up and declared that he will be declaring conflicts of interest when issues regarding Dakota Gold come up with the board of county commissioners, something that he has discussed with his employer. 

Flanagan agreed with what had already been said about declaring conflicts of interest and gave an example of recusing himself at the April 14 board of appeals meeting. 

“We acted as the board of appeals for assessment valuation,” Flanagan said. “My landlord, at the restaurant, had 2 parking lots that I rent from him that came up. He was contesting their evaluation. I saw that immediately when it came up and I said, ‘Hey I need to recuse myself.’ I don’t have any direct financial involvement in that. The only thing I do is pay him rent, and they happened to be lots that are part of a parking arrangement that I have with that owner. But, just to make sure that there was any appearance, I recused myself and we went on with business.”

Haiwick said that as an owner of an automatic transmission repair facility, if the county brought one of the highway patrol vehicles to his shop to be repaired while he was a commissioner, he would ask them to take that vehicle to another shop. 

“I certainly do not want a conflict of interest within my business,” said Haiwick.

Property tax relief

Candidates were asked if they believe Senate Bill 96, which authorizes counties the option to relieve owner-occupied property taxes by imposing a half-percent sales tax. 

“I think it’s an opportunity that is out there, but we haven’t been presented with that yet because we can’t implement it until a certain time frame,” said Comer. “So yeah, I think we’ll be looking at that as the board of county commissioners and make a decision of what kind of impact that will have to the community and the people of Lawrence County. I, right now, would say it’s a positive impact and I would say if it turns out that it is a positive impact, yeah I would be in favor of implementing that. But I want to see the details of that first. I don’t go by hearsay, like to see facts, and I like to make my decisions based on facts. So, I’ll wait for that to make that decision when it comes before the board.”

Davies said that he thinks the opinions of the county residents are important when deciding whether or not to implement the tax. 

“We have to make decisions based on facts, I agree with that,” said Davies. “At the same time, in my travels here over the last couple of months, I’ve heard a lot of concern about property taxes from people. I had one gal who was almost in tears because she got her property tax bill, and she already had 2 jobs and a house she could afford and didn’t expect the rise in property taxes that she faced. It’s a painful thing for a lot of people in our county. We live in the same house, and we had, the valuation goes up, we didn’t do anything different, but all of a sudden we have greater and greater and greater expense.”

Davies said the 2 tax relief bills passed in the legislature this year are the only options that he’s aware of when reducing property taxes.

“My sense is, why wouldn’t we take advantage of this? My understanding is, with the half cent sales tax that the commission could put in, an average family would have to spend somewhere between $60,000 and $100,000 more to pay more in tax than they would in savings from the sales tax,” Davies said. “If we did the sales tax and used it for property sales tax reduction, we would have many of our visitors, by the way tourism is our number one source of economic activity here in Lawrence County, why not ask those visitors to bear some of that burden that would help our residents have an easier life?”

Flanagan said that he’s cautiously optimistic about tax relief options passed by the governor’s office and the legislature. 

“There’s some things that have yet to be seen,” said Flanagan. “There’s some processes that yet to be worked out. We, of course, work very closely with the treasurer’s office and the auditor and the state for guidance and suggestions on how to implement those things. And that’s really the thing, don’t jump headlong into something only to have to undo it because it’s a mess. I don’t think there’s anyone on the board currently or candidate wise that wouldn’t say whatever we can do to reduce property taxes, we need to try it. But I don’t want to make amess and then have to tear down the whole project to start over because it was either poorly implemented or incorrectly implemented or didn’t do what the intent was. With that being said, anybody that owns property in this county would really like to see some property tax relief.”

Haiwick said that he agrees with his opponents. 

“If we can implement the half cent on the sales tax to relieve a lot of the elderly and, of course, even the young people, that’s going to help them purchase a new home possibly if it lowers the taxes,” Haiwick said. “So, if it comes about and I get elected, I would be for it.”

Jennings said that he testified in favor of Senate Bill 96.

“I was in favor of the bill because it gave the individual counties the option of implementing a sales tax for owner occupied property tax relief instead of a one size fits all approach,” Jennings said. “Senate Bill 96 doesn’t become law until July 1, and the county cannot act until then. We are still learning about the mechanics of the bill and what the logistics of implementation would be. I’m also currently listening to as many citizens as I can about whether or not we should implement it. I have not chosen a position yet and I will not do so until I feel I have all the information in front of me. It’s important to remember that a sales tax represents a tax shift. It shifts to the visitors, but it also shifts to the people who do not own homes. We need to think about that aspect of it. The only true method for property tax relief is through the budget and being mindful of the budget and being fiscally responsible is the only way that we’re going to hold property taxes in check.” 

When to vote

Absentee voting is currently on hold while the state struggles to get ballots to auditors. Election day is June 2. To find your polling place and check your voter registration, visit the Secretary of State’s website at vip.sdos.gov.

title: Spearfish gets a new steakhouse,  inspired by the 100 year-old escape  of 6 cloven foot critters
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: SPEARFISH — The newest restaurant to take up residence in the Lown Mercantile Building at 701 N. 5th Street in Spearfish is open for business. Six Goats Tavern is a modern, American ranch-inspired steakhouse owned and operated by experienced restaurateurs Brad Prescott and Chris Panaia.
_full_text:
SPEARFISH — The newest restaurant to take up residence in the Lown Mercantile Building at 701 N. 5th Street in Spearfish is open for business. Six Goats Tavern is a modern, American ranch-inspired steakhouse owned and operated by experienced restaurateurs Brad Prescott and Chris Panaia.

The pair is leaning into history as they launch the newest cuisine on offer in Spearfish’s culinary scene.

“We wanted to bring culinary craftsmanship and kind of a western heritage to an elevated hospitality experience,” Panaia said. “We wanted it to still be community-focused and comfortable. The duty within the community is to acknowledge its history. There was a steakhouse that was here. There is a bit of a revival essence to this.”

Panaia said the plan involved adding some of the menu items the community loved when Steerfish was up and running, alongside new dishes prepared in-house.

“The food is really steak-focused. We really do want to be a steakhouse. There is a lack of great steakhouses. I can name a few, but I think that we can bring something to the table that is a little bit different than what they’re doing here,” Prescott said. “At night, I like to say we are steak and potato focused, whereas during the day we are more ruffage. We are more focused on salads and soups, and quick service. We want to get people in and out and have a good quality meal.”

Prescott has mined the basement of the old mercantile, finding antique cast-iron fixtures to hold up new hard wood shelving inside the tavern and other unique display items for future use. The space, the two hope, will feel classy and cozy, with a spirit that harkens back to a particular moment in the region’s history — the escape of 6 mountain goats from Custer State Park zoo.

Some of those goats eventually made it to Spearfish Canyon, where they founded the local herd.

“100 years ago, during this time period that they escaped,” Panaia said. “We were like, ‘Hey, the great escape!’ It just kind of worked really well with our idea. We wanted to be something for the community first.”

Panaia envisions Six Goats as a community escape and gathering place. 

“Our success comes from people in the community saying this is our place,” Panaia said. “A place that we like to go to, where we seek community, connection, and gather. The tourists will be there, and if we could bring them in as well, with the history and all of that, that is great too.”

The other element of history Panaia and Prescott are leaning into is their own. The pair have worked together for decades, opening dozens of restaurants across the country. After Prescott relocated to the Northern Hills, they set their sights on expanding Spearfish’s culinary offerings with Six Goats Tavern. It won’t be their first steakhouse. Their first steakhouse, in Southern California, was the restaurant that brought Prescott and Panaia together.

“Our busiest days were Sunday and Monday, when all the hospitality industry wanted a night off, and they wanted to experience something comfortable and gathering. They would come to us,” Panaia said. “If we can replicate that into the local community here, it is a win-win.”

Panaia said that their first restaurant took off by word of mouth.

“I think we come from a place where that original steakhouse, we didn’t take reservations. If you didn’t know where we were, you would need to call your friend and ask,” Panaia said. “It was kind of an experience. (Customers) would come and realize that we delivered great food, a great environment, good music, and it was all the elements that brought it together.”

For weeks, a mountain goat silhouette has been up on the windows of the shuttered tavern as Prescott and Panaia prepared the restaurant for its soft opening. Building anticipation and opening with intention were part of the plan.

“I am in the 40s now, as far as the number of restaurants I have opened. You learn what to do and what not to do. You learn to make quick decisions operationally on things that aren’t working,” Prescott said. “Part of it is what we are talking about, the evolution of an opening.

You know, most places you open this day and it’s feast or famine. I don’t think that is the right way to do it. With us in full control of what we are doing and how we are doing it, it helps immensely.”

Prescott said the restaurant will ramp up slowly — starting with a limited menu and small staff before adding seasonal and limited time menu items and eventually expanding to serve brunch on the weekends.

“We hope to build our menu by input, and keep evolving,” Prescott said.

At one point, Prescott said he ran a handful of restaurants simultaneously — including 2 in Hawaii.

“The failures speak louder than the successes. You need to fail before you can succeed, and I’ve had both,” Prescott said. “I’ve had some huge successes and had several restaurants under my belt, and then in 2008, I lost all of those restaurants. You know, I like to say that I learned my operational savvy by being in Hawaii with 2 restaurants, and we were failing.”

Prescott said the most important lesson he has learned is that success in food service hinges on a restaurant’s culture.  

“The restaurant business is a love/hate, right? If you are hospitality-minded, and you like entertaining people, and you like community, and you like to have experiences. I grew up in an era where it wasn’t about technology. It was about customer experience. It was about showing up to places and having real, genuine relationships,” Panaia said. “When you are running restaurants, and you build a team, and you have culture, it is like having a family. If you do it right.”

The pair hope to make Six Goats a great place to work, and by extension a great place to dine. They’re aiming to cultivate a crop of regulars who come into the tavern to enjoy eventual favorites and to discover new flavors. Beef prices are high. Bringing people back over and over will require offering low-cost options alongside $100 steaks. Prescott and Panaia are OK with that.

“I think Six Goats will be shaped by the community,” Panaia said. “We are excited to see how we can connect in a community and bring back that customer service and hospitality community gathering. We want people to come in and know that they are here to have an experience and feel comfortable.”

title: Sturgis FFA celebrates state officer milestones as Cammack named president, Storm elected secretary
byline: By Tim Potts Black Hills Pioneer
description: Jackson Cammack, left, was elected State FFA President and Ellie Storm was elected to serve at State FFA Secretary at the State Convention last week in Brookings.  Photo by Kendra Storm
_full_text:
1. FFA.jpeg
Jackson Cammack, left, was elected State FFA President and Ellie Storm was elected to serve at State FFA Secretary at the State Convention last week in Brookings.  Photo by Kendra Storm 

STURGIS — The Sturgis Future Farmers of America (FFA) chapter reached a milestone last week as 2 of its members were elected to top leadership roles during the South Dakota FFA State Convention in Brookings.  

Jackson Cammack was elected state FFA president, while Ellie Storm earned the position of state FFA secretary, marking a rare achievement for a single chapter to have 2 state officers in the same year.

Cammack called the experience both humbling and unforgettable.  

“Having two state officers from the same chapter is really unheard of,” Cammack said. “To be able to do it with someone that I’ve experienced my entire FFA career with is very special, and it means the world to me. It was an unbelievable experience, one I’ll never forget, and I hope I can serve the organization with pride.”

Cammack was selected from a competitive pool of 19 candidates, following an extensive interview process held two weeks prior at Dakota Wesleyan University. The final announcement came during an emotional moment on stage at the convention.  “To hear my name, it was just unbelievable,” he said. 

As state president, Cammack said his priorities include strengthening relationships across the organization, particularly with alumni and local communities.

“The alumni is where it really hits home,” he said. “It’s a great source of fundraising and connection to the community. I want to continue building those relationships and connecting with students, advisors and chapters across the state.”

Cammack also credited FFA with helping him grow personally. 

“As a freshman, I was very shy and not outgoing at all,” he said. “FFA has really grown my leadership skills and changed my mindset.”

Storm shared similar excitement about the opportunity and the impact of the state convention experience.  

 2.. FFA.jpeg
Jackson Cammack, elected State FFA President, stands with advisor Stran Holben, and Ellie Storm, elected as State FFA Secretary during the state convention last week in Brookings.  Photo by Kendra Storm 

“It’s an amazing time,” Storm said. “You see thousands of blue jackets and realize just how big and impactful FFA is across South Dakota. Every year you think it can’t get any better, and it still does.”

Storm’s journey in FFA is closely tied to her family. Her brother, Ben Storm, previously served as a state FFA Sentinel and played a key role in encouraging her involvement.

“He’s the one who got our family into FFA,” she said. “We’re first-generation FFA members, and he really pushed me to get involved and pursue leadership opportunities.”

As a state officer, Storm said she looks forward to building connections with members statewide and helping bridge relationships between districts.

“I really want to connect with members from all across the state and help people find their passion for FFA and agriculture,” she said. “There’s a place for everyone in FFA, no matter your background.”

Storm added that her time in Sturgis has been foundational to her success. “Sturgis is where the passion started,” she said. “I’ve had the opportunity to work with amazing teammates and advisors who have made a huge impact on my journey.”

Both Cammack and Storm encouraged younger students to get involved early, emphasizing the leadership and personal growth opportunities FFA provides.

“Get into an ag class and take advantage of every opportunity,” Cammack said. “I especially recommend doing the Creed as a freshman, it’s something I wish I would have done.”

Storm echoed that message. 

“FFA will help you become a better leader no matter what you want to do in the future,” she said. “Don’t count yourself out, there’s truly a place for everyone.”

Sturgis FFA advisor Stran Holben praised both students for their leadership and lasting impact on the chapter.  “They’ve been incredible members over the past three years,” Holben said. “Without them, our chapter would not be where it is today. They’ve been outstanding leaders and great ambassadors for FFA, from mentoring younger students to promoting the program throughout the community.”

With their elections, Cammack and Storm will spend the next year representing South Dakota FFA, traveling across the state and beyond while continuing to inspire the next generation of agricultural leaders.

title: What data can,  and can’t tell  us about crime
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: In 2025, Spearfish police officers responded to an average of 30 calls for service a day. They made an average of 5 traffic stops each day as well. Pioneer file photo
_full_text:
Crime stats 1.JPG
In 2025, Spearfish police officers responded to an average of 30 calls for service a day. They made an average of 5 traffic stops each day as well. Pioneer file photo 

SPEARFISH — In 2025, the Spearfish Police Department responded to 11,192 calls for service (an average of 30.6 a day) and conducted 2,018 traffic stops (an average of 5 a day). That means law enforcement officers interacted with citizens more than 13,210. Spearfish, for reference, has about 13,276 citizens. 

Spearfish police issued 1,043 citations, not including verbal or written warnings and not limited to traffic infractions last year. Officers made 319 arrests last year alone, not including incidents that resulted in requests for arrest warrants. 

While the numbers can give us some idea of what those officers did over the last year, they aren’t necessarily a good gauge of how “busy” uniformed cops are in the city. 

“One data point for ‘assault’ can be drastically different from another assault. The time invested for one case with the same data point, is never the same,” said Spearfish Police Chief Collin Smith. 

Data can offer a generalized picture of crime in a community, however. We can infer which types of crime might be most common, and get a feel for the relative safety of a community based on its statistics. 

 Crime Stats 2.JPG
Pioneer file photo 

Instances of crime 

For example, the most common calls at the Spearfish Public Safety Dispatch Center in 2025 were (in order of frequency): traffic stops, emergency medical (EMS calls, accidental 911 calls, calls involving animals, and welfare checks. 

In 2025, the Spearfish Police Department recorded 11 rapes and 18 aggravated assaults. There were no murders or robberies recorded last year in the city limits. 

Those numbers reflect a larger, statewide trend. Violent crime is down, overall. 

There were 64,261 criminal offenses reported in South Dakota last year — a 6. 03% decrease compared to 2024’s data. Homicides, rapes, burglaries juvenile arrests, and drug offenses all decreased in 2025 in South Dakota. At the state level, the most common type of crime is property related — crimes like burglary and arson. Drug crimes and violent crimes are the second and third most common categories, respectively. 

In Spearfish, there were 83 cases of burglary, 202 cases of theft or larceny, and 10 cases of motor vehicle theft reported last year. Spearfish saw 76 drug offenses, and 56 “drug equipment violations.” 

State statistics come with a warning label. 

“However, readers are cautioned that crime rates cited are reliable indications of crime only to the extent that population estimates are accurate, that victims report crimes … and data is submitted to the Division of Criminal Investigation’s Statistical Analysis Center by South Dakota law enforcement agencies,” according to the state’s annual crime data report.

Not all crimes are reported. Some crimes are left out of data, because juvenile suspects are involved, the document clarifies. 

Understanding arrests

Data can also give us an idea of who is being arrested in our communities. According to state data, men are most likely to be arrested for violent crimes (834 men were arrested, compared to 206 women for crimes including rape, murder, robbery, and aggravated assault). There were 750 men were arrested for aggravated assault compared to 200 women; 24 men were arrested for rape compared to 0 women. 

Women were also roughly half as likely to be arrested for property crimes (including burglary, larceny-theft, motor vehicle theft and arson), 50.7%. Half as many women were arrested for drug crimes in 2025 as men. The numbers don’t tell us why that is. 

They do indicate that age correlates to with likelihood to commit, and be arrested for, a criminal offense. 

According to state arrest data, people between the ages of 20 and 40 are most likely to be arrested for almost any crime. 

Out of 1,040 arrests for violent crimes in South Dakota, 317 were between the ages of 20 and 29 — more than any other age group. 293 people between the ages of 30 and 39 were arrested for violent crimes, compared to 160 aged 19 and under, 182 between 40 and 49, and 88 were aged 50+. 

The lion’s share of drug arrests involved suspects between the ages of 20 and 40 as well. 

28% of people arrested for drug or drug equipment violations were in their 20s, 26% were in their 30s, and 17% were in their 40s. That’s compared to just 11%, who were 50 or older, and 7% who were 17 or younger. 

While correlation is not causation, SD Attorney General Marty Jackley said, law enforcement is best armed with clear ad accurate data as they conduct efforts to make communities safer. 

“Accurate and timely crime data is essential to informed decision- making. For law enforcement agencies, reliable data helps identify emerging trends, evaluate strategies, and determine how best to deploy officers, training, and resources,” he said. “For policymakers and community leaders, it provides the foundation needed to craft effective laws, prevention efforts, and public safety initiatives. For the public, access to this information promotes transparency, accountability, and confidence in the criminal justice system.”

title: Teamwork makes the dream work South Dakota technology educators surprise lucky kids with bikes
description: Members of the South Dakota Society for Technology in Education held their second annual spring conference in Deadwood this week, where they built bikes for local children as a team building exercise. Keynote speaker Tony Pannone, chief communication officer for Highmark Credit Union, said that members were separated into teams to build bikes without being told who the bikes were for. Ten lucky children from Lead-Deadwood Elementary were taken out of class on Tuesday and surprised with the bikes. Pioneer photos by Sara Morgan

title: How and when new SD laws will reduce property taxes for homeowners
byline: By Makenzie Huber Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: PIERRE — South Dakota homeowners will see the effects of 2 new property tax reduction laws as early as next year — automatically and without any formal notice in one case, and with notification from their county if it chooses to participate in the other case.
_full_text:
SearchlightLogo

PIERRE — South Dakota homeowners will see the effects of 2 new property tax reduction laws as early as next year — automatically and without any formal notice in one case, and with notification from their county if it chooses to participate in the other case.

Both laws, approved in March, allow higher sales tax revenue to be applied toward the reduction of owner-occupied property taxes. One law uses revenue from a July 2027 restoration of the state sales tax rate from 4.2% to 4.5%, after several years of a temporary reduction. The other uses a new, optional sales tax of up to 0.5% at the county level.

County-level relief timing will vary, state financial officer says

The county sales tax can be implemented by county commissions or a vote of the public. In counties that enact a sales tax, the revenue will be used to reduce the county portion of property taxes on owner-occupied homes. Homeowners will see the reduction as a credit on their property tax bill.

The Governor’s Office estimates the average credit could be $660, although that could vary widely depending on individual circumstances.

The earliest counties can implement the sales tax is January 2027. The earliest they can provide property tax credits is on property tax bills for that same year — usually sent out in January and February.

South Dakota Bureau of Finance and Management Commissioner Jim Terwilliger said counties with larger populations and budgets — such as Pennington, Minnehaha and Lincoln — could credit homeowners’ property tax bills as soon as next year with a “little planning and cash flow management.”

Smaller counties with less dependable sales tax income would likely have to wait until 2028 or a year after the county sales tax is implemented to issue credits based on what is collected over a full year, said Lawrence County Commissioner Eric Jennings.

That means, Jennings said, that homeowners in some counties could pay a “double tax” for a year — a full property tax bill and an increased sales tax — before they see any property tax relief.

“At the end of the month, that makes a difference for people’s budgets,” Jennings said, “whether they think about it or not.”

Statewide property tax relief to come next year

When the statewide sales tax rate increases in July 2027, South Dakota homeowners won’t see a credit on their property tax bill specifically listing how much money they’ve saved on their property taxes. Instead, lawmakers will put the extra sales tax revenue into state funding for public schools, which will reduce the funding schools need from local property taxpayers.

Even though the state sales tax increase doesn’t take effect until July 1, 2027, homeowners should see lower property taxes on their bills arriving in January or February of 2027. That’s because lawmakers are using $56 million in state reserve funds along with the first six months of revenue from the increase in the sales tax rate to fill a property tax reduction fund and kick-start the relief. 

Many homeowners will save $168.30 for every $100,000 of their home’s valuation, according to the state Revenue Department. That’s $547 in savings for a $325,000 home.

Those figures apply to school districts that use the maximum allowable property tax levy on homes. Some school districts set their levy lower because they collect enough in taxes at a lower rate due to higher property values. Taxpayers in those districts will see a smaller, though still “meaningful level of savings,” said Kendra Baucom, of the state Department of Revenue.

In future years, the property tax reduction fund will rely entirely on revenue from the increased portion of the state sales tax. That’s expected to be about $114 million annually at first.

Because the fund is dependent on sales tax collections, it can fluctuate.

“If those funds continue to grow, that means future levies could be reduced even further,” Terwilliger said.

title: Kiwanis serve up support Free breakfasts reach over 600 Meade students in April
byline: By Tim Potts Black Hills Pioneer
description: The Sturgis Kiwanis purchased breakfast for any Sturgis Brown High School student wanting to eat on Friday at the cafeteria.  Nearly 190 students enjoyed, biscuits and gravy, waffles and sausage and much more.  From left are: Mark Chaplin, Kiwanis Treasurer; Richelle Bruch, Kiwanis President; Rhonda Ramsdell, Meade Food Service director; Deb Looby, and Rick Little, Kiwanis Members. Pioneer photo by Tim Potts
_full_text:
Kiwanis serve up support Free breakfasts reach over 600 Meade students in April
The Sturgis Kiwanis purchased breakfast for any Sturgis Brown High School student wanting to eat on Friday at the cafeteria.  Nearly 190 students enjoyed, biscuits and gravy, waffles and sausage and much more.  From left are: Mark Chaplin, Kiwanis Treasurer; Richelle Bruch, Kiwanis President; Rhonda Ramsdell, Meade Food Service director; Deb Looby, and Rick Little, Kiwanis Members. Pioneer photo by Tim Potts 

STURGIS — The Sturgis Golden K Kiwanis continued their commitment to local youth in April, providing free breakfasts to students across the Meade School District, serving a total of 616 students at the high school, middle school and elementary levels.

At Sturgis Brown High School Friday, 186 students were treated to a hearty breakfast in the cafeteria, with menu options that included biscuits and gravy, waffles and sausage, and grab-and-go bagels. The effort is part of a twice-a-year program funded by the Kiwanis, which this spring came at a cost of $765.83.

Rhonda Ramsdell, Meade Food Service director, said the partnership with Kiwanis is invaluable.

“The Kiwanis are such good partners to us and such good friends to us,” Ramsdell said. “They provide breakfast twice a year for all the Sturgis schools, so every student has the opportunity to have a free breakfast. They come in here and they’re just so fun to work with. We could not do this without them.”

Richelle Bruch, president of the Sturgis Golden K Kiwanis, said the program reflects the organization’s core mission.

“The overall mission for Sturgis Golden K Kiwanis is to improve the lives of one child, which improves the lives of the community,” Bruch said. “By providing breakfast in the mornings, we’re giving kids a stable, nourishing start to the day. Hopefully that helps them get through the day and have a better attitude.”

The breakfast program has grown over the past several years. According to Kiwanis Treasurer Mark Chaplin, this is the third year the organization has expanded the effort to serve all three schools twice annually.

“We started doing it once a year in the spring,” Chaplin said. “Then we realized the need was there to do it both in the fall and spring. For the past 3 years, we’ve been able to provide breakfast to all the schools.”

In addition to serving meals, Kiwanis leaders hope the program inspires more community members to get involved.

“All someone has to do is show up on a Thursday morning at the Meade County Senior Center, have some breakfast, fill out an application, and become part of our mission at the Kiwanis,” Bruch said.

Bruch emphasized the long-term impact of investing in local youth.

“The future depends on them,” she said. “If we can give them a great foundation, they will do amazing things.”

title: SD TeamMates chapters awarded  $43K from collaborative bank fund
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: TeamMates Mentoring chapters in South Dakota have been awarded a total of $43,750 through the Federal Home Loan Bank of Des Moines’ Member Impact Fund, in partnership with First National Bank (Sioux Falls), First National Bank (Pierre) and Dakotaland Federal Credit Union (Huron). Photo courtesy Jess Karim, TeamMates of South Dakota
_full_text:
SD TeamMates chapters awarded  $43K from collaborative bank fund
TeamMates Mentoring chapters in South Dakota have been awarded a total of $43,750 through the Federal Home Loan Bank of Des Moines’ Member Impact Fund, in partnership with First National Bank (Sioux Falls), First National Bank (Pierre) and Dakotaland Federal Credit Union (Huron). Photo courtesy Jess Karim, TeamMates of South Dakota 

SPEARFISH — South Dakota TeamMates mentorship chapters were awarded $43,750 from First National Bank in Sioux Falls, First National Bank in Pierre, Dakotaland Federal Credit Union in Huron, and the Federal Home Loan Bank of Des Moines (FHLB) in Des Moines.

“We’re grateful for the generosity of our local banks and FHLB Des Moines for their support in making an impact on students in South Dakota through mentoring,” said Jess Karim, TeamMates of South Dakota state director.

TeamMates Mentoring takes place in Belle Fourche, Custer, Hermosa, Huron, Lead-Deadwood, Pierre - Ft Pierre, Sioux Falls, Spearfish, and Rapid City. The grant dollars will help support TeamMates communities with a shared footprint so that they can serve more students. Students matched with TeamMates mentors show an increase in grades, improved attendance, and fewer office referrals.

FHLB Des Moines provides funding solutions to more than 1,200 members to support mortgage lending, economic development, and affordable housing in the communities they serve. The Member Impact Fund provides FHLB Des Moines members with matching grant donations to strengthen the ability of non-profits or government entities to support the needs of their communities. That’s where these funds come from.

The Member Impact Fund is designed to help our members champion organizations like TeamMates Mentoring that make a real difference in their communities,” said Kris Williams, president and CEO of FHLB Des Moines. “By partnering with our members, we’re able to multiply resources and support their vital work as they continue to strengthen opportunities for the people they serve.”

Recipient organizations were selected based on the need for grant funding to support capacity-building or working capital necessary to strengthen their ability to serve affordable housing or community development needs, including job training, affordable housing, financial literacy, food banks, and youth programs.

title: SURF continues long record of clean water discharge
byline: By Mike Ray Sanford Underground Research Facility
description: Stephen Kenny caught this trout in Whitewood Creek. Today, trout are common in this stream. Photo by Stephen Kenny
_full_text:
SURF continues long record of clean water discharge
Stephen Kenny caught this trout in Whitewood Creek. Today, trout are common in this stream. Photo by Stephen Kenny 

LEAD  — The Sanford Underground Research Facility’s (SURF) release of treated process water improves habitat for downstream aquatic life, according to a report that monitors the health of the aquatic ecosystem in the creek that is downstream from the water discharge system. The report is the latest in 17 years of annual independent studies. 

“The annual aquatic biomonitoring results have consistently shown that Gold Run Creek, below our water treatment plant discharge, consistently supports more robust macroinvertebrate and periphyton communities,” said Bonita Goode, SURF environmental manager. “The increased presence of these small aquatic animals and submerged plants shows that SURF’s discharge is improving the biology of Gold Run Creek.”

SURF occupies the site which was once the deepest gold mine in North America. Mining does not take place on the site today; rather, SURF’s mission is dedicated to the advancement of world-class science, and education.

The vast network of tunnels that make up America’s Underground Lab continually fill with water that percolates in from the surface and underground sources. To keep the underground lab space dry, SURF continually pumps water from the depths. This water contains concentrations of iron and other naturally occurring contaminants that must be removed before it is released into Gold Run Creek.

SURF operates an onsite water treatment plant to clean the water pumped from the underground in the dewatering process. Treatment plant operators at SURF are on hand 365 days a year to complete daily water testing for contaminants like ammonia and total suspended solids. SURF also treats water from the legacy Grizzly Gulch tailings impoundment, which was operated by the former mining company. SURF treats and discharges 500 to1500 gallons of water per minute, which includes a mix of the water from underground and tailings impoundment water.

“Our water treatment plant has treated and discharged more than 10 billion gallons of water in the last 17 years, with zero Clean Water Act permit violations. This is a testament to the skill and care of the treatment plant operators,” Goode said.

One of SURF’s core values is protection of the environment, and the facility is committed to preserving water quality in the Black Hills in the decades to come. Process water treatment and monitoring will remain essential at SURF for as long as the facility operates and continues dewatering the underground laboratory space.

title: Meade Co. Sheriff to enter  memorandum with Meade School District
byline: By Brandon Woltkamp Black Hills Pioneer
description: STURGIS — The Meade County Sheriff’s Office provided a memorandum of understanding (MOU) with the Meade School District to provide a school resource officer (SRO) for Piedmont Valley Elementary and Stagebarn Middle School for the 2026-27 school year.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

STURGIS — The Meade County Sheriff’s Office provided a memorandum of understanding (MOU) with the Meade School District to provide a school resource officer (SRO) for Piedmont Valley Elementary and Stagebarn Middle School for the 2026-27 school year. 

This was after the Meade School Board voted to end the SRO position with the Summerset Police Department in March.  That officer served at both schools. The cost increase for the MOU will be $5,000 — an increase from the current contract amount of $36,294. 

It is a net decrease overall to the general fund said Meade Schools Superintendent Wayne Wormstadt.

title: New season of SDSU Extension Garden Hour starts May 5
description: BROOKINGS — The latest season of Garden Hour, South Dakota State University Extension’s popular webinar series for gardeners, will start on May 5.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

BROOKINGS — The latest season of Garden Hour, South Dakota State University Extension’s popular webinar series for gardeners, will start on May 5. 

Led by the SDSU Extension horticulture team, the webinar features short, topical presentations every week during the growing season and every other month during the off-season. 

Gardening enthusiasts of all skill levels are encouraged to attend. Master Gardeners can also receive continuing education credit for attending the live sessions. 

The regular season series starts at 6 p.m. every Tuesday, from May 5, through Aug. 25. Is it free to join, but registration is required to receive the Zoom link. To register, visit extension.sdstate.edu/events and search “garden”. One signup will register participants for the entire series. Confirmation Zoom links and reminders will be emailed. 

Amanda Bachmann, SDSU Extension urban entomology field specialist, will lead the first webinar of the series, accompanied by SDSU graduate student Kristina Harms to talk about growing sweet corn. 

The second week will feature Nick Volesky, SDSU Extension horticulture and specialty crops field specialist, and Prairey Walkling, SDSU Extension Master Gardener field specialist, will talk about growing raspberries in South Dakota.

Bachmann said Garden Hour provides aspiring and experienced gardeners with access to experts who can share new information and answer region-specific questions about growing conditions. 

“This is our sixth season, and I can’t wait to hear what questions South Dakota gardeners have for us,” Bachmann said. “I’m looking forward to visiting with our new and returning experts to chat about what’s growing this year.”

For those who can’t attend live, recordings will be published on the SDSU Extension YouTube channel. People can also submit questions any time via the Ask Extension widget or by contacting the Garden Hotline nearest them via https://extension.sdstate.edu/garden-yard/problems-and-solutions.

To receive regular updates and the latest resources on gardening and landscaping, South Dakotans are also encouraged to subscribe to SDSU Extension’s bi-weekly Garden and Yard newsletter at https://extension.sdstate.edu/about/newsletters. South Dakotans are also welcome to visit the SDSU Extension Garden and Yard Problems and Solutions page for additional resources and contact information.

title: Some local governments waiting to spend $9.6M in opioid dollars
byline: By Molly Wetsch South Dakota News Watch
description: South Dakota counties and cities that received the first $9.6 million of national opioid settlement money have spent less than half of it, according to Department of Social Services spending reports through the end of 2025.
_full_text:
SDNewsWatchLogo

South Dakota counties and cities that received the first $9.6 million of national opioid settlement money have spent less than half of it, according to Department of Social Services spending reports through the end of 2025.

While 41 South Dakotans died from opioid overdoses last year – up from 39 in 2024 – some officials from those 53 counties and 13 cities said they haven’t yet made plans on how to use the distributions, which began in late 2022. 

In 2021, a combined nearly $50 billion in settlements from opioid manufacturers, distributors and pharmaceutical companies began distributing funds to all 50 states, as part of the national settlement agreement.

South Dakota was allocated just under $99 million to be distributed through 2038. Funding to each state was determined based on a formula including the number of overdose deaths, number of opioids shipped to the state and number of people with opioid use disorder in the state.

The vast majority of South Dakota’s settlement funds will come from the distributor settlement, which includes major drug distributors McKesson, Cardinal Health and Amerisource Bergen.

Of South Dakota’s funding, 70% is directed to the state through the Department of Social Services. The other 30%, or around $29 million, will be divided among local governments in the state: the 53 counties and 13 towns and cities that signed a memorandum of agreement to spend those dollars on specific approved uses.

That relatively slow spending at the local level contrasts sharply with the state.

In December, some lawmakers expressed concerns that the state was not spending its funding quickly enough. In April, DSS secretary Matt Althoff and Gov. Larry Rhoden announced $7.82 million in grants, leaving the statewide share of unspent disbursements at $2.9 million of its total $23 million.

DSS told News Watch that the department does not dictate how or when local governments choose to spend dollars.

“The Department of Social Services (DSS) does not have oversight or regulatory authority over how local governments spend their share of the funds. Use of the local share is governed by the South Dakota Opioid Settlement Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) which all participating local governments joined in 2022,” Althoff said in a statement. 

While some local governments have spent large portions of, or nearly all of their shares, the general trend is still of slow, metered spending.

Eleven counties had not yet reported any spending to DSS by the end of 2025, and several others had spent just fractions of their settlements. 

Potter County auditor Tye Vander Vorst told News Watch that the county hasn’t begun allocating dollars because it is not yet clear how much the county will receive in a given year and where the best place to spend the money is. The north-central county, which has a population of 2,400, has received nearly $18,000 since 2022.

Vander Vorst said that the county hopes to make more concrete plans when more funding comes in and opens up spending possibilities, he said.

Face It Together, a nonprofit organization that provides addiction recovery services, was one of 10 recipients of the $7.82 million in statewide opioid settlement fund grants earlier this month.

The organization will use $750,000 over the next 3 years to administer individual peer-to-peer addiction programming at the state penitentiary in Sioux Falls.

Peer-to-peer programming connects those struggling with addiction with coaches who have been through similar experiences, allowing for deeper relationships and problem-solving.

CEO Megan Colwell said that while Face It Together has primarily focused on alcohol abuse rehabilitation, the organization is now seeing a nearly equal number of alcohol addiction and opioid addiction cases.

It is based in Sioux Falls but does work across the state and out of a satellite office in Colorado Springs, Colo.

The need for help is great in rural areas as well as in the state’s two urban centers of Rapid City and Sioux Falls, Colwell said. Face It Together currently provides some remote peer-to-peer coaching sessions with people outside of the Sioux Falls metro area.

Colwell cited remote coaching as a potential solution for counties that want to connect residents in need with support.

In low-population and large geographical area counties, there can often be a lack of understanding about where addiction resources are needed, and the visibility of programs that do exist can be low, Colwell said. That’s why it’s critical that counties are intentional with their spending programs and choose to put dollars in the right places.

“Some of these counties don’t have resources, even with the localized funding,” Colwell said. “You want it to make a difference. This is the one time we have funding that is specifically for this issue.”

Karen Scott and Ken Shatzkes, president and program director of the national Foundation for Opioid Response Efforts (FORE), told News Watch that the low spending trend in local governments is not unique to South Dakota.

Scott told News Watch that the organization has seen local governments nationwide have issues with effective spending programs – especially when the funding amounts are low and issues with substance abuse might not be as prevalent.

Prioritizing spending even when issues are not visible can help prevent future problems in a community, she said.

Much of South Dakota’s local government spending has been directed to law enforcement, correctional and emergency services provided by the counties themselves.

That type of spending is a natural pathway for counties that may not already have existing resource networks. And it provides a logical outlet for the dollars, which have specific spending guidelines set by the state’s memorandum of agreement.

Shatzkes said that while the spending can be an effective place for dollars to go, he also recommends that local government officials employ those with knowledge about substance abuse in the area.

Scott said that especially in rural areas, collaborating with neighboring counties or municipalities could help solve many concerns about effective spending.

For smaller counties receiving lower settlement amounts, drawing knowledge and resources from statewide networks like Face It Together and the charity Emily’s Hope, which provides naloxone boxes and substance abuse support across the state, could make all the difference, Colwell said.

“What’s out there? What can be brought in? What’s an easy lift? What’s low hanging fruit?” she asked.

title: AG, DCI Rule March 31 Sioux Falls officer involved shooting justified
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: Richard Olson Jr. is pictured here allegedly with a handgun. The state Division of Criminal Investigation ruled Sioux Falls Police officers were justified in firing their weapons at Olson on March 31. Courtesy photo
_full_text:
AG, DCI Rule March 31 Sioux Falls officer involved shooting justified
Richard Olson Jr. is pictured here allegedly with a handgun. The state Division of Criminal Investigation ruled Sioux Falls Police officers were justified in firing their weapons at Olson on March 31. Courtesy photo 

PIERRE — South Dakota Attorney General Marty Jackley announces that a South Dakota Division of Criminal Investigation (DCI) review has determined that two Sioux Falls Police Department Violent Crimes Unit (VCU) detectives were justified in their use of force during an officer involved shooting that occurred March 31 in Sioux Falls.

“This suspect pointed a weapon at the detectives and threatened to harm others at the scene,” said Jackley. “Based on the evidence collected in the investigation, the 2 detectives were justified in their use of force. This case demonstrates the importance of K-9 units keeping communities safe across South Dakota.”

The March 31 incident began when members of the VCU located Richard Olson Jr., 36 of Sioux Falls, in a vehicle at a Sioux Falls business. The unit had previously received information that Olson Jr. was allegedly in possession of a firearm while on federal probation. There was an active federal warrant for Olson Jr.’s arrest for a federal probation violation.

VCU detectives used their vehicles to block Olson Jr.’s vehicle from leaving the scene. Olson Jr. was seen in the vehicle pointing a pistol at his head. A female passenger left the suspect’s vehicle and was removed from the scene by law enforcement.

Olson Jr. later exited the vehicle and refused to comply with the detectives’ commands. He allegedly pulled a pistol from his sweatshirt and pointed it at the detectives. 

Two detectives fired at Olson Jr. He was not struck but fell backward to the ground. The suspect then tried to run from the scene, but a Sioux Falls Police Department K-9 was deployed and apprehended Olson Jr. 

Olson Jr. sustained minor injuries from the dog bite and was transported to a local hospital for treatment. He was later taken into custody. The two detectives were not injured.  

DCI processed the crime scene, conducted a forensic examination of evidence, interviewed those involved, and reviewed all available video from traffic surveillance cameras, in-car cameras and body worn cameras. The Smith and Wesson .40-caliber pistol, which was dropped by Olson Jr., was recovered at the scene.

Olson’s blood and urine samples tested positive for amphetamine and methamphetamine. Blood and urine samples of the detectives tested negative for alcohol or illegal drugs.

When Olson Jr. was arrested, he was in possession of a white powdery substance. The substance tested positive for methamphetamine.

Olson Jr. had a history of criminal offenses including assault against law enforcement, aggravated assault without a weapon, domestic assault, and possession of controlled substances. 

Law enforcement agencies that assisted DCI were the South Dakota Forensic Lab, South Dakota State Health Lab, the South Dakota Highway Patrol, and the Minnehaha County Sheriff’s Office. The Sioux Falls Police Department cooperated with the investigation.

Jackley said Olson Jr. is in custody, and charges are pending with the Minnehaha County State’s Attorney’s Office. 

This is the second officer involved shooting in South Dakota this year,

title: Spearfish weighs approval of TIF district for apartment complex at Elkhorn Ridge
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: SPEARFISH — On Monday, the Spearfish City Council is slated to vote on the formation of a new Tax Increment Finance District (TIF) near Peaceful Pines Senior Living, south of Interstate 90 on St. Onge Road.
_full_text:
SPEARFISH — On Monday, the Spearfish City Council is slated to vote on the formation of a new Tax Increment Finance District (TIF) near Peaceful Pines Senior Living, south of Interstate 90 on St. Onge Road.

The TIF, TIF 10, would help to finance the construction of a 98-unit apartment complex — “The Pines” at Elkhorn Ridge.

Tax increment financing is a public financing system that uses future increases in property taxes to reimburse the costs of public improvements built within a designated TIF boundary. The value of all the properties inside the district is assessed or calculated, and the total amount of property tax generated by all those properties is noted — the base amount of property tax revenues. All property revenue above this base amount, for the life of the TIF, is captured by the TIF to refund the costs of the public improvements or project. This amount — the amount over the base — is called the “tax increment.” Over time, property values are presumed to increase in part due to infrastructure improvements brought about by the TIF project itself. When the TIF is retired, the total taxation values revert to the original taxing entities.

Spearfish Community Development Director Peter Wysocki said the rationale behind establishing a TIF district for this project involves a general need for housing units —particularly housing units that meet the needs of the community’s “missing middle.” Most TIFs also meet what Wysocki called the “but for test.” But for the existence of the TIF, could the developer move the project forward?

Wysocki said both the planning commission and the city’s tax increment financing task force — which includes the planning commission, the mayor, city staff, and members of Spearfish Economic Development Corporation (SEDC) felt that this project met those requirements.

The problem of “affordability”

According to a 2022 housing study, about 45% of Spearfish residents qualify as housing cost-burdened. That means 45% of households spend more than 30% of their income on housing.

Councilman Dean Myers said that folks who can afford to buy a home might be spending as little as 22% of their income on housing. Considering the housing cost burden, he asked, “Is this affordable? Are these (units) affordable?”

“They are intended to be more affordable than market rate. Is it enough? That is a policy question on you all,” Wysocki told Myers and the rest of the council. “Is this a priority to the city?”

There are different metrics for determining “affordable housing.” The Federal Housing and Urban Development department (HUD) tends to set income eligibility requirements for individuals who qualify to rent affordable units.

The state of South Dakota uses a different metric. The state determines Area Median Income (AMI). Then, projects that offer “affordable” housing have to offer rent rates capped at a certain percentage of AMI.

In the case of The Pines, rent will cap at 80% of AMI for 10 years. That’s 5 years longer than the state requires.  

80% of AMI puts pricing for a studio at no higher than $1,431, one-bedroom units at $1,635, and 2-bedroom units at $1,840.

In the plan submitted by the proposed TIF developer to the city, The Pines anticipates their rent rates to be slightly lower than the state’s designated cap. For its 23 studio units, the Pines agrees to charge $1,100 per month. The 33 one-bedroom units will rent at a rate of $1,300 or $1,400 for “one bed extra large units,” of which there are 6. The complex’s 30 2-bedroom units will be set at $1,600, and 2-bedroom corner units (there will be 6) at $1,650.

Spearfish resident Jeremy Smith asked for some clarification on the numbers, noting that Spearfish’s median income is lower than 80% of AMI.

“Yeah, (AMI) is $102,000. Area median income is calculated differently than household income because it considers the number of persons in a household,” Wysocki said. “HUD defines area median income based on a 4-person household.” 

The median income in Spearfish, according to the federal census bureau estimates, is $65,688.

Meeting a need for housing

Wysocki noted that the housing that may be built in TIF 10 might be more appropriately classified as “attainable,” meeting the needs of the “missing middle” — residents who are not low income, but cannot afford premium prices.

“In 2022, there was a housing needs assessment completed for Rapid City and the Black Hills Area,” Wysocki said. The study concluded that the need for apartments, not single-family units or ownership units, but rental units, based on our projected population growth, would be anywhere between 224-280 units.”

Wysocki said it is likely that the number underestimates the community’s needs for rental housing.  

“In fact, the projected population for the city of Spearfish in that study is less than the estimated population from the Census Bureau for 2024. We have been growing at a slightly higher rate than projected,” Wysocki said.

However, the construction of The Pines could bring Spearfish close to meeting that projected need.

“With the recent approval of the TIF 9 apartment complex and the Hill Street Apartments currently under construction, combined with this proposed project, we feel that we will be able to meet that demand earlier than 2030,” Wysocki said. “That is not to say that the demand will not continue to increase.”

Eligible expenses

If approved, TIF 10 will make $3,893,000 of the developer’s expenses as they construct The Pines reimbursable. The developer is requesting $2,051,432 of that.

 “Capital costs associated with site improvements, acquisition of land, and site improvement: all of that qualifies as an eligible cost. The statute does prohibit the use of TIF for the actual vertical construction of residential buildings,” Wysocki said. 

If approved, public improvements like a new street, street lighting, sanitary sewer and water infrastructure, and storm water drainage facilities will need to be built in TIF 10.

“The total of non-eligible costs for this project is about $9 million, so certainly the TIF is used for a small portion of that,” Wysocki said.

Spearfish Resident Doug Markworth asked how much the TIF might cost the city over its projected lifetime, if approved.

“The city’s portion of that over 20 years is something along the lines of $250,000-275,000 over the life. It is not that significant. Remember, the TIF is the total mill levy. It’s not abatement. I would see it as an investment,” Wysocki said.

The city has 5 active TIF developments: Creekside (TIF 4), Sky Ridge (TIF 6), Hollow Gulch Apartments (TIF 7), Sawmill (TIF 8), and Stone Ridge Commons (TIF 9).

title: Here’s how much South Dakotans could save on property taxes after accounting for higher sales taxes
byline: By Joshua Haiar Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: PIERRE — Estimates of homeowner savings abounded recently as South Dakota lawmakers and Gov. Larry Rhoden approved property tax reduction legislation.
_full_text:
SearchlightLogo

PIERRE — Estimates of homeowner savings abounded recently as South Dakota lawmakers and Gov. Larry Rhoden approved property tax reduction legislation.

It’s been difficult, however, to find two other estimates: 1) the extra money consumers will spend to fund reduced property taxes with higher sales taxes, and 2) the net savings for homeowners after their extra sales tax spending is subtracted from their property tax savings.

South Dakota Searchlight’s effort to answer those questions led to these estimates: The average South Dakota homeowner’s total savings if they receive both forms of property tax relief could be $1,080 annually. Meanwhile, the average South Dakota household could spend $360 more per year if subjected to both sales tax increases. When it’s all said and done, that’s a net yearly savings of about $720 for homeowners.

To learn how Searchlight arrived at those rough estimates, keep reading. But first, a bit about the new laws.

The new laws

One of the new laws allows the statewide sales tax rate to return to 4.5% next year, after a temporary reduction to 4.2% since 2023. The revenue from the increase will be allocated to the school funding formula to reduce the amount of property taxes schools need from local homeowners.

The other new law allows counties to impose their first-ever sales tax at a rate of up to 0.5%. That revenue will go toward credits to reduce the county’s portion of homeowner property taxes.

Estimating property tax savings

To estimate average property tax savings for homeowners, Searchlight asked the state Department of Revenue for the average assessed value of owner-occupied homes in the state. The department did not provide that figure. 

But it did provide the total taxable value of all owner-occupied properties for taxes payable this year: $62,211,360,002. 

The department also provided the total number of owner-occupied properties in the state: 253,263.

Dividing the total taxable value by the number of owner-occupied properties yields an average value of $245,639.

 “However,” the department said, “this number may include both houses and additional structures such as unattached garages.” The department added that the impact of those additional structures on the average valuation is minimal.

The owner-occupied classification, which lowers the levy applied to an owner’s primary residence, can be applied to a single-family dwelling, an attached or unattached garage, and the parcel of land where a home stands. The new property tax reduction law applies specifically to single-family dwellings. 

To account for the minimal impact from additional structures, Searchlight rounded up to $250,000 as the average taxable value of homes in the state. 

Revenue from the increase in the statewide sales tax rate is expected to reduce property taxes by $1.683 for each $1,000 of a home’s taxable value, according to the state Bureau of Finance and Management. For the average home with a taxable value of $250,000, that’s about $420 of savings.

Homeowners’ savings if their county enacts a 0.5% sales tax to fund property tax credits will vary across the state, because counties have different property tax rates and varying levels of potential sales tax revenue. But the Governor’s Office has estimated that the average savings will be $660. The office arrived at that number by taking the total, estimated new revenue generated if every county implemented the plan, and dividing it by the number of owner-occupied properties, which should approximate the average savings per homeowner. 

Thus, the total annual property tax savings for the average homeowner receiving both forms of relief would be $420 plus $660, which adds up to $1,080.

Estimating extra sales tax spending

To arrive at an estimated extra amount of spending for the average South Dakota household (meaning a house or apartment) on higher sales taxes, Searchlight first needed an estimate of the average household’s annual sales-taxable spending. 

Searchlight spoke with the Dakota Institute, a nonprofit economic research and analysis organization in Sioux Falls. The institute suggested dividing the total of certain categories of taxable sales (excluding categories that are likely purchases by businesses) by the state’s 382,302 occupied housing units (including apartments), resulting in an estimate of about $82,000 in annual taxable spending per household. However, institute CEO Jared McEntaffer noted many of those purchases were still probably made by businesses and tourists, so the true average is lower. 

Gov. Larry Rhoden’s finance commissioner pointed Searchlight to a U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics report stating that the average U.S. household spent $77,280 in 2023. Sales tax does not apply to some expenses, such as prescription drugs or mortgage payments. After subtracting such categories of spending that are unlikely to be taxed and adjusting for inflation since 2023, Searchlight settled on $45,000 as the estimated average annual sales-taxable spending per South Dakota household.

If that average household is subjected to both of the new sales tax increases, it would be the equivalent of an additional 0.8% tax. On $45,000 of spending, that would be $360 of extra sales taxes annually.

In a household subjected to only the 0.3-percentage-point statewide sales tax increase (without a county sales tax), that would be $135 of extra sales taxes annually.

Estimating net savings, and complications

If the average homeowner saves $1,080 annually on property taxes from both forms of relief and spends $360 annually in extra sales taxes, that homeowner’s annual net savings would be $720. 

Homeowners in counties that do not enact a sales tax for property tax relief would receive, on average, the $420 in property tax relief from the statewide sales increase and spend $135 on higher sales taxes, for a net savings of $285.

Those are rough estimates. Actual situations will vary widely across the state. Household spending varies by income. Homes in rural areas are typically valued lower than in urban areas. Counties have different property tax rates, called levies. Some counties may choose to enact a sales tax for property tax relief, and others may not. In counties that do adopt a sales tax, the amount of revenue available for property tax relief will vary. And people who live in counties that do not adopt a sales tax will likely travel and spend money in counties that do.

And, for households that rent rather than own their home, it’s all just a sales tax increase.

title: Developer disputes Meade County discretionary tax changes
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: STURGIS — Changes in the Meade County discretionary formula caused at least one business owner to cry foul, Tuesday.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

STURGIS — Changes in the Meade County discretionary formula caused at least one business owner to cry foul, Tuesday. 

Previously, the Meade County discretionary tax formula did not require developers who built new commercial buildings worth at least $30,000 to pay property taxes for the first 5 years. But in August, Meade County Commissioners scaled that back, to require developers to pay 20% of property taxes the first year, 40% the second year, 60% the third year, 80% the fourth year, and 100% the fifth year. 

On Tuesday, Sam Crittenden told the county commission that the 0% for 5 years discretionary tax formula was a deciding factor, when he decided to build his shop in Summerset in 2024. But the shop was not completed until 2025, with a certificate of occupancy dated for Aug. 29, 2025. When it was assessed on Nov. 1, county assessors used the newly adopted discretionary tax law. 

“I would like for you guys not to reconsider the change, but to consider how unfair it is that the effective date catches me and probably a couple of other people in this middle ground, where we’ve invested our money and we used that discretionary formula as incentive to come to Meade County,” he said. “Now we are paying more taxes than we were basically promised. We came to Meade County because it was promised. It was advertised. We spent all our money to complete it by the time you changed it.” 

Meade County Director of Equalization Rhea Crane said there were 37 new properties added under the new discretionary formula, with taxes assessed in 2026 and payable in 2027. 

Crittenden used his certificate of occupancy as proof that his building was erected before the new discretionary tax went into effect in September. However, Meade County Director of Equalization Rhea Crane told the commission that a certificate of occupancy is not something assessors consider. Nov. 1 is the “magic date” for assessments, Crane said, and all new properties are assessed according to the laws that exist as of that date. 

“Much like when a house is completed Feb. 2, we don’t pick it up Feb. 2 and assess it,” Crane said. “Nov. 1 is the magic date. The rules and laws that are in effect Nov. 1 are what we are required to use.” 

Chief Deputy States Attorney Ken Chleborad said the tax change is required by law, to apply to all property owners. 

“So even if we wanted to bring him back to all zeroes, there is nothing statutorily in place for us to do that,” Commission Chairman Gary Deering said. 

Commissioner Doreen Creed was sympathetic to Crittenden’s situation. 

“I had assumed when we passed that law, anything that had already been completed by the date we passed the ordinance would have been included in the existing ordinance,” she said. “I believe this does pose a problem because the county, via its agent SEDC (Sturgis Economic Development Corporation), did always advertise the 0% discretionary formula, which was in effect when you got your building permit.” 

But Commissioner Talbot Wieczorek said tax laws change all the time, and scaling back the discretionary formula was the right thing to do for all taxpayers. 

“Nobody likes it when the laws change and you’re caught,” he said. “They can change the tax rates today and I’d owe more taxes next year. It’s just the way it sometimes happens. I don’t see SEDC as our agent. Not even close. I am more than vocal every year about the 20, 40, 60, 80, because all zeroes, all the other taxpayers in the county pay the difference. We shouldn’t be having neighbors subsidize somebody who might even be a competitor. On July 1 there are going to be new statutes about how new property taxes are done. That’s just the way it is.” 

Deering said the county’s existing discretionary tax formula is still an incentive for development, and the only other county left in the area that offers a 0% for 5 years property tax incentive is Butte County. 

“What’s available now is 20, 40, 60, 80,” Deering said. “What was available then was all zeroes. There’s gotta be a line somewhere and that’s the problem. Where that line hits is unfortunate, although the 20, 40, 60, 80 is still the most competitive rate than anywhere in the hills.”
