title: Deadwood Public Library’s children’s section set for an upgrade
byline: By Sara Morgan Black Hills Pioneer
description: The Deadwood Public Library received $8,899 in grants from the Stephanie Miller-Davis Charitable Fund, allowing the library to improve the children’s section of the building and bolster summer reading programming. Pioneer photo by Sara Morgan
_full_text:
Deadwood Public Library’s children’s section set for an upgrade
The Deadwood Public Library received $8,899 in grants from the Stephanie Miller-Davis Charitable Fund, allowing the library to improve the children’s section of the building and bolster summer reading programming. Pioneer photo by Sara Morgan 

DEADWOOD — The Deadwood Public Library was one of 19 small and rural libraries in the state to receive a portion of $96,000 in grant funding from the Stephanie Miller-Davis Charitable Fund, allowing the library to expand their summer reading programming and improve the children’s section of the building. 

Director Kennedy Penk said the library received $1,000 for the summer reading program and $7,899 to improve the children’s section of the library. She said the $7,899 will be used to purchase additional shelving, a couch, and a possible 3D printer. 

“We have a makerspace cabinet right now that the kids absolutely love,” said Penk. “It’s open to them whenever a child is in the library. They go downstairs where we have our children’s space, open up the cabinet, and we have play dough, markers, crayons, coloring pages, LEGOs, board games, puzzles, all sorts of things that they can have access to. I was thinking a 3D printer would be a fantastic addition to that makerspace to help improve creativity for the kids and kind of promote technology use.”

Penk said she has selected a child-sized couch that she hopes will encourage independent reading and improve the space during story time. 

Additionally, Penk said she’s making “intentional choices” when selecting new shelving for the area. 

“Shelving is pretty expensive, so this grant is huge for us,” she said. “It really helps us with our shelving needs so we can expand our children’s collection. I’m trying to be mindful, too, when curating these spaces to be intentional and strategic. A lot of the furniture that’s in the library is original to it, and we would like to see these purchases last us another 100 years.”

Penk said the $1,000 in funding to support the summer reading program will be used for bookend events, including a kickoff magician and a finale from the Black Hills Raptor Center. 

“The theme this year is ‘Unearth the Story,’ which is extremely fitting for the Black Hills area. We’re focusing on dinosaurs and fossils and all that fun stuff,” Penk said. “Our kickoff event is Jeff Quinn’s Jurassic Magic Show. Our program runs from June 1 through the end of July. On Thursdays we’ll have activities and Friday is our story time.”

Penk said the program will include themed activities and story times and reading logs, with prizes at the end of the program for their top readers. 

Penk said she’s grateful for the opportunity to improve and expand the children’s section of the library. 

“As a small and rural library, it’s really important for us to seek out those additional funding sources and find ones that align with our library’s priorities and goals,” she said. “This one fits us well, and we’re thankful to the Stephanie Miller-Davis family for selecting our library.”

“Following Miller-Davis’s passing, her family established a fund in her name through the South Dakota Community Foundation (SDCF). Since then, the grant program has distributed more than $317,000 to rural libraries, providing children and communities with countless books, educational materials, and summer reading events. 

Historically, the grant has supported summer reading programs. This year is the first year they have included additional funds for infrastructure. 

“Stephanie would be thrilled to hear about all the projects being undertaken this year, from new floors to new shelving, to makerspaces,” Kate Davis, one of Miller-Davis’s daughters, said. 

The program is made possible through a partnership between the family, SDCF, and the South Dakota State Library. 

“The South Dakota Community Foundation is proud to partner with the Miller-Davis family and the South Dakota State Library in promoting childhood literacy,” said Jeff Veltkamp, SDCF director of development. “It’s an honor to be a part of perpetuating Stephanie’s passion for literacy and learning.”

title: Meade School Board approves  open-enrollment cap increases
byline: By Brandon Woltkamp Black Hills Pioneer
description: STURGIS — The Meade School Board on April 13 approved an increase to open enrollment caps by 0.5 students for fourth through 12th grades.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

STURGIS — The Meade School Board on April 13 approved an increase to open enrollment caps by 0.5 students for fourth through 12th grades.

Wayne Wormstadt, superintendent of the Meade School District, said that the cap increase was not popular among teachers.

“It is not a popular decision to do this,” said Wormstadt.

School Board Member Megan Snyder expressed her concerns with the caps.

“My concern is knowing that this is just the open enrollment cap (numbers). For fourth grade, fifth grade, we are almost at 27 (students),” said Snyder. 

All open-enrollment caps represent the maximum amount of out-of-district students can fit into an enrolled into a classroom.

The previous waiver approved last year allowed a one-student increase to each grade to open enrollment. There is no official class size policy due to the ranging sizes of each school.

Open-enrollment caps only apply to those outside the district coming in to school.

The original motion raised the open-enrollment caps to 1.5 but was shot down in an 8-1 vote.

School Board Member Terry Koontz motioned to modify the student cap increase of 0.5 students for fourth grade and up for a year. It passed unanimously. 

Open-enrollment for kindergarten will not exceed 21 students, first grade at 23 students, second grade at 24 students, third grade at 25 students, fourth and fifth grades at 26.5 students, sixth through eighth grades at 27.5, and ninth up not to exceed attendance center building capacity.

title: Sturgis FFA shines  at state
byline: By Tim Potts Black Hills Pioneer
description: The Sturgis FFA Range Identification team took first place honors at the state FFA convention in Brookings last week.  This is the second consecutive win for the Sturgis Chapter.  From left:  Jacob Popkes, Auburn Treloar, Chloe Cammack, Ian Cammack, and Ryan and Kristi Cammack.
_full_text:
Sturgis FFA shines  at state
The Sturgis FFA Range Identification team took first place honors at the state FFA convention in Brookings last week.  This is the second consecutive win for the Sturgis Chapter.  From left:  Jacob Popkes, Auburn Treloar, Chloe Cammack, Ian Cammack, and Ryan and Kristi Cammack.   

BROOKINGS – The Sturgis Future Farmers of America (FFA) chapter delivered a standout performance at the state convention, capturing its second consecutive state championship in range judging, sending 30 members to compete across eight teams, and celebrating the election of 2 members to top state officer positions.

It marked one of the most successful conventions in recent years, combining team success, individual excellence, and leadership achievements on the state stage.

Sturgis FFA advisor Stran Holben said the size and performance of this year’s group stood out. 

“This is the most kids I’ve ever taken to state, so it’s been really fun to see the program grow,” Holben said. “We had 8 teams compete, and they all did well. To see multiple teams place and individuals in the top 10 really shows the strength of our chapter.”

Leading the way was the Sturgis range judging team, which claimed its second straight state title. The team was powered by strong individual finishes, including Ian Cammack, who placed third overall, and Chloe Cammack, who finished sixth. Jacob Popkes in 11th place, Auburn Treloar finishing in 14th place.

In livestock evaluation, Natalie Yates added to the chapter’s success with a 10th-place finish.

Holben credited the team’s work ethic and commitment to mastering technical skills for the continued success.

“It’s been so fun to coach these teams,” he said. “I can only take them so far, then it’s up to them to really buckle down and learn the plants and characteristics. One of my passions is range, so being able to share that with them has been special. And our past teams have done a great job mentoring the younger group.”

The back-to-back titles reflect a growing culture of excellence in range judging within the Sturgis program. Last year’s championship team continues its season, with members preparing to compete at the national land and range judging contest in Oklahoma.

Four chapter members also earned their FFA degrees, one of the organization’s highest honors. Jackson Cammack, Ellie Storm, Alexis Cermak and Chloe Cammack received the award after years of work developing supervised agricultural experience (SAE) projects and meeting rigorous requirements.

“They’ve been working toward this since their freshman year,” Holben said. “It’s a big deal, and I wouldn’t be surprised to see some of them go on to earn their American degrees in the future.”

For the student competitors, preparation and teamwork proved to be the difference.

Ian Cammack said the state contest brought a higher level of pressure, with competitors given just one minute per plant to identify 50 different species and evaluate characteristics. 

“It’s definitely more nerve-wracking at state,” he said. “But I was really happy with how I did. I focused a lot on plant characteristics, and I think that helped me place high.” He added that the team’s depth was key to the championship.

“If anyone would have placed much lower, we probably wouldn’t have brought home the title,” he said. “It really took all of us.”

Treloar said motivation came from the team’s shared commitment and preparation. “We all worked really hard and really wanted to win,” she said. “There’s a lot to learn, so we spent a lot of time studying, going over more than 100 plants and practicing twice a week.”

Competitors were required to identify each plant and classify it by traits such as perennial, annual or biennial, warm or cool season, and whether it is desirable for wildlife or livestock.

Chloe Cammack said constant preparation, both at practice and on her own time, was critical to her success. 

“It was a lot of studying, at practice, at home, even on the bus,” she said. “Our team had a great dynamic. We pushed each other and talked through what we got right and wrong. That really helped.”

She emphasized that mastering plant characteristics was the key skill. “That’s what really set us apart,” she said.

Looking ahead, Holben said the chapter will celebrate its accomplishments at the annual end-of-year banquet on May 13, recognizing members and thanking the community for its continued support.

“It’s a chance to celebrate what these students have accomplished and to thank everyone who helps make it possible,” he said. “We’ve had a great year, and this group has a lot to be proud of.”

title: Purple Up for Military Kids Day
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: Courtesy photo
_full_text:
Purple Up for Military Kids Day
Courtesy photo 

SPEARFISH – On April 15, the Spearfish Middle School Student Council participated in the National Purple Up for Military Kids Day by helping the Spearfish American Legion Auxiliary Unit 164 and Post 164 setting up the Military Children’s Table. The ceremony is more than decoration, it’s a tribute to the courage, adaptability, and love of military children. It fosters awareness, unity, and support within communities, reminding everyone that these children are heroes too. 

FacebookTwitterPrintMore

The entire month of April is designated as the Month of the Military Child, but April 15th has a special place in that observance. There’s a happier and encouraging aspect to April 15th each year in the form of an observance among military families, military family support communities, and others worldwide in the form of Purple Up! For Military Kids Day. Held on April 15th of each year, “Purple Up! For Military Kids” is a day to wear purple to show support and thank military children for their strength and sacrifices. Why Purple? There is a tradition in the military referring to the color purple; one use of the color involves the phrase “purple suit” which can refer to a military activity or organization that includes civilians and/or multiple branches of the military. An operation or an organizational unit that uses resources from various service branches  is called a Purple Suit. That is because there is not just one branch and so it is a combination of branches or you might say a mixture of uniform colors. An example of this would be some units that operate in the Pentagon. In the context of April 15th, this tradition continues. Purple indicates that all branches of the military are supported. All the service colors are represented including Air Force blue, Army green, Navy blue, Marine red, Space Force blue, and Coast Guard blue; said to combine for the mutual benefit of all into the color purple.

Purple Up for Military Kids recognizes the 1.6 million military children of active-duty members worldwide. There were 1.2 million in 2019. The average military family moves three times more often than their civilian counterpart. There are nearly two million “total-force dependent children”

title: New payment policy could help  county collect millions
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: STURGIS — A new payment and collections policy could help Meade County recover millions in unpaid debt.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

STURGIS — A new payment and collections policy could help Meade County recover millions in unpaid debt. 

On Tuesday, the Meade County Commission approved a new debt collections policy that establishes specific minimum dollar amounts for payments on large debts owed to the county, such as those for court appointed attorneys and court fees. Previously, the county policy established percentage amounts for minimum payments. 

“We were trying to give them a flat rate as to how to pay their debts,” said Chris Little, senior deputy auditor. “In our office we have established a little bit better relationship with the debtors, and we are seeing a better return. 

Unpaid accounts with debtors who have not established a payment plan, and that are 90 days past due, will be sent to AAA Collection Agency, according to the policy. Little said the county has not consistently followed through with the collections process since 2021. As a result, she said she is preparing to send 2,908 delinquent accounts, totaling more than $4.5 million, to the collections agency. From now on, Little said the auditor’s office intends to follow up on all delinquent accounts with quarterly reports and filings with AAA Collection Agency. 

“We have a lot of liens for court appointed attorneys,” said chief deputy state’s attorney Ken Chleborad. “We don’t collect all that much of it. I’ve been here for 20 years, and to my knowledge we’ve never enforced it.”

title: SD agrees to $700,000 contract for Rushmore fireworks, including backup light show
byline: By Meghan O’Brien Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: Fireworks explode over Mount Rushmore National Memorial in South Dakota’s Black Hills. Photo courtesy of Travel South Dakota
_full_text:
Rushmore fireworks
Fireworks explode over Mount Rushmore National Memorial in South Dakota’s Black Hills. Photo courtesy of Travel South Dakota 

PIERRE — South Dakota’s contract for a July 3 fireworks show at Mount Rushmore is worth twice as much as the last show 6 years ago.

The state Department of Tourism entered into a $700,000 contract on April 9 with California-based Pyro Spectaculars to produce the fireworks show, and for “lighting elements that can be utilized if environmental conditions require an alternative to fireworks,” according to the department. The contract includes a sliding scale of partial payments if the fireworks are canceled — the later the cancellation, the higher the payment.

Pyro Spectaculars did not directly respond to Searchlight’s request for detailed information about what visitors can expect from the “lighting elements,” but its website features the company’s work on drone shows.

The funding for this year’s show is from the Department of Tourism’s budget. Department officials have said the fireworks bring worldwide media attention to the state that’s equivalent to millions of dollars’ worth of free advertising.

The contract authorizes a “go/no-go” protocol, “which may be exercised by oral notice at any time” up to 7 p.m. the day of the show by the National Park Service for inclement weather, including rain, high winds or “other conditions” that could endanger visitors or damage the memorial.

One of the risks from fireworks is falling embers sparking wildfires in the forest surrounding the memorial. Parts of Pennington County, where Mount Rushmore is located, are experiencing some of the most extreme drought conditions in the state, according to the U.S. Drought Monitor. Two recent wildfires in the area burned more than 14,000 acres combined.

The event, officially known as South Dakota’s Freedom 250 Mount Rushmore Fireworks Celebration, in honor of the nation’s 250th birthday, will have 4,800 attendees who won tickets in an online lottery. That’s less than the 7,500 tickets awarded for the 2020 event, which was attended by President Trump. He has been invited to this year’s event but has not committed to attending.

The memorial will be closed July 3 except for visitors with tickets for the event.

title: Individual contribution limit doesn’t restrain big donors in SD governor race
byline: By Joshua Haiar and Seth Tupper Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: PIERRE — In South Dakota’s race for governor, a state law limiting individual campaign contributions is proving to be more of a suggestion than a requirement.
_full_text:
SearchlightLogo

PIERRE — In South Dakota’s race for governor, a state law limiting individual campaign contributions is proving to be more of a suggestion than a requirement.

The law caps individual contributions to a candidate for statewide office at $4,000 per year.

Yet South Dakota’s wealthiest man, the banking magnate and philanthropist T. Denny Sanford, has given at least $50,000 to support Republican Gov. Larry Rhoden. 

Meanwhile, one of Rhoden’s opponents in the June 2 primary election, U.S. Rep. Dusty Johnson, has benefited from about 130 individual contributions above the $4,000 limit. Those include contributions of $17,000 apiece from the Winklevoss twins, Cameron and Tyler, who famously sued Mark Zuckerberg, alleging he stole their idea to start Facebook.

The 2 campaigns have used a combination of federal and state fundraising committees to raise and transfer the money in ways that circumvent the individual state contribution limit.

It’s all part and parcel of the complicated modern status of campaign finance, explained Brendan Glavin, director of insights for OpenSecrets, a national campaign finance watchdog group. 

“While I don’t see anything in South Dakota law that would make this illegal, it clearly undermines the idea of contribution limits for candidates,” Glavin said. 

Here’s how it works in the case of the Rhoden campaign. Sanford gave $50,000 to the federally registered Free American Fund, which is an entity known as a “hybrid political action committee.” Other contributions included $30,000 from James Brown of Colorado. 

Hybrid PACs maintain two accounts: one for traditional PAC activity, which is subject to contribution and spending limits, and one for “super PAC” activity. The U.S. Supreme Court has ruled that because super PACs are prohibited from coordinating with candidates, they can receive and spend unlimited amounts of money.

The Free American Fund transferred $85,500 to the state-registered Rhoden for Governor campaign committee. The state does not limit PAC contributions to statewide candidates.

Rhoden spokesperson Ian Fury said, “Governor Rhoden is playing by the same campaign finance rules as everybody else.”

“He hasn’t been raising money for years, and he isn’t independently wealthy to bankroll his own campaign,” Fury said.

In the case of the Johnson campaign, individual contributions greater than $4,000 have gone to the federally registered Dusty Johnson Victory Committee, which is an entity known as a joint fundraising committee. Joint committees are made up of other committees that pool their contribution limits, allowing donors to make a larger contribution that’s split among the member committees.

Donations to Johnson’s victory committee have included the Winklevoss contributions, a combined $40,000 from Republican former Lt. Gov. Steve Kirby and his wife, Suzette, and a combined $20,000 from First Premier Bank CEO Dana Dykhouse (who works for Sanford) and his wife, LaDawn. 

One of the federal committees that comprises the victory committee, Friends of Dusty Johnson, has transferred $800,000 to Dusty for Governor, Johnson’s state-registered committee. Like Rhoden’s state campaign committee, Johnson’s state committee can receive unlimited contributions from PACs.

Campaign finance watchdogs have argued that joint fundraising committees can be used to circumvent campaign contribution limits. Saurav Ghosh is the director of federal campaign finance reform at Campaign Legal Center. 

“Allowing JFCs to pay for candidate advocacy and campaign activity erodes the legal guardrails that exist to prevent actual and apparent corruption, hold candidates and committees accountable to the public, and prevent big-money donors from drowning out the voices of everyday Americans,” Ghosh wrote in a column for the center.

Johnson’s campaign sent a statement from him acknowledging that “South Dakota’s campaign finance laws need a total overhaul.”

“There are too many loopholes and there is too little transparency,” he said. “In my first session as governor, I’ll work with the legislature to fix those problems.”

When asked why his campaign is using those loopholes, he said, “I certainly think we should raise the standard here in South Dakota, but until that happens, my campaign will continue to follow the rules and guidelines as they are currently written.”

Because state and federal campaign finance reporting deadlines vary, it’s difficult to know how much each candidate has raised and spent at any given moment. Johnson has clearly raised the most money, with balances in his various state and federal accounts adding up to about $7 million as of the most recent reporting deadlines. His state campaign committee had spent about $650,000 by the end of last year.

Rhoden’s state committee brought a balance of about $91,000 into last year, raised about $490,000 and spent about $71,000, ending the year with a balance of about $512,000.

Republican candidate for governor Toby Doeden of Aberdeen raised $2.1 million last year, primarily from a $2 million loan he made to his own campaign committee. He spent $2 million and had $41,000 left at the end of the year.

Doeden said critics and competitors are pushing a narrative that he is attempting to buy the governor’s race and that he couldn’t raise money. He said he could have raised a substantial amount of money from his supporters, but chose to self-fund.

Republican candidate for governor Jon Hansen, the speaker of the state House, reported raising $189,000 by the end of last year, spending $135,000, and ending with $54,000 on hand.

The only other candidate for governor, Democrat Dan Ahlers, announced his candidacy earlier this year and won’t have to file his first campaign finance report until the next deadline for state committees, May 18.

title: From obsession to fruition An interview with child psychologist-turned-historian  Phillip Hall
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: Philip Hall is a former pediatric psychologist and academic turned historical fiction and non-fiction writer. Photo courtesy Philip Hall
_full_text:
Philip S. Hall.jpeg
Philip Hall is a former pediatric psychologist and academic turned historical fiction and non-fiction writer. Photo courtesy Philip Hall 

SPEARFISH — Philip Hall is the author of 8 books, 38 articles in professional journals, and a South Dakotan through and through. He has penned multiple non-fiction books exploring the state’s history, and reimagining it as a romantic novel in “The Birney Day School.”

Hall’s one and only dive into historical fiction follows easterner Jonathan Wagner as he arrives in Birney, Mont., on the Northern Cheyenne Indian Reservation. There, he meets Sarah, the acting principal of Birney Day School. She is Northern Cheyenne and has a great deal to teach Jonathan about the experiences of Native Americans in the American West.

“If you were to pick it up and read the first page, you would think, ‘Well, this is a romance novel,’” Hall said. “When you get into the 2 characters in the story, they draw you into the Northern Cheyenne culture.”

 Birney Day School.jpeg
“Birney Day School” is Philip Hall’s one and only novel. Courtesy image 

Hall said a good romance novel should have a purpose, and good historical fiction should have the reader walking away with some actual understanding of the time, place, and setting of a story. He hopes “Birney Day School” does both.

“The takeaway would be an empathy for the Northern Cheyenne,” Hall said. “We have not done well by the Native Americans.”

Writing “Birney Day School” took Hall about 3 decades to write.

“Louis L’Amour, he did well writing. I read some of his novels,” Hall said. “He said, shoot the sheriff on the first page. Get the story going. That was good advice. I changed the whole first chapter around that.”

Wordsworth Publishing released “Birney Day School” Nov. 12, 2025. 

Almost 10 years earlier, Hall published an entirely different kind of book — “Educating Oppositional and Defiant Children.” Hall is a pediatric psychologist by training. He worked in the field for 40 years.

“I was the director of the school psych program at the University of South Dakota,” Hall said.

“Educating Oppositional and Defiant Children” is in its fifth edition. Aimed largely at teachers, it is meant to help develop a positive school culture.

“I think I wrote it in 1990 and I still get a few royalties from it,” Hall said. “Nobody has written another book like it, so it stays semi-current.”

He insists that his work in psychology doesn’t apply to his work now, as a writer, mostly because he focused on understanding how children think. The books he writes now are solidly focused on adult experiences and areas of interest for older readers.

What does come in handy is what he characterizes as an “obsessive, compulsive need” to dive deeply into historical topics.

“I have a lot of relatives who ranched around in the badlands and right on the edge of the badlands,” Hall said. “You just hear stories, you know. When you listen to enough people, you realize, ‘Oh, there is kind of a history here.’”

Those stories captured Hall’s imagination. Then, they turned into what he describes as an obsession.

“You get involved in a project, and at first it seems interesting. You feel like you have something to say and you have to write it,” Hall said. “Then, about halfway through… You are absolutely tired of it. You’re sick of it. You say, ‘Why did I start it?’ Then you say to yourself, ‘You’ve invested so much time, now you have to finish it.’”

Still, his desire to delve deeply into a topic — at least, initially — has produced multiple non-fiction works that focus on the history of South Dakota and the American West including “To Have This Land,”” Reflections of the Badlands,” “From Wounded Knee to the Gallows: The Life and Trials of Lakota Chief Two Sticks,” and “The White River Badlands: Its History and Characters.”

Despite those accomplishments, Hall doesn’t consider himself a strong writer.

“I probably go through 50, 60 drafts of anything that I write, because it starts out pretty poor. There are some people I know who can just write, and they are beautifully flowing sentences that are coherent, and neither one of them would call themselves a writer, but writing comes innately to them,” Hall said. “I really struggle with writing, so I edit, and I edit, and I edit, and I edit.”

He said he never sets out to write a great history or novel. To do so, in Hall’s telling, is folly.

“I don’t usually start out with the idea ‘This is going to be a great book,’ or even a book. It is more internally driven,” Hall said. “If you are interested in writing, just write.”

title: Quick picks: Mental Health
description: Courtesy photo
_full_text:
Quick picks: Mental Health
Courtesy photo 

May is mental health awareness month. It’s a good time to reflect on an often overlooked element of our overall wellness, and a chance to learn more about the challenges faced by roughly 1 in 5 American adults (according to the National Institute of Mental Health). Reading about the facts behind mental health conditions, and the experiences of people facing mental health challenges, is a great way to engage with the topic in May. This week’s recommendations were thoughtfully prepared by Kennedy Penk, the director of the Deadwood Public Library. 

1. “The Body Keeps Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trama” by Bessel Van Der Kolk

Genre: Nonfiction/psychology

Trauma is a fact of life. Veterans and their families deal with the painful aftermath of combat; one in 5 Americans has been molested; one in four grew up with alcoholics; one in 3 couples have engaged in physical violence. Dr. Bessel van der Kolk, one of the world’s foremost experts on trauma, has spent over 3 decades working with survivors. In The Body Keeps the Score, he uses recent scientific advances to show how trauma literally reshapes both body and brain, compromising sufferers’ capacities for pleasure, engagement, self-control, and trust. He explores innovative treatments — from neurofeedback and meditation to sports, drama, and yoga — that offer new paths to recovery by activating the brain’s natural neuroplasticity.

2. “I’m Glad My Mom Died” by Jennette McCurdy 

Genre: Memoir 

A heartbreaking and hilarious memoir by iCarly and Sam & Cat star Jennette McCurdy about her struggles as a former child actor — including eating disorders, addiction, and a complicated relationship with her overbearing mother—and how she retook control of her life.

3. “How to be Okay When Nothing is Okay” by Jenny Lawson 

Genre: Memoir

Jenny Lawson is full of contradictions. She’s a celebrated author but battles self-doubt, paralysis, and anxiety. She’s an award-winning humorist but struggles with treatment —resistant depression. The questions people most often ask her are, “How do you do it? How do you keep going even when it feels impossible? How do you keep creating?” This book is her answer.

4. “What My Bones Know: A Memoir of Healing from Complex Trauma” by Stephanie Foo

Genre: Memoir

By age 30, Stephanie Foo was successful on paper: She had her dream job as an award-winning radio producer at This American Life and a loving boyfriend. But behind her office door, she was having panic attacks and sobbing at her desk every morning. After years of questioning what was wrong with herself, she was diagnosed with complex PTSD — a condition that occurs when trauma happens continuously, over the course of years.

5. “American Daughter Gone to War: On the Front Lines with an Army Nurse in Vietnam” by Winnie Smith 

Genre: History 

A former army nurse during the Vietnam War describes her days and nights, dating fighter pilots, tending to wounded soldiers, and getting a taste of the tragedy of war while working in a Saigon intensive care unit.

6. “Burnout: The Secret to Unlocking the Stress Cycle” by Emily Nagoski and Amelia Nagoski 

Genre: Non-fiction/psychology 

This groundbreaking book explains why women experience burnout differently than men —and provides a simple, science-based plan to help women minimize stress, manage emotions, and live a more joyful life.

title: The Mercy Sloane Chronicles: Chapter 1
byline: By Eliza Blue A work of serial fiction, special to the Pioneer
description: Mercy Sloane stood on her front porch, surveying the waving sea of grass before her. The porch was only a few years old but it was already rickety and sloping, rusted nails hanging out at odd angles, gray wooden boards cracked along the seams from the dry heat. Her husband, Granger Sloane, had taken one look at the hastily framed building and the dangling porch, turned his back, and started to cry. “I’m sorry, Mercy, I should never have brought us here,” he’d said between heaving sobs.
_full_text:
The Mercy Sloane Chronicles

Mercy Sloane stood on her front porch, surveying the waving sea of grass before her. The porch was only a few years old but it was already rickety and sloping, rusted nails hanging out at odd angles, gray wooden boards cracked along the seams from the dry heat. Her husband, Granger Sloane, had taken one look at the hastily framed building and the dangling porch, turned his back, and started to cry. “I’m sorry, Mercy, I should never have brought us here,” he’d said between heaving sobs.

After he’d stopped crying, Granger had raged about the land trust company that had lied and sold them this good-for-nothing property. “Good for nothing but flies,” he’d shouted, kicking at the dust that whipped around the house in the oven-blasted wind, coating their clothes and faces, evidence of the last tenant’s failed crops. The plowed field beside the house was barren, the native grasses that remained on the other three sides already cured golden-brown even though it was only the middle of June.

Mercy listened to him, her back resting against the plywood boards of the house, as his shouts turned practical and he began plotting their return. He’d walk to the nearest town, Antelope, to find someone to take them back to the train. “We’ll have to spend the night, but I’ll have us heading home by tomorrow, I promise, Mercy,” he said as he set off on foot.

Later, when he’d come rattling back up the road in a rusted pickup and then started putting their trunks in its backseat, he was even more sheepish. “I can’t believe I was such a fool. I’m so sorry. All for some silly dream of the West.” He’d stopped to unbutton the sleeves of his white shirt, now sweat-soaked and stained, continuing, “Well, the folks are nice here, at least. Can you imagine someone lending their car to a stranger in Iowa City? The fellow at the store in town told me I could take it to load up tonight and then pick him up tomorrow so he can drive us to the station. Didn’t want more than the cost of gas, he said.” Granger shook his head and almost chuckled, then continued to load the trunks, not stopping to notice Mercy hadn’t said a word since they’d arrived.

 Eliza Blue TMC
Eliza Blue 

Editor's Note
Editor’s Note: For years, Eliza Blue has written non-fiction columns, detailing her experience of ranch and farm life in South Dakota. Blue is turning the page and pivoting to serial fiction. Each week, the Pioneer will feature a chapter of the Mercy Sloane Chronicles. We hope you’ll join us every week for Mercy’s adventures. Happy reading!

***

All that long night, Mercy lay next to Granger on the blankets they’d thrown down on the dilapidated house’s dirt floor, wondering if she was really going to do what she thought she was going to do. They’d left the door open to get a little breeze in the stifling heat, and over the sound of Granger’s snoring, Mercy heard the wind begin to rise with a quiet melody, hushed like a lullaby. 

Time passed. Mercy watched the stars move with aching slowness across the door’s dark rectangle of night, and just before dawn, as Mercy’s eyes finally grew heavy and the lullaby began to change to a hymn of absolution, she knew what she had suspected from the first: The wind had been singing this just song to her.

Granger boarded a train heading east the following day. Mercy and Granger had been married less than 12 hours when they’d boarded that same train heading west, but this time, Mercy wasn’t with him. 

If there’d been anyone else on the platform, which there wasn’t, they’d have seen a forlorn man peering out the train window, then angrily shake his head, tears welling up in his eyes. He was still wearing the same tailored traveling suit he’d put on after the wedding, and a gold band around his finger, but now he was alone. To both of their shocked amazement, Mercy had refused to go with him. She’d decided to stay.

Now it was a week later, and here she was, staring out across the western prairie on land that was deeded to a man who was and wasn’t her husband because, “I’m going to buy him out,” she said aloud, the first words she’d heard spoken since Granger called her a stubborn fool, tossed her trunk to the ground, and drove off, the pickup spitting gray fumes and hot dust. 

Then she turned to walk into the house and gathered a few things. It was time to go meet her new neighbors.

SPEARFISH — A 48-year-old Spearfish man is dead after his Subaru Crosstrek crashed into a piece of construction equipment inside the road closure area on East Colorado Boulevard and Aurora Avenue. The crash happened just after 2 a.m. Thursday.

Colorado Boulevard is closed from Rainbow Road to Aurora Avenue, now until Aug. 31 for the a road widening project. Spearfish’s contractor for the Colorado Boulevard Widening Project, Dakota Redi-Mix, Inc. of Black Hawk, and the Eastside Acres developer’s contractor, Woelber Excavating of Belle Fourche, are coordinating to perform work necessitating the road closure.

title: Spearfish schools receive workforce education grant
byline: By Mark Watson Black Hills Pioneer
description: SPEARFISH — The Spearfish School District received a $43,175 workforce education grant.
_full_text:
SPEARFISH — The Spearfish School District received a $43,175 workforce education grant.

The South Dakota Department of Education recently awarded the grants to four school districts, including Spearfish. The district plans to use the grant to purchase industry-grade equipment for its welding program.

John Thayer, the welding teacher at Spearfish High School, said the district is using the grant to purchase a CNC laser cutter.

The Computer Numerical Control, better known as CNC, laser will be used in the welding and manufacturing parts of the school.

“We used to use a CNC plasma table,” Thayer said. “These lasers are getting really popular in the industry and the tolerances are a lot smaller.”

Thayer said the district will order the cutter and get it installed over the summer.

“They are super pricy, but super cool,” he said. 

According to the South Dakota Department of Education, the Workforce Education Grant program was designed to drive meaningful innovation in career and technical education (CTE) across middle and high schools. The program highlights districts that demonstrate strong collaboration with postsecondary institutions and industry partners to design and deliver programs aligned to current and emerging workforce needs.

 “CTE programs create authentic experiences for our students,” said Secretary of Education Dr. Joseph Graves. “Securing grants, like the Workforce Education Grant, strengthens school districts’ CTE programs by ensuring students are performing essential hands-on learning using state-of-the-art equipment.”

Additional grants awarded from the state include:  

The four school districts receiving awards are:

• Rapid City Area Schools was awarded $223,830 to meet industry standards for the Culinary Arts lab and support expansion of course offerings.

• Hill City School District was awarded $18,229 to modernize its Manufacturing program with the purchase of industry-grade equipment.

 • Frederick Area School District was awarded $55,142 to purchase equipment for its newly expanded Agriculture program.

title: Meeting needs, one grocery cart at a time
byline: By Sierra Ferguson Black Hills Pioneer
description: SPEARFISH — For many of us, making a list is an important pre-grocery-run task. Maybe we mark out the basics we need to get through the week, staples like milk, eggs, and bread. Coffee might make the list. We might also take note of the things that make life a little bit healthier or easier: planners, reusable water bottles, and tote bags.
_full_text:
SPEARFISH — For many of us, making a list is an important pre-grocery-run task. Maybe we mark out the basics we need to get through the week, staples like milk, eggs, and bread. Coffee might make the list. We might also take note of the things that make life a little bit healthier or easier: planners, reusable water bottles, and tote bags.

Upon arriving at one particular grocery store in Spearfish, shoppers may notice pre-planned grocery lists available for pickup. 

Those lists detail items that are needed by Artemis House, and the people who rely on the non-profit’s services for critical shelter. 

“We started the Lueders grocery list in January as a simple way to get the community involved in supporting the day-to-day needs of the survivors we work with at Artemis House,” said Madison Eggen, a community advocate at Artemis House. With approval from Doug Lueders at Lueders Food Center, Artemis House staff placed a donation bin and started to distribute grocery lists.

The Victims of Violence Intervention Program, which operates Artemis House, works with Feeding South Dakota to fill many of its clients’ basic needs on the food front. However, there are items the shelter either runs out of or has found a need for that aren’t covered by federal funding.

“The grocery list drive has helped us bring in items we don’t always receive otherwise and offer a little more variety and flexibility in what we can provide. It has also allowed us to better meet specific needs when people have dietary restrictions or food sensitivities, like gluten-free options or other items that aren’t always part of what we typically have on hand,” Eggen said. 

Items like bread, shelf-stable milk, tortillas, mixed nuts, and rice or instant potatoes are on the list.

“The items on the list come directly from what we see clients needing most often. We try to keep it focused on practical, high-use items that people can easily pick up during their regular shopping trips,” Eggen said. Some items meet more than clients’ most basic needs — things like beef jerky, popcorn, and coffee.

Artemis House
Artemis House Grocery List:
 -Coffee and filters
 -Lof bread
 -Shelf milk
 -Horizon shelf stable whole milk
 -Pasta sauce 
 -Tortillas/taco shells
 -Almonds/mixed nuts
 -Juice boxes
 -Instant potatoes/rice
 -Jerky/beef sticks 
 -Dairy-free items
 -Snacks (granola bars, fruit snacks, trail mix, pretzels, popcorn, etc.)
 Artemis House non-grocery wish list:
 -Pillows
 -Small laundry baskets
 -Reusable water bottles
 -Journals/planners
 -Small-Large size bras/underwear
 -Shower curtains
 -Kids toys/fidgets
 -Grocery store gift cards
 -Rec Center day passes
 -Large storage totes
 -Carrier unlocked phones

“We have items we like to distribute to folks who come into shelter, like the journals, personal care products, things like that that maybe federal funding doesn’t provide,” Servaty said.

In addition to journals, those items include pillows and children’s toys, storage totes, shower curtains, and rec center day passes — items that clients need and want during difficult times.

“Some of those self-care and personal care products are important, but we can’t purchase those with federal funding, so we thought that would be a good way to engage with the community and let them provide those things.”

But those items don’t matter any less, even if they might look like “small luxuries. 

“It really matters because a lot of the people we serve are starting over or leaving difficult situations, and having basic items on hand can take some of that stress off. While we do keep some food available for clients as we connect them with longer-term resources like the food pantry or public assistance, it can be fairly limited in variety,” Eggen said. “These are items we use regularly in shelter or that we tend to go through quickly.”

In addition to the bin at Leuders Food Center in Spearfish, Artemis House has a collection bin at the UPS Store. 

“It’s been a simple but meaningful way for the community to directly support the people we serve, and we appreciate the continued involvement,” Eggen said.

title: Property tax relief depends partly on choices by counties
byline: By Makenzie Huber Southdakotasearchlight.com
description: PIERRE — South Dakota has two new laws intended to reduce homeowner property taxes, but one of them depends on counties opting in.
_full_text:
SearchlightLogo

PIERRE — South Dakota has two new laws intended to reduce homeowner property taxes, but one of them depends on counties opting in. 

The reaction of county commissioners across the state runs the gamut from opposition to uncertainty to eager support.

Republican Gov. Larry Rhoden signed a bill into law in March permitting counties to implement up to a half-percent sales tax and use the revenue to reduce the county-imposed portion of homeowner property taxes. When the law takes effect in July, it will be the first opportunity for South Dakota counties to charge a sales tax. 

Thirty-nine of the state’s 66 counties could collect enough from sales taxes to replace the entire county share of homeowners’ property tax bills, Rhoden’s office estimates, while the others could replace a portion. Homeowners would still pay property taxes to schools, cities, townships and other local entities such as water and fire districts, but the county portion of homeowners’ property taxes would be eliminated or reduced. If a county has extra revenue from the sales tax after eliminating the county portion of homeowner property taxes, the extra revenue must be used to reduce taxes on agricultural and commercial properties.

Statewide, according to the Governor’s Office, the average homeowner property tax savings from implementing the county sales tax would be an estimated $660.

South Dakota homeowners have complained about owner-occupied property taxes since the COVID-19 pandemic, when a surge of in-migration and other factors drove up taxes along with home prices. 

Yet that doesn’t necessarily mean South Dakotans favor the use of higher sales taxes to reduce property taxes. A recent poll commissioned by South Dakota News Watch and the Chiesman Center for Democracy found 49% opposition when registered voters were asked whether counties should have the option to raise sales taxes in exchange for lowering property taxes, with 33% support and 17% undecided.

The other law adopted by Rhoden and lawmakers during the recently concluded legislative session relies on next year’s expiration of a temporary state sales tax rate reduction. The revenue from allowing the tax to rise from 4.2% to 4.5% will be captured and used to reduce school-imposed homeowner property taxes statewide. 

Some commissioners eager to move quickly

Many county commissioners plan to discuss the new sales tax option with their colleagues and county residents in the coming months.

The county sales tax can be implemented by commissions or a vote of the public. In counties that enact a sales tax, homeowners will see the property tax reduction as a credit on their property tax bill.

Members of the Lincoln County Commission discussed the potential half-percent sales tax at the board’s March 24 meeting. Lincoln is the fastest growing county in the state based on the percentage increase in its population, according to U.S. Census Bureau data, and its homeowners pay the highest average property taxes in South Dakota, according to the state Bureau of Finance and Management.

Lincoln County homeowners would save an average of 12% on their property tax bill if the optional county sales tax is adopted, according to the Governor’s Office. That’s about $579 of savings on a home valued at $325,000.

“I think it’s critical we discuss this and we get on this as quickly as possible,” Commissioner Jim Schmidt said.

Commissioner Betty Otten said she would ask county staff to draft an ordinance ahead of the July 1 effective date of the new state law.

Schmidt told South Dakota Searchlight he expects a majority of the five-member commission to support the idea — it’s a matter of whether the sales tax should be implemented by the board or by a vote of the people.

Commissioner Joel Arends told his colleagues the proposal “doesn’t pass the common sense test” because it trades one tax for another, but he wants to see the issue taken to a public vote.

“There’s good, there’s bad, it’s suboptimal,” Arends said, “and we need to hear from the public.” 

Custer County — the third fastest growing county in the state, based on its percentage increase in population — should “start acting on this right away,” said Commission Chair Jim Lintz. Homeowners in the county would save an average of 12% on their property tax bill, or $428 on a home valued at $325,000, according to the Governor’s Office.

A sizable portion of the sales taxes in Custer County would be paid by Black Hills tourists.

“This doesn’t make the problem go away,” Lintz said, adding that valuations still remain high in the county and agricultural property taxes are also rising. “We’re still going to be looking at a problem, but this is a start.”

Pennington County Commission Chair Ron Weifenbach said he supports the sales tax option “100%.” He thinks residents would largely support the sales tax increase, since Rapid City and the Black Hills collect some of the highest sales tax revenues in the state, thanks to tourists and other visitors. Weifenbach said the board will consider the change carefully and ask for public input.

“Overall, it’ll have a lot of traction,” Weifenbach said. “I look at this as a positive step to alleviate taxes for people who are getting taxed out of their houses.”

Other counties deliberate

Interest in the sales tax proposal will depend largely on each county’s individual need for homeowner property tax relief, said Eric Jennings, a commissioner in Lawrence County in the northern Black Hills, and vice president of the South Dakota Association of County Commissioners. Tax increases on homes have not been as pronounced in some rural areas.

“I’d be surprised if there are very many counties away from the interstates that adopt this,” Jennings said.

He hesitates to take a position, since people with lower incomes pay a larger share of that income on sales taxes than wealthier people, and the county sales tax would come on top of the statewide sales tax increase.

“I’m back and forth every day,” Jennings said.

Minnehaha County Commission Chair Dean Karsky said he isn’t convinced Minnehaha, which includes Sioux Falls, will implement a sales tax, but the commission “needs to have a discussion.”

Karsky added that many counties will “wait and see” what happens in other counties before moving forward.

“It’ll be a lot of waiting on the fence to see what happens, waiting to see what our constituents want us to do,” Karsky said.

Some residents in lesser-populated counties across the state are concerned they’ll foot the tax bill for residents in more populated counties that implement the tax. Rep. Will Mortenson, R-Fort Pierre, warned lawmakers during the legislative session that the plan imposes a higher burden on people from smaller communities that won’t benefit from the property tax relief if their county doesn’t implement the plan.

“When we hear about these visitors, they don’t just come from out of state, they come from rural communities,” he said. “Because this bill is structured so that the property tax cut goes where the economic activity goes, you know who’s getting the biggest tax cut? The people in the biggest houses, in the biggest towns.”

Chad Erk, chair of the Butte County Commission, said the board will discuss the possibility of a sales tax increase, but he is opposed to the “tax shift.” Erk would rather cut spending within the county government to provide property tax relief.

Butte County is a rural, agricultural area just north of the Black Hills. It wouldn’t be able to take advantage of tourist sales tax spending to the extent some other counties could.

“So we’d literally just be taxing ourselves, but in a different way,” Erk said.

title: Lawrence County selects 5 roads  for graveling projects
byline: By Sara Morgan Black Hills Pioneer
description: Pioneer file photo
_full_text:
Lawrence County selects 5 roads  for graveling projects
Pioneer file photo 

DEADWOOD — Lawrence County Highway Superintendent John Bey announced Tuesday that he plans to spend the $500,000 gravel budget on a total of 17.6 miles of county roads this year. 

Bey said that 5 county roads are set to receive new gravel this season, ranging from a 2-inch lift to a 4-inch lift. He said that while the budget for gravel is smaller this year, he’s managed to treat approximately the same miles of roads as he does every year. 

“$500,000 is quite a bit less than our normal year for us, but we were asked to make some budget reductions so we could have a balanced budget,” Bey said. “The areas that our department has control over would be the cost of the purchasing of gravel and the purchasing of magnesium chloride, so those are the areas where we made some reductions.”

The average cost per ton of gravel is up 57 cents from 2025. 

“We built the budget around $13.50 per ton for gravel and the average came in right at $12.99,” said Bey. “A little less than what we budgeted for, but definitely higher than what we paid last year.”

The 5 roads set to receive new gravel this season are 5.3 miles of Merritt Estes Road, 6.2 miles of Weisman Road, 3.99 miles of 196th Street, 0.7 miles of Lookout Mountain Road, and 1.45 miles of Pahkamma Road. 

Bey said the county has reduced the amount of gravel that will need purchased to treat Weisman Road by utilizing gravel previously purchased from the Bestgen Pit in Meade County on Big Horn Road.

“We paid to have a gravel pile crushed a few years ago and the property owners would like us to remove the gravel because they want to crush again,” Bey said. “So, we had to move that road up a little bit in our rotation just because we had to get rid of that gravel.”

Bey said the gravel will be mixed with limestone to resurface the section of road. 

The nearly 4 miles of 196th Street are set to receive a 3-inch lift of gravel. 

“196th is a lot narrower road, so you don’t need quite as much gravel,” he said. 

Lookout Mountain, a connecting road between 196th Street and Johnson Lane, will be graveled in tandem with 196th Street.

“Lookout Mountain Road, by itself, is in very good shape and it’s not up in our rotation,” said Bey. “We always gravel it with 196th because it actually is continuous with 196th.”

Pahkamaa Road, a road Bey described as short and narrow, will receive a 4-inch lift. 

“It definitely needs it,” Bey said. “Our tonnage is pretty low on that road, just because of the width of the road. We’re only putting down roughly 3,000 tons on the one-and-a-half-mile section.”

Merritt Estes Road, located in the southern part of the county, is set to receive a 4-inch lift of gravel.

“That road gets a lot of ATV traffic. There’s not a lot of homes on it, but it’s a cut across between Nemo and Highway 385 and the Sugar Shack,” said Bey. “We haven’t graveled that road in a long time, but it’s a long ways from any of our shops so it’s hard to do a lot of maintenance on that road. This should get us in good shape for another 10 years on that.”

A 3.9-mile stretch of Tinton was not included in the total budget. The road was approved earlier this month for a 4-inch lift of gravel, tree removal, and calculating the centerline of the road. The county is expecting to be reimbursed for work on the road by the United States Forest Service.

Bey said graveling projects typically begin at the end of April, early May. Depending on the weather, he said work on the projects could now begin any time.

title: Key City Giving Gals award $20,000 to Sturgis Animal Shelter
byline: By Tim Potts Black Hills Pioneer
description: A large number of the 223 members of the Key City Giving Gals voted and the Sturgis Animal Shelter was the winner of the semi-annual donation on Monday at the Oasis Bar and Grill in Sturgis.  Pioneer photo by Tim Potts
_full_text:
Key City Giving Gals award $20,000 to Sturgis Animal Shelter
 A large number of the 223 members of the Key City Giving Gals voted and the Sturgis Animal Shelter was the winner of the semi-annual donation on Monday at the Oasis Bar and Grill in Sturgis.  Pioneer photo by Tim Potts 

STURGIS — The Sturgis Animal Shelter has been selected as the recipient of approximately $20,000 through the semi-annual meeting of Key City Giving Gals, held Monday at Oasis Bar and Grill in Sturgis.  

The local giving organization considered 5 nonprofit applicants before awarding the funds to the animal shelter. Other groups making presentations included the Monument Health Sturgis Hospital Auxiliary, the Society of St. Vincent de Paul, and the Sturgis Public Library. The Sturgis Kiwanis Food Pantry withdrew its application prior to the final vote.

Shelter representatives said the funding will go directly toward constructing much-needed indoor-outdoor kennels, a project aimed at improving safety and daily operations.

“We plan on doing our indoor-outdoor kennels, which we so need,” said shelter representative Trishelle Tammi. “That’s what we’re going to use it all for. It’s a safety issue, and we’re going to make it right.”

The project is expected to include 10 or more kennels, along with a designated “bite hold” kennel used to safely monitor animals involved in incidents, such as bites, during a required observation period.

The need for improved facilities became clear after a recent tragic incident involving two dogs. “They actually escaped their kennels and got to each other,” Trishelle said. “They came from an abusive life and were fighting dogs. Once they got to each other, there was no letting go. We tried to separate them, but it was too late. We couldn’t save them.”

The new kennel design will allow dogs access to both indoor and outdoor spaces, reducing the need for constant handling and lowering the risk of similar incidents. Outdoor areas will include concrete surfaces for sanitation, along with turf space.

Autumn Lyons, a shelter employee of more than five years, said the improvements have been needed for a long time.

“This donation is huge. I’ve been fighting for this since I started, it’s always been a need,” Lyons said. “Actually, my very first day, I got jumped by a dog on his way outside because it didn’t know me. From there on, I knew something different had to happen, because someone was going to get seriously injured.”

Lyons added the new setup will also improve quality of life for the animals. “The dogs are going to be happier because they can choose when they need to go outside,” she said. “They won’t have to wait for us. If it’s a nice day, they can be outside instead of us taking them out a few at a time.”

The shelter typically houses around nine dogs on average, with additional animals sometimes placed in foster care due to special needs. With only 11 kennels currently available, two of which must remain reserved for impound and bite-hold purposes, space and flexibility have been limited.

Key City Giving Gals founding board member Kerrie Applegate said the group continues to grow in both membership and impact within the Sturgis community.

“With the six meetings that we’ve had, we have grown from 113 members to about 223,” Applegate said. “After this meeting, we will have given more than $100,000 back into the community.”

Applegate said the impact of the donations goes far beyond the dollar amount. “I just can’t even imagine the impact that it’s made,” she said. “Like the animal shelter, they’re going to get the indoor-outdoor runs they need. Every project has mattered. The very first one, the rural ambulance, the impact that $11,000 made was huge. It’s just amazing to get together with all these ladies and see what we can do.”

Shelter staff said the kennel project will make a significant difference in safety, efficiency and animal care, marking a major step forward for the facility.

title: Black Hills Pioneer at 150: 1893
description: Pioneer file graphic
_full_text:
Black Hills Pioneer at 150 years
Pioneer file graphic 

Black Hills Pioneer
The Black Hills Pioneer is the oldest continuously operating business in West River Dakota Territory; and 2026 marks our 150th year of telling the stories of the Black Hills. Over time our newspaper has absorbed the titles of numerous other publications, and we would like to share some of the more compelling stories from those early editions.
 Every Saturday we will be publishing stories from our archives, focusing heavily on the gold rush era of the 1800s, starting with our very first publication on June 8, 1876. These stories are re-printed exactly as they were first published, leaving in place the historic typos, grammar, style and terminology – politically correct or otherwise. It is our belief that history is sacrosanct and should never be edited to reflect the societal norms of the present.
 We hope you enjoy this historic journey. It is an honor and a privilege to be a trusted permanent record keeper of the Black Hills community; we look forward to the next 150 years of history in the making in the magnificent Black Hills.

~ January 11, 1893 • Queen City Mail ~

The State School of Mines

The State School of Mines at Rapid City is now open for the fall term. Tuition is free to all residents of the state. A practical education is thus placed within easy reach. Commercial work, as heretofore, receives careful attention. For further information apply to:

W. P. Headden, Ph. D., Dean.

~ March 8, 1893 • Queen City Mail ~

Spearfish Normal School

There appears to be a diversity of statements going the rounds relative to the amount appropriated by the state for the Spearfish normal school. The Pierre papers put the amount at $28,800 for the two years 1893 and 1894. The Rapid Journal says it is $14,600, and the Deadwood papers, to avoid the possibility of making a mistake, fail to name the Spearfish normal school as having received any appropriation. Of course the omission was only an oversight, without “malice aforethought.” But Spearfish will manage to pull along in some way, if our normal school gets the appropriation.

~ June 7, 1893 • Queen City Mail ~

Another Business Block

Star & Bullock Make Arrangements for the Erection of Another Business Block.

It Will Be on Sixth Street, and Have a Frontage of One Hundred Feet.

When Seth Bullock, of the firm of Star & Bullock, was in town last week he made preliminary arrangements for building a business house adjoining their building on the corner of Sixth and I street, and running south 100 feet on Sixth and seventy-five feet east. The building will be of stone or brick, and taken in connection with the building now occupied by Valentine & Wolzmuth, hardware, and Charles Pierson, groceries, will be the largest business house in the city. Star & Bullock own a considerable quantity of real estate in Spearfish, and they are sagacious enough to know that now is the time to make improvements, which will give good returns. There never has been a time in the history of Spearfish when there was so much demand for good buildings and never a time when money could be invested to a better advantage. The B. & M. is now practically here. The chlorination mill is keeping step with the progress of the railroad, and it is but the question of a few months more when it will be turning out golden bricks to ass to the world’s wealth. These improvements are only initiatory to a system of works that will surprise our neighbors. No city with the natural advantages, which cluster around the Queen City ever, got one railroad but it was quickly followed by others. One plant for the reduction of the endless bodies of rich ore of gold, silver, tin and other less valuable metals, will surely bring more of the same sort. The unexcelled waterpower of the Spearfish River offers inducements to manufactures that are not to be found in any other place in South Dakota. With a volume of water equal to 5,000 miners inches, fed by never failing springs, and with an average fall of eighty feet to the mile for distance of more than eighteen miles, it needs but to be seen, and capital will be forth coming for its utilization for manufacturing purposes. Spearfish is located at the natural outlet for all the rich ores of Bald Mountain, Mineral hill, Carbonate and the Nigger Hill tin and gold mines. With all these facts properly presented to enterprising capitalists we will not to be compelled to wait long for further developments.

~ June 8, 1893 • The Daily Deadwood Pioneer-Times ~

The Transcontinental Celebration

St. Paul, June 7. — The entire northwest is taking part in the three days’ festivities which begun today in honor of the completion of the Great Northern and in honor of the president of the transcontinental route. It will surpass in interest the celebrations, which marked the completion of the Union Pacific, the Northern Pacific and the Canadian Pacific. Towns and cities all along the line, from the eastern to the western terminus are taking some part in the great demonstration. Many of the principal cities on the western division of the road sent floats for the parade of today. Outside cities in Minnesota and the two Dakotas came forward to lend a helping hand, and among the first to declare herself was Minneapolis. The five state floats, representing Minnesota, Wisconsin, the two Dakotas, Montana and Washington, showed in the parade today are artistic and beautiful designs. They were given one of the most honorable places in the line.

~ December 20, 1893 • Queen City Mail ~

Local Briefs.

Prof. F. L. Cook is in receipt of a letter from Gov. Sheldon in which the following reference is made to our state normal school: “Mr. Shannon, president of the board of regents, gave me his experience in the Hills in detail and spoke in the highest terms of your school. He says it is a model of excellence, and I do not think there is any man in this state, at least there is none within my acquaintance, better qualified that Mr. Shannon to understand thoroughly the character of any institution of learning which he may visit and inspect.”

title: News and Notes
description: Legal changes
_full_text:
Legal changes

STURGIS — Changes are underway for the judicial system in Meade County and the Fourth Circuit Court, Chief Deputy State’s Attorney Ken Chleborad said Tuesday. Gov. Larry Rhoden’s recent appointment of Fourth Circuit Magistrate Judge Chad Callahan as a circuit court judge leaves a vacancy, when Callahan moves to the new position, June 9. Chleborad said the United Judicial System hopes to fill the magistrate position in time for the Sturgis Motorcycle Rally. Additionally, Chleborad said Meade County is accepting applications for a deputy state’s attorney. 

— Wendy Pitlick  

Busy election season 

STURGIS — When the Meade County Auditor’s office opened its doors for absentee voting on Friday, Auditor Dawn Christenson said about 20 voters immediately cast their ballots. In Meade County this year, Christenson said there are 157 different ballot styles, for 41 different precincts. That includes different ballot styles for the Republican primary, non-political races, and the federal races. “There will be some precincts that have six ballots and there will be some precincts that have just three. It depends on the splits within the precinct,” Christenson said. “Our team has learned a lot and has worked well.” 

— Wendy Pitlick 

Lawrence Co. passes amended ordinance regulating open burns

DEADWOOD — Lawrence County Commissioners approved the second reading of an amendment to Ordinance 2025-01, regulating open burns in areas north of Interstate 90 in the county, at the Tuesday meeting. 

Amendments to the ordinance prohibit any open burns while the fire danger is considered high or above. 

All open burns must be attended and must be fully extinguished and cold to the touch before left unattended. 

Prior to commencing an open fire, it is required that the person planning the fire call Lawrence County dispatch to approve the burn date and location.

— Sara Morgan

85 Charles St. in Deadwood to receive new railing

DEADWOOD — 85 Charles St., in Deadwood, was approved to receive a porch railing as part of the new deck at the April 20 city commission meeting. The railing will cost $11,640.72.

The property was purchased in June 2024 by the city’s historic preservation department with the intention of restoring the exterior and saving an historic building that sat neglected for 2 decades. The interior of the building will be completely gutted before being placed back on the market. 

The city has so far invested more than $250,000 in the property. 

— Sara Morgan

Pickleball play

LEAD — The city of Lead has scheduled times for pickleball open play. The intent of scheduled open play is to set a time aside when players of every skill level will have someone to play with. Pickleball players are encouraged to attend, and extra paddles and balls are provided, with instruction available if needed. Scheduled open play is from 9-11 a.m. Tuesday and Thursday, and from 5-7 p.m. on Wednesdays, May 2 through October. 

— Wendy Pitlick

title: BHSU celebrates student service  at annual volunteer awards
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: Attendees gather onstage following the 34th annual Student Volunteer Awards Ceremony on April 20 at Black Hills State University, celebrating students and organizations recognized for their service on campus and in the community. BHSU photo by Sierra Hopp
_full_text:
BHSU celebrates student service  at annual volunteer awards
Attendees gather onstage following the 34th annual Student Volunteer Awards Ceremony on April 20 at Black Hills State University, celebrating students and organizations recognized for their service on campus and in the community. BHSU photo by Sierra Hopp 

SPEARFISH — Black Hills State University honored students and organizations at the annual Student Volunteer Awards Ceremony in the Jacket Legacy Room on April 20. 

This event, organized by the BHSU Student Engagement and Leadership Center, recognizes and expresses gratitude toward student leaders and organization volunteers for their dedication and commitment on campus and in the community. 

Quinn Kiefer was named outstanding new student, Liz Parsons received an award for outstanding involvement in residents’ life, and both Ragan Rae and Tam Smith were recognized for outstanding leadership in residence life.

Women in STEM (WiSTEM) and Psi Chi’s Slime Making and Three Peaks Review’s Found Poetry were recognized as outstanding programs. The Campus Activities Board’s Rake-A-Thon was named Outstanding Community Service Project, and the Campus Activities Board earned Most Improved Student Organization, and Bailey Smith was named outstanding volunteer. WiSTEM was recognized as an outstanding student organization.

The Dean of Students Rising Star Award was given to Jocelyn Corrales.

Alyssa Diaz, Ashlee Hanson, Cassidy Van Zee, Chance Robinson, Jalen Hohm, Kaitlyn Thompson, Karanda Kay Davis, Kaylee Jackson, Kendra Palmlund, Liza Stahl, Muira Cronin, Oliva Schaefers, Ryken Falkenburg Brown, and Teddi Dwyer each received leadership certificate recognition.  

Dr. Jane Klug, dean of students, announced the nominees for the Jane Klug Student Leadership Scholarship. Students must be an award nominee and have at least a 2.5 GPA to qualify. Sage Wilson was announced as the recipient. 

Dr. Rich Loose, associate provost and associate vice president for academic affairs, announced 3 student organizations that will be recognized this fall by the South Dakota Board of Regents for their contributions during the 2025-26 academic year.

The Board of Regents Award for Academic Excellence was awarded to Buzz Marketing for its contributions toward various enrollment management initiatives at BHSU. Buzz Marketing has completed a variety of applied marketing and market research projects that support university initiatives and drive enrollment growth.

The Board of Regents Community Service Award was given to the Residence Hall Association for their efforts in the RA Auction. Their 12th annual auction broke past fundraising records by more than $100, and the money was donated to the Spearfish Volunteer Fire Department. The Board of Regents Organizational Leadership Award recognized the members of the Psychology Club for their contributions to fostering awareness, advocacy, and connection among the BHSU community, with one of their most impactful contributions being “Take Back the Night.”

title: BHSU launches ‘Taking It to the next level’ capital campaign
byline: Pioneer staff reports
description: Vice President for University Advancement Steve Meeker announced the Black Hills State University Foundation’s public launch of the “Taking It to the Next Level” Capital Campaign, a multiyear effort to raise $58 million to expand student opportunities, strengthen academic programs, and support the university’s long-term future. Photo courtesy of Rylan Bruns, BHSU
_full_text:
BHSU launches ‘Taking It to the next level’ capital campaign
Vice President for University Advancement Steve Meeker announced the Black Hills State University Foundation’s public launch of the “Taking It to the Next Level” Capital Campaign, a multiyear effort to raise $58 million to expand student opportunities, strengthen academic programs, and support the university’s long-term future. Photo courtesy of Rylan Bruns, BHSU 

SPEARFISH — The Black Hills State University Foundation officially announced the public launch of its “Taking It to the Next Level” capital campaign Monday, launching a multiyear initiative designed to expand student opportunities, strengthen academic programs, and secure the university’s future for generations to come. 

The campaign, which began in May 2022, has already generated significant support. Through the generosity of 6,850 donors, the campaign has raised $54.1 million toward its $58 million goal. Of the total raised, $22.3 million has come through estate gifts and $32.1 million will be permanently endowed to support long-term sustainability and impact. 

Campaign funds have been directed toward key priorities that directly impact student success and institutional growth, including: $49.3 million in scholarships to expand access to higher education; $9.3 million in operational support, enhancing programs and campus initiatives; and $1.5 million for a nursing addition at the BHSU Rapid City location.

“We are not starting from zero — we are building on incredible momentum,” said Steve Meeker, vice president of university advancement. “With less than $4 million remaining to reach our goal, this campaign is a testament to what is possible when people come together with a shared vision.”

At the event announcing the campaign, speakers highlighted how the campaign will prioritize funding for student scholarships while also supporting essential university operations that enhance academic programs and the overall student experience. 

“Through this campaign, we are affirming our shared belief in the power of education, the promise of our students, and the lasting impact this institution has on the Black Hills region and beyond,” said Steve Elliot, university president. 

More details are expected to be announced in the coming months. To stay informed and get involved, community members, alumni, and supporters are encouraged to follow the BHSU Foundation on social media. 

For more information about the capital campaign or how to support the university, visit BHSUFoundaiton.org.

title: Onsite engineer to oversee Haines Ave. reconstruction
byline: By Wendy Pitlick Black Hills Pioneer
description: STURGIS — The Meade County Commission will have an engineer onsite for the duration of the North Haines Avenue reconstruction project, officials say.
_full_text:
BHPioneerLogo

STURGIS — The Meade County Commission will have an engineer onsite for the duration of the North Haines Avenue reconstruction project, officials say. 

On Tuesday, the commission approved a nearly $160,000 increase for the project, and an amendment that calls for HDR Engineering to provide onsite engineering services for the project. The amendment calls for daily construction inspection reports throughout each phase of construction for the project that starts this month and is scheduled to be complete in July. 

Western Construction is expected to start the $3.1 million project May 4, and the road will be closed to thru traffic May 5. Meade County Highway Superintendent Troy Eastman told commissioners earlier this month that once crews start putting down base stabilization, the road will be closed to all but local traffic, for at least 72 hours. Commissioner Rich Liggett took that one step farther, saying that people who do not live in the immediate vicinity of the project should plan alternate routes for the duration of the project. 

“People in the Golden Valley area need to drive around,” Liggett said. “I want to stress it is not open for traffic during that time. Anybody that lives to the north of the project needs to go around. Go down Erickson Ranch Road. Go down Elk Vale.” 

The project is located from a half mile south of Peterson Road to about a quarter mile north of Husker Place, and about 4 miles of the remaining Haines Avenue stretch.
